clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Interview Opening and Framing
clips.summary: Interviewer David Cline opens the May 11, 2013 Seattle session for the Civil Rights History Project, noting the Library of Congress and Smithsonian NMAAHC sponsorship and cameraman John Bishop, then invites Aaron Dixon to begin with the family into which he was born.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Maternal Line Out of Mississippi
clips.summary: Dixon traces his mother's side from a formerly enslaved ancestor born in 1867 through his great-great-grandmother Emma, who could find work only at the laundry under Jim Crow, and recounts his grandmother's mixed parentage with a Jewish father and her mother's ostracism in Mississippi.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Roy Sledge and a Mother's Music
clips.summary: Dixon describes his grandparents Roy Sledge, a part-Native American blacksmith's son who worked as a Pullman Porter and battled alcoholism before retiring from the post office, and Josephine, and notes his mother became a child piano prodigy who graduated high school at sixteen.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Father's Kentucky Roots and Brooksville
clips.summary: Dixon turns to his father's Kentucky family: a plantation inherited through a slaveholder relationship, two great-great-uncles among the first Black soldiers to volunteer for the Union who received land and founded the town of Brooksville, and a family line some traced back to Scottish royalty.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Father Trains as an Artist
clips.summary: In Chicago, Dixon's grandfather Elmer worked for a wealthy Jewish man and kept his wife from having to work, while Dixon's father won a young scholarship to the Art Institute, captained the ROTC, and rushed to enlist after Pearl Harbor only to be told the service did not take boys like him.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Drafted Into a Segregated Army
clips.summary: Dixon recounts his father being drafted and sent to Mississippi, where conditions grew so dangerous that he wrote his mother and she in turn wrote Eleanor Roosevelt. He relates the story of a white farmer who pointed a shotgun to bar the Black soldiers from crossing his field, and the barn they burned that night in response.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: A Sheriff's Threat in the Deep South
clips.topic: Father's Wartime Danger in the South
clips.summary: Dixon tells of his father insisting on walking a light-skinned woman home through a Southern town in uniform, drawing a town sheriff's warning not to be there when he returned, and stopping at the post office to write his mother that his life was in danger before making it back to base.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Okinawa, AWOL, and a Changed Man
clips.summary: After a barracks riot over a canceled furlough, Dixon's father shipped to the Pacific, fought in the Philippines and Okinawa, and nearly shot a Marine who mutilated a dead Japanese woman. He went AWOL, sheltered with a Japanese family, and returned home with his view of the nation transformed, drawn toward Paul Robeson's youth brigade and the Communist Party.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: From Champaign to Seattle
clips.summary: Dixon's parents married, had children, and his father worked as a technical illustrator at Chanute Air Force Base in Champaign, Illinois, where men in black suits Dixon assumed were FBI once came to the door. A Boeing job offer drew the family to Seattle in 1957 or 1958, a city his father remembered fondly from the war years.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Seattle's Wartime Black Community
clips.summary: Dixon and Cline discuss how Seattle's Black population grew during the war as people came to work at Todd Shipyards and the airplane factories, settling in the Central Area alongside Chinese, Filipino, Japanese, and Native American neighbors, before the Dixons bought a house in the Madrona neighborhood.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Growing Up in Madrona
clips.summary: Dixon paints Madrona and its neighboring districts (Madison Valley, Leschi, Mount Baker) as a beautiful, multiracial enclave of big houses where Black, Filipino, Japanese, and Chinese families went to school together and Chinese-owned grocers extended credit, while his mother still faced employment discrimination, notably at Frederick & Nelson.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: A Household of Art and Music
clips.summary: Dixon describes a home shaped by his father's love of other cultures: a multiethnic folk-dancing group that met at their house, a grandmother in Chicago who required every child to play an instrument, and exposure to Shakespeare, opera, jazz, classical music, and Paul Robeson recordings. His father showed his artwork at the Frye Museum.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Sports and the Racism Outside the Cocoon
clips.summary: Dixon recalls playing football, baseball, basketball, and tennis at the park across the street, his brother Michael earning a UCLA tennis scholarship, and the racial tension when their teams played in Ballard and Queen Anne, neighborhoods Black youth could not safely enter. The cocoon of Madrona always had racism pressing at its edges.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Stories at the Kitchen Table
clips.summary: Race consciousness came through oral tradition rather than direct instruction, with stories from grandparents and great-grandparents whenever the family was back in Chicago. Dixon recounts his parents' anger when he wanted to be a policeman at thirteen and to join the Marines for Vietnam at sixteen, his father drawing on his own military experience.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Marching With King, Working to Be Independent
clips.summary: Around twelve or thirteen Dixon marched with Martin Luther King Jr. during King's Seattle visit and demonstrated when George Wallace came to town, possibly drawn in through his church. He recounts working long hours from his junior year, buying his own clothes and records, determined to be independent, and graduating high school in 1967 by the skin of his teeth.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Stokely Carmichael Lights the Fuse
clips.summary: Turned down by Tennessee State, Dixon was fired up when Stokely Carmichael came to Seattle, started wearing the same glasses, and grew in his understanding of America's racial politics against a backdrop of the 1965 Los Angeles riots, the yearly summer uprisings, and the assassination of John F. Kennedy that had made his father weep.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Ray-Bans, Theater, and a Walked-Off Job
clips.summary: Newly radicalized, Dixon blew up at a supervisor who told him to remove his Ray-Bans and walked off a job he had held three years. He found part-time work performing racial skits for a poverty program, joined playwright Aaron Dumas's play General's Coup about a Black army seizing the country, and deepened his own poetry writing.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Into the University of Washington
clips.summary: A poetry reading won Dixon a five-hundred-dollar scholarship, and through an Urban League college program run by a Jewish woman named Mrs. Richmond he scored well on the SAT and entered the University of Washington, arriving as the movement around him was catching steam.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Founding the UW Black Student Union
clips.summary: Dixon helped start the first Black Student Union at the University of Washington, meeting in the HUB as Ron Dellums and San Francisco State organizers came up amid the San Francisco State takeover, and the group helped form a BSU at Garfield High School. He first heard of the Black Panther Party's 1967 march on the Sacramento capitol around this time.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: The Franklin High School Demonstration
clips.summary: Over a racial incident in which a Black student was expelled while a white student stayed, and a lack of Black or Asian staff, the BSU marched into Franklin High School, drove the principal out, and rallied the students in the auditorium. A week later Dixon was arrested at home for unlawful assembly.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: King's Assassination in a Jail Cell
clips.topic: MLK Assassination and Turn to Arms
clips.summary: On April 4, 1968, while Dixon sat in the King County jail, Walter Cronkite announced that Martin Luther King Jr. had been assassinated and riots broke out across the country. That night Dixon resolved that his picket sign would be replaced by a gun, an idea already planted through earlier organizing with SNCC and a contact who kept guns.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Little Bobby Hutton's Funeral
clips.summary: At the Black Student Union conference in San Francisco, Dixon went to the funeral of slain Panther Little Bobby Hutton, stopping for berets to fit in, and saw Marlon Brando out front talking with Bobby Seale. Inside, Hutton's mother wailed over the body as Panthers lined the walls with arms folded.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: The Shootout That Killed Hutton
clips.summary: Dixon recounts the circumstances of Hutton's death: after King's assassination, Eldridge Cleaver led some Panthers into a confrontation with police while Huey and Bobby were jailed. Cornered in an abandoned house and tear-gassed, Hutton surrendered and was told to run, then shot some twenty-five times, while Cleaver was wounded and David Hilliard and eighteen others were arrested.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Asking Bobby Seale for a Chapter
clips.summary: After Bobby Seale's emotionally charged speech that evening, Dixon and his brother made a beeline to him and asked for a Black Panther Party chapter in Seattle. Dixon reflects on why the radical Black Student Union invited the party, given the BPP's growing influence and its central role in the San Francisco State strike through figures like Emory Douglas and George Murray.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: The Founding Meeting in His Parents' Home
clips.summary: Bobby Seale and George Murray came to Seattle and met with about twenty prospective members over two or three days at the Dixon family home. Seale set the membership bar at two weapons and two thousand rounds of ammunition, assigned a twenty-five-book reading list and two hours of daily study, and the group pointed at Dixon, nineteen and still a student, as their captain.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Summoned to Oakland in Uniform
clips.summary: A week later Dixon was called to Oakland by the Central Committee, taking his first airplane flight in the full Panther uniform: black leather jacket, powder blue shirt, black beret and boots. Picked up by Vietnam veterans Robert Bay and Tommy Jones, he met the National Secretary and was taken to the home of captains Landon and Randy Williams, Special Forces veterans who showed off their armament.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Bitter Dog and the Shot-Out Television
clips.summary: Dixon visited Huey in jail, sold papers, met Stokely Carmichael and Don Cox at a Central Committee meeting where he was introduced as a new captain, then drank the street concoction later dubbed Panther Piss and smoked at the captains' house, where Landon shot out the television because the Indians always got killed in the Cowboys and Indians shows.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Baptism at Seventh and Wood
clips.summary: Two weeks after Hutton's death, Dixon and young Panther Orlando Harris cursed a passing police car, drawing reinforcements to Seventh and Wood, the same corner where Huey Newton's shootout had happened. The Panthers spread out armed as a tense standoff built, broken only when Landon tripped over a garbage can lid and the police silently drove off. Dixon calls it his baptism into the party.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Named First Captain Outside California
clips.summary: At the Saturday Panther meeting at St. Augustine's Church, with about 125 members from across the Bay Area, Bobby Seale taught the Ten-Point Program and introduced Dixon as first captain of the Seattle chapter, the first BPP office outside California. A false alarm sent them speeding back to the church armed before Dixon flew home to begin organizing.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Answering the Community's Calls
clips.summary: The Seattle chapter opened its office and took over 300 applications in a month, carrying legally and illegally acquired guns openly through the streets. The community began calling with domestic violence and landlord disputes, including a case where Panthers carried a removed door back and reattached it for a woman with seven children, until Bobby Seale ordered them to cut back.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Reading, Discipline, and the Lumpen
clips.summary: Dixon describes the party's political education: Mao's Red Book, the three rules and seven points, Fanon, Black Skin White Masks, and Black Rage, plus James Baldwin and Richard Wright. He explains how the required study lifted even Bobby Hutton to literacy, and how a combination of lumpen proletariat and college students came together in the membership.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Armed Intervention at Rainier Beach
clips.summary: After repeated pleading calls from mothers whose children were being beaten by bat-wielding white students at Rainier Beach High School, thirteen armed Panthers went out, kept their rifles legally unloaded by the chamber rule, and confronted the principal, warning him to protect the children. Dixon calls it the first major incident marking the chapter's emergence.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Robberies, Guns, and the First Purge
clips.summary: Dixon acknowledges members who joined for personal reasons and began robbing banks and stores, the same pattern emerging in Oakland because the party could not provide income. From prison Huey ordered a purge; both Oakland and Seattle expelled people, an upheaval that cost two lives and badly damaged the party's credibility.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: The U.N. Delegation and the SNCC Break
clips.summary: Dixon recounts the July plan for Bobby Seale, Eldridge Cleaver, and Emory Douglas to bring a free-Huey demonstration to the United Nations, with the SNCC coalition meant to provide entry through James Forman. A meeting at the SNCC office erupted into a near fistfight, the coalition died on the spot, and they turned instead to Mae Mallory for a small audience with a tenth-floor delegation.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: The Typewriter Setup and Seattle's First Riot
clips.summary: Back in Seattle, Dixon was arrested with Curtis Harris on a charge of stealing a typewriter, a setup arranged by an informer who had lured them to take it while plainclothes detectives watched. A Garfield High School rally and march on the jail erupted into Seattle's first major riot, three days of fire-bombing and overturned police cars, and Dixon was released at midnight.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Pork Chop Hill and the Summer of Sniping
clips.summary: Released into an ongoing riot, Dixon joined armed Panthers heading toward Garfield until a helicopter spotlight, shot out by a Panther, drove them into a sympathizer's house as police surrounded the area. The standoff escalated into a sniping and fire-bombing campaign, with Madrona Hill nicknamed Pork Chop Hill after the Korean War battle, lasting through the summer of 1968.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: The Metro Bus Standoff
clips.summary: Dixon explains the Panthers refused to negotiate with police but met a Seattle Metro delegation over bus parking: when a driver tested them by parking in front of the office during a meeting, Elmer and another Panther boarded and threw the driver's keys onto the roof. Dixon turned himself in on the resulting charge and a Black judge let him off.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: First Casualties of the Chapter
clips.summary: In the fall of 1968 the Seattle chapter lost its first member, a seventeen-year-old shot in the back by police while defending his mother, a part-time Panther and hustler. A December casualty followed when a member was sent to rob a grocery store and was killed by the owner, signaling to Dixon that something hidden was being orchestrated against them.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Acquitted, and the Two Camps Debate
clips.summary: At the December trial the prosecution's secret witness never appeared and Dixon was found not guilty. After another purge, the chapter opened its first breakfast program at Madrona Presbyterian Church in the winter of 1969, sparking debate within the party between those committed to armed guerrilla warfare, championed by Eldridge Cleaver, and those, like Bobby Seale, who favored community programs.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Hoover's Three Targets and a Justice Department Warning
clips.summary: With Bobby Seale kidnapped and tried in Chicago and Huey in prison, the party came under attack. Dixon recounts Hoover's vow to eliminate the Panthers by 1969, the January assassinations of Bunchy Carter and John Huggins and the December killing of Fred Hampton, and a Black Justice Department official from Seattle who tipped the chapter off that a raid meant to kill them was coming.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Sandbags and the Survival Programs
clips.summary: The chapter moved into a community-center duplex, fortified it with sandbags and steel, and spent the brothers' final 1,700-dollar financial aid checks on guns and protective gear. Alongside defense they expanded to five breakfast locations and started Seattle's first free medical clinic, first food bank, free legal aid, and a bussing-to-prisons program with a donated 35-passenger bus.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: An Intense Communal Life
clips.summary: Married in 1968 with a son born in 1969 and a wife in the party, Dixon describes selling a hundred papers a day, running early-morning breakfast programs with no government funding, eating communally, attending weapons and education classes, and pulling two-hour security shifts through the night, a constant, fearless, high-energy lifestyle with only twenty to twenty-five members doing the work of hundreds.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Wes Uhlman Blocks the ATF Raid
clips.summary: After raids killed Fred Hampton in Chicago and besieged the bunkered Los Angeles office until the Panthers ran out of ammunition, the ATF came to Seattle, but Mayor Wes Uhlman refused to allow it, citing the chapter's deep community ties and its sickle-cell testing, including the first mass testing at Walla Walla State Penitentiary. Dixon notes the city had only one Black officer.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Nixon's Enemy List and a Bounty
clips.topic: Nixon's Enemy List and the Bounty
clips.summary: Dixon describes how other mayors followed Uhlman in refusing raids, how Uhlman was frozen out at the U.S. Conference of Mayors under Nixon, and his own place on Nixon's enemy list. The Seattle Police put a 25,000-dollar contract on his head during the summer of 1968, when Time magazine ranked the city first in fire-bombings and second in sniping, hoping his removal would end the campaign.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Stolen Dynamite and an Escape in the Dark
clips.summary: Dixon recounts how dynamite the chapter had obtained vanished, taken by police informants, and how a couple of days later those informants and Curtis Harris offered to walk him home, then fired at a passing police car and disappeared, leaving him to flee through backyards. A man who knew his family pulled him into a house just as police searched the area.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: The Exploding Shotgun Rigged to Kill
clips.summary: In the fall of 1971, test-firing the chapter's weapons, Dixon raised his riot-18 shotgun to his shoulder, then an instinct told him to lower it. As the firing pin struck, the gun exploded and blew his arm halfway off, leaving him with a steel rod, two plates, and four surgeries. Tests showed the gunpowder had been replaced with high explosives, another assassination attempt; he was back at the office within three days.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: The Newton and Cleaver Split
clips.summary: Dixon describes Huey's release, the FBI letters fostering distrust between Newton and Cleaver, and the rupture when the New York Panthers chose to take orders from the Weather Underground and Newton expelled them. As officials were summoned to Oakland to declare sides, most of New York and New Jersey went with Cleaver, fighting broke out in the streets, and Newton chose not to escalate into all-out war.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Centralization and Elmer's Pardon
clips.summary: To strengthen Oakland as a base, the centralization campaign brought Panthers from across the country. With his brother Elmer imprisoned in Oregon over a leather-coat robbery, Dixon, his father, and his attorney won a first-of-its-kind gubernatorial pardon through a petition drive before relocating to Oakland, leaving a skeleton crew in Seattle that kept the clinic and programs running.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Ten Thousand Bags of Groceries
clips.summary: In Oakland the party entered electoral politics, first backing Shirley Chisholm's presidential run, then mounting the Bobby Seale for Mayor and Elaine Brown for City Council campaign. Five offices and a kickoff at the Oakland Auditorium gave away 10,000 grocery bags, each with a chicken still frozen the next day; Dixon recalls the floor covered with empty bags afterward.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Sidelined as Officer of the Day
clips.summary: Confined to the office as officer of the day, Dixon felt Newton was trying to get rid of him by keeping him off the security squad, telling others Dixon had too much power and commanded too much respect in the party. Dixon notes Newton ran off many good people the same way.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Building a Political Machine
clips.summary: Though Bobby Seale's mayoral bid fell short, Dixon explains how close it came and how it built a genuine political machine, registering and turning out hundreds of voters, many of them elderly people who had never voted, with vans carrying people from their homes to the polls. Politicians began coming to the party because it could deliver the vote.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Never Wedded to One Tactic
clips.summary: Dixon reflects on the party's adaptability, moving from armed revolution to survival programs to electoral politics, and its broad coalitions with the Brown Berets, Red Guard Party, American Indian Movement, Young Patriots, SDS, and Peace and Freedom Party, even as the left criticized both the coalitions and the survival programs as insufficiently revolutionary.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: The Oakland Community School and the Lamp Post
clips.summary: Dixon catalogs Elaine Brown's rebuilding: the Oakland Community School ranked among the country's best, a martial arts program on the cover of Black Belt Magazine, the Bay Area's first gang intervention program funded by a CETA grant, a seniors-against-a-fearful-environment escort program, the Lamp Post restaurant, a promotion business bringing Marvin Gaye and Chaka Khan to Oakland, and a portfolio of property.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Drugs, Extortion, and the Nation of Islam
clips.topic: Drugs and the Nation of Islam
clips.summary: Dixon describes a contentious relationship with the Nation of Islam tied to Huey's drug operation, recounting Newton's meeting with Louis Farrakhan dividing San Francisco and Oakland, his ban on heroin but tolerance of cocaine, and the policy of forcing after-hours clubs and pimps to pay the party, along with a movie theater acquired from the Mafia.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Elaine Brown at the Height of Power
clips.summary: Dixon describes Elaine Brown becoming one of the most powerful figures in California, courted by Leo McCarthy and Willie Brown, cultivating corporations that would write 400,000-dollar checks for the school, and securing six judgeships from Governor Jerry Brown by naming six Black attorneys, giving the party real influence over Oakland's judiciary.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Electing Oakland's First Black Mayor
clips.summary: Dixon recounts running Lionel Wilson's campaign and putting the first Black mayor into office in Oakland, then placing two Panther women on the port commission while a mostly-women Central Committee effectively ran the city. He describes the shift from the Panther uniform to suits after the 1969 order to stop dressing in ways that made them targets.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Felix Mitchell and the Redevelopment Fight
clips.summary: Dixon recounts Elaine Brown becoming president of the Oakland Redevelopment Corporation, forcing in 1,000 low-income housing units and getting Jerry Brown to release freeway-ramp funds, even as the chapter mobilized against Felix Mitchell's well-armed drug gang that had taken over a housing project near the school, a confrontation Dixon expected to turn bloody.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Newton's Return and the Party's End
clips.topic: Newton's Return and the Party's Collapse
clips.summary: Dixon describes Elaine Brown paying Huey's way back from Cuba to stand trial as a fatal mistake: Newton wanted her money and power, forced her out, resumed cocaine, and ordered a trial witness eliminated. The botched hit killed a Panther instead, whose body and abandoned M-16s surfaced in the Oakland Tribune, spiraling the party downhill to its effective end.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Dumping the Arsenal in the Bay
clips.summary: As a congressional investigation convened, Dixon's last assignment was to destroy the party's illegal weaponry. He spent two days crawling through crawl spaces and attics gathering machine guns, fifty-caliber and anti-aircraft weapons, M-16s, and AK-47s accumulated over the years, and dumping them off the Marin bridge at three in the morning in the longest U-Haul he could rent.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Leaving on His Own Terms
clips.summary: After a confrontation with Bill Cosby in which he had no defense for Huey, Dixon left the party, later warned that Newton had planned to have him killed. Unlike most who slipped away at night, Dixon arranged a year's leave of absence through Big Bob Heard, reflecting that none of them, with over thirty members killed between 1967 and 1972, had ever expected to live long.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Cocaine, Embezzlement, and a Year on the Run
clips.summary: Unable to imagine returning to college after life on the other side of the law and addicted to the adrenaline, Dixon got involved in the criminal life, selling cocaine and committing embezzlement that earned 100,000 dollars and a year as an FBI fugitive. He was eventually caught, served about a year, and came out determined to put his life back together.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Becoming the Best Counselor on Staff
clips.summary: Dixon describes becoming a substance-abuse counselor in Oakland despite never having used drugs himself, winning over skeptical clients through a natural gift for empathy and bringing Panther-style programs into the work, until an evaluation meeting where the controlling executive director of the West Oakland Health Center insulted him and Dixon cursed him out and walked off the job.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: The Crack Epidemic Hits Chocolate City
clips.summary: After a stint on a Northern California marijuana farm, Dixon returned to Seattle as the crack cocaine epidemic struck, the subject of his planned next book. He describes watching it descend like a black cloud on Oakland, the vibrant 1970s Chocolate City with its Black mayor and nightclubs, devastating families, friends, and former party members, then following him to Seattle a month later.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Nonprofit Work and a Finished Book
clips.summary: Dixon describes the nonprofit he started about ten years earlier: transition housing for 18-to-24-year-olds, a youth leadership project across five schools, Seattle's first high-school media literacy program, and trips with youth to a farm in Brazil. Burned out and a poor administrator who fell behind on taxes, he separated from the organization in August and released his book on September 17.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Why Things Got Worse
clips.summary: Asked about his legacy, Dixon says that although the health clinic survives, police brutality and community conditions are far worse because the unity among Black and working-class people is gone. He attributes the decline to the individualism, narcissism, and materialism pushed since the 1970s, Reagan-era neoliberalism and deregulation, and the Reagan-Bush cocaine pipeline into Black communities.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Mass Incarceration and a Lost Value System
clips.summary: Dixon points to two and a half million people in prison, disproportionately Latino and Black men, and the crack addiction that destroyed families and community. He contrasts this with the cultural value system of his own two-parent upbringing, where families stayed together for the children, a sacrifice and commitment he says no longer exists.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Closing: A Packed House at the UW
clips.summary: Dixon closes by describing a busy two weeks of speaking events in Seattle, including a well-attended University of Washington gathering where he brought Elaine Brown and Cha-Cha Jiménez of the Young Lords, and affirms he remains close with Elaine and Ericka Huggins, before the program's standard Library of Congress and Smithsonian sign-off.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Introduction and Mississippi Birth
clips.summary: David Cline opens the March 2, 2013 San Francisco interview with Reverend Dr. Amos C. Brown, who recounts being born in Jackson, Mississippi on February 20, 1941. He traces his great-great-grandfather Patrick Brown, born into slavery in Franklin County's Hamburg community east of Natchez.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: The Forks of the Road Market
clips.summary: Brown describes the area east of Natchez as a major dropping-off point for slaves from roughly 1820 to 1850, and recalls the 1830 cholera epidemic that quarantined incoming slaves at a site called Forks of the Road. He credits his father's sister with passing down this family history.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: George Lee, Emmett Till, and Eddie Noel
clips.summary: Brown notes his father succeeded Reverend George Lee, a Holmes County pastor murdered on Mother's Day 1955, the same year Emmett Till was killed. He then recounts the case of Eddie Noel, a 28-year-old World War II veteran and sharpshooter who shot a white man who had beaten him at a restaurant and evaded a posse of three to four hundred men for 18 days.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Arenia Mallory and the Saints School
clips.summary: Brown describes Arenia Mallory, first principal of the Saints Industrial School in Lexington and a vice president of the National Council of Negro Women founded by Mary McLeod Bethune. He notes the school was sponsored by the Church of God in Christ, founded by Bishop Charles Harrison Mason after the Azusa Street Revival.
related_events: National Council of Negro Women founding

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Eddie Noel's Surrender and Aftermath
clips.summary: Brown recounts that Mallory convinced Eddie Noel to surrender after learning he had killed two deputies in addition to the first man. The judge ordered Noel examined at the Whitfield Mental Institution, he was never tried or convicted, and was later found to descend from Governor Edmund Noel, who served from 1909 to 1913.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Named for the Prophet Amos
clips.summary: Brown recalls how the Eddie Noel and Emmett Till stories awakened him as a child of about eleven. He describes Miss Essie Collins, treasurer of the Jackson NAACP branch, telling him at age eight at Farish Street Baptist Church that his name meant the prophet who bears the burdens of the people.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Free by '63 and Sister Gloria
clips.summary: Brown describes learning of a 1954 Dallas NAACP meeting his sister Gloria attended with Medgar Evers, where board chairman Channing H. Tobias declared the goal of being free by 1963. Gloria returned predicting an end to segregation and the right to vote, which excited Brown about the coming change.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: First Youth Council and the Blue Goose
clips.summary: Brown organized the first NAACP Youth Council in Mississippi in 1955, and in the summer of 1956 Medgar Evers asked his mother if he could travel to the San Francisco National Convention. They drove Evers's 1955 Oldsmobile, the Blue Goose, cooling the engine with ice bags across the desert.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: First Hearing Martin Luther King
clips.summary: Brown recalls first hearing Martin Luther King Jr. on youth night at the 1956 convention, shortly after the Montgomery victory. King gave a panoramic history and closed by saying he had a dream that all of God's children would be significant on the Constitution's keyboard, a speech Brown still possesses a copy of.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Third Baptist Church and Its History
clips.summary: Brown recounts worshipping at Third Baptist Church during the 1956 trip and later returning as pastor to succeed Dr. Leon Howard Sullivan. He places its August 1, 1852 founding alongside the centennial of Caribbean abolition and the publication of Uncle Tom's Cabin, and credits Eliza Davis and her husband William with leaving white First Baptist over discrimination.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: A Father Who Led by Example
clips.summary: Brown explains that he had little direct conversation with his father, a busy country preacher who also worked as a janitor. Though aware of the movement and of discrimination, his father did not push him into activism, leaving Brown to find his own way through exposure to others.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Jackson Mentors and Movement Churches
clips.summary: Brown names the mentors who shaped him: Medgar Evers, John W. Dixon, Essie Collins, Mary Cox who refused to ride segregated buses, Carsie Hall the letter carrier turned attorney, and youth council advisor Samuel Bailey. He identifies the few Jackson churches that opened their doors: Farish Street Baptist, Pearl Street AME, College Hill Baptist, and New Mount Zion Baptist.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: The Sovereignty Commission File
clips.summary: Brown describes the Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission's ten-page file on him, opened in the 1980s, which reported that a vice president named LATAM at the Deposit Guaranty Bank broke confidentiality and told Mayor Thompson and the police chief about the bank account Brown had opened.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: The Cleveland Plain Dealer Interview
clips.summary: Brown recounts attending the 1958 NAACP convention in Cleveland with Medgar Evers and Joseph Broadwater, the year the Little Rock Nine were recognized. He gave an interview to the Cleveland Plain Dealer about Mississippi's double education system and unequal teacher pay, and someone forwarded the article to white superintendent Kirby P. Walker.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Barred from Jim Hill High School
clips.summary: Brown describes being told by principal Luther Marshall in fall 1959 that orders from the white and Black superintendents barred him from re-enrolling at Jim Hill High School and to keep his mouth shut. Medgar Evers got with his mother and father and threatened a lawsuit over the deterioration of facilities, forcing the school to let him back in.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Denied the Valedictorian Honor
clips.summary: Brown recounts that authorities abolished the student council he led to break his influence, though students elected him senior class president. He was valedictorian but was denied the honor and made salutatorian instead. Years later the principal broke down crying, explaining he had acted under pressure and threats to his job.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Spies and National Youth Chairman
clips.summary: Brown details how Sovereignty Commission spies recorded license plates at mass meetings and got activists fired from their jobs the following week. He notes his continued NAACP work and his 1959 election as National Chairman of the Youth Work Committee in New York, where he was photographed with Roy Wilkins and Dr. King.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Clara Luper Reports from Oklahoma City
clips.summary: Brown recalls presiding over the youth-unit reports at the 1959 New York meeting and receiving the report from Clara Luper, advisor of the Oklahoma City Youth Council, and Bob Rapose, its president. He marks August 28, 1958 as the first organized mass sit-down by young Black people under the NAACP umbrella.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: The True Origin of the Sit-Ins
clips.summary: Brown argues the student sit-in movement began in Oklahoma City on August 28, 1958, not Greensboro in 1960. He recounts that Ezell Blair Jr. carried the Oklahoma City model to A&T College and with three others sat down on February 1, 1960, after which the movement spread to Nashville, Tallahassee, Baton Rouge, and Atlanta. He credits National Youth Secretary Herbert Wright for laying the groundwork and notes the world press covered the story while American papers buried it.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Rosa Parks and the Birth of SCLC
clips.summary: Brown emphasizes that Rosa Parks's NAACP training as Montgomery branch secretary and youth council advisor prepared her for her stand. He recounts meeting King in San Francisco in 1956, and explains that after Alabama outlawed the NAACP in 1956, King and others organized the Southern Christian Leadership Conference under the protection of the faith community.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Getting to Know Medgar Evers
clips.summary: Brown describes Medgar Evers as a humble, warm, engaging man who became his surrogate father and elder brother, encouraging his talents and securing his travel to meetings. He recalls his mother never questioning Evers, and recounts the two of them disguising themselves as sharecroppers to hold mass meetings and investigate cases across the Mississippi Delta.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Fear and the Agitator Report
clips.summary: Brown reflects that he was too young and daring to feel fear, though his life was truly in harm's way. He recounts the Sovereignty Commission's summation labeling him a full-fledged agitator to be kept active in their files, with records of his arrests in Atlanta and Savannah.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Desegregating First Baptist Atlanta
clips.summary: Brown recounts desegregating First Baptist Church of Atlanta in 1961, which Dr. Benjamin Mays wrote about and which is now pastored by Reverend Charles Stanley. He met his wife, a Spelman student, there, after Morehouse and Georgia Tech students led a kneel-in and deacons physically threw them out of the church.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Committee on Appeal for Human Rights
clips.summary: Brown describes being the youngest in the room when Atlanta college presidents urged the students to explain their cause publicly. With Lonnie King, Charles Black, Herschelle Sullivan, Julian Bond, and Marian Wright Edelman, and funding from Drs. Rufus Clement and Benjamin Mays, he helped author the Committee on Appeal for Human Rights in the Atlanta Journal Constitution, then sat in and went to jail with King, A.D. King, Otis Moss, and David Satcher.
keywords: Committee on Appeal for Human Rights

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Rajmohan Gandhi and Moral Rearmament
clips.summary: Brown describes meeting Rajmohan Gandhi, Mahatma Gandhi's grandson, in 1957 through Moral Rearmament, an organization founded by Frank Buchman in England and known earlier as the Oxford Movement. Brown found its emphasis on absolute honesty and purity somewhat naive, believing legislative action was also needed to change the legal underpinnings of discrimination.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Mackinac Island and Quaker Camp
clips.summary: Brown recounts being invited to a world affairs gathering at Mackinac Island, Michigan, attended by representatives of some 87 countries, the same year King won the Spingarn Medal. In 1958 he spent a whole summer at a Quaker world affairs camp near Cornell University, broadening the horizons of a kid out of Mississippi.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Africa Scholarship and Crozer Admission
clips.summary: Brown describes finishing Morehouse in 1964 and receiving a scholarship for his first trip to Africa, the same year he was admitted to Crozer Theological Seminary with a recommendation letter from Dr. King. He notes that King himself entered Crozer in 1948 at age 19, where his father was a close friend of Dr. J. Pius Barber.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: J. Pius Barber and the Prophetic Tradition
clips.summary: Brown profiles Dr. J. Pius Barber, editor of the National Baptist Voice and the second Black student to finish Crozer, who became the unofficial dean of its Black students. He names the prophetic-tradition preachers Barber mentored, including William Augustus Jones, Samuel D. Proctor, Harold Carter, and Charles Booth, describing a gospel that never bifurcated personal salvation from social justice.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Mordecai Wyatt Johnson at Howard
clips.summary: Brown profiles Mordecai Wyatt Johnson, a Morehouse and Colgate Rochester graduate born in Paris, Tennessee, who pastored in Charleston, West Virginia before being elected Howard University's first Black president in 1926. He recounts Johnson's first agenda of elevating the night-school law school, asking Justice Louis Brandeis for advice on condition it stay secret until Brandeis died, and being told to teach young Black minds to master constitutional law.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Houston, Marshall, and Brown v. Board
clips.summary: Brown describes Johnson recruiting Charles Hamilton Houston to build the law school, whose first class included Thurgood Marshall, Oliver Hill, and Robert Carter, with the mentorship of James Nabrit. He recounts the team arguing before the Supreme Court from 1953 on the 14th Amendment's equal protection clause, reversing Plessy v. Ferguson and breaking the legal barriers to discrimination.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: The New Breed of Movement Ministers
clips.summary: Brown observes that reports showed only about three percent of Black preachers were involved in the movement, calling it romanticized to say everyone was with Dr. King. He names the new-breed ministers who did join: C.K. Steel in Tallahassee, Father Gibson in Tampa, Benjamin Lawson Hooks in Tennessee, and Samuel Woodrow Williams who co-taught King's seminar at Morehouse.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: King's Seminar on Personalism
clips.summary: Brown describes the small eight-student social philosophy seminar he took with the down-to-earth Dr. King at Morehouse, where King taught the theory of personalism he acquired from Brightman at Boston University. The doctrine holds that every human being is endowed with dignity and worth that should be respected regardless of color or religion.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Women Preachers and Gay Rights
clips.summary: Brown applies personalism to his embrace of women preachers, having ordained the city's first woman preacher at his church in 1984 despite most Black preachers opposing women in the pulpit. He extends the same logic to gay rights, arguing it is wrong to deny a marginalized people equal protection under the law.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: John Mercer Langston in 1866
clips.summary: Brown cites John Mercer Langston, the first Black congressman during Reconstruction from Virginia, who with Charles Satchel, the first pastor of Third Baptist, led Ohio's abolitionist movement before 1850. He keeps a copy of Langston's 1866 St. Louis speech demanding equal protection before the law and the right to vote.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Do Not Become What You Hate
clips.summary: Brown tells fellow ministers they must not become that which they hate, urging that those who suffered discrimination for their skin color must not discriminate against gay, lesbian, transgender, and bisexual people. He observes pointedly that the divorce rate is highest not in liberal regions but across the Bible belt.
keywords: do not become what you hate

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Religious Rites Versus Civil Rights
clips.summary: Brown notes the King James who gave the Bible its English translation was gay, asking whether his contribution should be burned. He distinguishes religious rites from constitutional rights, invoking Roger Williams, run out of the Massachusetts Bay Colony in 1638, to argue that a free country is a democracy and not a theocracy where private doctrines are imposed on others.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Refusing to Support Proposition 8
clips.summary: Brown recalls a young man trying to take over his pulpit one Sunday morning when he announced he would not support Proposition 8, an act for which the man later apologized before the whole church. Brown believes he has been vindicated by the change that has come, including President Obama's position, having borne the burdens not just for Black people and women but also for gay people.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: From Civil Rights to Human Rights
clips.summary: Brown frames human rights in a global picture, noting the Christian tradition holds that Christ died for the whole world, not just America. He warns that consuming a disproportionate share of the world's resources is unjust, and cites King's letter from the Birmingham jail to argue that injustice anywhere affects everyone everywhere.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Leakey, the Rift Valley, and Kinfolk
clips.summary: Brown invokes the paleontologist Leakey to argue that humanity's first home was the Rift Valley in Africa, so unkindness across races means messing with one's kinfolk. He recalls his mother's parting words to behave, lamenting that humanity grew arrogant and built an us-against-them dichotomy instead of recognizing one family sharing the world's resources.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: A Message to Young Men of Alpha
clips.summary: Brown describes addressing students from Stanford, UC Berkeley, USF, and UCLA pledging Alpha Phi Alpha, the fraternity of King and W.E.B. Du Bois, urging them toward scholarship, deeds, and love for humankind. He holds up King as the model, recounting that King entered Morehouse at 15, was a C-plus student under hard taskmasters, then excelled at Crozer and earned his Boston University doctorate.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Students of Promise and Education Crisis
clips.summary: Brown describes the Students of Promise program at Martin Luther King Middle School, working with underachieving Latino and African-American males. He cites the grim statistics for Black students in the city, with mean averages around D-plus and only about 30 percent finishing high school, and urges these young men to reach back and pull others along as Mary McLeod Bethune taught.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Origins of Operation Crossroads Africa
clips.summary: Brown describes Operation Crossroads Africa, founded by Presbyterian pastor James Robinson of the Church of the Master in Harlem in 1958, the same year his Cleveland Plain Dealer article got him in trouble. The program engaged American and African students in summer study, travel, and community development, and gave John Kennedy the idea for the Peace Corps.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: The African-American Summit With Sullivan
clips.summary: Brown recounts traveling to Liberia, Sierra Leone, Senegal, and Ghana on his first African trip and returning to the continent 22 times. He describes co-founding the African-American Summit with Dr. Leon Howard Sullivan, first held in Abidjan, Ivory Coast around 1991, born from a 1990 vision of pooling Black leaders, scholars, and business people to help Africa develop.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Resettling Ethiopian and Eritrean Refugees
clips.summary: Brown describes the 1970s war in Ethiopia and Eritrea and the unfair U.S. quotas that admitted far fewer African refugees than Soviet, Cuban, or Southeast Asian ones. Through his congregation he sponsored about 3,000 refugees from Africa and the Caribbean, first taking them into his own family room before leasing a Victorian house near the church.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Discovering Kofi, the Ghanaian Prodigy
clips.summary: Brown recounts attending Ghana's 50th independence anniversary and being moved by a 13-year-old keyboard prodigy who performed before Stevie Wonder. He arranged an 80 percent scholarship to Stuart Hall High School for Kofi, who went on to Morehouse and its conservatory, composing nine symphonies and two operas while planning a career in medicine.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Pass It On and Arriving at Morehouse
clips.summary: Brown recalls Dr. Benjamin Mays telling him that the least one can do for undeserved blessings is pass it on to someone else. He describes arriving at Morehouse in 1959 with only $87 against an $895 room and board, supported by his home church, living with Dr. Mays, and working as chauffeur for Atlanta University president Rufus Clement.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Learning of Evers's Assassination
clips.summary: Brown recounts how Morehouse classmates teased him about needing a visa to return to Mississippi, and how in the summer of 1963, while in summer school, a brother from Alabama told him a civil rights worker had been killed in Jackson. Brown immediately knew it was Medgar Evers, whom he had spoken with days before and who had sensed something would happen.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Freedom Rides and the Zoo Arrest
clips.summary: Brown describes traveling alone to Jackson during the 1961 Freedom Rides and, on Evers's instruction, organizing protests after most riders were sent to Parchman prison. He was arrested at the Livingston Park Zoo over segregated benches, and arrested again that summer for insisting a young doctor not call his 85-year-old neighbor boy at the University of Mississippi Hospital.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: The Evers Funeral and Police Violence
clips.summary: Brown recounts being at Medgar Evers's funeral and the march afterward, walking three abreast behind the body before it was shipped to Washington for burial at Arlington. He describes police officers using billy clubs on children, women, and horses galloping over mourners, calling it a horrifying experience, and notes he stays in touch with Mrs. Evers and remains on the NAACP national board.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: No Despair and Race Still Matters
clips.summary: Brown says he has never had moments of despair, only greater determination to continue the fight Evers would not have wanted him to abandon. He argues race in America is like an untreated condition that will destroy the nation, citing the derailed Clinton race commission chaired by John Hope Franklin and warning that Obama's election did not make America post-racial.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Gun Violence and Anna Green
clips.summary: Brown describes the daily climate of violence for African-American males between 15 and 30, leading funerals practically every month, and laments that the nation only got excited about guns after the Newtown shooting. He recalls Anna Green, the child killed in the 2011 Tucson shooting, and the clip of her at the piano singing Come Thou Almighty King.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Meeting with Vice President Biden
clips.summary: Brown recounts a Friday meeting with Vice President Biden's chief of staff alongside Franklin Graham and Richard Land of the Southern Baptist Convention on gun violence. They agreed to convene faith leaders at the White House around the April 4 anniversary of King's assassination and endorsed universal background checks, expanded mental health programs, and addressing violent video games and movies.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: America's Violent Culture and the Red States
clips.summary: Brown contrasts the United States' 12,000 homicides a year with the dozens in other countries, calling America a violent culture that should check itself. He observes that the states demanding their guns are the old red states of the Confederacy, argues the agenda is driven by Republicans, and asks what boogeyman they are fighting in a nation meant to have liberty and justice for all.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: The Beloved Community and the Salad Bowl
clips.summary: Brown invokes John Winthrop's 1630 Model of Christian Charity sermon envisioning a city on the hill of mutual responsibility, urging Americans to turn to each other rather than against each other. He recasts King's beloved community as a salad bowl rather than a melting pot, where distinct cultures remain visible and the nation is healthier for its multicultural diversity.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Mississippi's Unsung Heroes and She-Roes
clips.summary: Brown calls for resources to honor the movement's unsung heroes, naming C.C. Bryant and Doc Owens of McComb, Amzie Moore of Clarksdale, Samuel Bailey, Joseph Broadwater, Carsie Hall, and Reverend William Albert Bender of Tougaloo, who fought for equal teacher pay in 1946 and challenged the white primaries before Fannie Lou Hamer. He also names Gladys Bates, R.L.T. Smith, and the letter carriers who were independent of white employers.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: States' Rights as a Coded Message
clips.summary: Brown argues that segregationists' talk of states' rights and protecting against the tyranny of the government was a coded message against Black people, aimed at the federal government rather than state governments like Mississippi or South Carolina. He contends they invoked these arguments because they did not want equal protection of the law or justice for everybody.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: The Second Amendment and Django
clips.summary: Brown agrees with an argument, drawn from a Trudeau article citing the Constitutional Convention, that the Second Amendment protected the patrollers and southern militias that kept slaves suppressed. He references Andrew Delbanco's The Real American Dream on how weapons kept enslaved people from escaping, and the film Django, before the interview closes with the Library of Congress and Smithsonian sign-off.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Interview Opening in Selma
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, with filmmaker John Bishop, opens the Civil Rights History Project interview in Selma, Alabama on May 31, 2011, welcoming Annie Pearl Avery to discuss her life and engagement with SNCC.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Born in Birmingham, Sent to Pittsburgh
clips.summary: Avery describes her birth in Birmingham, Alabama, her father being 54 at her birth, and being the first of four children. Her childless great-aunt in Hazelwood, Pittsburgh, recently widowed, wanted company, so her mother sent her north to live there for a few years.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: A Nickel for the Emmett Till Story
clips.summary: Living on Sylvan Avenue and attending Gladstone Elementary in Pittsburgh, Avery was nine or ten when Emmett Till was murdered in 1955. With no newspaper at home, she paid a nickel at a neighborhood grocery store to read about the killing, understanding only that a Negro child had been murdered.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Mack Charles Parker and Birmingham Ministers
clips.summary: Back in Birmingham at fourteen, Avery followed the 1959 lynching of Mack Charles Parker, taken from jail and killed in Poplarville, Mississippi. She recalls the Montgomery bus boycott, Autherine Lucy at the University of Alabama, and Birmingham ministers Reverend Shuttlesworth, Reverend Charles Billups, Reverend Oliver, and Reverend J.L. Phifer working to desegregate Phillips High School.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Buying a Ticket and a Knife
clips.summary: At sixteen, excited by news of young people in the movement, Avery purchased a Trailways ticket from Birmingham to Montgomery to join the Freedom Rides and bought a knife to protect herself on the bus. She recalls the bus being attacked in Anniston, Alabama, and her own preference for the familiar Greyhound station her grandfather frequented.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Wilson Brown Explains Nonviolence
clips.summary: At the roped-off Greyhound station, Avery met Wilson Brown, part of a Birmingham welcoming committee for the Freedom Riders. When she said she wanted to go, Brown told her she could not because she did not understand nonviolence, explaining that when someone hits you, you cannot hit back. She got a refund for her ticket.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: The Nation of Islam and Muhammad Speaks
clips.summary: Avery describes her first contact with the Nation of Islam when Mack Charles Parker was jailed, drawn by Muhammad Speaks as the only paper covering his case daily. She attended some meetings but found the organization not visibly active enough, and turned toward the sit-ins and Freedom Rides for more direct action.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Life on the Back of the Bus
clips.summary: Asked about her Birmingham life before the Atlanta meeting, Avery contrasts riding the back of the bus with her Pittsburgh years, where her aunt told her she could sit anywhere and Northern Black children corrected her speech, telling her to say pop instead of drink. Returning to segregation in Birmingham helped motivate her to get involved.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: First SNCC Meeting in Atlanta
clips.summary: Wilson Brown invited Avery to a Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee meeting in Atlanta, where she left her knife at home. There she met her first decent white people, including Anne Braden, Bob Zellner, Dottie Miller Zellner, Penny Patch, and Bill Hansen, and Black organizers Ms. Ella Baker, James Forman, Ruby Doris Smith, and Julian Bond.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Confused and Stopped in Marietta
clips.summary: Heading back from Atlanta, Avery, Wilson Brown, Nathaniel Lee, and a white girl who wanted to meet Reverend Shuttlesworth got confused and ended up in Marietta, Georgia. Seen as a mixed group, they were followed by police, who stopped and arrested Wilson, impounded the car, and left the others on the street with no way to get around.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Becoming Customers at the Bus Station
clips.summary: Unable to find any Black community, the group returned to the Marietta bus station, where an attendant said they could not stay because they were not customers. Recalling interstate commerce law, Avery had them buy tickets to become customers so they could not be removed, using the payphones and switchboard to reach the SNCC office.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: A Hostile White Crowd Gathers
clips.summary: As darkness fell, a growing white crowd packed the Marietta station. The three planned to take refuge in the phone booths if things worsened. Word reached the press, and the Associated Press began calling the payphone every few minutes to check whether they were still safe.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Rescue from Marietta at Midnight
clips.summary: Around midnight the crowd grew hostile, beating on seats with sticks and shouting threats to kill them. James Forman, Julian Bond, attorney Howard Moore, and people Avery believed were from the Justice Department arrived from Atlanta in dark government cars, got Wilson out of jail, and decided the group should stay in Atlanta overnight rather than risk Birmingham.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Meeting Shuttlesworth and the First Sit-In
clips.summary: Back in Birmingham, Avery met Reverend Shuttlesworth at his home in his pajamas. He took her to what she calls Birmingham's first sit-in at a Woolworth's lunch counter, where a white man went down the counter spitting and pouring ketchup. Not yet adjusted to nonviolence, she was made to stand beside Shuttlesworth and Reverend Charles Billups.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Arrested in Albany, Georgia
clips.summary: Avery traveled to Albany, Georgia, where she was arrested and met attorney C.B. King along with Rutha Mae Harris, Bernice Johnson Reagon, Charles Sherrod, Bob Mants, and Reverend Prathia Hall. She describes herself as in a learning stage, saying little and absorbing from the experienced and often more educated organizers around her.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Emotions of Protest and Being Free
clips.summary: Reflecting on the mix of emotions during hostile protests, Avery says she did not walk around angry, having been exposed to a different life in the North. She invokes the idea that once you have been free you do not want to be a slave again, which she believes shaped her subconscious drive to act.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Volunteer Pay and Ten Dollars a Week
clips.summary: Avery explains that SNCC volunteers were unpaid, staying in people's homes, helping with housework, doing voter registration, and joining demonstrations. She suspects the government saw the lack of pay as a way to discredit SNCC, and recalls workers eventually being given about ten dollars a week.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Adapting to Jail in Albany
clips.summary: Avery recounts going out for voter registration one day in Albany and being locked up the next after a demonstration, jailed three or four weeks. She describes putting the experience in the back of her mind and adapting to her environment, obtaining cigarettes and reading material, a skill she says she has been good at in and out of jail.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Finishing William Moore's March Alone
clips.summary: After postal worker William Moore was killed completing his solo march, Avery decided to pick up a sign and finish what he started, even alone, not realizing she was recreating his solitary walk. She was arrested minutes after she began walking and was placed in jail in Gadsden, Alabama.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Diane Nash and Madeleine Sherwood
clips.summary: The day or two after her Gadsden arrest, other women were jailed, including Diane Nash and actress Madeleine Sherwood, who had played the pregnant woman in Cat on a Hot Tin Roof and was bonded out right away as the white woman in the group. Avery refused to give interrogators any information about how she got there or who brought her.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: The Hole at Tutwiler Prison
clips.summary: Citing a threat to take the women from jail, authorities sent the men to Kilby and moved the women to Tutwiler prison, where they first spent a night crawling in a dark sealed hole. The next morning they were placed in the hospital ward, where a trustee from Tuscaloosa serving a life sentence recognized them as the freedom fighters everybody knew about.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Nonviolence as Tactic, Not a Way of Life
clips.summary: Avery distinguishes herself from those who took nonviolence on as a way of life, saying she was among those who accepted it only as a tactic. She frames this as a matter of individual conviction within the movement rather than a single shared philosophy.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: The Decoy March in Birmingham
clips.summary: Avery recounts a Birmingham demonstration where Wyatt Tee Walker had her join a decoy march of ten or fifteen people to throw the police off before a larger march came later. She tells of a friend who first refused to go but joined the second march rather than be left out once others went.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Waking the Sleeping Judge in Danville
clips.summary: Working in Danville, Virginia, Avery and Freedom Singer Matthew Jones, alongside Avon Rollins and an SCLC worker, arrived at a crowded courthouse to find the judge asleep during the case. They went limp at the front of the podium to wake him; when an officer explained it was called going limp, the angry judge gave them 90 days for contempt.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Awakened by the 16th Street Bombing
clips.summary: Released by project director Avon Rollins to take a break, Avery returned to Birmingham late on a Saturday. The next morning she was awakened by the bombing of the 16th Street Baptist Church, about sixteen blocks from her home, and walked to the scene near the A.G. Gaston Motel.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Gathering at the Gaston Motel
clips.summary: At the roped-off A.G. Gaston Motel, Avery saw Ms. Ella Baker, Dr. King, Reverend Abernathy, Jim Forman, and Andrew Young, and met a Jet editor for the first time. Emotional over the murdered children, the group sat in a room that night discussing more aggressive direct action that was not violent but went beyond marching and going to jail.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Project Director in Hale County
clips.summary: Avery describes settling into Hale County as project director for voter registration, finding a house to rent and letting the community know who they were and why they came. She recalls meeting an Army sergeant who said he did not believe in nonviolence, yet stood outside the first mass meeting to make sure everyone was safe.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: The Ten-Gauge Shotgun as Self-Defense
clips.summary: The community gave the workers a ten-gauge shotgun, and St. Clair, who also taught Avery to drive, taught her to use it behind their rented house. She frames the gun as a psychological deterrent against cowardly Klansmen, calling it self-defense rather than violence, and says they never had to use it.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: From Clarksdale to Natchez
clips.summary: Avery recalls another tight situation where Chico Neblett was project director and the workers slept in a woman's rented room because no one would house them, until St. Clair bought a cheap house. She then came to organize voter registration in Natchez, Mississippi, where she was arrested, after working in Clarksdale.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: The Coin Flip Before Bloody Sunday
clips.summary: After Jimmie Lee Jackson was killed, the plan to carry his body to Selma became a march. The night before, a call from Attorney General Katzenbach's office warned that Martin's life was in danger, so King was taken away, and Hosea Williams and Andrew Young flipped a coin to decide who would represent SCLC, which is how Williams led alongside John Lewis, Amelia Boynton, and Marie Foster.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: The Only Arrest on the Bridge
clips.summary: Avery explains that the troopers' intent on Bloody Sunday was to terrorize and brutalize the marchers into not marching, lining up past the first traffic light beyond the Edmund Pettus Bridge. After scouting the route, she was the only person arrested, jailed following a physical disagreement with a policeman over where she would stand, while most marchers faced beatings and tear gas.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Warning Dr. King at the Gaston Motel
clips.summary: Avery recounts being chosen by Dr. Wyatt Tee Walker for a discreet task while the State Patrol searched the A.G. Gaston Motel to serve Dr. King an injunction. Walker whispered for her to go to the room where King was staying with Andy and Bernard Lee, rather than the registered master suite, and quietly tell them police were looking for him.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Bonded Out by Peter Hall
clips.summary: Avery describes Bloody Sunday as the most vivid memory of the three bridge marches and notes it was the shortest time she ever spent in jail. Birmingham attorney Peter Hall, one of their lawyers, drove in and bonded her out of his own pocket, returning to Birmingham before authorities finally released her near midnight.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: An African Name in the White House
clips.summary: Avery shares her reaction to the election of the first African American president, saying she never expected it in her lifetime and assumed any such president would have a name like Charles or John rather than an African name. The election fell on her sixtieth birthday, which she took as a turning point and a sign things could not get better.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Sixteen and Joining the Movement
clips.summary: Clarifying the timeline, Avery confirms she was sixteen at her first Atlanta meeting, turning seventeen that November, so she was seventeen when first arrested. The interviewer and Avery settle that her first SNCC meeting was in 1961, making her a very young person quickly moving into significant roles.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: A Modern-Day Harriet Tubman in Jail
clips.summary: Avery reflects that she was later called a modern-day Harriet Tubman, though she did not know who Tubman was at the time. She describes the psychological shock of the iron jail door and how she kept others from breaking down, since a crying jag invited jailer retaliation and made other prisoners think they could be run over.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Appearing Strong Behind Bars
clips.summary: Avery explains the small economies of jail survival: pooling fifty cents for cigarettes, securing cards or dominoes, and slipping out phone numbers so someone could check on a situation. She emphasizes that it was better to appear strong than weak, a discipline she practiced before realizing it echoed Harriet Tubman.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: SNCC and CORE as the Infantry
clips.summary: Comparing the organizations, Avery says SCLC, SNCC, CORE, and the NAACP each had a place, but older people moved slowly while the young wanted change now. She frames SNCC and CORE as the infantry who entered places where you could be arrested just for walking the street or for vagrancy.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Forman as Mentor, Friends in the Field
clips.summary: Avery names Jim Forman as a mentor and lists the people she worked with personally, including Janet Moses, Ruby Sales, Gwen Patton, Bob Mants, Ruby Doris Smith, Prathia Hall, Sam Block, James Peacock, Willie Peacock, Frank Smith, Charles Sherrod, and Charles Jones, recalling Sam Block and the Peacocks from Mississippi.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Judy Richardson and Bonds Forged in Jail
clips.summary: Avery describes Judy Richardson, who came from California and worked mostly out of the Atlanta office, as one of her best friends, recalling going to jail with her. She notes the deep bonds formed by those who spent time in jail together, observing you really get to know someone over ten weeks behind bars.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: The Klan Surrounds the Clarksdale Office
clips.summary: Avery recounts the most frightening night of her movement life in Clarksdale, Mississippi, when the Klan surrounded the SNCC office during a curfew on Black people. Unable to leave or see faces, the workers could only see headlights and hear screaming and slurs all night, taking cover under desks. Lafayette Surney headed the project there.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Staying with SNCC After 1965
clips.summary: Asked about her role after 1965, Avery says many dropped off, expecting things would get better. She went to New York around 1967 and worked from the New York SNCC office, remaining with SNCC partly because SCLC, made up of older and more mature people, did not put her on staff.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Black Power as Lifting Ourselves Up
clips.summary: Avery returned to Atlanta to run a tax service and frames the late-1960s shift toward self-defense and Black Power, which she calls radical and culturally conscious. She argues Black Power was about lifting Black people up, not the violence many assumed, distinguishing the self-defense of Hale County from overt violence.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Meeting Mandela in Selma
clips.summary: Avery describes learning African American and African history as part of thinking well of herself, recalling her arrest in Atlanta for trying to see an African dignitary. She finally met one without being arrested when Nelson Mandela came to Selma and Rose Sanders introduced them, and she hugged him with gratitude.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Honored to Have Been Among Them
clips.summary: Closing the interview, Avery says it was an honor to be associated with the SCLC, NAACP, and CORE people, and with those now working with the Bridge Crossing Jubilee, the National Voting Rights Museum and Institute, and the Mississippi Civil Rights Veterans. The interviewers thank her, and the recording ends with the Library of Congress and Smithsonian credit.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Introductions in St. Augustine, September 2011
clips.summary: Interviewer Joe Mosnier opens the Civil Rights History Project session at the Hilton Bayfront in St. Augustine, Florida, naming the interviewees Audrey Nell Edwards Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer along with Dr. Robert B. Hayling and the Conways. He invites the women to reflect on growing up in St. Augustine and how they became teenage activists.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Growing Up and Joining at St. Paul
clips.summary: Hamilton remembers a St. Augustine with thriving Black businesses on Washington Street and the segregated downtown where her mother could buy school supplies at Woolworth's and McCrory's but could not drink from the whites-only fountain. Young people began gathering at St. Paul AME Church, where Mr. Clyde Jenkins, Dr. Robert B. Hayling, Mrs. Lucille Plummer, and Mrs. Fannie Fuller Wood supplied them with picket signs for daily downtown demonstrations.
related_events: NAACP Youth Council formation in St. Augustine

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Deciding to Sit In, July 18, 1963
clips.summary: On July 18, 1963 the youth decided to escalate from picketing to a sit-in, leaving their signs at St. Paul and walking downtown in pairs. Barber and NAACP member Ernest Wells gave Goldie and Big Jimmy Jackson money for the counters, and the group split among the service drug store, McCrory's, and Woolworth's, where Hamilton, Ulmer, Willie Carl Singleton, and Samuel White sat down.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: JoeAnn Ulmer Joins the Youth Council
clips.summary: Ulmer describes growing up on the west side of town "across the track," where Black children had no activities, and getting her mother's permission to stay with her sister near St. Paul AME Church so she could take part. She recalls the youth council's singing meetings with Janelle at the piano and a handful of dedicated young people who picketed every day before deciding to go in and ask for service.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Refused Service, Arrested, and Jailed
clips.summary: When the youth asked for service they were told the counter "do not serve Niggas here" and were ordered out by Sheriff L.O. Davis and arrested by Black deputy DJ Johnson, whom Hamilton had grown up knowing. Sixteen youth were taken to the jail, the four girls and three boys separated from the rest, and they sang in their cells expecting release but were held overnight.
related_events: Arrest of the St. Augustine Four

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: The Petition and the Parents' Refusal
clips.summary: In court the judge demanded that the youths' parents sign a petition pledging no further demonstration until age 21, and when twelve youth went home Hamilton, Ulmer, Singleton, and White stayed because their mothers refused. NAACP representative Mr. Shaw vowed to appeal to a higher court, and the four were sentenced to reform school after their parents held firm despite a threat to charge Dr. Hayling.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Dr. Hayling's Leadership and the Timeline
clips.summary: Both speakers describe Dr. Robert B. Hayling as a youth advisor who motivated rather than restrained them and "never left our side," calling him the inspiration and father of the St. Augustine movement. Hamilton stresses the chronology, that they were released from jail on January 23, 1964, roughly three months before Dr. King's SCLC campaign arrived, work Hayling had laid the groundwork for.
related_events: Release of the St. Augustine Four

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Faith, Song, and Reform School Suffering
clips.summary: Hamilton and Ulmer explain that faith and uplifting songs like "I Love Everybody" let them bypass the hatred, insisting "the violence was afflicted on us but we did not afflict violence back." They recount 77 days in the county jail and 52 days in isolation at the girls' reform school scrubbing floors on their knees, and the anguish of Miss Nora, who fainted in court yet never signed the petition.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: The Boys' Silence About Marianna
clips.summary: Hamilton says the experience showed them they needed to do more, which is why she went back to jail months later with Dr. King. She recounts how Willie Carl Singleton and Samuel White refused ever to speak of what happened to them at the Marianna reform school, taking it to the grave, and how she later learned of the terrible things done to boys there.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Meeting Dr. King and Ralph Abernathy
clips.summary: In June 1964 Dr. Hayling brought the four youth to meet Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and Ralph Abernathy in a small room, where King rose from a little red couch, hugged them, and told them they "went beyond the call of duty."
