clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Booker Boards the Freedom Ride
clips.summary: Booker explains he went on the Freedom Ride as an observer with a photographer, riding from Washington toward Atlanta and Anniston, noting the crowds did not distinguish observers from participants. He recalls the bus burning at Anniston, with Newson aboard that bus and himself on the other.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: A Bureau Chief's Washington Access
clips.summary: Booker reflects on the contacts he built as Washington bureau chief, including the FBI and the administration, and on his role as a Westinghouse radio commentator three times a week, which let him explain Black concerns to white audiences. He notes he is finishing a book about his front-line experiences.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Dixie Official Flunks Her Own Test
clips.summary: Newson recalls answering James Farmer's call to join the journey and a New Orleans voting-rights hearing en route, where a registrar testified that Black applicants could not pass her simple tests until Civil Rights Commission lawyers asked her the same questions and she could not answer any of them.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Three Rulings Behind the Rides
clips.summary: Newson traces the legal foundation of the Freedom Rides: CORE's 1947 attempt without legal support, a Baltimore woman's 1946 interstate-travel victory, the Rosa Parks situation and the four women represented by Attorney Fred Gray, and Bruce Boynton's case covering bus-station facilities.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: John Lewis at Rock Hill and King's Warning
clips.summary: Newson watched the riders test facilities in twos and threes, saw John Lewis attacked and bloodied at Rock Hill, South Carolina, and recalls Martin Luther King warning Booker and others that the KKK planned to keep the riders from getting out of Alabama alive, just as James Farmer left after his father's death.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: The Greyhound Reaches Anniston
clips.summary: Newson, riding the Greyhound, describes arriving at the Anniston, Alabama station to an angry mob that beat on the bus with chains and sticks while the driver locked the door, and a woman returning from a funeral who knelt terrified between the seats, asking why this was happening.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: The Firebombing and Trooper Cowling
clips.summary: Newson recounts the bus being chased and trapped outside Anniston, plainclothes state troopers aboard, and trooper E.L. Cowling drawing his pistol in the doorway to keep the mob off. A firebomb landed in the empty seat behind him, the bus filled with smoke, and the riders barely escaped before it burned out.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Hospital and the Shuttlesworth Caravan
clips.summary: Newson describes losing his camera in the burning bus and later recovering it, the injured riders being taken to an Anniston hospital that wanted them gone, and the young leader standing in for Joe Perkins reaching Rev. Shuttlesworth in Birmingham, who sent a caravan of cars to rescue them.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: How Close the Danger Came
clips.summary: Reflecting on May 14, 1961, the journalists weigh how close each came to serious injury or death, at Anniston and at the Birmingham terminal where riders were badly beaten, with Booker noting it was simply part of the life that they accepted.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Perspectives After the Freedom Ride
clips.summary: Recalling John Lewis's frustrated March on Washington speech, Booker frames his own mission as covering the Black underclass so Jet and Ebony could push education and opportunity, while Newson notes that Black aspirations did not match the administration's view and that reporters glimpsed what J. Edgar Hoover was doing.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: At the March on Washington
clips.summary: Newson recalls the March on Washington as a historic event he attended with his wife and a colleague's wife, hearing King's speech and witnessing white union members, church leaders, and others coming together to demand change, which cast the day in a completely different light.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: The Last Interview With King
clips.summary: Booker describes his final extended interview with King during Poor People's Campaign planning, conducted in a car to the Atlanta airport with Andrew Young, King's wife, and Harry Belafonte present. Asked about charges he was failing, King answered that by that logic Jesus Christ failed because He got himself hung on the cross.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. 1968

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Booker on King's Resolve
clips.summary: Booker remembers King as never appearing disappointed or defeated, always on the go and sustained by a private prayer, and a brave man who carried great stress and rumors of communist associations while plugging away day after day. Asked about Malcolm X and Stokely Carmichael, Booker says he paid them little attention.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Abernathy Carries On
clips.summary: Newson says he mostly watched King speak rather than covering him directly, and recalls how King and Ralph Abernathy worked as a team, Abernathy passing the basket while King delivered the speeches. After King's death Abernathy tried to carry on the work in the Alabama Black Belt without King's personality.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Covering the Political Conventions
clips.summary: Asked about the 1964 Atlantic City convention and the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, Booker recalls covering four conventions, including the Republican gathering in Kansas City where the conservative wing took over the party, but notes he did not cover 1964.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Black and White Reporters at Work
clips.summary: Both reflect on relations between Black and white journalists. Booker notes the presses worked to different standards, while Newson recalls being unable to stay at the central press hotels in Memphis, trading help with white colleagues on a professional basis, and keeping his distance from the hostile Mississippi papers.
related_events: Black journalists hired by white papers

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Television Transforms the Movement
clips.summary: Booker argues that when the white press and TV networks covered the movement, nothing greater could have happened: showing people beaten, hosed, and trampled by horses moved a public that, deep down, valued justice, and accelerated the movement in ways the Black press alone could not.
related_events: Television coverage of the Civil Rights Movement

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: A Suspect Arrest in the Coverage
clips.summary: Booker faults mainstream outlets like the New York Times and Washington Post for their handling of the 2009 Henry Louis Gates Jr. arrest, a disorderly-conduct case he says would not hold up because citizens may question and demand identification from a policeman, yet papers rarely explained why the charges were dropped.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Robert Kennedy, Johnson, and Obama
clips.summary: Closing on Robert Kennedy's 1968 assassination, Booker calls him the first official of that rank sensitive to racial matters and willing to act, contrasts him with Lyndon Johnson who had to be boss, and observes that the political structure disintegrated until it was finally rebuilt when Obama came through.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Interview Opening Through the Storm
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill opens the recording at the Luper household in Oklahoma City on May 24, 2011, with filmmaker John Bishop, framing the session within the Library of Congress and Smithsonian NMAAHC Civil Rights History Project. Cal Luper welcomes them and jokes about the heavy weather outside, riffing on We Shall Overcome.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project interview series

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Brother President and the New York Trip
clips.summary: Cal recalls that his sister Marilyn Luper described the 1958 NAACP Youth Council trip to New York City with the play Brother President. He explains he still holds a film of the Brother President presentation, kept at a local Oklahoma City photo shop and being duplicated onto disc, and dates the performance to 1958.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: A Family Affair in the Youth Council
clips.summary: Cal describes the early Youth Council as a family affair where the whole household moved together because Clara said so. He names his cousin Betty Germany and his cousin Richard Brown, one of the original sit-inners, and notes the meetings happened in the Luper house.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: The Sit-In Couch as Relic
clips.summary: Cal describes the Luper living-room couch where Youth Council meetings were held, recovered around the 1980s after surviving the sit-in movement. He recalls it was scorched by a Molotov cocktail thrown through the front door and frames the recovered couch as a physical relic of the campaign.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Lunch Counters and the Hudson Elevator
clips.summary: Memories return as Cal recalls a New York lunch counter where he could put his money in, choose his own sandwich, and be served, unlike home where he was told he could not eat. He remembers riding the elevator up and down in the Hudson hotel and walking its lobby without fear of being told to leave.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Downtown Segregation and Employment
clips.summary: Cal describes how downtown Oklahoma City hotels the Skirvin and the Huckins were off limits to Black patrons unless they worked there, and how being a Luper made even that work hard to find. He reflects that getting employment was one of the difficult things he and his sibling faced during the campaign.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Katz Drug Store at Main and Robinson
clips.summary: Cal recalls the first nights at Katz Drug Store, walking across Main Street and entering through the doorway on the Main Street side, with Main and Robinson running east to west and Broadway north to south. He notes the site is now the home of the Ford Theater and that passing it always returns him to the mornings and evenings spent there.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Grandmother's Commute From Carverdale
clips.summary: After noting he was never allowed to try on hats or shoes downtown, Cal turns to his grandmother, who lived in the Carverdale addition, a mostly Black neighborhood near Northeast Tenth and what is now Martin Luther King. He describes her daily two-bus commute with a transfer near Johnny Browns, gives her address as 1236 Windermere, and remembers her as a willing babysitter.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Self-Discipline and the Commanders
clips.summary: Cal explains the self-discipline the Youth Council maintained within the city and describes an organization called the Commanders, whose job was both to keep outsiders from harassing the demonstrators and to keep members from misbehaving on the inside.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Schoolbooks at the Lunch Counter
clips.summary: Cal recalls his mother insisting that members bring schoolbooks to sit-ins at Katz Drug Store and Bishop's Restaurant, which locked its doors against demonstrators. Those who got inside used the time to study, and Cal worked on his first year of Spanish while keeping watch over his sister and the younger girls, including the one everyone nicknamed Baby.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Clara Luper's Public Bearing
clips.summary: Asked what an observer would have seen in his mother, Cal describes how she met restaurant owners, governors, mayors, and police chiefs by looking them straight in the face rather than performing the deferential shuffle expected of Black Oklahomans at the time.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: The Clara Luper Radio Show
clips.summary: Cal describes working with his mother on the Clara Luper radio show at the station on Southeast 44th and Bryant for more than thirty years. He recalls a comedic call-and-response format, likened to Amos and Andy, where she threw out a topic and he batted it back, and notes the family still holds hundreds of tapes.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Oklahoma University and the Electrical Trade
clips.summary: Cal describes studying history at the University of Oklahoma while distracted by thoughts of his mother and sister back home, which hurt his grades. He left for trade school for electrical work, earned a license, and worked alongside his father, also an electrician, for a couple of years before his father's death.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Weighing Nonviolence Against Black Power
clips.summary: Cal recalls that after gaining weapons proficiency in military service he was tempted toward Black Power organizing, but argues against armed confrontation: a fist cannot beat a gun, while nonviolence reaches a person's heart. He observes that even nonviolent activists studied judo because there is always somebody tougher, and concludes he would rather concede a point than be beaten to prove one.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Family Cost and the Morning Routine
clips.summary: Asked whether the family paid too high a price, Cal reflects that the question has crossed his mind over the last twenty years, while noting how the sit-in routine set a lifelong rhythm of rising and being ready by nine. He recalls meeting at Calvary Baptist under pastor Reverend Morris Kerry, close enough to walk to Katz Drug Store, Bishop's, or Johnny Browns, where many photographs of the children were taken.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Ministers Bratzinger and Alexander
clips.summary: Asked about his mother's key allies, Cal says he thinks everyone mattered to her, then names Reverend Bratzinger, a minister at Fifth Street Baptist, and Reverend Alexander, the AME minister at the church at 13th and Kelham, both of whom Clara would stop whatever she was doing to talk with.
related_events: Oklahoma City civil rights ministerial network

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Dr. Charles N. Atkins and Hannah Atkins
clips.summary: Cal describes Dr. Charles N. Atkins, a Bahamian-born physician who debated strategy intensely with his mother, helped with her speeches and with keeping the nonviolent discipline right, brought many babies into the world, and treated arthritis on the East Side. He notes Atkins was married to Hannah Atkins, who became an Oklahoma state representative, and that their son Ed was a close friend.
related_events: Oklahoma City civil rights ministerial network

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Melvin B. Porter Boards the Bus
clips.summary: Cal recounts meeting Melvin B. Porter on the return from the Brother President performance, when the bus passed through Nashville and Porter, then at Vanderbilt Law School, saw the Oklahoma banner, dashed aboard, and pledged to open a law firm in Oklahoma City. Porter did so and rose to the State Senate, where he argued issues eloquently on the floor, and his sons Melvin Jr. and Joel continue the firm on Northwest 23rd and Hudson.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Sam Cornelius and the East Side YMCA
clips.summary: Cal remembers Sam Cornelius, director of the East Side YMCA, where the Youth Council held Monday-night meetings before the Freedom Center opened. He recalls Cornelius's family, his son Bruce and his daughter Charlene, a friend and Douglass High School classmate of Cal's who lived out in Garden Oaks, and Mrs. Cornelius as a second mother who would report any misbehavior to Clara.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: The Board of Education Joke
clips.summary: Cal explains the family joke of the Board of Education, his mother's wooden disciplining board, made before the era of child-abuse concerns and broken in well during the sit-in movement. The board was carved by Alexander Jones, a mechanical arts teacher at Dunjee School who taught woodworking and carpentry, commuted with Clara from the 1500 block of Northeast Park to her 1800 block home, and drove her because her car was not running.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Professor McClure and Closing
clips.summary: Cal recalls Professor McClure, a man older than his mother who was part of the group that helped desegregate the university, though Cal did not know him personally and remembers a relative who was often around. The interview ends with the standard Library of Congress and Smithsonian NMAAHC sign-off.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Interview Opening at the Highlander Center
clips.summary: An opening verse of "Ain't You Got a Right to the Tree of Life" gives way to Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program introducing the September 19, 2011 joint interview with Candie and Guy Carawan at the Highlander Center in New Market, Tennessee, recorded with videographer John Bishop for the Civil Rights History Project.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: The April 1960 Highlander Sit-In Gathering
clips.summary: Candie recalls Highlander hosting the April 1960 gathering of sit-in students at Monteagle, Tennessee, where she arrived with the Nashville delegation. Guy taught freedom songs including "I'm Gonna Sit at the Welcome Table," "Keep Your Eyes on the Prize," and "We Shall Overcome" that the Nashville students lacked.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: The Highlander Questioning Process
clips.summary: Candie describes Myles Horton and others using the Highlander process of hard questions to push students' thinking, with breakout sessions on whether non-violence was tactic or philosophy and on the jail-no-bail strategy. Guy kept the singing going with James Bevel and Bernard Lafayette when sessions grew heavy.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Driving Septima Clark Around the South
clips.summary: Guy recalls being Septima Clark's driver in the early Highlander days, including police stops because Clark, a Black woman, sat in the front seat beside a younger white man. Candie remembers Clark mentoring Guy on his dress and conduct.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Septima Clark, Tough and Sensible
clips.summary: Candie remembers Clark as a sensible neighbor in Charleston who advised letting their son keep his toy guns, and insists Clark was a tough woman who had to be strong working atop Monteagle in an all-white community, contradicting her popular image as merely sweet.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Myles Horton the Educational Visionary
clips.summary: Candie and Guy characterize Myles Horton as an educational visionary whose method built on people's own experience, down to earth and never intimidating yet unwilling to let foolish statements pass. They credit his vision to the civil rights and labor movements while noting Highlander was always a team that included women.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: A California Upbringing Toward Fairness
clips.summary: Candie traces her motivation to a progressive California family, a Unitarian fellowship, and the Girl Scouts, all stressing fairness and equality. The Montgomery bus boycott on the new family television set and high-school study of Gandhi awakened her sense that change in the South might be possible.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Pomona College and the Fisk Exchange
clips.summary: Candie describes choosing Pomona College partly for the Fisk Exchange Program, gaining a roommate through an earlier exchange, and applying in her junior year. She arrived in Nashville in January 1960, lucky to land in the crucial sit-in semester between January and June.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Preparing in Nashville Before Greensboro
clips.summary: Asked about an NAACP sense that Greensboro was coming, Candie recalls non-violent trainings under Reverend James Lawson at Fisk University, the feeling of preparing for something undefined, and the electric reaction when the Greensboro sit-in began and showed the Nashville students what to do.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Adapting Church Songs Into Freedom Songs
clips.summary: Candie recalls early Nashville singing drawing on rock and roll, camp songs, and church hymns, including the forerunner "Amen" reworked toward civil rights justice. Meeting Guy brought the practice of taking religious songs and changing a few words to make freedom songs, alongside the powerful community singing of the Nashville mass meetings.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Guy's Musical Roots in Los Angeles
clips.summary: Candie recounts Guy's formation: people-songs work in Los Angeles, the Oliver family who connected him to political musicians like Pete Seeger and the labor-music tradition, folklore study at Occidental, and a sociology master's at UCLA where he absorbed Library of Congress recordings and the Lomaxes' work on Southern music.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Wayland Hand's Warning and Seeger's Tip
clips.summary: Candie tells how UCLA folklorist Wayland D. Hand warned Guy to keep folk music politically neutral, citing Nazi use of folk music, advice Guy ignored. After meeting Pete Seeger and dreaming of the banjo, Guy followed Seeger's tip to visit Highlander Folk School, touring the South in summer 1953 with Rambling Jack Elliott and Frank Hamilton.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Carrying Songs Into Southern Churches
clips.summary: Married in 1961, the Carawans traveled to civil rights communities where Guy taught and learned songs in Black churches, welcomed as a novelty though barred from playing banjo inside. Candie describes joining him and the warmth of communities opening their churches and homes.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Jailed After the Clarksdale Concert
clips.summary: Candie recounts a Clarksdale, Mississippi joint concert arranged by Mrs. Pigee pairing Guy's folk songs with opera student Mary Jane Pigee. Community members escorted them out of the dangerous Black part of town, but police followed and took them to jail while Candie was heavily pregnant.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Singing Keep Your Eyes on the Prize
clips.summary: Guy fetches a second guitar and the Carawans perform verses of "Keep Your Eyes on the Prize" and "We Shall Overcome," including the line about the truth making us free.
keywords: Keep Your Eyes on the Prize

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Jamila Jones and We Are Not Afraid
clips.summary: Candie identifies Jamila Jones, a thirteen-year-old with an adult Montgomery group, as the person who hummed and inserted the "We Are Not Afraid" verse during the 1959 raid on Highlander, in the dark with police inside the building.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Encouraging Song Leaders and Workshops
clips.summary: Candie stresses that Guy's lasting contribution was nurturing music and song leaders, helping take the Freedom Singers to the Newport Folk Festival, and organizing five or six song-leader workshops with Myles Horton's backing in Atlanta, Mississippi, and at Highlander.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: From Song Leader to Field Recorder
clips.summary: Candie describes Guy's 1961 realization at the Jackson release of the Freedom Riders, who emerged from Parchman already overflowing with songs, that his role should shift to documentation. He bought a big Ampex tape recorder and began recording movement songs across the South, knowing Moe Asch's Folkways could distribute them nationally.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Folkways, Sing Out, and the First Songbook
clips.summary: Candie and Guy credit Moe Asch of Folkways Records and Irwin Silber of Sing Out! magazine for circulating freedom songs, with Oak Publications issuing the first songbook of movement music. She notes broadside carried Northern folk-revival writers like Tom Paxton, Phil Ochs, and Bob Dylan more than Southern material.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Singing Paul and Silas, Hold On
clips.summary: Asked again about Nashville mass-meeting community music, Guy responds with a performance of "Keep Your Eyes on the Prize / Hold On," the Paul and Silas verse.
keywords: Keep Your Eyes on the Prize

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Building a Beloved Community Through Song
clips.summary: Candie reflects on the movement's hope of building a beloved community, contrasting it with present cynicism. She quotes John Lewis that a movement without its music is a bird without wings, and notes Guy's point that there were movement songs for every mood, jubilant, sorrowful, and humorous.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Bernice Reagon on Music as Force
clips.summary: Candie recalls working with Bernice Johnson Reagon on the We Shall Overcome Fund, which returns commercial royalties to Southern communities, and relays Reagon's account of movement singing as an aggressive, powerful non-violent tool that drew her into the Albany Movement and now carries into other struggles.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Satirical Songs and We'll Never Turn Back
clips.summary: Candie offers examples of the satirical Nashville Quartet songs and recalls that when "We Shall Overcome" came to feel too optimistic and overused, people switched to the harder-edged "We'll Never Turn Back," noting Bernard Lafayette's gift for the comic numbers.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Confronting Fear in the Movement
clips.summary: Asked how she handled chronic fear, Candie says her own fear was nothing next to that of Black Southerners who organized despite far greater danger, and reflects that being among courageous colleagues let her overcome fear at the time, even as she carries it more now, having raised a child in the movement between 1962 and 1965.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Singing Tree of Life From Johns Island
clips.summary: The Carawans perform "Ain't You Got a Right to the Tree of Life," a song collected on Johns Island, South Carolina, with its verses on planting, struggle, and friends tried and true.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: The Story of Ed Cabbell
clips.summary: Candie tells the story of Ed Cabbell, a West Virginia African American friend and historian who insists on the long, strong history of Black people in Appalachia, recalling his surprise at the mixed singing group when he first came to a Highlander workshop before meeting Guy.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: When White Activists Were Challenged in 1965
clips.summary: Candie describes the 1965 moment when white people were voted out of SNCC amid growing cynicism about racism, and credits Myles Horton for taking it seriously, affirming that Black people led the civil rights movement and that white organizers should build allies and work where racism lived in white communities.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Highlander Turns Toward Appalachia
clips.summary: Candie explains how Septima Clark moved to SCLC's Citizenship Education Program with Bernice Robinson following, and how Highlander brought Appalachian staff aboard in the mid-1960s, sending them into mountain communities to find places with both anger and hope where people might be moved to act.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Leaving Johns Island for New York
clips.summary: Candie recalls the couple's years on Johns Island, South Carolina working with Bill Saunders and older tradition-bearing musicians, their self-questioning about being so immersed in a Black community while putting on festivals of its music, and their move to Pittsfield, New York for two years to publish collected materials and revive Guy's singing career.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: A Highlander Stint in Pike County
clips.summary: While living in Pittsfield, the Carawans got a call from Highlander to join the team listening to Appalachian communities, and spent about six months in Pike County, Kentucky, a sharp contrast and a major learning experience after Pittsfield and the Black South.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Recruited to Pitzer College
clips.summary: Candie recounts a call from a political scientist named Lucien at the new, experimental Pitzer College in Claremont, California, inviting Guy to teach. With their son Evan ready for kindergarten and their next move uncertain, the family relocated to Claremont.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: The Appalachian Field Study Program
clips.summary: Candie tells how their son found kindergarten dull after a life of moving, and how Guy, after one semester, created an Appalachian Field Study Program taking Pitzer College and Pomona College students into the mountains. The interview closes with Candie performing Joan Boyd's "Come Home," a song from Big Stone Gap, Virginia, and the LoC and Smithsonian closing card.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Born at the Alcorn Infirmary
clips.summary: James Miller recalls being the only one of his siblings born in a hospital, at the Alcorn State infirmary in 1953 because his mother had a difficult pregnancy, in a community where a midwife named Jessie delivered most of the babies.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Growing Up in a Segregated Society
clips.summary: He describes a carefree boyhood on the Alcorn campus that changed when the movement arrived and gave him something to focus on, in a society where white folks ran everything but poor Black and poor white families sometimes built quiet one-to-one coalitions, his own father a World War II veteran of the European theater.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Paternalism and the Sharecropper's Bargain
clips.summary: James reflects on the paternalistic bonds his father held with powerful white men who lent him money and never scolded James for joining the movement, and on the manhood his father compromised, jumping through hoops, to support his family, threaded through with questions of class within the Black community.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Interwoven White Power Structure
clips.summary: He explains how the same white men ran city hall, the courthouse, the school board, the banks, and the stores, why that interlocking power made the boycott of the merchants also a boycott of the politicians, and how the daily denial of being called Mr. and Mrs. came down to respect.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Carolyn's Upbringing in Hermanville
clips.summary: Carolyn Miller describes a more middle-class childhood in Hermanville, raised by grandparents, her grandmother a longtime schoolteacher and her grandfather a farmer who built up some eight hundred acres and Black workers through hard work alone, earning a different kind of standing with whites.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Sheltered Farm Childhood
clips.summary: She recalls a self-sufficient farm where the family ate everything they grew and neighbors lived a mile apart, where she never played with white children and was taught by her grandmother that she was just as good as anyone, until middle school brought her to Port Gibson and her parents' separation.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Fathers Who Went to War
clips.summary: Both Millers trace their fathers' military service, a World War II and a Korean veteran, and how men who had never traveled returned from overseas to face a democracy that would not let them participate, an experience that quietly empowered a generation and that the movement used to expose America's hypocrisy.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Shell Shock and a Lawyer's Humanity
clips.summary: Carolyn recounts her father returning shell-shocked and schizophrenic, unable to get the care he needed, and the heartbreaking work of restraining him during episodes, helped each time by the white attorney Alvin Binder, whose phone calls she remembers as an act of plain humanity.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: All-Black Schools and a Teacher Who Fed the Children
clips.summary: They describe the all-Black Hermanville school where teachers lived in the community, where Carolyn's grandmother Mrs. Turnipseed fed the hungry children she knew would need it, and where ragged hand-me-down books made clear, only in hindsight, how unequal the schooling was.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Teacher, a Father, and the Plow
clips.summary: James, by his own account a troublemaker, remembers his teacher Miss Rachel Wilson walking out to the field where his father was plowing, and his father telling her to do her job and discipline the boy, with the promise that he had her back.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Integration's Hidden Cost
clips.summary: James argues that the close all-Black school system was strong and that integration came at the expense of Black culture and heritage, assimilated and dropped for what was assumed to be better, even as he holds to education as Malcolm X's passport to the future.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The County Administrator Job
clips.summary: James recalls coming back to Claiborne County to serve as county administrator, helped into the post by Alvin Binder, and turns to the lesson that Black officials must be held accountable to act in everyone's interest rather than their own, building on trust and mutual respect in a democracy.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Fair Street: A Thriving Black Main Street
clips.summary: They paint Fair Street, a single block that felt like a carnival every Saturday, lined with thriving Black-owned cafes, juke joints, and grocery stores, with only the fish market in white hands, a place so alive you could hardly walk the sidewalk.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Blues on Fair Street
clips.summary: James remembers the blues greats who played the Fair Street joints, Bobby Bland, John Lee Hooker, Muddy Waters, in a thriving community that knew its one block and knew not to wander up to Main Street.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Bumping Into a White Girl
clips.summary: James tells of running down a lit-up Main Street at Christmas as a boy, accidentally knocking over a little white girl, and her redneck father throwing him to the concrete with a slur and a threat, a brush with death he kept to himself and later understood as his own Emmett Till moment.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: From Emmett Till to Tamir Rice
clips.summary: He draws the line from his own near-killing to the deaths of Emmett Till, Tamir Rice, and Laquan McDonald, and back to his recurring theme that Black leaders placed in authority must be held accountable to those who sacrificed in the movement.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Killing of Dr. King
clips.topic: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.
clips.summary: They recall the assassination of Dr. King rippling through the community, the sky going dark, and Carolyn, a sheltered girl until then, suddenly afraid to get on the school bus or leave the house, learning for the first time to be afraid.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Talk, and the Courage of an Open Casket
clips.summary: Carolyn describes the talk Black families gave their children after King's death about what they could and could not do, and James honors the courage of Mamie Till in showing her son's body and the power of those Jet magazine images to reach every Black household.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Knife Across the Bridge
clips.summary: James recounts tense, sometimes violent encounters walking home across the bridge with students from the white military academy, including a night a thrown object and a fight left him certain the white boys would kill him.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: George Henry and the Youth NAACP
clips.summary: They describe finding the movement through the NAACP meetings at First African Methodist Episcopal Church, Carolyn's grandmother walking the family miles into town, and a youth chapter under organizer George Henry that trained the brighter kids to canvass and register voters.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Veterans Who Brought Militancy Home
clips.summary: James frames Rudy Shields and a whole cadre of returning veterans across World War II, Korea, and Vietnam as men who brought a militant energy and real strategy, including weapons training, back to their communities.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Black Men With Guns
clips.summary: He recalls the power of imagining twenty-five or thirty armed Black men keeping the peace, and the moment Dan McKay came to the organizers to de-escalate, trading a no-public-carry ordinance for the guns being put away.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Raising Cane on the Boycott-Breakers
clips.summary: James recounts the dehumanizing but effective discipline of the boycott, including the night a revenuer caught breaking it was whipped with a switch in an alley off crowded Fair Street, a story that circled the Black community for years.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Boycott and How It Held
clips.summary: They explain the boycott of the white merchants who were also the politicians, how the Deacons quietly watched and listed who entered the stores, and why most people honored it less from fear than from a shared seriousness about the atrocities around them.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Watching a Leader Materialize
clips.summary: James describes a big church meeting where Rudy Shields drew a sharp question that knocked back a reluctant bourgeois man, then called him up front, and from that moment watched a community leader materialize before his eyes.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Rudy Shields, the Chameleon Organizer
clips.summary: They describe Rudy Shields, an unremarkable-looking man from out of state by way of Chicago, as a genuine and gifted organizer who got things done through other people, empowering the local community in a model James calls beautiful for community development.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Sophisticated White Response
clips.summary: James argues that white Claiborne County put the open-Klan model in check and answered the movement institutionally, infiltrating the boards and parties, organizing lawyers, building a segregation academy, and assembling a biracial committee of handpicked Black members to keep separate-but-equal in place.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Showdown at First AME
clips.summary: They recall the charged meeting at First AME where the white-backed Human Relations Committee, Charles Evers and the NAACP, and Rudy Shields each pressed competing aims, James voicing respect for Evers's bluntness, and connect it to the boycott case that became NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Rudy Shields Takes the Gun
clips.summary: They describe the most profound thing they saw Rudy Shields do, walking up to an armed man named Davis and taking the gun from his hand, and the face-to-face confrontations in which Black men spoke to white men as equals, something that, in that time, could get a man killed.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Mass Meetings at First AME
clips.summary: They close on the mass meetings at the overflowing First AME, people crowded outside the open windows, Deacons standing guard, and freedom songs pouring from the rafters, James grateful to have lived in revolutionary times.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Welcome in Little Rock
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier opens the September 2011 interview at Carrie Lamar Young's Little Rock home, and she begins with her parents Lazarus and Lottie Lamar and her place as the youngest of four girls in a family of six children.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Sharecropping in Lexa
clips.summary: She describes growing up sharecropping in Lexa, raising the family's own gardens and livestock and working the cotton fields, riding the train into Helena to shop, and a father who reached only the third grade yet was sharp with numbers.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Midwife Mother and a Family Renamed
clips.summary: Young recalls her mother, a skilled midwife who could turn a breach baby and who concealed that she could not read until Young was twenty-three, and the family history an uncle shared, including the plantation name Raymond that a great-great-grandfather changed to Lamar.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Grandfather Who Whittled, and the Shotgun House
clips.summary: She remembers a grandfather who lived to one hundred four and whittled rings and bamboo flutes for the children, the triplex shotgun house they shared with two other families, and the communal hog slaughters and crackling-making of a deeply rural life.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: School Between the Harvests
clips.summary: Young describes a schooling pulled apart by the harvest, with backless hand-me-down books from the white schools, and how being the youngest girl gave her a head start and spared her some of the field work her older siblings lost school to.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Learning Jim Crow, and a Father's Armed Watch
clips.summary: She recalls learning to navigate Jim Crow by watching her parents and by her mother's counsel never to fear asking for what she needed, and the night her father slipped out with a gun to join men guarding the road to protect a young Black man a white merchant's daughter had accused.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Girl in the Cotton Wagon
clips.summary: Young tells of a white girl her own age who taunted her in the cotton wagon until Young nearly threw her out, stopped only by her father passing by, and of running into the same woman decades later at a Western Union counter, where Young made her serve the waiting Black customers.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Other Side of the Tracks
clips.summary: She maps Lexa's racial geography around the railroad tracks, the Black side with its train station, cafe, and garage, and the Italian and German farming families like the Muscalinos that her family chose to work with.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Picking Cotton, and the Weighing Scale
clips.summary: Young describes a childhood of picking and chopping cotton from sunup to sundown, the whippings under the weighing scale when she fell short of a hundred pounds, the kind landowner Homer Tiles who would beg her mother to spare her, and her father's work at the ice plant and gin.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Church, Singing, and Salvation
clips.summary: She recalls her love of church and singing at First Baptist in Lexa, the revival pastor who had her sing, Miss Molly Richardson's Saturday sunshine band, and a Christian experience she only fully understood decades later.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Blood in the Earth at Elaine
clips.summary: Young recounts working the hard, cracked Phillips County ground as a teenager and asking why it was so hard, until the elders told the children about the 1919 Elaine massacre and the blood they said was in the earth.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Sister Lost, and the Colorism of School
clips.summary: She traces her determination to leave to a sister who died studying at Columbia, a loss that broke her own pursuit of higher education, and to the colorism in Black schools that discriminated by skin tone and family, which she fought through in her talents for singing, spelling, and the stage.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: SNCC Comes to West Helena
clips.summary: Young recalls SNCC organizers arriving, Howard Himmelbaum, Jim Jones, Bill Hansen, who met with local churches around voter education and abolishing the poll tax, and how she, one of the few girls to step up, got her parents' permission to work with them.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The White Backlash
clips.summary: She describes the white reprisals against the civil rights workers, Molotov cocktails thrown at their homes, arrests for vagrancy, while figures like Julian Bond, Andrew Young, Stokely Carmichael, and Marion Barry came through for multi-day church meetings.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Poll Tax Petition
clips.summary: Young recounts the work she and Howard Himmelbaum took on once they realized people could not read or write: teaching them to read and at least sign their names in a church basement so they could register and meet the new application requirements, against the threat of the vigilantes.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Desegregating the Bowling Alley
clips.summary: She tells the story of the SNCC athlete Stanley Tillman taking her to bowl at the only alley in the area, where the staff refused them shoes but could not deny them a lane, so they bowled in their socks until the police arrived and made them leave, her first game ever.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Making Sense of the Organizers
clips.summary: Young describes coming to understand the young organizers through meetings in their homes and traveling to multi-day workshops at the Presbyterian village in Ferndale, wanting to be in everything while much of the community held back.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Where the Fearlessness Came From
clips.summary: She traces her fearlessness to Lexa, a small self-sustaining community with its own post office, station, and businesses where, despite the demarcation, both sides of the track came together in a crisis, before age and family circumstance moved them to West Helena.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: An Assault in the Snow Lake Field
clips.summary: Young recounts being attacked by a group of young men on a cotton-picking trip to a Snow Lake field, an assault interrupted by the water boy that left her with an injury requiring surgery years later, an ordeal that hardened her resolve to leave the Delta.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Storefront Petition, and Deciding to Leave
clips.summary: She remembers the lonely work of gathering poll-tax-abolition signatures alone on a storefront porch, writing names for those who could only mark an X, and her decision to leave for Washington even while secretly engaged to Howard Himmelbaum.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Loving Howard Himmelbaum
clips.summary: Young describes the secretive courtship with the white organizer Howard Himmelbaum, the few in the Black community who objected, her mother's open affection for him and her father's deeper fear, less of Howard than that his daughter would be hurt.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Marches, and Memphis
clips.topic: On the Road With the Movement
clips.summary: She recalls the marches and trips she took, sharing rooms with Stokely Carmichael in Memphis around the time Dr. King was killed, and the poll-tax focus that white residents never faced because the tax existed only to keep Black people from voting.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Arrested for Asking for Food
clips.summary: Young tells of returning to West Helena with the Black United Youth one hungry summer, asking a grocery manager for food, being refused, taking what she needed, and being jailed until the police recognized her father and let her go.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Wider Delta, and Judge Hester's Threat
clips.summary: She sketches the wider Arkansas effort in Gould, Pine Bluff, and Forrest City, the shootout that made people wary of Forrest City, the Helena judge who threatened the young engineer James Heel to leave town, and the Masons who quietly took protective action.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Divided Community, and an Open Home
clips.summary: Young describes a Black community divided over SNCC, with absentee pastors and the deacons and church mothers who controlled access, the freedom houses, and her own family's openness, which once cost an uncle his life but never stopped their helping.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Myrtle Glascoe, a New Big Sister
clips.summary: She recalls Myrtle Glascoe arriving from Oakland in a Volkswagen to work the SNCC summer program, a fair-skinned Black woman who lived with the family and became the big sister Young had lost, and who carried her along on travels with her mother's blessing.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Atlanta, and the Tensions in SNCC
clips.summary: Young remembers a week of constant meetings and strategizing in Atlanta and the SNCC tensions she heard but stayed out of, trusting they would pass, alongside her impressions of Bill Hansen and Ben Grinage and the privacy the organizers kept.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Marriage, and Living the Struggle
clips.summary: She describes marrying Howard in 1967 and living the daily struggle of an interracial couple, refusing to hide who she was, balancing movement work with finishing high school, and the protection Jerry Casey and others gave the freedom house.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Nonviolence, and the Turn to Self-Defense
clips.summary: Young recalls the questioning of nonviolence as Dr. King's presence drew danger, and how the Black Panther Party and the armed students of the Cornell University protest, one of them from Little Rock, grew from a determination to protect Black lives and leaders.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Key Punch, and Suing for the Job
clips.summary: After marrying in New York she trained as a key-punch operator at the Opportunities Industrialization Center and sued employers who would not hire a qualified Black woman, winning class-action suits in Pulaski County against companies including AMF and National Old Line Life Insurance.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Fired From the Capitol for Her Marriage
clips.summary: Young describes becoming the first Black office worker in the State Capitol administration, placed in the demanding Motor Vehicle Department, and being targeted for dismissal by a director who learned she was married to a white man and ordered her gone.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Rockefeller, and Winning the Job Back
clips.summary: She recounts taking her case through attorney John W. Walker to Governor Winthrop Rockefeller, who fired the department head on the spot, the barefoot walk through hot asphalt that a reporter put in the paper, and the test she aced four times over to win the job back.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Black United Youth and the Night in Earle
clips.summary: Young describes her years with the Black United Youth and the violent night in Earle when the schools were desegregated by closing the Black high school, state troopers beating people and one aiming a Magnum at her, and the armed friends waiting back at the house.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Bobby Brown, Bob Broadwater, and Electing Rockefeller
clips.summary: She portrays Bobby Brown of the Black United Youth and the radio newsman Bob Broadwater, their push for student representation and a Black media perspective, the power struggles and Brown's anti-white stance against her own, and the negotiations that helped elect Rockefeller.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Protecting Dr. King, and a Verdict on the Late 60s
clips.summary: Young explains that she went to Memphis not to hear Dr. King but to be eyes, ears, and protection, knowing guns would be aimed at him, and her hard verdict that the wrong people stood closest to him and that the change she wanted never came.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: What the Movement Did and Didn't Change
clips.summary: She names economic change and equal access as her own goals, argues the struggle was about civil rights and the Constitution rather than measuring up to white people, and points to the drugs that came home from Vietnam and the execution of Troy Davis as signs of how far the country still fell short.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Reading the Obama Election
clips.summary: Young describes the numbness she saw toward Barack Obama until after the primary, her offense at his being asked for a resume and a birth certificate, and her firm support for a qualified Black man whose election her parents and children had died for.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Faith and Free Speech
clips.summary: She reflects on her Christian faith and her wish that the President governed more from it, and voices a strong conviction that freedom of speech must be protected even for words she considers offensive, a liberty she argues should be met with maturity rather than law.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Malcolm X Liberation University
clips.summary: Young recounts her 1971 year at Malcolm X Liberation University in Durham, drawn partly by a need for acceptance after Black peers in West Helena had not embraced her, where she studied African ethnic groups and martial arts but still met people who could not see past color when her white husband visited.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Closing: Faith, Myrtle, and Worth Long
clips.summary: In closing she thanks the Lord and Myrtle Glascoe for recognizing the field workers who gave all they had, thanks her interviewers and her employer for the time to come home, and remembers Worth Long as a gentle, free-spirited man who always visited her parents.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Opening the Interview in Orangeburg
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier and videographer John Bishop open the Civil Rights History Project interview with Cecil J. Williams at his Orangeburg, South Carolina home and studio on June 9, 2011. Williams is invited to begin with his childhood, family, and early education.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Family and a Lifelong Orangeburg Home
clips.summary: Williams describes being the third child of Ethel and Cecil J. Williams, his father a self-employed tailor in Orangeburg and his mother a lifelong educator at the college, high school, and elementary levels. He explains why he chose to remain in Orangeburg despite its being a battleground for his race.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Becoming Aware of the Segregated Bus
clips.summary: Williams recalls becoming conscious of race around age seven or eight, when his mother explained that they had to sit in a designated section of the Orangeburg city bus. He describes the shifting seating line and how a crowded section meant standing or not boarding at all.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Parallel Black and White Societies
clips.summary: Williams describes Orangeburg as two parallel societies with separate playgrounds, schools, and movie theaters, where Black patrons sat in a designated section and could not use downtown lunch counters. He notes that interstate Greyhound and Trailways travel was likewise segregated.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Wilkinson High, Quick Street, Two Colleges
clips.summary: Williams recounts attending segregated schools and graduating from Wilkinson High School, with his family living on Quick Street two blocks from South Carolina State College. He situates Orangeburg as an agricultural community midway between Columbia and Charleston, roughly forty percent African American, shaped by Claflin and South Carolina State College.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Tailor Shop and a First Camera
clips.summary: Williams explains that his father's tailoring business served an eighty percent white clientele, doing alterations for downtown stores such as Belk-Hudson, Bar Shays, and Limehouse. He recalls receiving a Kodak Baby Brownie inherited from his brother and beginning to photograph people in their Sunday finery for a dollar or two, his first photographic hustle.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Edisto Gardens and Saturday Movies
clips.summary: Williams describes Edisto Gardens, open to all races, alongside contradictory barriers such as drive-in theaters closed to Black patrons. He recalls Saturdays at the Black movie theater watching serials like Captain Marvel and The Phantom, and how motion pictures, including an early viewing of Gone With the Wind, shaped his development as an aspiring photographer.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Mixed-Race Household Under Jim Crow
clips.summary: Williams reflects on a caring low-income household where his grandmother's wisdom helped raise him, and on the puzzle of his mixed-race ancestry: Indian and Black on his father's side, Black and white on his mother's. He describes the confusion this created for a young child navigating racial placement.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: State-Sanctioned Legal Segregation by Law
clips.summary: Williams distinguishes the legal, state-sanctioned segregation he grew up under from other forms, describing courthouse signs for white and colored water fountains and the federal, state, county, and city regulations that governed where Black citizens could attend school or ride a bus.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: E.C. Jones and the Clarendon County Churches
clips.summary: Born in 1937, Williams recalls connecting his camera to civil rights work around age eleven through mentor E.C. Jones of Sumter, whose Majestic Studio was called to photograph churches in Clarendon County where the Briggs, DeLaine, and Pearson families were waging the battle that became Briggs v. Elliott. Jones drove him to those Sunday church meetings.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Photographing for State and the NAACP
clips.summary: Williams describes how E.C. Jones, who bought out-of-date supplies near the Sumter Air Force base, pulled him into shooting for South Carolina State College to save an eighty-mile round trip. As the NAACP and Rev. Joseph A. DeLaine called Jones into action, Williams accompanied him to make many of the Briggs v. Elliott photographs, connected through his teacher Robert Howard.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Thurgood Marshall Off the Train
clips.summary: Williams recounts taking one of his first flashbulb photographs, of Thurgood Marshall stepping off the train in Charleston, after Orangeburg NAACP branch president Squire Morgan took him to the Briggs trial. He explains why movement leaders valued careful photographic documentation when the local press ignored African American news.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Correspondent for Jet at Fifteen
clips.summary: Williams describes becoming an official Jet magazine correspondent at age fifteen, shooting also for the Afro-American and the Pittsburgh Courier, after Jet came to Orangeburg from Chicago to cover the developing story. He recalls the Clarendon County gatherings he and E.C. Jones photographed, where many Briggs participants, run off their land, returned for commemorations and reunions at St. Mark.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Clarendon County as the Catalyst
clips.summary: Williams argues that Clarendon County and Orangeburg were the underrecognized origin of the civil rights movement, predating the 1955 Montgomery framing. He traces how the Briggs case, beginning as a bus-transportation effort, led Thurgood Marshall to see it as the template that became Brown v. Board of Education rather than suing every school district.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Muted Response to Brown
clips.summary: Asked about the 1954 Brown decision, Williams recalls the danger that surrounded it, with Briggs and DeLaine petitioners already forced to leave. He notes his mother taught under Rev. J.A. DeLaine, giving the family close ties, and explains that white control of employment put livelihoods and lives at risk for anyone associated with the effort.
related_events: Brown v. Board of Education decision May 1954

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Violence and Warnings to the Family
clips.summary: Williams reflects on the extrajudicial racial violence in Orangeburg County, Elloree, and Cameron, including people who simply disappeared. He recalls white businessmen approaching his father perhaps three times, warning that outside agitators were using him, and how his mother, mindful of Rev. J.A. DeLaine's fate, spoke harshly out of fear while his parents' generation formed accommodations to survive.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Parents Who Bought the Cameras
clips.summary: Williams affirms his parents never asked him to stop, instead supporting his work and buying his cameras. He recounts begging his mother during a thunderstorm, with a captive audience, for a Brownie Hawkeye he had seen in the Sears Roebuck catalog, the first camera he owned with a flash unit attached.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Photographing the Klan on Belleville Road
clips.summary: Williams clarifies that he attended a Ku Klux Klan rally on Belleville Road while contributing photographs to the Orangeburg Times and Democrat. Arriving after the rally ended, he found a highway patrolman and the Grand Dragon putting out a cross burning, and the three re-erected the burned cross so he could photograph them, an image he has since lost and a danger that angered his mother.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Breaking Barriers as a Photographer
clips.summary: Williams reflects that his lighter skin complexion let him break barriers for his race, recalling how at eighteen he sought membership in the South Carolina Professional Photographers of America, which had no African American members. After calling membership chairperson Dan Bohrn, he was welcomed to the Hilton Head meeting as the only Black photographer in the group photo.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Meeting John F. Kennedy in New York
clips.summary: During a Claflin semester break in January 1960, Williams reads that Senator John F. Kennedy will speak at the Roosevelt Hotel. As security began to remove him from the ballroom, Kennedy and his wife arrived and stopped them, and after Williams mentioned his Claflin political science class, Kennedy cleared two seats and sat him beside Chet Huntley and David Brinkley for close-up photographs.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Aboard the Caroline and Kennedy's Death
clips.summary: Williams recounts sending his photographs to Kennedy and later being the only press person allowed aboard the campaign plane Caroline when Kennedy landed in Columbia, South Carolina. He reflects on his association with a future president, on the Civil Rights Act that followed under Johnson, and on his distress at Kennedy's early death.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Orangeburg as a Second Battleground
clips.summary: Returning from a break, Williams frames Orangeburg as the second of four incidents that created the modern civil rights movement. With most Briggs participants run out of Clarendon County, the movement spread to Orangeburg, a battleground rarely mentioned in history books other than his own.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Marshall's Rally at the Claflin Gymnasium
clips.summary: Williams describes Thurgood Marshall returning after the Brown victory to speak at the Claflin University gymnasium, inspiring the Orangeburg community around 1955 to be among the first to send their children in a test of the Supreme Court ruling. From his inside position he photographed Marshall with leaders such as James Sulton and Matthew McCollom.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Preserving Marshall's Deteriorating Negatives
clips.summary: Williams recalls photographing the packed Seabrook gymnasium rally of about 1,100 people, and how his deteriorating four-by-five and 120 negatives of Thurgood Marshall and Senator Kennedy were later scanned onto floppy disks while he worked on Freedom & Justice. He did not at first connect this Marshall with the man he had photographed years earlier in Charleston during the Briggs hearings.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Bearing Witness for Future Historians
clips.summary: Williams calls his record of the unsung Elloree School teachers his defining moment as a photographer, with their pictures in no other publication. He argues the movement will hold greater importance fifty to a hundred years from now, having established America's integrity before a watching world and brought morality, freedom, justice, and equality that made the country better for everyone.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Fred H. Moore and the Counter-Boycott
clips.summary: Williams begins the story of his rescinded scholarship by explaining the pressures on state-funded South Carolina State College versus Methodist-rooted Claflin. He describes how student leader Fred H. Moore was expelled for leading students who supported the Orangeburg counter-boycott, after parents petitioned School District 5 and white pressure whittled the signers from around twenty down to nine.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Rescinded Scholarship, a Matching Offer
clips.summary: Williams describes the Citizens' Council pressuring petition supporters and the Black counter-boycott of selected merchants, including the Coca-Cola plant owner. After president B.C. Turner, embarrassed by his Jet photographs of Fred H. Moore, had Harold Crawford rescind his South Carolina State College scholarship, Claflin's new president Dr. H.V. Manning matched it exactly, and Williams enrolled to study art and photography.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Melting Pot of Student Activism
clips.summary: Williams describes Orangeburg's two African American colleges, South Carolina State and Claflin, drawing students from across the state and nation into a melting pot of activism. He explains that students, unlike their parents, wanted the Supreme Court ruling realized now, placing Orangeburg at the epicenter of student activism even before Greensboro.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Erasing the Petition by Pressure
clips.summary: Williams confirms that the Citizens' Council moved quickly because the parents' petition came soon after Brown, seeking to erase every name so the petition would become moot. He recalls that his art teacher Arthur Rose of Charleston was one signer, often turning art education classes into discussions of the movement he was willing to give his life for.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Elloree Teachers Resign
clips.summary: Williams describes South Carolina in turmoil after Brown, with a school in Cameron closing and the all-white legislature requiring teachers to declare NAACP membership before reemployment. The Elloree elementary teachers resigned in April 1956 rather than sign, and principal Mr. Davis called Williams to photograph them, an image that spanned two pages and became Jet's largest published picture at the time.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Cost the Teachers Paid
clips.summary: Williams describes the NAACP being branded communist-inspired, making membership risky in white-controlled employment. He laments that the Elloree teachers' positions were quickly filled by others outside the movement, that many never regained employment in South Carolina, and that fewer than three remain alive, representing the heroism of countless nameless grassroots people whose actions he has tried to record.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Front Seat to History on Boulevard
clips.summary: Newly married after graduating in 1960, Williams explains how a Black business drew support from Black citizens, with South Carolina State and Claflin as main contractors for yearbooks. His studio on Boulevard, opposite the campuses and alongside other Black businesses, gave him a front-row view as marches passed his door, what he calls his front seat to history.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Continuous Orangeburg Movement
clips.summary: Williams contrasts the episodic way history books record civil rights incidents with Orangeburg's continuous protest from about 1947 through 1968, attributing this steadfastness to citizens close to the Clarendon County situation. He points to African American soldiers returning from the world's theaters only to be treated as second-class citizens, naming James Sulton as a postwar Orangeburg leader.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Harvey Gantt's Admission to Clemson
clips.summary: Williams recounts Harvey Gantt of Charleston winning admission to Clemson around 1963 through Matthew J. Perry Jr. and Constance Baker Motley after a District Court order. He covered the event exclusively for Jet magazine and the Black press as one of only three African Americans on the campus that morning, amid awareness that violence could erupt as it had for James Meredith.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Closed Campus and a Denied Letter
clips.summary: Williams explains that authorities closed the Clemson campus and ordered Black employees, faculty, and students away, leaving only Gantt, reporters, Strom Thurmond, and police. Traveling in a convoy with Perry and Motley, he reflects that Gantt achieved a dream he once held, recalling his own ambitions toward architecture and a letter to Governor Strom Thurmond about out-of-state tuition that went unanswered.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Setting the Pace and Mourning Kennedy
clips.summary: Williams notes that Gantt's quiet admission set the pace for integrating Clemson and the University of South Carolina, where African Americans had attended during Reconstruction. Asked about Kennedy's 1963 assassination, he recalls a community, Black and white, deeply shaken, with a silent ceremony at State College, crediting Kennedy with setting the mid-1960s Civil Rights Act in motion under Johnson.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Turned Away at St. Paul Methodist
clips.summary: Williams describes his 1961 photograph of friends Dorothy Vann and Emmanuel Hixson, students from out of state, being turned away from a downtown Orangeburg church. Tipped off, he ran up the steps as Mayor Clyde Fair, a member of St. Paul Methodist Church, told the students it was a segregated church and to worship with their own people.
related_events: 1961 St. Paul Methodist Church integration attempt

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Photograph That Circled the World
clips.summary: Williams recounts selling the church photograph to the Associated Press in Columbia for thirty-five dollars. The next day it ran worldwide, including front pages in Germany, the Washington Star, and the New York Times, showing a southern mayor refusing a Black worshipper and revealing Sunday morning as one of the most segregated times in America.
related_events: 1961 St. Paul Methodist Church integration attempt

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Selective Service Drafts Him
clips.summary: About a month later, Williams, then pursuing his master's at South Carolina State College with a deferment, is summoned to the Selective Service office on Doyle Street, where a clipping of his church photograph floated to the floor from his file. Given thirty days, he joins the Air Force, closes his studio, is injured in flight training, and returns to Orangeburg to rebuild.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Landmarks and an Attempted Bribe
clips.summary: Mosnier lists the landmarks Williams documented, including 1962 State Capitol protests and the 1963 University of South Carolina desegregation. Williams recounts being approached twice by the Citizens' Council, including a man known only as Jack who tried to buy his photograph of a meeting at Trinity AME Church, an attempted bribe he refused and reported to Rev. Matthew McCollom.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Naming Agitators, Tokenism, Resistance
clips.summary: Williams counters the press framing of outside agitators, communists, and infiltrators, insisting the movement was an African American one that Black citizens started. Asked how Orangeburg changed after the 1964 and 1965 legislation, he describes a period of tokenism and Black hiring, with changes appreciated yet pockets of resistance remaining in segregated churches, bowling alleys, and restaurants.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The All-Star Bowling Alley Protest
clips.summary: Williams describes the All-Star Bowling Alley on Russell Street with its small white-only sign, which students passed walking uptown from South Carolina State and Claflin. After students who had been driving to Columbia to bowl were arrested, others joined the protest beginning around February 6, and as the yearbook photographer Williams was present among 250 to 300 students facing highway patrolmen and police.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Broken Window and a Beating
clips.summary: Pressed against a vacant store's glass in the packed strip mall, a student broke a window, and police raised their clubs and began hitting people as if it were intentional, sending everyone running. Fleeing toward campus with his twin-lens camera, Williams looked back near Sutcliffe's furniture store to see two city policemen beat a fallen female student on the head, three days before the Orangeburg Massacre.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Nine Patrolmen Fire on Students
clips.summary: On the night of the massacre Williams returns from Columbia but cannot reach State College, finding police blocking College Avenue, an obstruction he later realized probably saved his life. After students built a bonfire, nine highway patrolmen crept onto campus and fired shotguns into a crowd of about 250, killing three and wounding about twenty-seven, almost all shot from the back.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Recovering the Shotgun Shells
clips.summary: Citing Jack Bass's book The Orangeburg Massacre and his own Orangeburg 1968, Williams recounts driving to the campus on a freezing, overcast morning to find the maintenance crew clearing debris where the students were killed. He gathered shotgun shells into his pockets and posed a photograph of a student holding the seven, eight, or nine shells, which appeared in Time, Newsweek, and papers across the United States.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Evidence Confiscated, Hoover's Offer Refused
clips.summary: Williams explains that the FBI confiscated the shells, later used at the Florence, South Carolina trial where the nine patrolmen were found not guilty. The agent who took them relayed that J. Edgar Hoover had asked whether Williams wanted to become an FBI agent, an offer he refused, suspecting they wanted to make an informant of him, before warning local leaders McCollom, Sulton, and Oscar Butler.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Defenseless, Forgotten, and Finney's Protest
clips.summary: Williams insists the killings were a massacre because the students were defenseless and unarmed, far less known than the Kent State Massacre. Mosnier notes that Ernest A. Finney Jr., then head of the South Carolina Human Affairs Commission, and another member resigned in 1969 over the lack of prosecutorial effort, which Williams confirms before a break.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Pink Palace and 388 Arrests
clips.summary: Williams turns to his books and the Pink Palace, the city police jail where he was jailed twice but bailed out by the NAACP so he could keep photographing. He describes a front-page image of the stockade where 388 demonstrators were arrested in 1960, so many that penitentiary buses were called from Columbia, with soaked students funneled behind the jail in the wake of the Greensboro sit-ins.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Well-Dressed Students and a Prayer Demonstration
