clips.summary: At the December 13, 1982 foreclosure auction on a roughly 153,000-dollar mortgage, the mortgage holder's son bid for the lender while Bailey arrived with about fifty thousand dollars raised through radiothons, until the union's Jim Smith appeared thirty minutes early with a twenty-five-thousand-dollar check.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Bidding to $144,000
clips.summary: On the courthouse steps A.W. Willis proposed a fifty-thousand-dollar loan from the Black-owned Tri-State Bank, whose head Jesse Turner was in Bailey's group, backed by last-minute guarantees from Jim Smith and cosmetics man Paul Shapiro; Bailey bid the lender up to a hundred forty-four thousand dollars and called a halt, and the hammer fell.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Bank Man Now
clips.summary: Bailey called Peabody Hotel owner Jack Belz that night to raise the final four thousand dollars for the cashier's check and suddenly owned a museum with no plan, letting Walter Bailey keep running the place; a threat from Coretta Scott King's lawyer to sue over the King name led him to drop it and rename the group the Lorraine Motel Civil Rights Museum Foundation, a blessing in disguise.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Place to Make New Activists
clips.summary: Freed by the name change from clearing every decision with a King family that called the project morbid, Bailey describes a vision drawn from his own movement experience: not a memorial to King but a place of magnetism that would rekindle the movement's spirit and convert the young into new activists, with volunteer architects drafting the first rough plans.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: An $8.8 Million Dream
clips.summary: A fifty-thousand-dollar grant raised through a Peabody gathering funded consultant proposals, and Bailey chose Benjamin Lawless, whose design captured the spirit he wanted: dynamic, immersive exhibits, a burned bus, the tear gas and barking dogs meant to put visitors on the scene, at a price tag of 8.8 million dollars.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Lobbying the Legislature
clips.summary: Lawless's formula called for half from the state and a quarter each from city and county; after the county refused and a legislative bill stalled, Bailey called Governor Lamar Alexander to find it, then lobbied Nashville office to office, presenting the museum as a Martin Luther King project to a wary Democratic legislature that feared it would stir antagonism.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Black Legislators' Priority
clips.summary: A white legislator told Bailey frankly that the Black Memphis legislators had to make the museum their number-one priority over their own projects, and when they did the bill passed the House 83 to 17 and the Senate 33 to nothing; the funding settled at 2.2 million each from city and county and 4.4 million from the state, with Paul Shapiro's ten thousand dollars the only local business gift.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Giving Title to the State
clips.summary: With A.W. Willis falling to cancer and the three governments refusing to hand money to a nonprofit, Bailey deeded the property to the state on condition it build the museum to Lawless's plans, glad to limit local political interference; an architect fight was resolved by giving the job to the Nashville Black firm McKissack and McKissack under the state architect's control.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Keeping the Board at Bay
clips.summary: As construction raced ahead, Bailey stonewalled and filibustered his own board to keep their politics out of the exhibit design, while the board pushed new elections and bylaw changes to gain control and a millionaire member, J.R. Pitt Hyde, stepped into the vacuum and aligned with the faction against him.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Better to Ask Forgiveness
clips.summary: Bailey raced to build the museum right before the board could seize control, on the principle that it is better to ask forgiveness than permission, while the opposition recruited Benjamin Hooks, a longtime absentee board member, to stand for president against him.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Resignation
clips.summary: A nine-to-six vote installed Hooks as president and Hyde as executive-committee chairman, and Bailey read a resignation letter he had already prepared; his plan, marked by bringing Bob Moses, Julian Bond, and John Lewis to the opening, had been to make the site a center of action rather than the corporate institution it became.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Center, Not Museum
clips.topic: What He Wanted It to Be
clips.summary: Bailey explains naming it a museum rather than a center for political safety while he still needed government approvals, his real aim being exhibits that would re-energize visitors into activism; he voices his disappointment that it never became that, and the board's answer that if he wanted such a thing he should build his own.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Captured by the Corporations
clips.summary: Bailey names the corporations seated at the board, FedEx, International Paper, AutoZone under Pitt Hyde during a discrimination suit, First Tennessee, and charges that corporate funding has killed the movement from the museum to an NAACP that will not cross its fast-food donors, closing on his creed that institutions serve only as long as they make change and become obstacles when they do not.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Sit-Ins Before They Had a Name
clips.summary: Introduced by David Cline in December 2015, Diamond traces his first eighteen years to a totally segregated Petersburg, Virginia, where he staged haphazard, solitary protests before the sit-in had a name, refusing to leave the five-and-dime lunch counter until the police came and venturing past the colored entrance into the library stacks.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: A Summer in the Catskills
clips.summary: At sixteen Diamond left home for Monticello, New York, in the Catskills, and when the laundry job fell through he bluffed his way into a short-order cook position he had no experience for, phoning a friend to vouch for a fabricated reference; the integrated resort town was his first real exposure to white people.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: The Nonviolent Action Group
clips.summary: Enrolling at Howard University in 1959 as a physics major, Diamond found the campus doing nothing about segregation until Greensboro, when he and others formed the Nonviolent Action Group and, because the District itself was not segregated by law, crossed into Virginia to sit in at a segregated lunch counter.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: The Drug Fair and the Nazi Party
clips.summary: Diamond recounts the group's first sit-in at the Drug Fair lunch counter in Cherrydale, Arlington, desegregating the area within two weeks, and the day George Lincoln Rockwell and his American Nazi Party surrounded the demonstrators, a danger his youthful exuberance kept him from fully registering.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Nonviolence as a Tactic
clips.summary: Looking at the old photographs of his own calm, Diamond explains his lasting position that nonviolence was a tactic, not a philosophy or creed: the police and the army held the guns and the demonstrators had nothing, so violence made no sense, though off-camera he would gladly have fought back.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: The Glen Echo Picket
clips.summary: After the Virginia victory the group turned to Glen Echo Park, a Maryland amusement park closed to people of color, picketing it through the summer of 1960; Diamond recalls the stunt arranged with labor figure Hyman Bookbinder, arriving by black limousine in an African gown with a white chauffeur and interpreter to test the gate in broken French.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Into Jackson
clips.summary: When the Freedom Rides nearly collapsed after the Anniston bus burning, Diamond was among those who continued the ride from Montgomery to Jackson under a heavy National Guard, state-police, and helicopter escort; only later did he learn of the deal between Robert Kennedy and Mississippi's governor that spared them a beating but guaranteed their arrest for breach of peace.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Performing for the Cameras
clips.summary: Diamond describes the press as the riders' salvation and his own conscious restraint for the cameras, knowing a single thrown punch would cost them their goodwill; he had no formal nonviolence training, only the instinct that a fight he was outnumbered in could not be won, and recalls a Jet photograph that wrongly seemed to show him throwing a punch.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Running Out of Space
clips.summary: Arrested the moment he stepped off the bus, Diamond was held in the Hinds County jail, then moved to the county prison farm and the state penitentiary, the prisoners segregated by race and sex; it was weeks before he understood that the constant transfers were because the jails kept running out of space as more buses arrived.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: A Cell With James Farmer
clips.summary: On the penitentiary's death row Diamond shared a cell with CORE director James Farmer and later a McComb house with Bob Moses, and describes being humbled into silence by both, his own vociferous leadership met with monosyllables; he marvels that Moses turned out to have relatives back in Petersburg.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Parents Who Learned From Reporters
clips.summary: Diamond reflects on a lifelong independence, leaving home at seventeen and holding office from class president onward, that meant he never asked permission for what he did; like other young activists, his parents learned of his arrests only when a reporter called, proud of his involvement but wishing it had been someone else's child.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Into SNCC at McComb
clips.summary: Having lost the semester to Parchman, Diamond flew to Atlanta and took his first SNCC assignment on the voter-registration project in Pike County around McComb, Mississippi; connections made through his Washington work and a Nashville leadership seminar had already linked him to the movement's network, bonds that have lasted half a century.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: What We Unleashed
clips.summary: Recalling that there was not one Black officer or elected official when he was arrested, Diamond describes returning for the fiftieth-anniversary reunion to a Black mayor of Jackson and a Black prison warden, change he never imagined; he closes on the inscribed warning that what is past is prologue and his belief that education is the surest way to keep the gains.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: No Long-Range Plan
clips.summary: Asked whether his involvement at nineteen felt like a choice or a necessity, Diamond says he had no long-range plan and could not have defined what he was contributing to, only that he wanted to break down the barriers of segregation; Mississippi in 1961, he found, was worse than Petersburg and far more fearful.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: A Shotgun Through the Window
clips.summary: Diamond describes the McComb house he shared with Bob Moses, run by an independent woman they called Mama Cotton, where his bed sat beneath a lattice window; one night a shotgun blast came straight through it and passed over his slender body, making the danger of the work suddenly and permanently real.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Charged With Overthrowing Louisiana
clips.summary: Sent from McComb to Holly Springs for voter registration, Diamond crossed to Baton Rouge to recruit students whom Southern University president Felton Clark had expelled over sit-ins; when Clark reopened the school and wrecked the plan, Diamond organized a class boycott to reinstate the leaders and found himself, a stranger, leading a protest on a campus that had never heard of him.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Solitary Confinement
clips.summary: Charged with criminal anarchy for trying to overthrow the state, Diamond was jailed in the East Baton Rouge general population alongside men awaiting transfer to Angola, gaining their respect as a freedom rider even as the guards offered them time off for roughing him up; an NAACP lawyer reached the FBI and he was moved to a solitary cell, where he learned he could endure isolation and told the time only by his meals.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Thirty Arrests
clips.summary: Diamond recounts voter-registration work on Maryland's eastern shore alongside Gloria Richardson, where local families took him in, and tallies roughly thirty arrests in connection with civil rights activity, more than he can recall, telling job applications to check with the FBI for the dates and locations.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: The Wisconsin Transfer
clips.summary: Raising funds for SNCC at the 1963 National Student Association Congress in Columbus, Diamond realized his Howard class was graduating while he had been in the field for two years, and a chance remark to the University of Wisconsin's student-government president led, within days, to a transcript and enrollment; he switched from physics to history and sociology to ask why organized protest had taken a century to come.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: The Harvard Offer
clips.summary: After anti-poverty work in Washington with the United Planning Organization, Diamond was asked in 1968 by a colleague at the federal Office of Economic Opportunity whether he would consider Harvard; he treated it as a silly question and went, studying race relations and crediting both his grades and the era's push to diversify the student body.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: The Only One in the Room
clips.summary: At both Wisconsin and Harvard Diamond was often the only Black student in the room, deferred to by professors to verify or refute on behalf of all people of color, a role he found uncomfortable but assumed; his one regret was forming no lasting friendships there, unlike at Howard, leaving Harvard with a degree in education and a thesis on race and ethnic relations.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: The Stokely Carmichael Story
clips.summary: Asked about a famous jailing with Stokely Carmichael, Diamond gently corrects the record, placing it at the Mississippi penitentiary rather than Nashville, and says he can neither confirm nor deny Carmichael's published account of him taunting an armed guard to shoot; he allows that he was capable of it, knowing a guard would never kill in front of a cellblock of witnesses.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: The Stolen Geometry Test
clips.summary: Diamond tells, without pride, how he learned his high IQ in high school and, terrified of a standardized geometry exam, used a baseball tossed onto a shop roof as a ruse to steal the test, only to ace it without the answers; the episode taught him he had badly underrated the education and the attentive teachers of his segregated school.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: The Bittersweet of Desegregation
clips.summary: Diamond calls desegregation bittersweet: teachers in the small segregated community knew his parents and took a deeper interest than large modern schools allow, the close binding friendships have thinned, and the historically Black colleges have been robbed of their most gifted athletes by the big recruiting institutions.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: The Glass Ceiling
clips.topic: One or Two at the Top
clips.summary: He describes a glass ceiling for any person of color, pointing to a Black CEO of American Express or Holland America as exceptions that mask how little the rest of the infrastructure has changed, and puzzles at a society that prizes athletes and entertainers over the educated; raised by a mail-carrier father and a homemaker mother, he credits an early-instilled horror of ending up broke.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Chocolate City
clips.summary: Diamond credits Marion Barry, the former SNCC chairman turned mayor, with bringing so many movement veterans into Washington government jobs in what was then called Chocolate City; the old relationships endure without much socializing, drawn together mainly now by the deaths of comrades like Julian Bond and Barry himself.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: A Career in Reform
clips.summary: Diamond sketches a winding career in reform: a high federal grade at the Labor Department secured through a Glen Echo connection, training African Americans in economic development, and then his own consulting firm advising HUD and HEW on segregated housing, race relations, and detecting fraud in Medicaid and Medicare.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Creating Wealth, Not Just a Job
clips.summary: Semi-retired since the 2008 collapse, Diamond traces the through-line from civil rights to a life of reform he would never have led as the physicist he trained to be, and argues that the next hurdle is economic: too many people seek the security of a job rather than the harder work of creating wealth, a lesson that turned him fiscally conservative.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: A Self-Described Recluse
clips.summary: Diamond admits disappointment in himself for becoming a recluse who no longer advances civil rights or mentors the young, partly because he cannot relate to their culture; he believes the work should be generational, and voices ambivalence about Black Lives Matter, fearing the framing segments an issue he would rather see as a shared human relationship.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Trump Rallies and Ellis Island
clips.topic: How Far There Is to Go
clips.summary: Pointing to the Charleston church shooting and the crowds at Donald Trump rallies that struck him as almost a Klan gathering, Diamond warns again that what is past is prologue, that a country could revert and forget the Ellis Island on which it was built; well-traveled, he is glad to have been born in America yet certain there is a long way still to go.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: A Hell of a Mosaic
clips.summary: Diamond names his most memorable moment as the 1964 Civil Rights Act and the Black officials it made possible, and recalls the 1968 crossroads of a Marshall Islands anti-poverty post, the Robert Kennedy campaign, and Harvard, ending at Harvard only because Kennedy was killed; he calls the Civil Rights Act and Obama's election the highlights, noting it took a Southerner in Lyndon Johnson to deliver, all of it rooted in the movement.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: They Took Our New Car
clips.summary: Juandalynn, the eldest, opens with her earliest memory of the movement: police seizing the family's new car when she was two or three, the only transportation they had, which the children later understood had been auctioned by the state of Alabama, along with a piece of the family property, to pay costs charged against the Montgomery bus boycott.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: The House Was Bombed
clips.summary: Donzaleigh, born just after their Montgomery home was bombed while her mother was pregnant with her, recounts how Juandalynn awoke singing in the blown-open house, the family's refuge with church member Margaret Brown, and her own infant memories of shaking, of church kisses, and of the Freedom Riders who came to stay.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: The Freedom Riders in Our Living Room
clips.summary: The sisters remember the Freedom Riders gathered in their Montgomery living room, the night Juandalynn cut Donzaleigh's braids, and the nearly a year the family spent in Margaret Brown's house before Donzaleigh's birth; Juandalynn recalls feeling alone as an only child until her sister arrived and they could talk in their own baby language.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: A Disciplined Train Ride to Atlanta
clips.summary: Donzaleigh recalls the threatening phone calls and the segregated train ride their father took the two girls on to visit the Kings in Atlanta, telling them they could come only if they asked for nothing; they rode the whole way from Montgomery without using a toilet or taking a drink, a lesson she carries that anything can be done with discipline, met at the old station by Uncle Martin.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: A Memory of Fear
clips.summary: Ralph III, born in 1959, says his earliest memory was fear: terrified the house would be bombed again as it had been in January 1957, and made more afraid when the move to Atlanta left him sleeping alone for the first time, the one child in his own room on the front of the house.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: A Light in the Hall
clips.summary: Ralph describes the hall light and open door his mother left for him, the wall he and his sisters knocked on to comfort each other, the back roads of Alabama and Georgia where they could not stop to eat or use a restroom, and the car that once followed them; over the fear lay an unspoken sense of God's protection, the Black angels his mother hung over the bed, and a nightly prayer to live until morning.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Did You Talk About the Fear
clips.summary: Asked whether they spoke of the fear among themselves, the siblings recall their father telling them plainly he could be killed, the weekly tearful goodbyes, the dinnertime phone calls threatening the whole family, and their father's counsel that a sense of humor would carry them through life's hardest moments.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Children Adapt
clips.summary: The three agree that, like children anywhere, they simply adapted, knowing the fear but not letting it dictate their lives because it was the only life they knew; Juandalynn, told she had to set the example for her siblings, was grounded by religion, by parents she could not have bettered, and by a Montgomery church community that walked her to school and embraced them.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Our Constant Companions
clips.summary: Because other parents feared to let their children near the Abernathy home, the King children were the siblings' steady playmates, through school, ballet, and football; Donzaleigh, who turned introverted and played alone in a closet, recalls jumping up at the March on Washington when Dr. King spoke of his four little children, and the birthday TV surprise she could not keep secret.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Onto the Refrigerator
clips.summary: Juandalynn remembers the tradition Uncle Martin began at their Montgomery home of lifting her onto the tall refrigerator to jump into his arms and then their father's, a treat that marked the men's return; Dr. King was at their house at least five days of the week, and in Atlanta the families swam together every Tuesday at the YMCA.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: The Family Vacations
clips.summary: The siblings recall Uncle Martin telling them how he had survived the Harlem stabbing, crawling over him as he showed where the letter opener had struck, and the vacations the two families always took together, to the 1964 New York World's Fair, to California, and in a leaner year to an Atlanta airport hotel, the boys, girls, and parents each in their own rooms.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: He Must Have a Church
clips.summary: Turning to the King-Abernathy partnership, the family describes how dependent Uncle Martin was on their father, the planner and strategist who pressed to go into Selma and Birmingham; lonely in Atlanta, King called nearly every day asking them to come, but Juanita held firm that her husband would move only with a church of his own, not as an SCLC staff member.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: We Were Content in Montgomery
clips.summary: The family recalls that the movement itself was formed the night their home was bombed, that their father pastored Montgomery's largest Black church where the boycott's mass meetings were held, and that Juanita refused to let him move merely to earn a living by speaking; only when the Kings helped find West Hunter Street Baptist Church did the family, content in Montgomery, agree to Atlanta.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Need, Love, and Friendship
clips.summary: Dated to 1961, Ralph III reads the relationship as one of need, love, and friendship: his father drawn to Uncle Martin's oratorical gift, and Martin drawn to his father's courage as a World War II sergeant, his intellect, his two pastorates, his student demonstrations at Alabama State over housing and cafeteria conditions, and his sociology degree, all the skills King lacked and could lean on, in a partnership the histories reduce to the King and Abernathy show.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: The Strategist and the Why
clips.summary: The siblings credit their father, schooled in boycotts at Alabama State, with organizing the Montgomery ministers and the first mass meeting after Rosa Parks was arrested while E.D. Nixon was away, his colleague Jo Ann Robinson and their mother running off the flyers; in the partnership their father set the strategy, the what and when and how, and Uncle Martin gave the philosophy, the why, a pairing they liken to prophets sent out two by two.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: The Civil Rights Twins
clips.summary: Donzaleigh recalls the two men dressing alike from the same stores down to the same shoes and socks, calling themselves the Civil Rights Twins, a Capricorn and a Piscean who dreamed together, finished each other's sentences, and had only one disagreement ever; nothing their wives or anyone else could do came between them, closer than brothers.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: All in Divine Order
clips.summary: Juandalynn affirms that even A.D. King, Martin's brother, felt no jealousy of how close their father and Uncle Martin were, an arrangement Ralph III calls divine order; among the tokens of that bond was the solid gold Movado watch King gave their father, engraved to his dearest friend and closest associate.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Two Black Kids in the Park
clips.summary: Ralph III recounts how he, Dexter King, and their sisters integrated Spring Street Elementary, the first integrated elementary in Georgia, where he was the undefeated running back and Dexter his blocker, the only two Black kids on the field; the integration followed the Selma to Montgomery march the children had walked, and the taunts soon followed, a classmate calling Juandalynn a cruel name.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: We Were a Family
clips.summary: Donzaleigh, small but spirited, describes pulling Juandalynn from class to rescue Ralph from a cruel teacher and standing up to the boy who threatened her sister, while Yoki King defended Juandalynn in turn; on the first day, angry parents and newspapers met them, and each Abernathy and King child was placed alone in an otherwise white class.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: You Have to Be the Best
clips.summary: Juandalynn recalls teachers, Ms. Gray and Mrs. Montague among them, who drilled into her that she had to be better than everyone because all eyes were on her, a weight made heavier after Dr. King was killed and she was told her father was now the leader; a principal even told a classmate's mother not to let her son host Ralph, with only the art teacher remembered as kind.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: The Learning Tree
clips.summary: Donzaleigh remembers her loving third-grade teacher Ms. Elliott, who pinned her A papers to the top of the class tree until the PTA objected and, in tears, taught her about racism by moving them to the bottom; after later teachers ignored her, their mother sent the children to a learning center where a white monk, Brother Clint, gave them the warmth the classroom withheld, and a Black librarian taught them French.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: The Poster Children
clips.summary: Ralph III explains the strategy behind involving children, that the young seek change while the old hope nothing changes, and that their father, asking others to bring their children to the movement, made sure his own took part; resilient and fearless, the Abernathy children became its poster children, present at every march, with the documented photographs other children lack.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Marching to Montgomery
clips.summary: Donzaleigh recalls the 1965 drive down Highway 80 to walk the Selma to Montgomery march, the one-legged white marcher Jim Letherer on the front lines, the mass meeting crowded with movie stars including Sammy Davis Jr. and Peter, Paul and Mary, and watching from her father's lap as he counted the stars' hundred-dollar bills, the treasurer who always made sure the money reached where it belonged.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Thrown Into the Cars
clips.summary: Donzaleigh remembers reaching the capitol for Dr. King's How Long, Not Long address, the tear gas and bottles at Cicero near Chicago in 1966 when the adults threw the children into the cars, and the James Meredith march where their mother defied a trooper to take Ralph to a restroom; their father, she says, wanted them above all to witness and be a part of history.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Little Ralph Is Going to Preach
clips.summary: Ralph III describes resisting the pulpit because of what the movement had cost his father and Uncle Martin, serving instead in the Georgia House and Senate before accepting his calling and earning a doctorate in theology; his sisters recall him preaching full sermons to them at home on Sunday mornings from the age of four or five, his amen corner of two.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Born Singing
clips.summary: Donzaleigh recalls deciding at the March on Washington to become an actress like the socially minded movie stars, safer than the dangerous work of their fathers, while Juandalynn says singing was simply her destiny, encouraged by Mahalia Jackson, who would stay at their home and marvel at the three voices the child could already produce; the marches gave them the freedom songs and spirituals to sing.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: It Needs to Be Continued
clips.summary: The interviewers begin to close, and the siblings protest that so much is left untold, their father's arrest, the Poor People's Campaign, the food stamp program he helped bring about, affirmative action, and the King holiday they say originated in his soul, prompting a plan to record a second part.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: The Mule Train
clips.summary: After the connection to Juandalynn in Germany is lost, the conversation resumes with Ralph III recounting his June 1968 arrest on the Poor People's Campaign Mule Train from Marks, Mississippi, stopped in Douglasville, Georgia, by Governor Lester Maddox along with all sixty-seven marchers and held overnight in a National Guard armory; nine years old, he told his worried parents that his father and Uncle Martin had done this all the time, while Donzaleigh, barred as a girl, longed to be arrested too.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: FunTown and the Fair
clips.summary: Donzaleigh recalls the privileges the boys were given, like the small plane ride the girls were denied, the Southeastern Fair where their farmer father and Uncle Martin let them ride and eat their fill, the FunTown amusement park closed to them that King wrote of in his Letter From Birmingham Jail, and the Sunday dinners and leftover communion grape juice the families shared each week.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: The Bomb Went Off
clips.summary: Donzaleigh remembers her father asking the congregation to bow in prayer the Sunday the Sixteenth Street Baptist Church was bombed, and how, running along the church afterward, she froze in terror until her brother came back and took her hand, leading her gently through her fear past the alley and the barbecue; she never used the church's public restroom again until Juandalynn's wedding, because that was where the girls had died.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Turn On the Television
clips.summary: The siblings recount learning Dr. King had been shot when a friend's mother called and told their mother to turn on the news, Juandalynn screaming and calling Yoki as the families gathered and Andy Young told their mother to pack for Memphis; Donzaleigh, insisting King would survive as he had the stabbing, rode to the airport and began to cry only when she saw her mother and Coretta embrace on the tarmac, where Mayor Ivan Allen had brought the news of his death.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Nothing Could Fill the Void
clips.summary: Donzaleigh recalls her mother going home to sleep on Uncle Martin's side of the bed so Coretta could tell her children face-to-face, the body returning in a box, and their father sitting in the dark, depressed beyond belief, comforted not even by the pool table they had built for King; Ralph III says his own first fear at the news was for his father, who had shared the Lorraine Motel room with King.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: It's Ralph, Everything's Going to Be Alright
clips.summary: Ralph III relays his father's account of King's last moments: dressed first by their arrangement, he had stepped back inside for his cologne when the shots came, and reaching the balcony he cradled King, told Andy Young to fetch towels rather than cry out, and rode in the ambulance to hold King as he took his last breaths; by King's own design, Ralph David Abernathy became SCLC president by acclamation.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: You Cannot Kill the Dream
clips.summary: Ralph III answers those who deem the movement spent after the assassination, arguing that bringing the poor to Washington to demand that the powerful feed the hungry and clothe the naked was itself a success, and that his father's words calmed a nation aflame, that nonviolence is the weapon of the strong, and that they might kill the dreamer but never the dream.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: A Date With Destiny
clips.summary: The siblings recall their father teaching that courage is going forward in the face of fear, the bomb threat that held the Memphis plane on the ground, and the Mason Temple night in 1968 when, taunted with death, King rose above it; warned by Medgar Evers's killing of what might come, the family understood the men lived on borrowed time, King leaving sweaters behind to be remembered by, having written of a date with destiny.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: More Bees With Honey
clips.topic: How They Worked as a Team
clips.summary: Donzaleigh frames her father's gift as keeping the background, calming SNCC and the other organizations into one united front so the movement could have a single head, the first time, she says, that Black Americans had truly organized where DuBois and Booker T. Washington could not; their father out-thought opponents by over-asking in demands, funded Andrew Young's run for Congress from the SCLC budget, and gave Daddy King the money for King's burial land.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Servant of All
clips.summary: The siblings close on their father's giving: handed six million dollars in HUD money for Abernathy Towers, he deeded it to his church and kept nothing, telling their mother on his deathbed that his life had been about helping people, not making money; Donzaleigh recalls his last proud phone call before he collapsed, the children taking turns at his bedside to the end, and Ralph III seals it with the scripture that the greatest among you shall be servant of all.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: I Was in the Wrong Place
clips.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Ithaca in July 2011, Dorothy Cotton opens with the pervasive childhood feeling that she was in the wrong place, that her home on North Greenleaf Street in Goldsboro, North Carolina, with its outhouse and its hardness, was alien to something in her very being.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Losing My Mother
clips.summary: Cotton recalls her mother dying when she was three, leaving her father, a skinny man with a third-grade education raised on a tenant farm, to raise four small girls through the Depression in a segregated town; she digresses to question the very habit of describing people by color, a language she now finds silly.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Strap, and What Was Done to Him
clips.summary: She describes the strap her father used on the girls, the anger it still stirs in her, and her later understanding that his own mother, home late from caring for a white family, had whipped him the same way; that knowledge leaves her feelings toward him a deep mixture of anger and forgiveness.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Airport Was Special
clips.summary: Cotton recounts her father's move to Buffalo and his work at Bethlehem Steel, the finger he lost to an axe in a Goldsboro tobacco factory, and the poignancy of how special an airport was to a man who never flew, once sullen and hurt simply because he had not been brought along to one.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Real Mixture
clips.summary: She remembers her father's pride at managing a Christmas doll, a tea set, and a shoebox of fruit, and his nervousness before white people, and sums up her feeling for him in 2011 as a real mixture of love, anger, and sadness over the deprivation of his life.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: There's Your Ready Girl
clips.summary: Cotton recalls the anger she still feels at a white boy who rode through her street singing a slur, then turns to her high school English teacher Rosa Gray, who gave her the lead in every school play and, after she recited a poem, called her a ready girl, a phrase that has stayed with her ever since.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: She Pleaded for the Prom
clips.summary: Realizing only now that Ms. Gray was the surrogate mother she had lost, Cotton describes the teacher coming to the porch to plead with her proud, poor father to let his star pupil attend the junior-senior prom, and her decades-later understanding that he refused because he could not afford to dress her as well as the other children.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Nothing But a Racket
clips.summary: Cotton remembers a brief stepmother, the churchgoing Susie Mae her father admitted he never loved, her father's own scorn for the church as nothing but a racket, and her maternal grandfather, the itinerant preacher Reverend Seymour Pellum, whose visits charged the house with an uneasy energy after he and her father, who had run off with her young mother, came to hate each other.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Three Jobs at Shaw
clips.summary: Cotton recounts how Ms. Gray, knowing college was out of reach, called Shaw University and arranged her admission with two jobs, in the dining hall and cleaning the teachers' dormitory; she worked her way through with as many as three jobs and was eventually invited to keep house for the president, Dr. Robert Prentiss Daniel.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Part Daughter, Part Housekeeper
clips.summary: She describes the complicated, often unhappy years in the Daniels' three-story home, treated as part daughter and part servant, made to work even with the flu, her father marveling at the polished artificial flowers; through a Shakespeare classmate, Harris Sims, she was introduced to an older man named George Cotton.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: He Came With a Ring
clips.summary: Cotton recalls George arriving with a ring to ask Dr. Daniel for her hand, the president warning that she was very ambitious, and the small wedding held in the president's residence where she had scrubbed and served; she followed Daniel from Shaw to Virginia State College in Petersburg, taking six years in all to finish her degree.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Consciousness Evolving
clips.summary: In Petersburg, Cotton traces a growing consciousness of the wrongness of the system to her childhood, then to the church she joined under Reverend Wyatt Tee Walker, its pastor, a regional NAACP head and a good preacher whose extracurricular work and easy company drew her in.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Library We Couldn't Use
clips.summary: As Walker's NAACP secretary, Cotton helped organize the campaign against the segregated public library and Woolworth's, training youngsters in the church to carry picket signs and a simple idea of nonviolence; the movement held mass meetings and invited a young Montgomery preacher, Martin Luther King, whom Walker had met at Virginia Union, to come and speak.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: He Wanted to Meet the Poet
clips.topic: The Three Months That Became Twenty-Three Years
clips.summary: Cotton recalls reciting a poem on the program the night King spoke in Petersburg, and King asking at the parsonage dinner to meet the young woman who had said it; before leaving he asked Walker to come build the new Southern Christian Leadership Conference in Atlanta and to bring his two aides, James Wood and Cotton, so she told her husband she would help for three months, and stayed twenty-three years.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Warring Organization
clips.summary: Cotton describes finding the Southern Christian Leadership Conference still a tiny operation, three or four people in an office, among them Lily Hunter and Ella Baker, when she arrived a wide-eyed young woman who had never traveled, sensing she was on a track to something new she could not yet name.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Finding a Home in Atlanta
clips.summary: She recounts her first weeks in Atlanta, staying briefly with Dora McDonald, who became King's secretary, and longer with an elderly landlady, Ms. Mosley, having her purse snatched soon after her husband left, and slowly learning the ropes of the growing organization.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Master's Degree
clips.summary: Cotton notes that she earned a master's degree from Boston University across these years, hungry for something on the horizon, deciding to march in the 1960 commencement after the struggle of fitting the work in, and going to Atlanta only days after receiving it.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: King Pulled Up a Chair
clips.summary: Working as Walker's administrative assistant, Cotton recalls King pulling a chair to her cluttered desk to ask about her life, then sending her to the Highlander Folk School, and realizing only later she had been interviewed; Highlander was then under siege as a so-called communist school, its name on a roadside billboard beside Eleanor Roosevelt and King.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Esau Jenkins and Johns Island
clips.summary: At Highlander Cotton met Septima Clark and learned the story of Esau Jenkins of Johns Island, who, watching a white man kill a Black boy over a dog while the powerless community could not protest, began teaching riders on his bus why registering to vote mattered; Clark, fired from the Charleston schools for the NAACP, called Jenkins a man with a third-grade education and a PhD mind, the germ of the citizenship education program.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Inheriting the Citizenship Program
clips.summary: Cotton describes how cursive writing was demanded at registration as a trick to exclude Black voters, how the state confiscated Highlander's property, and how the Marshall Field Foundation, wanting the literacy and citizenship training to continue, moved the funds to SCLC, which named Cotton director of its Citizenship Education Program.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Dorchester Center
clips.summary: With a church facility secured by Andrew Young at the Dorchester Center in McIntosh, Georgia, Cotton, Young, and Septima Clark traveled the South recruiting people from hotbeds of activity into five-day residential workshops; she recalls a self-described junk man, Albert Minnis, teaching her to lead the invitation by promising up front to pay their way.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Septima, Andrew, and Me
clips.summary: Cotton describes the three of them traveling like mother, son, and daughter, Septima Clark teaching the cursive writing needed to register, Andrew Young pressing why a single vote matters, and herself laying out the week's sessions, with itinerant preachers among the students who had to learn that dialogue, not preaching, was how people learn.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Start a Conversation
clips.summary: Cotton explains her method of starting a conversation rather than lecturing from the front, and tells of a woman who came back day after day, energized and indignant that the city clerk would test the white families' well water but not hers.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Why Aren't You the City Clerk
clips.summary: Cotton recalls answering the indignant woman by asking why she did not become the city clerk herself, the moment the room transformed; King would come to close out the workshops with his metaphor that nobody can ride a back that is not bent, the line that became the subtitle of her book, from victim to victory.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: It May Be the Last Time
clips.summary: Cotton estimates that between five and eight thousand people came through the monthly workshops and carried the training home, among them Fannie Lou Hamer of Ruleville, who became a regular recruiter; she remembers standing with Andrew Young as a busload from the Delta pulled away and singing that this may be the last time, the two of them crying, because some who left would be killed.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Only Woman at the Table
clips.summary: Cotton reflects that she rarely thought about gender at first, going along with the pattern of the time as the only woman at the executive table, asked to pour the coffee or take the notes until staffer Jack O'Dell insisted she keep her seat; she knew her job was important and that she was smarter than some of the men, and let them be who they thought they needed to be.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Team of Wild Horses
clips.summary: She describes King as a focused listener presiding over what he fondly called his team of wild horses, the fighting energy Hosea Williams brought to budget arguments, the others like C.T. Vivian and James Bevel debating where to go next, and the day word of James Meredith's march came and King, who waited until he needed to speak, simply said they were going to Mississippi.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: He Was My Best Friend
clips.summary: Cotton insists a movement is nothing like a formal office, and speaks of being almost always with King, as Andrew Young and Bernard Lee were, running barefoot across a Birmingham street with food for people jailed in a fenced yard; she felt he was her best friend, a closeness she illustrates with an easy evening over gin and tonics.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Greens and Pork Chops in Paris
clips.summary: Cotton remembers the 1964 Nobel Prize journey, teasing King that he had come all the way to Paris and wanted collard greens and pork chops from an expatriate's soul-food restaurant, the staff traveling like a family; on the flights to Oslo, King and Coretta rode separate planes for their children's sake, and King's mother, Alberta, rose in the aisle to sing as the sun came up.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The First Time He Cried
clips.summary: Cotton recalls how King loved to sing and to slip away to Jamaica or Bermuda to write, and how the first time she ever saw him cry, tears running down his cheeks, was when J. Edgar Hoover publicly called him a notorious liar, a wound she then had to help him bear.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Giggly Like a Child
clips.summary: Cotton describes the Atlanta staff meeting where the team debated whether King should speak out against the Vietnam War, and how he left it to come up to her apartment, giggly like a child, and sit in her gold rocking chair simply telling her what was going on.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Maybe I Should Take a Sabbatical
clips.summary: She remembers King in her chair another time, weary and pondering whether to take a sabbatical or a university post offered in England, worn by the mounting criticism, including from the young like Stokely Carmichael, who argued at his kitchen table, quoting Frantz Fanon, that nonviolence had outlived its use.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Last Lone Voice
clips.summary: Cotton recounts King's resolve that if he were the last lone voice for nonviolence he would still speak, holding that the end sought is pre-existent in the means used; when a board member, Marion Logan, signed a letter urging him not to denounce the war, the criticism only deepened his commitment to say what he truly felt.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Threats Didn't Control Us
clips.summary: Cotton says the threats came fast and furiously but were never overt enough to make them stop or change plans; it was those around King, not King himself, who grew anxious when a stranger rushed toward him at an airport, and Andrew Young who, sensing the hatred abroad, surrounded him at marches with men who looked like him to take a bullet meant for the leader.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Coming Out of Mourning
clips.summary: Cotton reflects on the hard transition after the assassination, a young staffer observing three years on that they were only then coming out of mourning, the loss of their inspiration and best friend leaving a void no one could fill; Ralph Abernathy, the vice president, walked into the presidency while she could barely relate to the change.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: In Over His Head
clips.summary: Cotton offers a candid, self-described reluctant assessment that it was unfair to ask Abernathy to lead King's team of wild horses, a role she felt was over his head, even as she honors him as a caring pastor, recalling him refusing to pass a police blockade unless the choir members behind him were let through too.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: I Don't Have Anybody to Help Me
clips.summary: Cotton recalls overhearing Abernathy through the office wall say he had no one to help him, and her tears at the truth of it, and how Andrew Young, sensing she needed to move on, arranged for her to become a Head Start director in Jefferson County, Alabama, three years after King's death, in an SCLC that was no longer the one she had known.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Racism Is a Sickness
clips.summary: Asked whether King's violent death changed her, Cotton says she still believes in nonviolence as the way, that intensifying the violence would only have brought many more deaths without breaking what she calls American-style apartheid; the deep racism they faced, she holds, is a sickness, and when people are sick you work to heal them, just as she now wishes she could have approached her own father.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Tell Me Who You're Going to Shoot
clips.summary: Cotton recalls challenging Stokely Carmichael, who kept urging people to take up the gun, by asking whom exactly he meant to shoot and whether it would move him toward his goal, and his unanswered silence; she argues that without a clear goal one can justify anything, that nonviolence brings her closer to the world she wants, and even reflects, on the killing of Osama bin Laden, that her belief in nonviolence does not require her to condemn that order.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: An Answer to a Prayer
clips.summary: Cotton describes how two friends, including Anke Wessels of Cornell's Center for Transformative Action, came to her kitchen table with an idea conceived in the shower to build a Dorothy Cotton Institute, an answer to her private worry over what would become of her papers and awards, and to the absence of a civil rights center in the Northeast to match those in Birmingham, Selma, Montgomery, and Memphis.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Certain Spirit
clips.summary: Cotton insists the institute carry the spiritual quality she felt in the movement, in figures from King to Esau Jenkins to Septima Clark, not merely a head trip; above all she wants people to know the movement was more than marching, which is why she highlights the citizenship education program in her nearly finished book, calling its graduates the people who in a real sense made the movement.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Three or Four People
clips.summary: Cotton voices her trouble, some of it she is reluctant to record, that three or four people are lifted up as having made the movement when so many did the daily work, that John Lewis is remembered on the Edmund Pettus Bridge while Amelia Boynton, knocked down by a trooper's horse, is forgotten, and that figures like Jesse Jackson, an occasional worker flown in from Chicago, are wrongly cast as King's intimates by a media that wants a controversial face.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Real Story
clips.summary: Cotton closes on her gratitude for the interview and for the chance to set the real story straight, describing the book manuscript spread across her dining table with an editor's help, her hope not for a media blitz but to keep the history from going down as though three or four people made what so many built.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Born to Immigrants
clips.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby in Baltimore in December 2015, Dottie Zellner traces her birth in New York in 1938 to immigrant Jewish parents, a father from Lithuania by way of England and Toronto who became a dentist because Jews could not teach in Canada, and a mother from Kiev, both secular leftists running a mom-and-pop practice out of their apartment.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The War Was Another Member of the Family
clips.summary: Zellner recalls World War II as an ever-present specter in the household, the air-raid drills and blackout curtains, her eccentric father rushing between patients to catch the war news on the radio, and a newsreel of a maimed child that so frightened her she crawled under the theater seat.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: You Never Throw Away a Book
clips.summary: She remembers finding a book of concentration-camp photographs in her parents' library and, protective of her younger siblings, throwing it in the garbage, only to be scolded not for her terror but for discarding a book; underneath it all simmered a real, unspoken question of whether the Jews would survive.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: How Did They Know Who We Were
clips.summary: Zellner describes her parents' left politics, born of an anti-Semitic Toronto they had fled, and how their fear for Jews extended outward to other marginalized people; she recalls asking her father at eight how the Nazis knew who the Jews were, and realizing that they would always have known who a Black person was.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Few Black Friends
clips.summary: She recounts a childhood faux pas asking aloud on a bus why two Black strangers were not speaking, her parents' few Black friends and patients, and her father's lifelong devotion to the veterans of the Spanish Civil War, whom he treated free, a practice he later extended to civil rights workers.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: His Jaw Was Wired Shut
clips.summary: Zellner tells how her father unwired SNCC worker Bill Hansen's broken jaw, only for Hansen, a Catholic, to find it was a Friday and he could not have his longed-for steak, and recalls the left-wing friends who filled the house, including the one who armed her with a New York Times article to correct a teacher who denied the United States was aiding France in Vietnam in 1954.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Arrested Under the Smith Act
clips.summary: She describes the wartime camaraderie dissipating by 1949, the Communist Party family in their building whose father was arrested under the Smith Act in 1951, the FBI tramping up the stairs at dawn, and her own sense of growing up marginalized, in a group of very unpopular people, under extreme McCarthyism.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Rosenbergs
clips.summary: Zellner recalls her high school nearly shutting down over a class wish to invite the atomic scientist J. Robert Oppenheimer to speak, and the signal event of her teenage life, the execution of Julius and Ethel Rosenberg, when she stood at fifteen picketing all night before the White House beside a young Mary Travers, soon of Peter, Paul and Mary.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Bring As Many As You Can
clips.summary: A teenage expert on the fate of the Jews, Zellner describes the indelible impression left by the Warsaw ghetto uprising, where desperate people held off the German army for six weeks, and the obligation she has carried since: that when you are up against the fascists with no other recourse, you must bring as many of them down as you can.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: This Was Our Moment
clips.summary: Zellner recalls joining the NAACP in high school, glimpsing her hero Paul Robeson crossing Second Avenue, and the thrill of Brown v. Board and the bus boycott; in simple psychological terms, she says, the civil rights movement replaced the Jewish revolt she had experienced vicariously, becoming her own generation's moment.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: At the Counter
clips.summary: She remembers exactly where she sat, at a coffee-shop counter near the 42nd Street library, when she read the small newspaper item about the Greensboro sit-ins in February 1960, electrified not that the students could not be served but that they did not get up.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Your Time Will Come
clips.summary: A college graduate living at home with a short-term job at the left-wing National Guardian, unsettled while friends married, Zellner recalls being told at a wedding that her time would come, and finding, through a friend, a CORE workshop she could enroll in rather than simply appearing in the South unannounced as others did.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Miami Workshop
clips.summary: Zellner describes the June 1960 CORE workshop in Miami, four months after the sit-ins began, among people who became known, Rudy Lombard, Hugh Murray, the Stephens sisters, and Angeline Butler, and her sense looking back that the heavily Northern CORE was seeking a new generation of organizers and a wide geographical spread.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Only White Woman
clips.summary: Zellner recounts the sit-in and arrest, realizing as they sang that she was the only white woman in the group, and the segregated jail where, petrified but not menaced, she lay on an unmade upper bunk and was handed a thirty-pound mop; she uses the day in her talks to show young people how, from waking to sleeping, an entire life was formulated on the basis of color.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: You Slept on That
clips.summary: She describes the white women in the cell making ruthless fun of her, being called out to meet the lawyer and bonded out while some, like the Stephens sisters, chose to stay in, and her later realization, as an organizer, that no one had thought through what a lone white woman in jail might mean or discussed beforehand whether to remain.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: How Easy It Was
clips.summary: Zellner recalls the role-playing of the nonviolence training, made to play the screaming attacker and unnerved by how easily one could become a fascist, and admits she had no consciousness of herself as a woman, following an unorthodox path that nonetheless assumed she would marry and have children.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Casing Woolworth's
clips.summary: Begging to go on to New Orleans, Zellner stayed with a Black woman near Claiborne Avenue and helped prepare the city's first sit-in, which she notes came from the CORE people rather than the local students; with her art-school training she made the signs, cased the Woolworth's on Canal Street, and was sent to do white-community outreach, finding a willing ally in Father Joseph Fichter of Loyola.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Rabbi Threw Me Out
clips.summary: Zellner tells of the rabbi, Julian Feibelman, who threw her out of his office, a story she has used as a Jewish activist for fifty-five years, and of coming to understand the unenviable position of Southern Jews she met traveling the region, like the storekeeper in a Hayneville-style town, never accepted, always trying desperately and vainly to be white.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Go to SNCC
clips.summary: Zellner recalls Bernard Lafayette advising her to go to SNCC, the spectacular New Orleans sit-ins where her assignment was to drive, and the outsized role of cars in a movement that had almost none; she learned that a woman never drove if a man was present, a theme full of legendary drivers and life-or-death escapes that runs through the book Hands on the Freedom Plow.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Stokely and the Gravel
clips.summary: She paints Stokely Carmichael as inseparable from a car, roaring into the second district of Mississippi with the gravel flying before smoothing down his clothes, and confesses she herself was a terrible driver who, sent to drive after the New Orleans sit-in, nearly killed everyone in a traffic circle while they screamed; the same day Hugh Murray's father stormed in and dragged his son from the counter.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Letters in the Garbage
clips.summary: Back home, set on SNCC, Zellner wrote letters that went unanswered into the garbage for want of anyone to open them, worked a dreadful proofreading job and then the welfare department, and recounts how a fired Black professor she had defended as a student editor, James A. Moss, turned up as research director at the Southern Regional Council just as she applied.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: My Name Was Not on It
clips.summary: Hired in three weeks at the Southern Regional Council, Zellner did the research for its report on the first eighteen months of the sit-in movement, which landed on the front page of the New York Times under no name of hers, as was the style of the day; living on the white side of Atlanta, she led a double life, crossing to the Black side where she could.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The White Side and the Black Side
clips.summary: Zellner reflects on the guilty double life of living white while her Black colleagues had to come to her house after dark, on rooming with Jane Bond when a woman renting alone was rare, and on the agony of knowing SNCC was twenty minutes away on foot, full of the people in her own reports, yet unreachable to her for months.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Can You Type
clips.summary: Zellner recounts finally walking into SNCC in October 1961, a month after James Forman arrived, and the momentous question he asked, whether she could type, which feminists later deconstructed but which thrilled her; knowing a white Northern woman's options in the South were narrow, she happily typed, then told him she could write too, and he put her to work with Julian Bond.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Switchboard in His Head
clips.summary: Zellner describes Forman's genius as a switchboard in his head that held the whole organization, instantly knowing where each person was needed, and recalls typing the field secretaries' affidavits and, in time, writing his letters from a one-line instruction; she also explains the deadly combination, in the white mind, of a white woman and a Black man.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: White Women Were Anathema
clips.summary: She lays out the century of lynchings charged to Black men accused of raping white women, the famous cases like Willie McGee and Mack Charles Parker that were in fact relationships ending in betrayal and death, and how white women were anathema in the rural South, so that Charles Sherrod in Southwest Georgia treated them as charges about to be detonated.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Misconstrued by History
clips.summary: Zellner argues that historians who never knew Forman have misconstrued him, a highly intelligent, cultured French speaker who read Africa before anyone, short on small talk but the inventor of the friends-of support groups and the hub-and-wheel model that served the rural work, with Ella Baker keeping SNCC autonomous.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Laser Beam
clips.summary: She describes typing the field affidavits, fast and inaccurate, that Forman sent off to Robert Kennedy on the theory that the 1957 Civil Rights Act already gave grounds to jail the registrars, and the rare human contact Forman made, like a laser beam, his fine teeth, his sense of humor, and his famous wind-it-up gesture at people who talked too long.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Hands on the Hood
clips.summary: Zellner recalls two narrow escapes with Forman: a night near Greenwood in 1963 when interracial carloads were stopped and made to put their hands on the hood, and Forman handled the police with total calm; and Danville, where as they were about to be hosed and beaten he saw disaster coming and moved to prevent it.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Seeing Stars
clips.summary: She describes the Danville pamphlet Forman sent her to write, the fire hoses that had already knocked her down, and the night a policeman struck the hundred-six-pound, shoeless woman over the head as she knelt, the blow making her see actual flashing lights, captured in Danny Lyon's photograph of Forman taking her affidavit.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Get Up and Get the Hell Out
clips.summary: Zellner explains the SNCC method of always going back to where the danger was, and the day forty people sat on the city-hall steps facing deputized drunks and fire hoses while Forman, sensing they would go stir crazy, ran a Black-history workshop about Harriet Tubman, then distracted the police chief and, behind his back, signaled them all to rise with dignity and leave.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: He Had the Stature to Retreat
clips.summary: Zellner credits Forman twice with saving her life, having the stature to know when to retreat rather than let people be killed, and the nerve to buy twenty cars at once; she bitterly rejects the portrayals of him as petulant or unstrung, including in the film Selma, insisting he was brusque but truthful and never spared himself.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Then He Crashed
clips.summary: Zellner reveals that through all the years she knew him Forman showed no sign of the psychiatric history he carried, until accumulated stress brought a series of breakdowns; his wife, Dinky, got him to Puerto Rico where Betita Martinez worked on his book, but a final refusal of treatment left him a changed man, even a poisoning fear she once met by offering to taste his food first.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: He Gave Himself to Us
clips.summary: She honors Forman as the man at the center of the hub who invented the campus travelers and the friends-of groups to serve the rural work, ruthless for publicity for the cause but never himself, and mourns that he has not been treated as the great man he was; his sons James Jr., a Yale professor, and Chaka, an actor, carry on, and she recalls a late flash of his old self over a claim to be Native American.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: With God on Our Side
clips.summary: Zellner recalls Pete Seeger and Bob Dylan at the 1963 Greenwood festival in a big field, Dylan singing his With God on Our Side, seemingly incomprehensible to an audience whose religious faith had held them together for three hundred years, and the whole Mississippi summer as a living nightmare from the moment you passed the welcome sign.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The FBI Came Asking
clips.summary: Zellner recounts how the FBI came to Leslie Dunbar, head of the Southern Regional Council, with a photo of her, and how Dunbar fired her months later as redundant, a story she still weighs both ways; already in talks with Forman, who had no money to pay her, she now had her chance and joined the SNCC staff she had longed for.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: This Is the Man Who Fired Me
clips.summary: Years later Zellner took immoderate pleasure in introducing her daughter to Dunbar as the man who fired her when the FBI came, and notes he later fired another good organizer who also went to SNCC; she suspects the photo was from a Cuban Missile Crisis demonstration organized by Howard Zinn, who told her the FBI had every one of the twenty-three names.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Two Opposite People
clips.summary: Zellner describes meshing at once with Julian Bond despite being opposites, she rigidly punctual and hard-driven, he relaxed and a lover of gossip and funny stories; he became the confidant she trusted with personal matters he never repeated, one of the funniest people who ever lived, and she keeps every email he ever sent her.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: He Took Up for Me
clips.summary: She recalls wishing in 1963 that the two of them had written a book, the meaningful day the conflict-averse Julian defended her in a staff meeting against criticism for her punctuality, and the disbelief of learning, from an early-morning message Dorothy Burlage sent, that he had died.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Always Tell the Truth
clips.summary: Zellner describes laying out dummies of the Student Voice and the principle she and Julian taught volunteers and have followed since: always tell reporters the truth, never inflate a crowd of forty-two into five hundred, because the reporter will not return; some press people they genuinely liked and trusted.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: We Said Negro Deliberately
clips.summary: She recalls the live phone interviews where the witty Julian gave the quotable line and she gave the earnest facts, and the deliberate practice, strange-looking now, of identifying Black people by race in press releases so the public would know that Black people were leading and being arrested at the front of the movement.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Staff Member, Not Friends Of
clips.summary: Married to Bob Zellner in August 1963 after stints in Danville and Greenwood, Zellner went to Boston and built the Northeast regional SNCC office, insisting always that she was a staff member taking direction from Atlanta, not a volunteer-run Friends of SNCC group, and interviewed prospective Freedom Summer volunteers.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: A Year's Leave
clips.summary: Zellner recounts the year's leave when Bob managed the campaign of Robert Cook, an early anti-Vietnam-War candidate, in New Haven, with one of them always at staff meetings, and how, pregnant in 1965 when custom stopped a woman working after her seventh month, she stepped down from the office that Bob then ran, their daughter born that July before they returned to Atlanta.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: I Went Down to Marry the Movement
clips.summary: Zellner recalls the December 1966 Peg Leg Bates meeting, which Bob attended and where he abstained, and Betita Martinez's account as the most coherent of many contradictory ones; the substantive discussions about SNCC becoming all Black were held without white people, and learning of them left her, who had gone down to marry the movement, completely beside herself.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: We Would Not Surrender Our Vote
clips.summary: Determined not to disappear without a trace, Zellner wrote a statement proposing that she and Bob remain full voting staff members while organizing in the white community, arguing you cannot organize white people in a vacuum; she eyed the tolerant artists' town of Fairhope, Alabama, but feared they would be killed, a fear Bob's mother shared when she begged him not to bring the danger home.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: One Vote for Us
clips.summary: Zellner reflects that the chances were good they would have been killed organizing in white Alabama, citing the interracial organizing Robin Kelley documented and traces Martha Prescod Norman Noonan found decades later; the statement endured as a record, but the proposal was rejected, with only one vote in their favor, after Bob presented it to the central committee.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: He Could Not Hold Them Back
clips.summary: She remembers confronting Forman in his deep psychic distress before his first breakdown and his refusal to deny he disagreed with the change; his comfort zone, she believes, was an interracial organization led by Black people, not one swollen with white volunteers, and though he could not stop the shift, his silence spoke, and she calls SNCC's disappearance within three years a horrible tragedy for the country.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: There Was No Plan B
clips.summary: Zellner names the deep disillusionment after Atlantic City, and a faith in lowercase democracy that left SNCC unprepared with no plan B, as central to its fracturing; when their proposal failed she and Bob went to Anne Braden's SCEF in New Orleans, declining the Bradens' road-and-home division of labor, and after ten years as professional organizers became regular people, he a union carpenter, she a practical nurse.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: What If SNCC Were Called
clips.summary: Zellner describes the unsung but vital spring 1963 civil-liberties workshop, born of the bond between Anne Braden and Ella Baker, both survivors of a still-living McCarthyism, to decide what SNCC would do if subpoenaed; she lays out the cruel choice activists faced between the Fifth Amendment, which the press equated with guilt, and the First, which sent Carl Braden and Frank Wilkinson to jail.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: They Would Not Cooperate
clips.summary: Recalling the 1954 Eastland hearings that smeared the Southern Conference Educational Fund, Zellner describes the agonized workshop debate over loyalty oaths and volunteers' politics, and the stunning decision that SNCC would not cooperate and would go to jail rather than name names; she is convinced an undercover agent carried that resolve to a government that, Julian later heard, ran thousands of Black informers.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Are You Ready to Die
clips.summary: Zellner recounts the hard-headed work of interviewing prospective Freedom Summer volunteers in black-and-white pairs against a COFO questionnaire, screening out adventurers and divas and writing candid reports, and the woman who told her decades later that being asked whether she was ready to die was one of the worst and most honest things she ever went through.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: We Let Abbie Hoffman Through
clips.summary: She admits the one she slipped up on was Abbie Hoffman, then in a suit and tie, who later went public gnashing his teeth about his treatment as a white person, as did Susan Brownmiller; Zellner refused all interviews about the white exit, unwilling to join the chorus crying over the poor white people, asking whether, after Atlantic City, anyone should be surprised.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: We're Going to Win
