Bennett tells how Gayle was repeatedly turned down for nursing programs before completing training in New Orleans, then was barred from working in Bogalusa hospitals and commuted to Covington for her entire nursing career.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Monroe Jenkins and Entry into the Movement
clips.summary: Bennett recounts that Monroe Jenkins, Gayle's husband, brought the family into the movement after a Friday-night confrontation in which he held a pocketknife to a Klansman's throat to prevent the killing of CORE workers on Fourth Street. The Deacons for Defense subsequently escorted Monroe to the paper mill because of threats on his life, and a white man attempting to kill him in the family store was driven off when Monroe drew a gun from beneath the counter.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Local Geography and the Voters League Transition
clips.summary: The witnesses situate the Jenkins, Hicks, and Young homes in Bogalusa's Black neighborhoods (Poplas Quarters, Richardson Town, the Bogue Projects, River Terrace) relative to the Freedom House on Sullivan Drive. They describe the January 1965 transition in Voters League leadership when Andrew Moses of the NAACP resigned and Gayle Jenkins approached A.Z. Young to take over the presidency, with Robert Hicks as vice-president and Gayle herself as secretary-treasurer.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: The Deacons for Defense in Bogalusa
clips.summary: Jenkins describes the Deacons for Defense gathering at the family home, naming Charles and Roy Sims, Sam Barnes, Fletcher Anderson, and Bill Crawford (Bennett's father and the witnesses' grandfather). The Deacons positioned men at the front and sides of marches with women in the middle, returned gunfire on a car that fired on Monroe Jenkins driving his children home from a Freedom House meeting, and drove white vendors out of the Black quarters.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Gayle Jenkins on the School Board
clips.summary: Breaux describes Gayle Jenkins's twenty-seven-year tenure on the Bogalusa City School Board, including an incident in which Jenkins drew a .38 from her purse and laid it on the table during a confrontation with the mother of a Genco family member running for superintendent. Breaux portrays her mother's profanity, her willingness to help white people who were being mistreated, and her decades-long withholding of the words 'I love you' from her own children.
related_events: Bogalusa school board confrontation with Genco family

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: 1965 Marches and Bogalusa-to-Baton Rouge
clips.summary: Breaux remembers a Klansman pulling a gun on A.Z. Young near the old junior high, and Hattie Mae Hill being sprayed with a fire hose that lifted her dress during a Columbia Road march. Jenkins describes the march down Columbia Road where police ran over the white attacker Applewhite as he charged with a crowbar. Bennett recounts the 1967 march to Baton Rouge starting with twenty-five or thirty people and reaching five thousand strong, with violence at Satsuma where Don Duane Exposé and other marchers used straight razors stolen from Albany barbershops to cut Klansmen jumping over cars.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Don Duane Exposé and School Integration
clips.summary: Breaux describes her late brother Don Duane Exposé, who led the first student walkout when she was a senior and Don was a junior, as more militant than their nonviolent mother. Jenkins recounts his integration of Bogalusa Junior High in fall 1967 under principal Mr. Flemings, who had announced on the radio that 'niggers' were invading the schools. Jenkins describes beating a white student who attacked him with a crutch and the principal's response that Jenkins should not let anyone know he had carried a pocketknife.
keywords: Willie E. Jenkins v. Bogalusa School Board

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Post-Movement Employment Retaliation
clips.summary: Breaux describes superintendent M.J. Israel's hostility when she applied for her first teaching job in Bogalusa, her decades of being passed over for principalships despite a master's-plus-thirty credential, and the role of board member Applewhite (whose family she names as Klan-affiliated) in blocking her rehiring after retirement. She recounts confronting Applewhite during a board meeting after he degraded the district's first Black superintendent.

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Lawyers, Visitors, and Daily Marches
clips.summary: The witnesses recall attorney Richard Sobol's frequent presence at the Jenkins home and the New Orleans CORE-affiliated law firm of Robert Collins, Nils Douglas, and Lolis Elie, who offered Breaux a Tulane scholarship she declined after three days. They describe James Farmer being smuggled out of Bogalusa in a casket from Richmond's funeral home, Dick Gregory addressing police at Sweetville Baptist Church when the lights were cut, and the twice-daily marches that proceeded with or without city permits.
related_events: Dick Gregory Sweetville Baptist Church speech

clips.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
clips.title: Final Reflections on Gayle Jenkins
clips.summary: The witnesses describe Gayle Jenkins's refusal of Governor John McKeithen's fifty-thousand-dollar buyout offer and her counter-demand that every Black woman in Bogalusa receive a job. Breaux recounts state senator Ben Nevers seeking Gayle's support twice, Gayle's friendship with the openly racist board member Mr. Creel, and Gayle's decision shortly before her December 13 death to burn all her civil rights memorabilia in her backyard. The interview closes with reflections on attorney Richard Sobol's unfinished book project about Gayle and on her stated belief that education was the central goal of the movement.
related_events: Gayle Jenkins's burning of movement papers

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: Introductions and El Monte Origins
clips.summary: David Cline of Virginia Tech and Guha Shankar of the Library of Congress open the interview in El Monte, California, with John Bishop behind the camera. Arellanes introduces herself, gives her 1946 birth year, and notes her brief attendance at East Los Angeles College.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project recording session

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: Tongva Ancestry and the Houtgna Village
clips.summary: Arellanes identifies as a Tongva elder and locates the El Monte area as the historical Houtgna village. She describes her family's earlier residence in East Los Angeles, the Maravilla projects, and her father's wrought-iron shop across the street from her grandmother's home.
related_events: Pre-contact and Spanish-mission-era Tongva habitation of the Los Angeles basin

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: Racial Covenants and the El Monte House
clips.summary: Arellanes recounts her father's purchase of the family home under GI benefits and the sellers' attempt to rescind the sale after meeting her dark-skinned Tongva mother. Her father, a self-employed businessman, threatened to sue and kept the house. Decades later, through interviews with the South El Monte Arts Posi group, she learned that El Monte covenants had barred home sales to Mexicans and Asians.
related_events: Post-WWII racial-covenant enforcement in Southern California suburbs

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: High School Racism and Race Riots
clips.summary: Arellanes describes growing up in a predominantly Anglo El Monte and experiencing racism through elementary and high school. She recalls race riots in which only Chicano students were arrested, being mocked for eating bean burritos, and walking home in groups for safety as objects were thrown at them.

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: Identity Confusion: Chicano, American, Tongva
clips.summary: Arellanes describes her father's insistence that she was Chicano, her own childhood claim of being American, and her mother's contradictory pressure to call herself Mexican rather than Indian. A visit to her mother's cousin revealed family genealogy, including an uncle who wrote books in the 1920s and worked as a vaquero in Bakersfield.

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: Mission System and Tongva Survival
clips.summary: Arellanes describes the Spanish-mission system's separation of Tongva families, the forced labor and forced dress imposed on women and children, and the mass burials at the San Gabriel Mission. She names John Peabody Harrington's recordings as a source for contemporary Tongva language recovery and notes the community's continued growth despite historical claims of extinction.

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: Father's Conservatism and Family Life
clips.summary: Arellanes describes her father as a conservative businessman who belonged to the Masons and the Arts Club, refused to let his children speak Spanish to avoid accents, and wanted her to marry while also pushing her toward college. She contrasts him with her affectionate mother and describes his family's background as traveling musicians from Chihuahua.

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: First Activism: Youth Council and Counselor Jack Barton
clips.summary: Arellanes traces her activist beginnings to a neighbor mother who hosted a youth council where Chicano students gathered, played music, and did community activities. At El Monte High School, Counselor Jack Barton convened meetings between Chicano students and Anglo surfer students to address fights and race riots, with junior-high outreach to prepare incoming students.
related_events: 1960s El Monte high-school surfer-vs-Chicano subculture conflict

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: Size, Bullying, and Becoming a Fighter
clips.summary: Arellanes reflects on being five-foot-eight and bullied through elementary and high school, and on reaching a point in her twenties when she stopped tolerating ridicule. She credits this experience with preparing her for the Chicano movement and describes using her size as intimidation while rarely fighting physically.

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: Early Jobs and the Anti-Poverty Programs
clips.summary: Arellanes describes her first job through the White Tap Program / Youth Temporary Employment Project in South El Monte, a factory job assembling Christmas-tree ornaments and artificial flower arrangements, and her move to East Los Angeles to work in the anti-poverty Neighborhood Adult Participation Project (NAPP). She also describes cruising Whittier Boulevard socially.

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: La Piranya Coffee House and Joining the Brown Berets
clips.summary: Arellanes is invited to La Piranya Coffee House on Olympic Boulevard, where she finds the lights off because sheriffs were sweeping the windows with floodlights. She describes the coffee house as a Brown Berets antecedent run by a young community-action group, returning multiple times before joining. She names La Piranya as the birthplace of the Chicano movement.
related_events: Birth of the East Los Angeles Chicano movement

clips.subject: Gloria Arellanes
clips.title: Brown Berets Discipline and Coalition Movement
clips.summary: Arellanes describes Brown Berets training in not arguing as a discipline of nonviolent communication, a UCLA flyering encounter with a hostile Anglo family, and the broader landscape of Chicano-movement organizations: UMAS in the colleges, Lucha among ex-convicts, and La Junta in the housing projects. She also describes regular visits to US Organization, ties to the Black Panthers through Brown Beret men, and the Watts-rebellion-era non-aggression coalition pact. The chapter closes with the Brown Berets opening the East Los Angeles free clinic with David Sanchez, Physicians for Community Action, and Psychologists for Community Action.
related_events: Founding of the East Los Angeles free clinic
Brown Berets coalition with US Organization and Black Panthers

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Grandparents and Family Migration
clips.summary: Grinnell traces her grandparents' move from Virginia to Philadelphia and the family split that followed when Philadelphia would not hire Black teachers. Her grandmother took in laundry while her grandfather refused that work; the older children went to college and became teachers and helped pay for the youngest's education. A great-great-grandparent was among the founders of Virginia Union University.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: San Francisco Childhood
clips.summary: After her parents divorced, Grinnell's mother took her to San Francisco, settling first in the Western Addition and then the Presidio. At St. Dominic's on Pine and Steiner she was the only Black child; a nun slapped her during morning prayers and marked her papers with failing grades until her mother confronted the school. At a public school called Presidio a white boy told her his mother forbade him to hold the hand of a 'nigger'-her first encounter with the slur.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Bay Area Black Adult Memories
clips.summary: From the family mom-and-pop grocery store Grinnell overheard her parents discussing a Black teacher reduced to substitute work because of a dishonorable World War II discharge after he jumped ship rather than fight for a country that would not free him. She remembers her mother caught in the union-job catch-22, told to join the union first to get a teaching job, told to get the job first by the union. She also recalls the unmarked rope across Santa Monica's beach and an awkward Filipino picnic where a gatekeeper questioned her presence.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Virginia Union and Southern Racial Awakening
clips.summary: Grinnell arrived at Virginia Union University in the late 1950s. The first thing she did in Richmond was sit at a soda fountain and order a banana split; the lady went to make it then returned to say she could not be served. She describes her initial anger at Black students who would not sit in, before later understanding that their parents would lose jobs. Her favorite instructor was Dr. Bob Johnson, who held two PhDs.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Thalhimer's Sit-in and Arrest
clips.summary: Grinnell joined Charles Sherrod's student organizing meetings at Virginia Union. The students walked downtown to Thalhimer's department store. Standing rather than sitting because of her height plus heels, she was visible to police; the sit-in group of 34 was arrested and loaded into a Paddy wagon. The jail cell was smelly, the courtroom was segregated, and she watched a drunken Black man brought in and realized the courts themselves operated by color line. Her aunt called to say she had seen her on the news being helped into the Paddy wagon.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Adam Clayton Powell and Nonviolence
clips.summary: Grinnell describes attending an Adam Clayton Powell civil-rights meeting in a Richmond-area church and being startled by the congregational clapping and foot-stomping, she had never been in a large gathering of Black people. Adam Clayton Powell had worked with her uncle. She committed to nonviolence, reasoning that protected response was not really fair but was the position she would hold. She acknowledges she might have responded differently if she had been a man.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Return West and the Arrest Record
clips.summary: Grinnell graduated from Virginia Union in 1962 and moved through DC and San Francisco before settling in Los Angeles. A DC hiring officer told her he had to hire her because of her test score but that her arrest record from the sit-in meant she would not be happy there. She passed the Los Angeles Unified School District written exam ten times before being admitted; LAUSD failed her on training and experience because of the sit-in arrest record. She eventually got in when someone mistakenly sent her a contract.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Obama Era and Henry Kissinger
clips.summary: Grinnell describes the surfacing of negative racial sentiment during the Obama presidency, the shouts during congressional sessions, the Hitler comparisons. Her brother, who worked as part of the staff supporting Henry Kissinger in Switzerland, came back insisting that the racial framing in U.S. politics was beside the point compared to actual world problems. She names herself elated at Obama's election not because he was Black but because he was intelligent and would work for everyone.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: LAUSD Expulsion Counseling and Busing
clips.summary: As an expulsion counselor Grinnell sat on three-person committees reviewing students brought up on weapons or drug violations. She describes one case of a white student with affluent parents who was guilty of drug involvement but not expelled because, the other committee members argued, he would go to college. She wrote a three-page letter protesting the disparate outcomes. She also describes the LAUSD busing program that moved Black children out of high-achieving Black-neighborhood schools like the one near her View Park home and notes parents' resistance.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Reflections from Ohio
clips.summary: Grinnell, now living in a small Ohio town for nine years, observes that change has come but in coded form: covert rather than overt racism, a Black child's locker labeled with a slur in a nearby Ohio high school not long before she moved there. She kept her Los Angeles house in case a cross was burned on the Ohio lawn. She describes the town, boutiques, a theater favored by local directors, walking trails, helicopters that are private rather than the LA 'ghetto birds' she remembers from working the projects.

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Family Legacy and Norfolk Greyhound Integration
clips.summary: Grinnell's uncle, J. Hugo Madison, the Virginia NAACP attorney who handled Norfolk-region legal work, called her 'screwball' and connected her to attorney Martin A. Martin for the sit-in defense. Her aunt, traveling from Virginia to DC by Greyhound bus with Grinnell's mother and another sister, deliberately sat in the white section and was arrested to test the post-Morgan v. Virginia integration ruling that Virginia was not enforcing. She coordinated the arrest with Hugo Madison and Adam Clayton Powell. The interview is conducted during the Supreme Court's Shelby County voting-rights challenge.
related_events: Shelby County v. Holder oral arguments February 2013

clips.subject: Gloria Claudette Grinnell
clips.title: Green Book Era and Integration's Costs
clips.summary: Grinnell remembers traveling with her parents who would stop at Black homes asking where Black travelers could stay, learning of Mrs. Jones's spare room. She recalls The Negro Motorist Green Book, the AAA-equivalent guide that listed where Black motorists could stay. She names Peg Leg Bates's upstate New York resort as an example of Black-owned hospitality businesses that closed when integration arrived. She wrote letters to the Los Angeles Times and local papers protesting the lowercasing of 'black' in print, arguing that 'white' is generic but 'black' designates a specific American population.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Howard University Years
clips.summary: Richardson recalls enrolling at Howard University around 1938 at age 16, studying under E. Franklin Frazier (whose class she audited and who critiqued the Black Bourgeoisie) and Rayford W. Logan. Her first protest was on campus against women's curfews, followed by picketing Woolworth's, likely in her junior year.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Family and Cambridge Geography
clips.summary: Richardson situates her family in Cambridge, Maryland: her grandfather had served on the City Council as the third Black member, her father ran a drug store, and her cousin Frederick St. Clair was a physician without hospital privileges. She describes the strict segregation of the town along Race Street, including separate hospital maternity rooms.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Drug Store and Healthcare Segregation
clips.summary: Richardson describes her father's drug store and her work running it after his heart attack and death. She recounts segregated hospital access, the family's reliance on Provident Hospital in Baltimore, and the distrust of Johns Hopkins later confirmed for her by the Henrietta Lacks story.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: SNCC Arrives in Cambridge
clips.summary: Richardson recounts the arrival of SNCC field workers Bill Hansen and Reginald Robinson in late 1961 through her cousin Frederick St. Clair's bail work with the Freedom Riders. Her daughter Donna Richardson became an organizer of high school students. After early demonstrations effectively shut down the town, parents asked Richardson to take a leadership role.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: The Survey and Movement Priorities
clips.summary: Richardson describes traveling to Atlanta to meet SNCC and the survey CNAC structured with Junior CNAC (grammar school students), using methods she had learned from E. Franklin Frazier's graduate course. Swarthmore volunteers analyzed the results, which established housing, jobs, and health as higher priorities than public accommodations. She credits the data with shifting Robert Kennedy's understanding.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Housing Conditions and Federal Pressure
clips.summary: Richardson details Cambridge housing conditions, including the use of actual chicken coops as dwellings, and 60 percent Black unemployment. She recalls the Cambridge fuel-business owner who defied the city council to deliver fuel to Black customers and lost his business in urban renewal as retaliation.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: NAACP Conflicts and Federal Offers
clips.summary: Richardson describes her break with NAACP class-conscious legal practice and refusal of federal job offers brought to her by Hobart Taylor Jr (a Johnson administration figure). Carnegie Hall events featuring Frank Sinatra and Lena Horne were used to pressure her toward supporting the referendum.
related_events: Federal job offers to deflect leadership

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Cambridge Community and Gender Roles
clips.summary: Richardson describes the 10-14 core CNAC neighborhood leaders, modeled on her grandfather's network of community contacts. She rebuts the characterization of Cambridge as "a women's movement," describing instead a division of labor where men returned to wage work and night defense while women conducted daytime negotiations. She recounts women on the street resisting state police incursions.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Armed Defense and General Gelston
clips.summary: Richardson recounts the night National Guard members shot up Pine Street and were fired upon by Black men in the community, with a soldier's machine gun brought home from war involved. She describes her relationship with General George M. Gelston ("fair" and trusted) and his number-two officer who shuttled communications because both knew their phones were tapped by Fort Belvoir.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Referendum Refusal and Kennedy Negotiations
clips.summary: Richardson states her position that civil rights should not be put to a popular vote and describes the meeting at the White House with Robert Kennedy, where the route from Cambridge to Washington was lined with police cars. She recounts being reported killed by mistaken backfire/gunshot on the return trip. The Kennedys, NAACP, Baltimore ministers, and outside groups all pushed her toward the referendum she refused.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Jail, the Wallace Visit, and CS Gas
clips.summary: Richardson describes her arrest and the spring 1963 jailing of Donna Richardson and her mother. The town poured out at midnight when adults learned police had beaten the jailed teenagers; surrounding towns refused to take Cambridge prisoners. Judge Harry Hanky fined her one cent. She recounts George Wallace's 1964 visit to the segregated Cambridge fire hall, the deployment of CS gas (the first reported civilian use), and the death of an infant and elderly man whose autopsies were performed in Baltimore.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: March on Washington
clips.summary: Richardson recounts the August 1963 March on Washington: being called to be told not to wear jeans, searching the Eastern Shore for a denim skirt, arriving to find the program had become a picnic from her perspective. Her chair on the platform was removed; she was reseated near W. Robert Ming Jr from Chicago. She and Lena Horne were dispatched to introduce Rosa Parks to international media, then put in a taxi back to the hotel before Martin Luther King Jr's speech.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Adam Clayton Powell Jr and the Food Crisis
clips.summary: Richardson identifies Adam Clayton Powell Jr, Robert Kennedy, and Malcolm X as the three figures she most respects. She describes Powell's intervention to deliver surplus federal food to Cambridge over the objections of the city council and governor, the National Guard's role distributing food, and the cross-racial organizing this prompted when poor white residents were also denied food.
related_events: International Ladies Garment Workers Union organizing

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Malcolm X and Detroit
clips.summary: Richardson describes her first meeting with Malcolm X at Reverend Albert Cleage's church in Detroit during the SCLC/Reverend C.L. Franklin event, where she sensed Malcolm would leave the Nation of Islam. She recounts inviting him to Cambridge for "The Ballot or the Bullet" speech, her presence at the Audubon Ballroom the night before his assassination, and her mother's offer to house his family after the bombing. A Nation of Islam emissary warned her months earlier to stay off the stage with Malcolm X.
related_events: Ballot or the Bullet speech Cleveland 1964

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Independence and SNCC Coordination
clips.summary: Richardson reflects on the source of her independence as a movement leader: family training to judge people by character not title, and the determination of Cambridge residents whose support backed her. She describes SNCC's policy of letting local people decide direction, James Forman's mild objection to her Malcolm X alliance, and an exchange with Burke Marshall where SNCC representatives waited to see what Roy Wilkins would say.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Resolution and Move to New York
clips.summary: Richardson assesses what CNAC accomplished by the signed agreement and reflects on the unexplained gap before SNCC went to Atlantic City. She describes the eventual desegregation of schools and the election of a Black woman as mayor of Cambridge. Her move to New York City in 1965 followed her marriage to Frank Dandridge; CNAC was a volunteer position, not a salaried career.

clips.subject: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
clips.title: Cambridge Recognition and Final Thoughts
clips.summary: Richardson recalls a Cambridge community event held in her honor, which she experienced as overwhelming. She mentions an honor at Washington College in Chestertown, Maryland, where the president had learned about Cambridge through Malcolm X's grassroots speech. She closes with concerns about contemporary politics, the Supreme Court, and the absence of an opposing entity to right-wing organizing.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Family and Upbringing in Baker County
clips.summary: Miller introduces herself as Grace Hall Miller, born July 21, 1932, the youngest of six children of farmer Joe Nathan Hall and homemaker Nanny Hall. She describes her parents as church workers, her father as deacon of the church, and the moral instruction she received about honesty, not stealing, and treating others fairly.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Schooling and Teachers
clips.summary: Miller describes walking two miles to a one-room rural school where students supplied their own firewood while white children rode the bus past throwing spitballs. She attended Newton Colored High School in Baker County and names teachers including Miss Granberry and Miss Smith from Albany and principal Bradley. She recalls being taught Bible verses, songs, poems, the Pledge of Allegiance, and home economics.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Secret Marriage at Fourteen
clips.summary: Miller recounts meeting Hosie Miller Sr. at church Bible Training Union, secretly marrying him at age 14 in 1946 at the probate court when no blood test was required, and his brother later informing her parents. She describes her parents' shock, learning to cook and keep house as a young bride, and the eighteen-year marriage that followed.
related_events: Marriage of Grace Hall and Hosie Miller Sr., 1946

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Husband's Murder in 1965
clips.summary: Miller describes the March 1965 killing of her husband Hosie Miller Sr., shot in the back by George Williamson, a white relative, during a dispute over a cow that had been pastured at the Miller farm for three months. Hosie lingered about a week and died at the Albany hospital. Miller was pregnant with their son Hosie Jr., born that June; her husband had been certain the baby would be a boy and had painted the nursery blue.
related_events: Killing of Hosie Miller Sr., Baker County, Georgia, March 15, 1965

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Failed Prosecutions and Family Ties
clips.summary: Miller describes three failed attempts to bring her husband's killer to trial: an all-white Baker County jury that did not indict, a venue change to Albany, and a federal proceeding. The shooter was never convicted. Miller notes he was a relative of her father and a man she had grown up around; she avoided him until his death.
related_events: Three failed prosecutions of George Williamson, 1965

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: First Voter Registration in Baker County, 1958
clips.summary: Miller and her husband became the first two Black voters to register in Baker County in 1958, accompanied by her sister Josephine Miller, who registered the same day. Miller describes the registrar's hands shaking too badly to write. They organized neighbors to register; county officials then replaced the small citizenship card with a long-form sheet, prompting Justice Department intervention that reverted the form.
related_events: First Black voter registration in Baker County, 1958

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Bombings and Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson
clips.summary: Miller describes 1958 retaliations against Baker County Black registrants: the bombing of her uncle Joshua Williams's house, the bombing of Mr. Ackie King's car and the car of Mr. Cunningham (also called 'card brought away' in the transcript), and the framing of Mr. Cunningham with planted stomp whiskey, leading to his two-week jailing until a Bainbridge, Georgia lawyer bonded him out. She names Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson as the local enforcer.
related_events: 1958 retaliatory bombings against Baker County Black registrants

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Charles Sherrod, SNCC, and Home as Movement Base
clips.summary: After her husband's 1965 killing, Miller's home in Baker County became a base for the Baker County movement. Charles Sherrod of SNCC came in, later married Miller's oldest daughter Shirley, and SNCC stayed in Baker County about two years. The local FmHA representative tried to seize her house for hosting movement workers; Miller called the Justice Department in Washington, which confirmed her mortgage was current and warned the agent off.
related_events: Marriage of Charles Sherrod and Shirley Miller

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Cross-Burning and School Integration
clips.summary: Miller describes the cross-burning at her home during the school-integration period, when her daughters were among the first Black students at the integrated school. Armed with her late husband's pump gun, she fired at the men in her yard from her porch. A bullet later broke her window at night. She also describes a daughter being summoned to the white superintendent's office on a fabricated complaint about liking a white boy.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Running for the School Board
clips.summary: Miller describes paying her thirty-five-dollar filing fee, learning she lived in a different district than expected but choosing to run anyway, defeating two white candidates in a runoff, and serving on the Baker County Board of Education until 2013. She campaigned door-to-door, helping farm families shell peas and pick butter beans while talking with voters. She names Mr. Roy Kelly, an elderly white voter who praised her father Joe Nathan Hall for once returning his lost billfold.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Education Today and the New Board Conflict
clips.summary: Miller describes a recent conflict in which an all-white voting bloc on the Baker County board moved to oust the Black superintendent who had taken the district from debt, against her judgment and during the CRCT testing window. She describes a community meeting on February 28 with about 200 attendees opposing the action in a 90% Black school system.
related_events: February 28 community meeting against superintendent removal, c. 2013

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Shirley Sherrod and the Spooner Family
clips.summary: Miller recounts the 2010 USDA controversy in which her daughter Shirley Sherrod was forced to pull over on the highway and resign her position via BlackBerry over a selectively edited NAACP speech. Miller describes calling Shirley after hearing the news, and recounts the underlying Spooner family story: Shirley's earlier work to save a white farm family from foreclosure, the Spooners' call to CNN to defend her, and their continued friendship including an invitation to a ninetieth birthday party.

clips.subject: Grace Hall Miller
clips.title: Legacy and Closing Reflections
clips.summary: Miller reflects on turning eighty, having a school road and a community road named for her during her lifetime, and the importance of teaching younger generations the history of segregation. She notes that younger people often do not know about segregated water fountains and waiting rooms, and worries about generational gaps in historical knowledge.
related_events: Naming of Grace Miller Road and school road in Baker County

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Introduction and Family Genealogy
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier introduces the interview at the Bayfront Hilton in St. Augustine on September 13, 2011. Duncan describes her birth in Lincolnville in 1956 and traces her family back five generations to her great-great grandfather Thomas A. Finley, who migrated from the Carolinas through South Carolina and Georgia to Moultrie. Her great-grandmother Abby Finley was a founder of St. Cyprian's Episcopal Church in Lincolnville.
related_events: Family migration from the Carolinas to St. Augustine
Founding of St. Cyprian's Episcopal Church

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Growing Up in Segregated Lincolnville
clips.summary: Duncan describes life in segregated Lincolnville as a child of eight sisters. She recounts the Black-owned businesses on Washington Street, the centrality of the church, and her great-grandmother who died in November 1963 the same month as President Kennedy's assassination.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Klan Marches and Dr. King's Visit
clips.summary: At seven and a half years old, Duncan witnessed Ku Klux Klan marches through Lincolnville, which she initially mistook for a parade. She recalls Black residents lining the streets at Central Avenue (now Martin Luther King Avenue), classmates' homes being firebombed, and her mother holding her sister's hand telling her not to look back. She remembers Dr. King coming across the street from Mr. Proctor's house to ask her mother if she would participate in the marches.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: The Colored Sign at the Doctor's Office
clips.summary: Around ages 10-12, Duncan ventured downtown and experienced segregation firsthand at the doctor's building near Flagler Hospital. She describes the colored sign on the wall pointing Black patients around to the back entrance, and later, after the sign came down following the Civil Rights Act, taking her younger sister to the doctor through the previously forbidden front hallway and being met with hostile stares.
related_events: Passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Forced School Integration
clips.summary: Duncan completed fifth grade at Excelsior Elementary School before forced integration closed the school. She describes the white teachers' refusal to teach, classmates' refusal to sit beside her, and the violence at the junior high school where students had to walk back to Orange Street to eat because the junior high had no cafeteria, with white students forming lines to block them. She graduated from St. Augustine High School.
related_events: Forced school integration in St. Augustine

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Founding of 40th ACCORD
clips.summary: Following Mrs. Catherine Twine's death in 2002, David Nolan, JT Johnson from Atlanta, Jane Billings of Brown (Spelman professor), Mrs. Cora Tyson Johnson, Dwight Hines, and Sandra Parks met at South Seas restaurant to plan the 40th anniversary of the St. Augustine civil rights movement. Duncan proposed framing the commemoration as a family reunion to bring participants back to St. Augustine. She and Mrs. Cora Tyson Johnson set up a strategic meeting at the Galimore Center where older residents came out and shared their stories.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Bringing the Movement's Memory Back
clips.summary: Duncan recounts JT Johnson's response at a meeting when a demonstrator asked why bring this history up again: Johnson said it was time to just say thank you. Duncan reflects on St. Augustine's suppression of its Black history, the founding of Fort Mose as the first free Black settlement in the United States, and her grief that despite the sacrifices of Dr. Robert Hayling and Dr. King, much remains unchanged in the town.
related_events: Founding of Fort Mose as first free Black settlement

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Northrop Grumman Funds the Civil Rights Markers
clips.summary: Duncan describes how Northrop Grumman funded 30 historic civil rights markers in and around St. Augustine after she wrote a letter requesting funding. The previous occupant of the site, identified as Fairchild, had been hostile to civil rights demonstrators and Black employees. Markers honor Dr. Robert Hayling, the St. Augustine Foot Soldiers, Audrey Nell Hamilton, JoeAnn Anderson, and Mrs. Reena Ayers, who at 107 had given lodging and food to demonstrators and Northern attorneys.
related_events: Installation of 30 historic civil rights markers in St. Augustine

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: What Has Not Changed in St. Augustine
clips.summary: Asked what problems remain, Duncan cites the absence of jobs and opportunities for Black residents, the lack of Black businesses (fewer than one hand could count), minimal Black representation in city government, and the departure of young people. Her son with engineering degrees from the University of Florida could not find work in St. Augustine and now lives in Jacksonville. Her daughter, a doctor of physical therapy, is considering leaving.
related_events: Departure of Black young professionals from St. Augustine

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Freedom Trail Markers and Ambassador Young
clips.summary: Duncan discusses the city's reaction to the Freedom Trail markers and Foot Soldiers Monument. She credits Ambassador Andrew Young's film 'Crossing in St. Augustine' with bringing national exposure that shifted local attitudes. She suggests the city's eventual embrace of the history is partly motivated by tourism revenue, but acknowledges that at minimum the city did not block the private foot soldiers monument project.
related_events: Premiere of Ambassador Young's 'Crossing in St. Augustine' film

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: St. Augustine's Future and Industry
clips.summary: Duncan reflects on St. Augustine as a quiet town she loved raising a family in, while citing Hank Thomas's description of the city as paternalistic. She argues that while the historic city should remain old, St. Johns County needs industry so Black families can return. Thousands of Black residents have left and she wants to see the community restored.
related_events: Demographic decline of Black St. Augustine community

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan
clips.title: Teacher Workshops and the Museum Vision
clips.summary: Duncan describes 40th ACCORD's upcoming Civil Rights Act anniversary commemorations including past speaker Dr. Dorothy Cotton, Sonja Plummer (granddaughter of Reverend Goldie Eubanks), and upcoming speaker Taylor Branch in July 2012. She details two teacher workshops conducted with St. Johns County, one at St. Augustine High School featuring Dr. Robert Hayling and Lillian Twine Robeson, funded by Northrop Grumman. Long-term, the organization aims to establish a civil rights museum in St. Augustine by the 50th anniversary in 2014, with Richard Burton (NAACP) and JT Johnson as co-chairs.
related_events: Teacher workshops with St. Johns County
Planning for 50th anniversary of Civil Rights Act (2014)

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Arriving at Tuskegee, Fall 1961
clips.summary: Patton describes her arrival at Tuskegee Institute as a 17-year-old freshman, greeted by National Guard troops during the integration of Macon County schools. She recounts the campus architecture designed by Booker T. Washington with doors facing inward, and her family's grounding in Black history that prepared her to identify as a "race person" before college.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Robert Russa Moton and Tuskegee History
clips.summary: Patton recounts the history of Robert Russa Moton's struggle over the Tuskegee VA hospital staffing in the 1920s, when Moton insisted on Black professional staff against Woodrow Wilson administration policy and Klan threats. She frames this history as the institutional foundation for her later activism and the 1967 paper she wrote in John Andrew Memorial Hospital.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Freedom Riders and the Mother's Day Massacre
clips.summary: Patton describes the May 1961 Freedom Rider attack at the Greyhound bus station in Montgomery and the all-night mass meeting at First Baptist Church. She recounts her aunts Chick and Flora hosting Freedom Riders at their home, and Aunt Chick's instruction that no white person enter through the front door of her home while she still had to enter white homes through the back.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Benson Sanatorium, 1963-1964
clips.summary: Patton describes her fourteen-month tuberculosis convalescence at the segregated Benson Sanatorium in Lafayette, Alabama. She recounts staging informal protests over library access for a nine-year-old Black patient, the food menu, and the use of the chapel and sitting room. She watched the March on Washington and the Birmingham church bombing on the ward's small television.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: SNCC Arrives at Tuskegee, Fall 1964
clips.summary: Patton describes returning to Tuskegee in fall 1964 and encountering SNCC organizers, including women who became tough mentors. She articulates her opposition to integration as an end in itself, rooted in her grandmother's 1956 instruction after the Montgomery Bus Boycott that the boycott had been about sitting anywhere she pleased, not about sitting next to white people. She analyzes the class dimension of Macon County school integration plans.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: TIAL and the Scholar-Activist Principle
clips.summary: Patton describes the founding of the Tuskegee Institute Advancement League (TIAL) and her refusal to let SNCC subsume the campus organization, citing the Montgomery Improvement Association's refusal to become an SCLC chapter. She recounts confrontations with Stokely Carmichael over college dropouts joining SNCC, and Bettina Aptheker's reaction to the campus ROTC program. She explains the scholar-activist principle and the practical reasons Tuskegee students needed ROTC scholarships.
related_events: SCLC formation at Holt Street Church 1957

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: The March That Wouldn't Turn Around
clips.summary: Patton recounts the Tuskegee response to Bloody Sunday and Dr. King's turnaround at the Edmund Pettus Bridge, which she experienced as a broken promise. She describes mobilizing approximately 1,500 students via organized buses and a relay-march system that allowed students to walk to Montgomery without missing classes. She details the night at Dexter Avenue Baptist Church when deacons cut off heat, water, and lights, and her dispute with George Ware and James Forman over student agency.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: Lowndes County and Electoral Self-Determination
clips.summary: Patton describes the Lowndes County organizing strategy after the Voting Rights Act, including Tuskegee providing food for marchers. She articulates the Lowndes County Freedom Organization's decision to target sheriff, board of education, tax assessor, and coroner offices rather than state delegates or city government. She rejects the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party model and recounts the 1966 election of Lucius Amerson as Macon County sheriff against the consternation of the Black middle class.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn M. Patton
clips.title: 1967 Car Accident and New York Convalescence
clips.summary: Patton recounts the February 10, 1967 car accident on the eve of the Tuskegee Black Power Conference, when she was driving a young man to her grandmother's home en route to Canada to avoid the draft. She describes refusing Dr. Hume's amputation recommendation at John Andrew Memorial Hospital and being retrieved by her paternal grandparents. Her transition to New York convalescence brought her into contact with Bettina Aptheker, Julia Wright, Mary Kochiyama, and Communist Party, Socialist Workers Party, and Progressive Labor Party organizers.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Spelman, SNCC, and Atlanta Mentors
clips.summary: Simmons describes her first impressions of the Atlanta SNCC office as a Spelman College student: James Forman at the center of activity, Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, Japanese American colleagues, and Ella Baker observed at gatherings. She names Staughton Lynd, Howard Zinn, and Vincent Harding at the Mennonite House as the white professors who pulled her toward engagement while she risked her scholarship by violating Spelman College's prohibition on student political activity.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: The Pull Toward Mississippi
clips.summary: Simmons recounts the family and college pressures against involvement, the impact of John Lewis and the Nashville staff, and Willie 'Mukasa' Ricks's challenge that degrees would not change Black status. She describes the terror of being assigned to Laurel after the disappearance of Goodman, Chaney, and Schwerner shattered her belief that white volunteers' presence would deter killings.
related_events: Disappearance of Goodman, Chaney, and Schwerner (June 21, 1964)

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Arriving in Laurel
clips.summary: Sent to Laurel, Mississippi with James 'Jimmy' Garrett and Lester McKinney because the town was too dangerous for white volunteers, Simmons describes basing the trio in Hattiesburg and driving thirty miles north to Laurel to find local hosts. Mrs. Eberhart Spinks opened her door, recognized Simmons as a Freedom Rider, and committed on the spot; Mrs. Carrie Clayton across the street housed the two men.
related_events: Local-host recruitment in Jones County, Mississippi

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Becoming Project Director
clips.summary: When Lester McKinney is jailed in Jones County, Mississippi on a prior charge and barred from returning for five years, James Forman calls Simmons and asks her to become Laurel project director. She accepts on the understanding that a replacement is coming; no one is ever sent and she stays sixteen months. She credits her church, high school, and Girl Scout leadership roles for preparing her.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Freedom Schools and MFDP Organizing
clips.summary: Simmons describes the Laurel office on Mrs. Clayton's back porch, the eventually rented and rehabilitated nightclub fire-bombed soon after, the Freedom School covering grades 1-12, the Freedom Nursery School, adult literacy classes, and the mock voter registration project that fed the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party state convention. The chapter covers the MFDP delegation's Atlantic City challenge as a turning point that 'jaded' the season SNCC workers.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Staying Past Summer
clips.summary: Simmons explains her decision to defer Antioch College admission, secured through Staughton Lynd with a recommendation letter from Coretta Scott King, in order to stay in Laurel. Two California women, Lynelle Barrett and Marion Davidson, stayed with her at Mrs. Eberhart Spinks's home, where the three shared a bedroom for another year.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Gender, Race, and Project Leadership
clips.summary: Simmons reflects on her organizing style as one of three women project directors in Mississippi that summer: ultra-democratic consensus-building, fighting Jackson for resources and good cars, navigating class and race differences with Stanford, Yale, and Goodyear-family volunteers, and instituting an absolute prohibition against sexual harassment after her own pre-orientation sexual assault by a trusted figure.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Violence, Bombings, and the Year After
clips.summary: Simmons describes the second Laurel office (a duplex shotgun shared with a neighboring family) fire-bombed when a Molotov cocktail diverted the gas line away from the house. She names the post-summer project of six: Ulysses Everett, Charles 'Ben' Hartfield from Hattiesburg expelled from public school, and John Handy from the Mississippi Delta. She describes leading a desegregation attempt at which the Laurel sheriff drew a gun and threatened her.

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Mississippi Fairgrounds and Pivot to New York
clips.summary: Simmons describes the mass arrest of roughly a thousand demonstrators in Jackson, the fifteen-day incarceration at the Mississippi State Fairgrounds in livestock houses with beatings, and two head-on car accidents in the two weeks after her release that prompted James Forman to pull her out to work with the Friends of SNCC in New York. Three months in Harlem expose her to Nation of Islam street preaching, LeRoi Jones's Black Arts Theatre, and Black nationalist thought.
related_events: New York Friends of SNCC work

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Atlanta Project and Black Power
clips.summary: Recruited to Atlanta to run Julian Bond's re-election campaign after he was denied his Georgia House seat, Simmons describes the nucleus that became the SNCC Atlanta Project based in Vine City near the Nation of Islam Mosque #15. Michael Simmons (her future husband), Dwight Williams, and a Mississippi former second lieutenant joined. The group's Wollensak-recorded discussions, typed by Simmons and two other women, became the Black Power position paper later credited to Stokely Carmichael alone.
related_events: Julian Bond Georgia House seat denial and re-election

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Whites in SNCC and Expulsion
clips.summary: Simmons situates the question of white organizers within SNCC's expanded post-1964 ratio, including the Atlanta National Headquarters dynamic where Black projects had to justify resources to predominantly white staff. She describes her 2009-era reconciliation conversation with Bob Zellner about his clan-family background that made SNCC his home. She narrates the Atlanta Project's expulsion from SNCC over a $3,000 check the project refused to relinquish, framed atop deeper disagreement about direction. She also covers the SNCC anti-Vietnam war statement (which she helped draft) and the SNCC Palestinian/Israel statement (which she did not).

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: AFSC and COINTELPRO Investigation
clips.summary: As assistant director of the American Friends Service Committee's project on government surveillance and civil liberties, Simmons traveled the country interviewing American Indian Movement, Brown Berets, Black Panther Party, and Rodolfo 'Corky' Gonzales-affiliated organizers about COINTELPRO. Ken Lawrence in Mississippi worked the Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission. She visited the Fred Hampton assassination apartment in Chicago and interviewed survivors, learning his bodyguard had drugged them. Documentation lives at AFSC's 1501 Cherry Street, Philadelphia archive.
related_events: Freedom of Information Act file releases

clips.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
clips.title: Trauma, Sufism, and Return to Nonviolence
clips.summary: Simmons reflects on the long-term effects of movement-era trauma, the AFSC trip into Cambodia after the fall of Pol Pot to view the bone-and-skull mountain and interview survivors in Phnom Penh, the assassination of Dr. King while she was at a National Council of Negro Women meeting in Manhattan (running uptown to Harlem where she was caught in a riot), and her 1971 meeting with the Sufi mystic M.R. Bawa Muhaiyaddeen of Sri Lanka. Seventeen years of study brought her around to re-embracing Dr. King's teaching of nonviolence as the only sustainable means of social change.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Upper West Side Childhood and Canada Lee
clips.summary: Geiger's New York City childhood, skipping grades through public school, the magnet high school Townsend Harris, graduating at 14. The Rapp-Coudert Act firings of his teachers. A 1940 backstage visit to Canada Lee after seeing Native Son leads to a years-long second home in Lee's Sugar Hill penthouse at 155th and Edgecombe, where Geiger meets Paul Robeson, Langston Hughes, William Saroyan, and Adam Clayton Powell Jr.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Wisconsin, the March on Washington, CORE
clips.summary: Admission to the University of Wisconsin at age 15 in September 1941. Pearl Harbor follows months later. Geiger receives a January-February 1942 letter from Bayard Rustin recruiting campus organizers for the first threatened March on Washington that A. Philip Randolph organized to protest defense-plant discrimination; FDR's resulting executive order bars racial discrimination in defense-plant hiring. Geiger meets James Farmer in Chicago in early 1943 and helps start one of the earliest CORE chapters.
related_events: FDR Executive Order 8802 on defense-plant hiring

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Merchant Marine and SS Booker T. Washington
clips.summary: Geiger enlists in the merchant marine in 1943 because it is the only desegregated branch (the National Maritime Union had won the fight to integrate ships). After radio-radar officer school on Gallops Island, he serves on the SS Horseshoe in the Pacific (Ulithi, Leyte Gulf) before joining the SS Booker T. Washington under Hugh Mulzac, the only Black captain in the American fleet, with an integrated officer crew. Repeated dry-docking in segregated Norfolk produces weekly integrate-the-white-waiting-room confrontations.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Chicago Hospital Strike of 1947
clips.summary: Enrolling pre-med at the University of Chicago in 1946, Geiger works overnight at International News Service and chairs civil-liberties work for the American Veterans Committee. A documented campaign against the University of Chicago hospitals' refusal to admit Black patients culminates in a December 7, 1947 student-faculty strike, with Jeremiah Stamler arriving from Northwestern with a sound truck. The breakthrough comes from following the development office's foundation-visit schedule.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Journalism Career and Western Reserve
clips.summary: After being warned by Dr. W. Montague Cobb at Howard that AMA blacklisting will prevent admission to any standard medical school, Geiger pivots to science journalism at International News Service. By 1953 he assigns himself to the Association of American Medical Colleges meeting, where Jack McCoy of Western Reserve recruits him into the new integrated curriculum's class of bent-arrow nontraditional admissions.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Discovering Social Medicine
clips.summary: On the steps of the medical school in his second year, Geiger experiences what he later recognizes as the rediscovery of social medicine, traceable to Virchow and John Simon in 19th-century Europe. Through Margaret Mead and Barry Commoner he reaches Warren Weaver at the Rockefeller Foundation; rather than join Weaver's staff, Geiger learns from him about Dr. John B. Grant's funding of Sidney and Emily Kark in apartheid South Africa.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Pholela and Lamontville
clips.summary: Geiger spends four and a half months of 1958 in South Africa at Sidney and Emily Kark's Pholela rural health center in Natal province (halfway between Durban and Pietermaritzburg) and at the Lamontville Zulu public housing project near Durban. He observes the pre-Verwoerd window when more than 30 community health centers operated in South Africa, learns the model of community health workers and community-oriented primary care, and reads Sidney Kark's Social Pathology of Syphilis on the political economy of male labor migration.
related_events: creation of South African community health centers under the wartime national health service window

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Freedom Summer 1964 in Jackson
clips.summary: Charles Hudson recruits Geiger to be the August 1964 Medical Committee for Human Rights field coordinator in Jackson at 12 and a half North Farish Street. MCHR provides medical presence for SNCC, CORE, SCLC, and the COFO umbrella, with Tom Levin and others rotating through. Geiger meets Aaron Henry, recruits Dr. Count D. Gibson Jr from Tufts, and develops the COFO creep technique for railroad-track stops with Count's deepened Georgia accent.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Greenville Meeting and the OEO Proposal
clips.summary: At the Delta Ministry's December 1964 Greenville meeting, Geiger first articulates the community-health-center concept rooted in Pholela. On the grounded flight back to Boston, Count Gibson offers Tufts as sponsor. Geiger pitches Dr. William Kissick, then Elizabeth Shore, and in January 1965 secures from Sanford Kravitz at OEO research-and-demonstration funds; he returns with a $1.2 million two-site proposal for Columbia Point Boston and a Southern-rural site.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Selma 1965 and John Lewis
clips.summary: Two days after Bloody Sunday on the Edmund Pettus Bridge, Geiger flies to Atlanta and rides with Dr. King's car back to Selma, where he listens to King, James Forman, and Hosea Williams debate whether to violate the injunction against the Montgomery march. Geiger is arrested with Dr. Richard Hausknecht by the Alabama State Medical Society; he diagnoses John Lewis's skull fracture and arranges with Count Gibson to transport Lewis and Ivanhoe Donaldson to Boston for treatment. Reverend James J. Reeb is killed days later.

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Choosing Mound Bayou
clips.summary: Geiger learns of the abandoned J.C. Campbell Junior College buildings in Mound Bayou and visits, meeting Father Guidry and discovering the all-Black town's autonomy, the 40-bed Taborian Hospital of the Knights and Daughters of Tabor, and the smaller Sarah Brown Hospital of the United Order of Friendship. Confronting R. Sargent Shriver Jr's stonewalling by occupying his office with the Tufts vice president, Geiger secures site approval from Dr. Julius B. Richmond. He then maneuvers the state medical society at the AMA Atlantic City meeting into endorsing Mound Bayou as the only acceptable Mississippi site.
related_events: OEO veto-proof routing of grants through Northern universities
Senator John C. Stennis attacks on Child Development Group of Mississippi

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Building the Tufts-Delta Health Center
clips.summary: Dr. John W. Hatch picks cotton in Bolivar County for three weeks during reconnaissance, then builds ten local health associations organized on the Black Baptist Church model. Recruitment brings Dr. Robert Smith, Dr. Aaron Shirley, Dr. Alvin F. Poussaint, Sister Mary Stella, Joe Disparti, Phyllis Cunningham, Dr. Helen Barnes, Dr. Harvey Sanders, Dr. Charles Humphrey, and clinical director David Weeks. Andrew B. James leads environmental work digging wells and sanitary privies. Clinical care begins in a rented church parsonage; daily volume rises from 15 to 100 patients in three days.
related_events: Senator Edward M. Kennedy 1966 visit to Columbia Point
creation of OEO office of health affairs

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: North Bolivar Farm Cooperative and Community Programs
clips.summary: Asked to organize 100 vegetable gardens, thousands of families volunteer; recognizing displaced sharecroppers sitting on twelve-foot-deep topsoil, Dr. John W. Hatch and L.C. Dorsey build the North Bolivar County Farm Cooperative on twenty-acre donations into a 500-acre irrigated triple-crop farm involving four to six thousand people. Mrs. Robinson organizes the Round Lake meal program for isolated elderly. The health council runs bus transportation, WIC food, a library-bookstore cultural center, and an early-childhood intervention program.
related_events: collapse of the sharecropper system with mechanized agriculture

clips.subject: H. Jack Geiger (partial read)
clips.title: Legacy and Later Career
clips.summary: Geiger details the federal community-health-center system's growth to over 1,200 centers serving 20 million people at 8,000-plus sites, with the 1975 statutory requirement that 51 percent of each center's board be current patients. He moves from Tufts to Stony Brook in 1971, then to the new CUNY Medical School (Sophie Davis School of Biomedical Education) in 1978 to train minority students for primary care in underserved areas. He cites the Medical-Legal Partnerships model and an Institute of Medicine honor.
keywords: Sophie Davis School of Biomedical Education

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: York Pennsylvania Childhood
clips.summary: Brown describes growing up in York, Pennsylvania in a segregated colored community where he encountered chain-works factory workers hurling racial insults on his walk to elementary school. He attended segregated schools through sixth grade. He cites his junior high homeroom teacher Cassandra Shea and a football coach as early figures who introduced him to college and professional aspirations.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Athletics and College Path
clips.summary: Brown recounts his selection as Pennsylvania all-state basketball player, a brief failed tryout with the St. Louis Browns minor league in Thomasville, Georgia, and his Penn State basketball scholarship that paid only tuition. He explains how two Navy veterans from York who played at San Diego State encouraged him to transfer west.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Army Service
clips.summary: Brown describes being drafted at the end of the Korean War, basic training at Camp Gordon in Georgia where he encountered signs reading 'no Jews or niggers allowed,' and permanent station at Fort Monmouth, NJ. He details his refused attempt to opt out of Operation Sagebrush in Louisiana and his brief visit to Shreveport, Louisiana.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: San Diego State and Awakening
clips.summary: Brown describes his time at San Diego State University as one of approximately 25 Black students out of 7,000 enrolled. As chair of the Student Council Constitutions Committee, he refused to approve white-only sorority and fraternity charters. He recounts being called a slur on the streets of San Diego and identifies anger as the catalyst for his decision to join the civil rights movement after graduation.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Teaching and Founding San Diego CORE
clips.summary: Brown describes being denied a high school coaching position in La Mesa because the district would have to poll teachers about hiring a Negro. He joined the El Cajon Valley Open Housing Committee and was recruited around 1960 to help form the San Diego chapter of the Congress of Racial Equality. He also joined the Afro-American Association for Black history self-education, reading Lerone Bennett Jr. He became chapter chairman after his first year of teaching, having earned status as one of San Diego's five outstanding new teachers.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: San Diego CORE Campaigns
clips.summary: Brown details San Diego CORE's campaigns against employment discrimination, citing 19 segregated schools and grocery chains that refused to hire Black baggers or cashiers. He describes housing discrimination, including covenants and rental refusals affecting his future wife when she relocated from Ohio to work at General Dynamics in 1967. He recounts demonstrations against the California Real Estate Association and a previous Woolworths sit-in led by Dr. Jack Kimbrough.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Bank of America and SDG&E Campaigns
clips.summary: As chairman of CORE's Western Region (California, Oregon, Washington, and Colorado), Brown led statewide direct action against Bank of America and San Diego Gas & Electric to open employment to Black workers. Tactics included marches, sit-ins, mail-ins (mailing payments above or below balance owed), and coin-ins (paying in pennies at teller windows while others sang on the floor). Brown was jailed for trespassing, serving sentences on weekends while continuing to teach. He was summoned to Sacramento to defend his teaching credential.
related_events: CORE Western Region direct action campaigns

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: CORE Membership and Internal Conflict
clips.summary: Brown notes that San Diego CORE membership was approximately two-thirds white during his tenure. He describes the difficulty of recruiting Black members, attributing fear of reprisals. He details internal conflict with the peace movement and Trotskyites attempting to influence CORE's direction, accusations of communism, and what he describes as an organized effort to remove him from leadership. He resigned in 1967.
related_events: FBI infiltration of civil rights organizations

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Peace Corps in Lesotho
clips.summary: Brown describes his year as Peace Corps deputy director in Lesotho, beginning with a layover in Johannesburg where he and his wife were confined to a Blacks-only room. He recounts King Moshoeshoe II's house arrest for opposing apartheid, Prime Minister Chief Leabua Jonathan's accommodation with the regime, and the surprise of Basotho leaders when white Peace Corps volunteers arrived. He held a memorial service at an AME church in Lesotho after Martin Luther King's assassination, attended by King Moshoeshoe II. Brown returned to the United States following King's death.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Floyd McKissick Enterprises and Return
clips.summary: Unable to find a position in San Diego after Peace Corps service, Brown worked with Floyd McKissick at McKissick Enterprises in Harlem and then at Marine Midland Bank on Wall Street as a loan officer. He returned to San Diego in September 1971 when an old acquaintance recruited him as assistant to the president of San Diego State to organize what became the African American Studies program.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: African American Studies and Administrative Career
clips.summary: Brown became San Diego State's first Black administrator, hired faculty, and established a major and minor in what is now Africana Studies, securing university approval for the courses to fulfill general education requirements. He later directed campus computer services, then served 26 years as associate dean of the College of Business Administration, founding the Community Economic Development certificate program before retiring in 2004 to pursue real estate development.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Black Economic Development and Reflections
clips.summary: Brown founded the Black Economic Development Task Force in 1987 and Community Leaders Undoing Biases (CLUB) to address what he identifies as the unfinished work of the civil rights movement: economic self-determination and capital formation. He critiques the movement's omission of economic education, notes Tony Brown's KPBS programming on Black business, and reflects on what he describes as post-traumatic stress carried by civil rights veterans. He calls for Black studies to be marketed across the university curriculum.

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Path to the LDF: Gellhorn's Legal Survey
clips.summary: Greenberg recounts arriving at Columbia Law School with no civil rights career in mind, taking Professor Walter Gellhorn's seminar 'Legal Survey' (a euphemism chosen to avoid faculty objections in the McCarthy era), and working on actual cases for the few civil-liberties and civil-rights lawyers then practicing. He took the seminar for four semesters and became chair of the student body enrolled in it.
related_events: Walter Gellhorn's congressional testimony on Communist Party membership
Origins of clinical legal education at Columbia

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Joining LDF in 1949
clips.summary: Greenberg describes the size of the civil rights bar in the late 1940s: NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund had three or four lawyers; the ACLU had one; three Jewish organizations had one apiece. After Marian Wynne Perry left LDF to take a New York State position, Walter Gellhorn recommended Greenberg, and Thurgood Marshall hired him in March 1949.
keywords: NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund
related_events: Greenberg's March 1949 hire by Thurgood Marshall
Marian Wynne Perry's transition to New York State

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: The Marshall Team and LDF Independence
clips.summary: Greenberg describes the LDF staff he joined: Thurgood Marshall as Director-Counsel after the restrictive-covenant and voting-rights victories, Robert L. Carter as first assistant, Franklin H. Williams (later ambassador to Ghana and head of the Phelps Stokes Foundation), and a staff sociologist. He explains LDF's separation from the parent NAACP in 1957 for tax-exemption reasons and the resulting tensions with Roy Wilkins.
related_events: Franklin H. Williams' ambassadorship to Ghana

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Trying the Kansas Component of Brown
clips.summary: Greenberg and Robert L. Carter tried the Kansas school segregation statute case in Topeka. He explains the dual evidentiary strategy: documenting measurable inequalities in Black versus white schools (books, teachers' salaries, square footage, gymnasia, laboratories) while also urging the court, through expert witnesses, to find that segregation itself constituted inequality.
related_events: 1951 Topeka trial of Brown v. Board of Education

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Brown Decision and Southern Reaction
clips.summary: Greenberg recalls the long path from the 1950-51 argument through the 1952 Supreme Court appeal and two reargument cycles to the May 1954 decision. He describes the stunned reaction at LDF and the recognition that Brown swept away all state-imposed segregation, far broader than school segregation alone. He distinguishes the earlier higher-education cases (involving one or two plaintiffs) from school integration's mass-numbers implications, including the segregationist taboo against mixed-sex contact in integrated schools.
related_events: May 17, 1954 Brown v. Board of Education decision
Late-1950s Southern resistance to school desegregation

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Sit-In Defense and Race Relations in American Law
clips.summary: As Director-Counsel from 1961, Greenberg led LDF's representation of approximately 3,500 sit-in defendants, securing acquittals in all of them. He also discusses his 1959 book Race Relations and American Law, the first treatise on civil rights law since approximately 1919. The Columbia Law Review published paired pro and anti reviews, an unusual treatment Greenberg attributes to the topic's perceived controversy.
related_events: 1960 lunch-counter sit-ins beginning in North Carolina
1959 publication of Race Relations and American Law

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Critics: Herbert Wechsler and Edmond Cahn
clips.summary: Greenberg recounts the academic critique of Brown v. Board of Education by Herbert Wechsler in 'Toward Neutral Principles of Constitutional Law' and by NYU legal philosopher Edmond Cahn, who attacked Brown's use of social-science evidence. When Greenberg asked Cahn to review his book, Cahn declined on conflict-of-interest grounds related to his son's interracial marriage.
related_events: Wechsler's 1959 'Toward Neutral Principles of Constitutional Law'
Cahn's critique of Brown's social-science footnote

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Airport Desegregation and the Tureaud Story
clips.summary: Greenberg discusses the segregation of railroad and airport facilities into the 1960s, including at federal facilities north of the Mason-Dixon line. He recounts Coke v. City of Atlanta (desegregating the Atlanta airport in the home city of Coca-Cola) and his case against the New Orleans airport with co-counsel A.P. Tureaud, then the only Black lawyer in New Orleans. After winning, Tureaud took his granddaughter to the airport so she could buy an ice cream cone for the first time.
related_events: Coke v. City of Atlanta airport desegregation case

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Cooperating Attorneys Network
clips.summary: Greenberg describes the 1963 program he launched to seed Black legal practices in Southern cities that had no Black lawyers. With foundation funding, LDF gave each new attorney $10,000 to buy a typewriter, a rug, and law books. The program produced Julius Chambers in North Carolina, Mel Watt (later a Charlotte congressman), and Marian Wright Edelman, who set up practice in Columbus, Georgia. Greenberg names another beneficiary who became a Georgia congressman from Columbus.
related_events: 1963 LDF launch of Southern cooperating-attorneys grant program
Marian Wright Edelman's Columbus Georgia practice

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Civil Rights Act of 1964: Public Accommodations and Employment
clips.summary: Greenberg recounts LDF's litigation under the Civil Rights Act of 1964: public accommodations (restaurants, theaters, hotels) came easily because businesses wanted the trade, although some hotels created sham private clubs to evade the law. Title VII employment cases proved harder because labor unions, while nationally pro-civil-rights, maintained segregated white and Black locals at the local level. LDF brought some of its 'bitterest cases' against the labor unions.

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Alexander v. Holmes and the Common-Law Certiorari Writ
clips.summary: Greenberg narrates the procedural maneuver in Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education. After Senator John C. Stennis blocked the armed services appropriation bill to pressure Nixon, HEW Secretary Robert H. Finch asked the Fifth Circuit to remand the desegregation cases without ruling. LDF could not appeal because there was no final judgment, so Greenberg invoked the ancient common-law writ of certiorari (distinct from the statutory certiorari) to get the Supreme Court to take the case. The Court agreed and ruled with LDF, ordering immediate desegregation.
keywords: Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education
related_events: 1969 Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education decision

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Swann and Burger's Vote Switch
clips.summary: Greenberg describes Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education (1971) as the first substantive case heard under Chief Justice Warren Burger, who wrote an opinion calling for quotas, busing, and teacher integration. Following the argument, the preliminary conference vote favored upholding the lower court against LDF. As conferences continued, Burger found himself outvoted and switched his vote to assign the opinion to himself. Greenberg recounts being told this by Chief Justice Earl Warren, who used the phrase 'there he stood naked.' Burger included a paragraph signaling that desegregation requirements would eventually end, a line that proved consequential 10-15 years later.
keywords: Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education
