clips.title: Charles Sherrod Recruits for Southwest Georgia
clips.summary: Joe and Embry describe being recruited by SNCC co-founder Charles Sherrod, who circulated through Union Theological Seminary cohorts identifying like-minded students for his Southwest Georgia project. They acknowledge they had little idea what awaited them and that people were still being killed; they accepted because the moment felt morally clear.

clips.subject: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell
clips.title: Wake Forest Training and Arrival in Albany
clips.summary: Joe describes a pre-deployment training conference at the old Wake Forest campus run by an unidentified radical Christian ecumenical institute, which he found cult-like. The Howells then drove all night to the SNCC office in the Harlem neighborhood of Albany, Georgia, where Black SNCC organizers in white t-shirts greeted them with 'we are Black Panthers, you are roosters, Panthers eat roosters, go home, whitey, we don't need you.'

clips.subject: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell
clips.title: Black Power Split and Daily Life in Albany
clips.summary: Joe describes the 1966 SNCC split between John Lewis (integration, retain white workers) and Stokely Carmichael (Black self-determination, white workers confuse the message at this point) playing out during their Southwest Georgia summer, with Sherrod caught in the middle. Embry observes that despite the public rhetoric, Black SNCC organizers were personally courteous to her. She describes being unable to see a Black doctor for an infection, having to go to a white physician instead. Recording truncates mid-sentence as Joe begins describing housing arrangements for the married couple.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Batesville Childhood and Section 7
clips.summary: Henderson recounts her birth in Batesville, Mississippi, her family's small farm in Section 7 of Panola County, her father's death when she was four or five, and her mother's remarriage. She describes her grandmother's church-affiliated one-room school where she began at age three, working in Brown's Cafe with her mother, and the segregation she first noticed at the restaurant.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Early Awareness and West Camp Church
clips.summary: Henderson describes learning about the NAACP through her mother's secret membership card, the Emmett Till coverage in Jet magazine, and Reverend Middleton Godfrey's Black history sermons at West Camp Missionary Baptist Church. She recounts the Kirkendall family incident where the sheriff demanded a father beat his daughter, the community's armed protective response, and the family's flight to Indiana.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Frank Smith and the Greenwood Workshop
clips.summary: Henderson recalls meeting SNCC organizer Frank Smith at her cousin's house at Thanksgiving 1962, traveling to a Greenwood voter-registration workshop in June 1963 with her cousin Jimmy and John Hardy, arriving the morning Medgar Evers was assassinated, meeting John O'Neal, Willie Peacock, and the Peacock brothers, and learning about Lawrence Guyot, Fannie Lou Hamer, June Johnson, and Euvester Simpson's disappearance and beating.
related_events: Winona beating of Hamer, Johnson, Simpson

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Batesville Movement and Sit-ins
clips.summary: Henderson describes returning to Batesville to organize, her mother and stepfather registering to vote in 1964, the 1964 Mississippi Summer Project arrival, restaurant sit-ins with white farmers attacking demonstrators, her juvenile-court arrests, and Robert Miles's protective intervention. She recounts the older generation's armed self-defense, women carrying pistols to West Camp meetings, alongside the nonviolent movement work.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Mount Beulah and the Jackson Fairgrounds
clips.summary: Henderson recounts her decision after high-school graduation to delay college and join the movement, hitching a ride to Jackson with Lewis 'Lou' Grant, staying at Mount Beulah with Batesville women, being arrested at a Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party demonstration, and spending two weeks at the Jackson Fairgrounds with roughly a thousand women. She describes Mrs. Thelma Glover's prayer that defused a police standoff over a mattress.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Freedom Corps and the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union
clips.summary: Henderson describes joining Curtis Hayes in McComb, then Cleveland, Mississippi as part of the Delta Ministry's Freedom Corps stipended by the National Council of Churches. She lived with Owen Brooks and the Washington family, then moved to Rosedale to organize with Frank Davis, Billy Kochiyama, Morton Thomas, Isaac Foster, and Homer Crawford for the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union among Delta plantation workers earning thirty cents an hour.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Bishop College and Federal Prison Visits
clips.summary: Henderson describes leaving Mississippi for Bishop College in Texas in 1966 with Marjorie Hyatt and Linda Wilson, the campus reaction to her afro, working in the laundry and then the library, and her Sunday visits to Lafayette Loche at Seagoville federal prison with a Louisiana woman whose fiance was a war resister. She recounts the Parchman state-prison overnight detention after a Rosedale arrest, where women were given a forced laxative drink.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Poor People's Campaign and Resurrection City
clips.summary: Henderson recounts Dr. King's 1968 Batesville visit, her work with Leon Hall organizing the Mule Train for SCLC's Poor People's Campaign from Memphis through Selma and Montgomery to Birmingham and Washington. She describes the contrast between Resurrection City tents on the National Mall and SCLC staff housing at the Pitts Motor Inn, meeting Charles E. Cobb Jr. on 14th Street, and being extracted by Frank Smith to the Institute for Policy Studies.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Drum and Spear Bookstore
clips.summary: Henderson describes her job at Drum and Spear Bookstore in Washington, founded through Afro-American Resources, working with Charles E. Cobb Jr., Tony Gittens, and Ralph Featherstone, the connection to the Center for Black Education and Federal City College, the Sauti ya Watoto radio program with Mimi (Curtis Hayes's wife), the tear-gassing during Lerone Bennett Jr's book signing, and Featherstone's death by car bomb with Che Payne on March 10, 1970.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Panola County Drums and Family History
clips.summary: Henderson describes the Panola County drum-and-fife tradition played every July 3-4 by Othar Turner and earlier Sid Hemphill, traced by Smithsonian recordings to West African Mali rhythms. She recounts her family history: her great-great-grandfather Duncan C. Henderson as slave owner, the 1952 family incident in Pope involving her uncles, and her uncle's electrocution. She also recounts Benjamin Brown's killing at Jackson State.

clips.subject: Juadine Henderson
clips.title: Journalism Career and Reflections
clips.summary: Henderson recounts her path through Children's Defense Fund, the Interreligious Foundation for Community Organization, the National Urban Coalition, the Michele Clark Fellowship at Columbia in 1974 working with Earl Caldwell, her first job at the Daily Argus in Mount Vernon, and twenty-eight years as a journalist. She reflects on the 1966 Meredith March, the SNCC Nashville meeting electing Stokely Carmichael, the Deacons for Defense, and her concerns about the current political climate.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Tarrytown Childhood and UAW Father
clips.summary: Richardson describes her birth on March 10, 1944, in Tarrytown, NY, her mother Mellie Louise Richardson (née Tucker) as seamstress and jazz pianist, and her father William King Richardson's role in organizing the UAW local at the Fisher Body Chevrolet plant. She recalls neighbors leaving anonymous bags of groceries on the porch to support the organizing drive, and her father's death of a heart attack on the assembly line when she was seven.
related_events: UAW Local organizing at Tarrytown Fisher Body plant

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Mother, Birth of a Nation, Early Racial Awareness
clips.summary: After her father's death, Richardson's mother takes county-secretary work in White Plains (after Mr. Slaven advises her to change her registration to Republican), books numbers locally for white ethnic neighbors, and plays jazz piano. Richardson recounts her mother's memory of seeing Birth of a Nation in 1915 and being unable to lift her head walking out, and Richardson's own parallel humiliation in an AP history course at Sleepy Hollow High School where a textbook illustration of Reconstruction (later identified by John Dittmer as nationally prescribed) drew classmates' stares.
related_events: Release of Birth of a Nation (1915)

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Swarthmore, SPAC, Cafeteria Workers
clips.summary: Richardson enters Swarthmore College in 1962 as one of eight Black freshmen (four men, four women), all housed with Quaker children. She joins the SDS chapter SPAC, co-chaired by Mimi Feingold (just returned from six weeks in Parchman Prison after the Freedom Rides) and Carl Wittman. As a work-study waitress, Richardson is scalded by an uncovered hot-water pipe in the dining hall and works with the all-Black cafeteria staff to leverage her injury into improved conditions for the workers.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Cambridge, Maryland and Gloria Richardson
clips.summary: Through SPAC, Richardson joins weekend bus trips to Cambridge, Maryland to picket the Choptank Inn and skating rink alongside the Cambridge Non-Violent Action Committee. She meets Reggie Robinson as her first SNCC contact, stays in Pop Herb's funeral parlor attic, and observes that Gloria Richardson Dandridge was never philosophically nonviolent, Dandridge tripped a heckler at a picket line and warned that if white vigilantes crossed Race Street into the Black community, she could not guarantee their safety.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Taking the Year Off, Arrival in Atlanta
clips.summary: Penny Patch recruits Richardson to take a semester off after sophomore year to work full-time with SNCC and Cambridge, instructing her to go through Ruby Doris Smith Robinson for application approval. Richardson takes a detour with Reggie Robinson to Cincinnati for Bill Hansen and Ruthie Harris's wedding, then arrives at the Atlanta office at 8 1/2 Raymond Street, where she mistakes James Forman in overalls sweeping the stairs for the custodian before Forman conscripts her on discovering she can take shorthand and type 90 words per minute.
related_events: Bill Hansen and Ruthie Harris wedding (1963)

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Forman's Office and JFK Assassination
clips.summary: Richardson describes her first SNCC staff meeting in November 1963, immediately after the Kennedy assassination, where Forman urged strategic caution and Dinky Romilly cried openly. She catalogues the office: Julian Bond and Mary King in communications, Bobbi Yancy and Robert "Billy" Stafford coordinating campus travelers for HBCUs, Jimmy Bolton at the reception desk as the only openly gay staff member, Chesselonia Johnson handling bookkeeping for field-staff stipends.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Jack Minnis and the Mississippi Chronology
clips.summary: Richardson describes Jack Minnis's research department and the Mississippi Chronology of Violence and Intimidation in Mississippi Since 1961, compiled from WATS-line reports where field staff used person-to-person operator calls to evade segregationist phone operators. She traces her later use of the chronology methodology at Rainbow TV Works in Los Angeles and at the United Church of Christ Commission for Racial Justice in documenting police killings of Eleanor Bumpurs and Michael Stewart in the 1980s.
related_events: Fred Hampton and Mark Clark assassinations (1969)

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Field Coordination and Ruby Doris
clips.summary: Richardson describes the daily WATS-line reporting protocol that connected field staff (Margaret Block, Emma Bell, Hollis Watkins) to the Atlanta National Office, the friends-of-SNCC network in Chicago (Mrs. Shaw, Mimi Shaw) that called segregationist sheriffs to signal external scrutiny, and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson's enforcement of monthly field reports as the precondition for $9.64 weekly stipends. Ivanhoe Donaldson recalled receiving daily letters from Ruby Doris while jailed during the Aaron E. Henry pharmacy drive in Clarksdale.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Office Work and Prathia Hall
clips.summary: Richardson describes typing 33-page executive committee minutes on green stencils in the workroom amid flying water bugs, running the mimeograph for the Student Voice newsletter, and transcribing a Prathia Hall speech (probably Selma) while tears streamed down her face. Hall, raised by Pentecostal ministers, later confronted SCLC field staff in Selma for telling local residents they could not demonstrate unless they were philosophically nonviolent, which Hall called blackmail.

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: No Minutes, No Freedom Sit-In
clips.summary: Richardson recounts the women's sit-in in James Forman's Atlanta office staged by herself, Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, Mary King, Bobbi Yancy, and Mildred Forman with placards demanding rotation off secretarial work. Forman's second edition of The Making of Black Revolutionaries (mid-1980s) claimed he had suggested the action himself. Richardson confronted him at the book party and again at a Duke 40th-anniversary panel; Forman conceded she had "another interpretation" while she insisted she had the facts.
related_events: SNCC women's sit-in in Forman's office

clips.subject: Judy Richardson
clips.title: Residential Freedom School and Oginga Odinga
clips.summary: Richardson describes proposing a residential freedom school after 1964 that paired northern and southern young people in Chicago and Cordele, Georgia, with Ivanhoe Donaldson securing funding without anyone questioning her inexperience. She begins to describe a Toddle House sit-in tied to James Forman's discovery that Kenyan foreign-affairs official Oginga Odinga was staying at the Peachtree Manor Hotel, framing the cold-war diplomatic pressure on US segregation. Recording terminates mid-sentence during the Route 40 / Atlanta diplomatic-incident narration at 02:14:52, before the Toddle House arrest itself is recounted.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: FSA Homestead in Humphreys County
clips.summary: Burns describes her family's move from Greenwood to Humphreys County around 1942, when her father purchased 123 acres through the Farm Security Administration. She recounts the family economy of cotton, corn, livestock, and self-sufficiency, with her mother's early death forcing her to take on household responsibility at thirteen.
related_events: Black landownership in the Mississippi Delta

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Early Country Schools
clips.summary: Burns describes attending a two-teacher, eight-grade country school with a pot-bellied stove, followed by the consolidated Womack and Hodge school and Humphreys County Training School in Louise. She notes that Mississippi made no equalization investment in Black schools before Brown v. Board of Education, with new textbooks appearing only in ninth grade alongside a new Everyday Science class.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Emmett Till and the 1950s Climate
clips.summary: Burns recalls reading about Emmett Till's murder in Jet magazine while finishing high school in 1955, and describes her father warning her brother to stay clear of white girls in Belzoni. She notes that Attorney Boyd and other activists later cited the Till case as the catalyst for their movement involvement.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Church Life and Mentorship to College
clips.summary: Burns describes alternating between the Methodist and Baptist congregations near her family's farm, with the family pastor visiting on the fourth Sunday of each month. She credits her tenth-grade English teacher with directing her toward an English-teaching career, and recalls reading Alfred Lord Tennyson and later Eldridge Cleaver's Soul on Ice.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Mississippi Valley State and Student Teaching
clips.summary: Burns enrolled at Mississippi Valley State University around 1955-1956, when the institution had only two or three buildings and a class of sixteen to thirty-two students under founding president Dr. James Herbert White. She describes commuting by bus to college and completing her student teaching in Belzoni, where Clarice Reid was among her students.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Belzoni, Chicago, and Return to Tchula
clips.summary: Burns describes Belzoni's reputation as a violent town that Black workers entered quickly and left quickly, with neighbor families and Civil Rights workers wary of overnight stays. She married, moved briefly to Chicago where her husband worked at Wieboldt's department store, then returned to Tchula in 1962 where principal S.B. Marshall hired her at Marshall High School that fall.
related_events: Belzoni's reputation among Civil Rights workers

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Behind-the-Scenes Movement Work
clips.summary: Burns describes how Greenwood SNCC organizer John Ball came to Marshall High School to recruit her, after which the principal warned she would lose her job if she continued. She hosted Freedom Summer workers, including Henry and Sue Lorenzi, at her home with refreshments after they parked behind the house to avoid being seen. She names Abe Osheroff, who helped build the Mileston Community Center, and Rev. Arrell Whitaker, Rev. Russell, F.D. Marshall, Bernice Mangum, and Emma Lou Williams as fellow Mileston-area organizers.

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Tchula School Desegregation
clips.summary: Burns describes her son's participation in the 1965 first-grade cohort, approximately sixteen children, that desegregated Tchula Consolidated School after a federal court ordered Holmes County to integrate. She recounts the school being burned in stages as white residents discovered Black children would attend, until the white population withdrew entirely to establish what became Cruger-Tchula Academy. Christopher Epps was among her son's classmates.
related_events: 1965 federal school desegregation order in Holmes County

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Teaching Return and College Aspirations
clips.summary: Burns returned to teaching in 1967 after a two-year absence and describes the gradual shift in students' college destinations from historically Black institutions like Mississippi Valley State and Jackson State toward the previously all-white Ole Miss. She notes Antoine Patrick of Tchula as one local resident who completed his degree there.
related_events: James Meredith's integration of Ole Miss (1962)

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Holmes County School Board Service
clips.summary: Burns ran for the Holmes County School Board around 1971 against incumbent Martin Smith, who died the night before the election. She served until approximately 1975 or 1976 with Dr. Arenia C. Mallory of Saints Industrial School as one of two Black members on a five-member board. She describes hiring younger white teachers as faculty integrated, and notes that William Dean became the county's first Black superintendent in the late 1980s.
related_events: Black electoral politics in Mississippi post-Voting Rights Act

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: Holmes County Health Research Program
clips.summary: Burns served as secretary of the Mileston Cooperative, which funded the Holmes County Health Research Program led by University of Illinois professor Dr. Demitri Shimkin. The door-to-door health surveys documented unexpected hypertension rates, including in a six-year-old child, and the work informed the Robert F. Kennedy Economic Development initiative and the school breakfast and lunch program. The research also documented Black migration from plantations into apartment housing in Lexington, Pickens, Goodman, and Durant, and northward to Chicago, Peoria, and Wisconsin.
related_events: University of Illinois community health research

clips.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
clips.title: French Teaching and Continuing Career
clips.summary: After leaving the school board, Burns taught English and literature at Mississippi Valley State until 1976, then returned to the Holmes County public schools. When Mississippi mandated foreign-language coursework for state-college admission, she added French certification through summer study at Delta State and continued teaching French part-time after her 2006 retirement.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Bryn Mawr and Lower Merion
clips.summary: Becton describes his birth in Bryn Mawr, Pennsylvania, his father's work as a janitor at Bryn Mawr College, and growing up on the Main Line in a household one and a half blocks from the college. He attended Lower Merion High School in Ardmore, an integrated school that produced James H. Billington, Alexander Haig, and Henry "Hap" Arnold, while local restaurants, theaters, and swimming programs remained segregated.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Army Air Corps and OCS
clips.summary: Recruited from Lower Merion's football team in December 1943, Becton enlisted in the Army Air Corps reserves hoping to become a Tuskegee Airman. After failing the eye test at Keesler Field on a new machine-based exam, he was reassigned to MacDill Field's aviation engineering unit. A barely-literate Black first sergeant recommended him for Officer Candidate School; Becton entered Fort Benning, Georgia in January 1945 and was commissioned an infantry second lieutenant in August 1945.
related_events: Army Air Corps reserve induction December 1943

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Pacific and Morotai
clips.summary: Becton joined the 93rd Infantry Division on Morotai in late 1945 alongside the Dixie Division, a unit flying Confederate regimental flags. As a 19-year-old second lieutenant he rode joint Provost Marshal patrols to keep peace between the two divisions and led a patrol into the hills with a Japanese translator attempting to inform holdout Japanese soldiers that the war had ended. He then served as a signal platoon leader in Manila stringing communication poles before the division deactivated in January 1946.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Muhlenberg and Executive Order 9981
clips.summary: Returning to the States in 1946, Becton enrolled at Muhlenberg College in Allentown on a football scholarship, the first Black student admitted. After a shoulder separation from ill-fitting equipment he focused on pre-med under the GI Bill, married Louise (a nurse from Suffolk) on January 29, 1948. He was at Aberdeen Proving Ground for reserve training in summer 1948 when Truman's Executive Order 9981 was read aloud; the post commander immediately declared no change to segregated officer clubs, swimming pools, and NCO clubs as long as he remained in command.
related_events: Brown v. Board 'all deliberate speed' precedent

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Van Horn Incident
clips.summary: While leading a signal unit convoy from Fort Bliss to Fort Bragg as a junior officer, Becton was chased out of a Van Horn, Texas drug store by an armed sheriff for sitting at the soda fountain in uniform. The next morning his unit took emergency action at a civilian car accident, transporting two injured women to a Van Horn hospital. The same sheriff, learning who saved the women's lives, was visibly chastened, a moment Becton frames as evidence that contact with Black soldiers could shift local racial attitudes.
keywords: Fort Bliss to Fort Bragg convoy

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Korea and Hill 201
clips.summary: Becton deployed to Korea in summer 1950 with the segregated 3rd Battalion, 9th Infantry Regiment (which included future Congressman Charles B. Rangel as an enlisted soldier). He led patrols at Pohang protecting an airfield, broke out of the Pusan Perimeter at Hill 201 where his platoon was caught between Japanese-position fire and a friendly 2nd Battalion engagement, and received his first wound. He earned the Silver Star and two Purple Hearts during the campaign north of the 38th Parallel before being wounded a second time at Thanksgiving 1950 as the Chinese entered the war.
related_events: Chinese entry into Korean War November 1950

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Integration by Military Necessity
clips.summary: Becton describes how the 9th Infantry Regiment integrated in August 1950 when Col. C.C. Sloan ordered incoming replacements assigned by MOS rather than race, accepting a Mexican American soldier from Texas as his first non-Black platoon member. Becton frames integration as a battlefield decision driven by replacement shortages rather than compliance with Executive Order 9981, with Black company commanders eventually leading white-majority battalions. He returned to Camp Edwards in Massachusetts to find one segregated Black company assigned to police duties, complained up the chain, and was reassigned within an hour to Indiantown Gap as a basic training company commander.
related_events: Korean War integration of replacement system 1950-51

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Germany and Prairie View
clips.summary: Becton served as communications officer with the 2nd Armored Division's 42nd Armored Infantry Battalion in Germany, where his mother and mother-in-law visited and Louise's mother stayed long-term. Reassigned to Prairie View A&M University as ROTC instructor (the only captain among a Lt. Colonel and three majors), he simultaneously completed his bachelor's degree in mathematics over the objections of his Professor of Military Science, graduating in 1960 as Prairie View's celebrated 5,000th graduate. His fourth daughter Karen was born during this assignment; she would later become an Air Force Colonel.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Leavenworth and Selma
clips.summary: After Command and General Staff College at Fort Leavenworth (16 Black officers in a class of 600) Becton was assigned to communications zone in France. While in the Pentagon's Office of National Guard and Reserve Affairs in 1965, he was the duty officer at the Army Operations Center during the Selma to Montgomery march, tasked with extracting deployment data and crew-served weapon locations from a Brigadier General commanding the Alabama National Guard who initially refused to provide them. He frames the era's racial reform as advanced not by participation in marches but by Black officers demonstrating professional competence in command.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: Vietnam and 1st Cavalry Division
clips.summary: Becton commanded a squadron in the 101st Airborne Division in Vietnam (the only Black commander at that level in the division) where Gen. Creighton Abrams, the MACV commander, made repeat visits to his armored unit. After serving as Deputy Brigade Commander, branch chief for all armor officer assignments, and Deputy Commander at Fort Dix where he lobbied with Mayor Frank L. Rizzo of Philadelphia against the post's closure, Becton was assigned to command the 1st Cavalry Division at Fort Hood in 1976 on Abrams's written instruction. He commanded the division during its conversion to a pure armored configuration.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: VII Corps Germany
clips.summary: Becton commanded VII Corps in Germany, the largest combat corps in the Army, with Gen. George S. Patton IV as his deputy. The Army held his promotion announcement for several months to test how the German government and senior officers would receive a Black corps commander before going public. He delivered his change-of-command speech alternating English and German, having drilled the bilingual translation during German language school in lieu of conversational German. He terminated the 1980 Reforger exercise mid-deployment after a sudden thaw threatened German civilian property, with Supreme Allied Commander Alexander Haig (a Lower Merion classmate) flying in to deliver the cancellation order.

clips.subject: Julius W. Becton Jr.
clips.title: USAID, FEMA, and DCPS
clips.summary: After retiring as a Lieutenant General in August 1983, Becton directed disaster assistance at USAID where he coordinated the U.S. response to the Ethiopian famine, then served as Director of FEMA under Reagan and Bush. His refusal to approve New Hampshire Governor John H. Sununu's Seabrook nuclear plant evacuation plan led Sununu (now Bush's chief of staff) to force him out in 1989. Bush offered an ambassadorship to a Caribbean nation which Louise refused. Becton later served as Superintendent of DC Public Schools during the financial control board era, departing after 18 months at his family's insistence, and chaired the Military Leadership Diversity Commission that opened combat positions and Ranger School to women.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Richmond Childhood and Family Music
clips.summary: Williams describes his birth in Suffolk, Virginia in 1943, his parents' careers as music teachers (his father the first Black band director in Richmond, his mother a music coordinator), his Howard-and-Penn-educated mother's plans for his education, attending Armstrong High School over Maggie Walker, the Armstrong-Walker football classic at city stadium, and the Tobacco Festival Parade integration. Family friend Joe Kennedy taught music at Walker; Arthur Ashe was an early tennis acquaintance.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Early Civil Rights Awareness in Richmond
clips.summary: Williams recalls the Emmett Till photograph in Jet magazine, Montgomery bus boycott news, the integration of Richmond buses, and Charles Sherrod's Virginia Union sit-ins at downtown department stores in 1960-61. His parents kept him out of the high school demonstrations using a fabricated claim that college students excluded high schoolers. Family friend Oliver Hill, whose firm Hill, Tucker and Marsh argued equal-pay cases for teachers, shaped Williams's path toward law.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Amherst College and Class Analysis
clips.summary: Williams describes the culture shock of arriving at Amherst in 1961 with a four-student-per-class Black quota, joining Alpha Delta Phi as their first Black member over Theta Delta Chi, struggling with calculus, and finding intellectual transformation through Professor Colston Warne's economics course and through summer 1964 work in Harlem with Julian T. Houston and a young woman nicknamed Pigeon (Norman Cousins's daughter), who introduced him to Marxist class analysis.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: SRE, Operation Crossroads Africa, and the March on Washington
clips.summary: Williams led Students for Racial Equality at Amherst after defending the Movement in an editorial. Marian Wright Edelman's campus visit led to a scholarship through Operation Crossroads Africa to spend ten weeks building a hospital extension in Kigezi District, Uganda. Returning by ship, he landed in New York the morning of the August 1963 March on Washington and attended with his brother. SNCC singers and the Freedom Singer Cordell Reagon connected him to the Movement core.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: The February 1965 Reform-or-Revolution Conference
clips.summary: Williams organized a thousand-person Movement conference at Mount Holyoke for February 12, 1965 titled Civil Rights Movement: Reform or Revolution. He cold-called Malcolm X for the keynote at a thousand-dollar honorarium and Malcolm agreed; Ossie Davis and Michael Harrington also accepted. Malcolm was stranded in France in a passport dispute; Harrington was blocked by a New York ice storm; Ossie Davis performed all three planned roles in a single keynote. Malcolm X was assassinated nine days later at the Audubon Ballroom.
related_events: Civil Rights Movement: Reform or Revolution conference Feb 12 1965

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Drive South to Selma and SNCC's Montgomery Operation
clips.summary: In March 1965 Williams and six others drove from Amherst to Alabama in his father's Buick station wagon (loaned after a Volkswagen breakdown), avoiding arrest in South Carolina through Fred Arano's quick-witted cover story about a Florida spring fling. Arriving in Montgomery, Stokely Carmichael diverted them from Selma to a parallel SNCC operation distrustful of SCLC, with Ivanhoe Donaldson directing logistics. Williams describes mounted-police charges against marchers and the Latino Movement organizer who stopped retaliation against an injured officer to preserve nonviolent discipline.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Seven Days in Kilby Prison
clips.summary: Arrested on a sit-down in front of the Alabama state house with the Confederate and American flags overhead, Williams was held in Kilby Prison's seventy-five-man bullpen for about a week. Worth Long stood on a table and led an immediate redistribution of dusty blankets, the first time Williams saw organizing produce material results within minutes. When Long was moved to solitary, Williams and Detroit SNCC member Stu House held the cell together for the remaining days. After Williams's bail money arrived from Massachusetts supporters, he reluctantly left and witnessed the march's final mass rally at the City of St. Jude with Harry Belafonte, Nina Simone, and Ivanhoe Donaldson in a yellow rain slicker.
related_events: Selma to Montgomery March final rally

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Newark Community Union Project Organizing
clips.summary: Tom Hayden recruited Williams to the Newark Community Union Project in summer 1965, declaring the Southern Movement over after the Voting Rights Act. NCUP had moved from working with Clinton Hill Neighborhood Corporation homeowners to lower-hill renters around bad landlords, store credit fraud, and police brutality (which CORE handled). Williams worked the central-ward stoplight block-organizing assignment with organizer Karenna. Office staff included Terry Jefferson and the husband-and-wife organizing team Bessie and Thurmond Smith. Williams attended Yale Law fall 1965–summer 1967 while returning to Newark each summer.
related_events: SDS Economic Research and Action Project
Stokely Carmichael replacing John Lewis at SNCC

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Newark Rebellion 1967 and Witness Documentation
clips.summary: Returning from Philadelphia's Black Power Conference, Williams entered a sealed Newark and survived a four-officer shotgun stop only because law textbooks identified him and his companions as law students. He directed VISTA-funded law-student volunteers in interviewing witnesses to state-police shootings, including a young man shot forty-six times in a south-ward bar. Amiri Baraka photographed the corpse and circulated flyers. Williams was called before a grand jury where he was asked whether the flyers were inflammatory. He had not yet been targeted by HUAC; a 1968 hearing surfaced his correspondence with Tom Hayden, accused him of cohabitation with Phil Hutchings, and tied NCUP to the Rebellion.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Medical School Agreement and Gibson Administration
clips.summary: Williams and the Newark Area Planning Association, working with Louise Epperson's Committee Against Negro and Puerto Rican Removal, fought the proposed 200-acre College of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey land seizure. Yale planners produced an alternative nineteen-acre footprint. The 1968 medical school agreement under DCA Commissioner Paul N. Ylvisaker and Higher Education Chancellor Ralph A. Dungan produced sixty acres of community-controlled housing land, a construction-trades training corporation, and a Model Cities veto structure. Williams became Kenneth A. Gibson's first campaign manager in 1970, then Model Cities director. After he raised an additional fifteen-million-dollar Project Rehab grant, Gibson fired him over an interpretive dispute on five-and-a-half-million in federal expenditures; Rep. Joseph G. Minish's front-page Star-Ledger story was answered by a Williams op-ed at editor Mort Pye's invitation. Raymond A. Brown headlined Williams's rally. A federal prosecutor, told by the auditor there was no case, never indicted.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: National Bar Association Presidency and Zimbabwe
clips.summary: Williams's law-practice base widened through O. T. Wells's invitation to plan the 1973 NBA convention. Working with the Young Lawyers Division and women lawyers, he became NBA's youngest president in 1978 at age thirty-four. The NBA International Affairs Committee, including Julius LeVonne Chambers, Elaine R. Jones, and Theodore M. Shaw, produced a legal paper on the inadequacy of the proposed Zimbabwe-Rhodesia constitution. SNCC contacts brought it before the UN African group and the Organization of African Unity; it was adopted by the UN General Assembly and translated into seven languages. Williams reports it was used to keep Jimmy Carter from lifting sanctions. Louis Farrakhan keynoted the 1979 NBA convention. Williams ran unsuccessfully for Newark mayor in 1982 against Gibson, lacking fundraising appetite.

clips.subject: Junius Williams
clips.title: Abbott v. Burke and Training the Next Generation
clips.summary: After serving as Irvington town attorney under Mayor Sara Bost, Williams built an organizing institute at Rutgers-Newark to train parents around the Abbott v. Burke decision, which required New Jersey to fund urban schools at suburban levels under the state constitution's thorough-and-efficient clause. The institute also trains youth advocates in media skills, interviewing, and documentary production. Williams names electoral co-optation of street-organizing bases as the central failure to address, his manuscript Unfinished Agenda: Urban Politics in the Era of Black Power, then forthcoming from a university press, was his summary statement.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: 1954: Brown, Vietnam, and Tuskegee
clips.summary: Cleaver, age nine in 1954, identifies the year as a hinge: the Brown decision in the United States, the French defeat in Vietnam, and a wave of anti-colonial liberation movements. She situates her family at Tuskegee Institute, where her father served on the faculty and her mother worked, before the family relocated abroad.
related_events: French defeat at Dien Bien Phu (1954)

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: India, the Philippines, Sierra Leone
clips.summary: Cleaver describes her father's recruitment into Truman's Point Four foreign-aid community-development work in Jawaharlal Nehru's India, followed by postings in the Philippines and Sierra Leone. Growing up among dark-skinned governing populations dismantled white-supremacist narratives before she encountered them.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: George School and Quaker Nonviolence
clips.summary: Returning for high school at the George School, a Society of Friends boarding school in Pennsylvania, Cleaver responds to a 1963 Albany jail-in photograph by proposing a solidarity fast. A classmate's counter-note sends her to Fellowship House in Philadelphia, where she seeks out Prathia Hall and instead receives an introduction to nonviolence from a resident painter.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Family Politics and the March on Washington
clips.summary: Cleaver's parents refuse to take her to the August 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom, citing predicted violence. She situates their caution against her father's earlier desegregation work in Texas and her mother's involvement in the Southern Negro Youth Congress and an NAACP consent-decree route to graduate school in Michigan.
related_events: March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom (August 1963)

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Oberlin College and the Kennedy Assassination
clips.summary: Cleaver enters Oberlin College in fall 1963 to find a hotbed of civil-rights activity she had not anticipated. She locates her freshman year against the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing, the Kennedy assassination, and her own decision not to sign up for Mississippi Freedom Summer.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Community Relations Service in DC
clips.summary: Cleaver quits Oberlin College and takes a Peace Corps job in Washington, then moves to the Community Relations Service under the 1964 Civil Rights Act. She types reports for Richard W. Thornell on the Cleveland riots, encounters Roger Wood Wilkins as deputy director, and meets Marian Wright Edelman and Eleanor Holmes Norton at a Yale Law School moot court.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Barnard, George Ware, and TIAL
clips.summary: Cleaver enrolls at Barnard but disengages within weeks. She describes meeting George Ware of TIAL and the Tuskegee Student Movement, his analysis of Tuskegee Civic Association accommodationism under Dr. Charles Gomillion, and the church-integration tactics Ware deployed to break local complacency. Through Ware she enters the SNCC orbit.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: SNCC New York Office, Summer 1966
clips.summary: At Ernest Stevens and George Ware's NYU-area apartment, Cleaver meets Stokely Carmichael and Ivanhoe Donaldson, who hires her as a SNCC secretary in New York roughly two weeks after the Black Power call in Greenwood, Mississippi. She describes Elizabeth Sutherland's tenure as office director and the post-Black-Power dynamic in which white staff were asked to leave leadership roles.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Sammy Younge Jr and Lowndes County
clips.summary: Cleaver narrates the January 1966 murder of Sammy Younge Jr, a Navy veteran and Tuskegee organizer she had known since childhood, shot at a gas station by Marvin Segrest while registering voters. She links his work in Lowndes County, Alabama, to the Lowndes County Freedom Organization Black Panther symbol and to SNCC's anti-Vietnam-War statement, which cost Julian Bond his Georgia legislative seat until the Supreme Court intervened.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: SNCC Atlanta and the Fisk Conference
clips.summary: Cleaver moves to Atlanta in January 1967 to work for George Ware in SNCC's campus program. She describes the financial collapse of SNCC, the Stern Gang newsletter that alienated Jewish donors, the 14-hour meetings that produced the report later published as Black Power: The Politics of Liberation in America with Charles V. Hamilton, and the planning for the Liberation Will Come From a Black Thing conference at Fisk University.
keywords: Liberation Will Come From a Black Thing conference

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Meeting Eldridge Cleaver
clips.summary: When Fisk withdrew rooms two weeks before the conference and a blizzard grounded most speakers, a Nashville Episcopal priest, Fr. Woodruff, offered his church as venue and Leroy Eldridge Cleaver arrived from California. Cleaver describes their meeting in Nashville, the Nashville riot that followed, and the parole restrictions that confined Eldridge to San Francisco and forced her to travel west to continue the relationship.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Joining the Black Panther Party
clips.summary: Cleaver enters the Black Panther Party through Eldridge in summer 1967 Oakland, observing the SNCC-to-BPP migration through figures including Bobby L. Rush, Joudon Major Ford, and Chico Neblett. She traces the Panther name back to the Lowndes County Freedom Organization symbol and discusses Pete O'Neal's Kansas City breakaway as the Sons of Malcolm.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Community Revolution Reframed
clips.summary: Cleaver argues against the periodization that treats the movement as good before King's death and bad afterward, and against the framing of desegregation as integration. She describes the Black community's mechanisms of survival under segregation, the political opening in unorganized cities like Oakland, and the convergence of Vietnam-era youth, returning veterans, and grassroots organizing into what she calls community revolution.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: COINTELPRO and the Algeria Section
clips.summary: Cleaver describes the FBI's key-extremists list and the 1967 expansion of COINTELPRO beyond the Communist Party, the demonization of the Panthers in mainstream press, and the international section she and Eldridge established in Algiers after his flight via Cuba. She characterizes the alliance-building between Black militants and African and Arab revolutionaries as the threat the intelligence agencies were most determined to prevent.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Bobby Seale's Trial and Panther Defense
clips.summary: Cleaver recounts proposing courthouse demonstrations after Bobby Seale's October 1967 arrest on murder charges following a shootout in which a policeman died. She describes a campaign that began with eight people and grew into an international support movement by the time of trial, and connects the Panthers' confrontational posture to the colonial-warfare logic the FBI and intelligence agencies applied to them.

clips.subject: Kathleen Cleaver
clips.title: Distance, the Mixtape, and What Was Lost
clips.summary: Closing the interview, Cleaver reflects on how the 40-year distance changes the telling. She enumerates the deaths that foreclosed an alternative trajectory: Carmichael, Malcolm X, King, Fred Hampton. She frames the Black Power Mixtape's appeal as a glimpse of a country that might have been, populated by young, brilliant, engaging people including Miriam Makeba and Angela Davis.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Metropolis Childhood and Anne Braden's Book
clips.summary: Tillow describes her birth in Paducah, Kentucky in 1942, growing up in Metropolis, Illinois in a German-American farm family of modest means. Her Presbyterian minister Maynard Elftman gave her Anne Braden's The Wall Between in high school, exposing her to the Wade case in Louisville and the sedition charge against Carl and Anne Braden.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: University of Illinois NAACP
clips.summary: Tillow recounts integration of Metropolis schools, nominating an African-American student for a high school girls' club and being rebuffed by the teacher. At the University of Illinois she joined the NAACP and walked her first picket line against a Champaign, Illinois store that refused to hire African-Americans.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Cairo and the Ghana Year
clips.summary: In summer 1962 Tillow traveled with the local NAACP to SNCC demonstrations in Cairo, Illinois, where she met Mary McCollum, who had a deep gash from a demonstration. She then spent a junior year abroad in Ghana, witnessed W.E.B. Du Bois receive an honorary degree, and learned about decolonization from Conor Cruise O'Brien's To Katanga and Back.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Atlanta Delegation and Hattiesburg
clips.summary: Back at the University of Illinois in fall 1963, Tillow joined her NAACP chapter's semester-break delegation to Atlanta, where she heard Prathia Hall and James Forman. The group traveled to Hattiesburg, Mississippi during voter registration demonstrations. Bob Moses had been jailed, and a white movement worker had been put in with white prisoners and beaten beyond recognition.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Highlander and the Bradens
clips.summary: Tillow describes meeting Walter Tillow at Highlander Folk School around 1964, where she encountered Carl and Anne Braden and James Dombrowski. The Bradens shared The Southern Patriot and mentored young SNCC workers, drawing connections to the 1930s Southern Conference for Human Welfare. Tillow credits Carl Braden with persuading SNCC to refuse the liberal anti-communism of Hubert H. Humphrey.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Eastern Kentucky Coal Miners
clips.summary: Tillow worked through 1964 with the Appalachian Committee for Full Employment among Eastern Kentucky coal miners who had been jailed on a federal conspiracy charge for trying to revive the miners' union. She edited a newspaper she recalls as Kentucky Jobs with Justice and joined the March on Frankfort, Kentucky for Jobs and Freedom on March 5, 1964.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Local 1199 in Pennsylvania
clips.summary: Tillow describes leaving the United Electrical, Radio and Machine Workers in 1969 to work for National Union of Hospital and Health Care Employees, Local 1199. She organized in Lewistown, Wilkes-Barre, Spangler, Huntingdon, Canonsburg and other rural Pennsylvania hospitals, winning units that were majority-white against employer campaigns that branded 1199 a black union. Coretta Scott King supported the Johns Hopkins campaign in Baltimore.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Pittsburgh Hospital Strikes
clips.summary: Tillow describes the 1969-70 1199 campaign in Pittsburgh where the National Labor Relations Act did not yet cover hospital workers. After the 1970 Presbyterian Hospital strike, Governor Milton Shapp's collective-bargaining legislation added hospital workers, opening Pennsylvania to organizing. A Christmas Eve 1969 sit-in at the Jewish Home and Hospital, led by Henry Nicholas, won a secret-ballot election overseen by a rabbi from Rodef Shalom Congregation.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Family, FBI, and Movement Foundations
clips.summary: Tillow recalls her parents' opposition to her leaving school before graduation and their distress when the FBI visited them about her anti-war activity. She situates the anti-war movement, labor organizing, and civil rights work as a single moral project, citing Fannie Lou Hamer at the 1964 Democratic convention and Diane Nash's response to the Justice Department about the Freedom Rides.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: CLUW and Pay Equity
clips.summary: Tillow served as president of the Pittsburgh chapter of the Coalition of Labor Union Women, founded at the March 23-24, 1974 convention in Detroit. She describes the difficulty of getting recognition at the Allegheny County Central Labor Council and how CLUW chapters became focal points for women raising civil rights, anti-war, and union issues.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: HR 676 and Single Payer
clips.summary: Tillow describes her current work on the All Unions Committee for Single Payer Health Care behind John Conyers's HR 676, which would remove the for-profit insurance industry from healthcare. She cites Adewale Troutman and David Satcher on 83,570 excess deaths in the African American community attributable to healthcare disparities, and notes that the 1965 Medicare law desegregated southern hospitals by conditioning payment on integration.

clips.subject: Kay Tillow
clips.title: Voting Rights and Movement Legacy
clips.summary: Tillow connects the threats to voting rights from Shelby County v. Holder to the unfinished labor and healthcare agenda. She reflects on what she gained from the movement, the importance of expanding Social Security and Medicare, and the use of anti-immigrant politics to divide working people. She closes by emphasizing that movements are built from many ordinary people doing their parts.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Sitting on the Mall, Opening Question
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier introduces the joint interview with Dorie Ann Ladner and Dr. Joyce Ann Ladner at the Library of Congress and frames an opening question about the ambivalence and complicated feeling they carried about the 1963 March on Washington.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Under Siege on the Front Line
clips.summary: Dorie describes the ambivalence of coming to the March while the movement was under siege: Medgar Evers killed June 12, his funeral June 16, Lawrence Guyot beaten, Fannie Lou Hamer jailed and beaten in Winona, a Jackson store boycott, and arrests in Greenwood. The sisters knew they had to return to those towns.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Ruby Doris Sends Joyce to Harlem
clips.summary: Joyce recounts learning of Medgar Evers's death by phone from Ruby Doris Smith Robinson while at a student conference in Lake Forest, Illinois. Ruby Doris persuaded her to go to New York to work under Bayard Rustin's direction at the Harlem March headquarters, where Joyce raised money, bringing back enough to run another bus each trip.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Bob Dylan and the Statler-Hilton
clips.summary: Joyce remembers late New York nights with Bob Dylan strumming on the sofa and seeing Malcolm X speak on 125th Street, then traveling to Washington to stay at the Statler-Hilton, the March hotel. Early the next morning the group picketed the Justice Department over Don Harris, John Perdew, and Ralph Allen, jailed for sedition in Americus, Georgia.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Lena Horne Yields the Interview
clips.summary: On the Lincoln Memorial platform, Lena Horne declined a television interview and led nineteen-year-old Joyce by the hand up a scaffold to Nancy Dickerson, telling her to interview the young woman who lived in the South. Joyce recalls Josephine Baker, Marlon Brando, and her mother later reporting that the neighbors in Hattiesburg had seen her on TV.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Look White Men in the Eye
clips.summary: Joyce recalls her mother teaching them at four and five to look white men in the eye and never look down, and the family mantra that they were as good as anybody. She then describes the on-platform crisis over John Lewis's speech, with James Forman and Courtland Cox, and SNCC's anger that an authority figure presumed to change their words.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: De Lawd and Breaking an Area
clips.summary: The sisters explain their ambivalent relationship with Martin Luther King, whom SNCC called De Lawd, contrasting his arrival at the crisis stage with SNCC's work of breaking an area: staying under the toughest conditions until a local community let them in. They left the day after the March, without lasting euphoria.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Bob Moses Drives to Birmingham
clips.summary: Three weeks after the March, Bob Moses drove a school bus of SNCC workers to Birmingham for the funeral of the four girls killed in the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing. Joyce reflects that the killing of children struck deeply, raising the fear that if they could do that to a child they could kill her brothers.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Stomach Illness and the Holiday Inn
clips.summary: Dorie describes the stress-induced stomach illness she developed in Natchez, going to bed not expecting to wake up, with neighbors leaving phones off the hook for safety. Frustrated trying to bond out arrested demonstrators, she took a cab to the segregated Holiday Inn and paid the cashier to summon the chief of police.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Rage at the Masonic Temple
clips.summary: The day after Medgar Evers was killed, Dorie ran up to two white police officers on motorcycles outside the Masonic Temple, screaming at them in a rage she cannot fully describe. They sat with heads bowed and never responded, and only late in life has she come to think they practiced non-violence with her.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The Beckwith Trials and a Volkswagen Escape
clips.summary: Dorie attended both Beckwith trials, watched Ross Barnett shake the defendant's hand, and saw the Klan give Beckwith a standing ovation. She recounts district attorney William Waller's openly racist jury selection, being ejected by the judge, and escaping a mob with Dave Dennis and Mattie Bivens in a Volkswagen bus offered by a passerby.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Guilt, Courage, and a Black Scarf
clips.summary: Joyce describes the guilt she carried in graduate school while Dorie stayed in dangerous Natchez, and her conviction that courage was acting in spite of fear. She recalls covering her white roommate Joan Trumpauer's blonde hair with a black scarf and putting her on the floor on the unlit Hanging Moss Road to Tougaloo.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The House and Mr. McCarthy's Still
clips.summary: Asked to describe their home, the sisters detail the three-bedroom Palmers Crossing house with its front porch and the fenceless compound shared with grandparents and great aunt and uncle. They recount Mr. Joe McCarthy's bootleg whiskey still and the Friday-evening Mississippi National Guard raid that broke crates of liquor all night.
related_events: Mississippi National Guard raid on bootleg operation

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Camp Shelby, the Crossing, and Miss Bama
clips.summary: The sisters explain how their community served as a recreational outpost for Black soldiers from Camp Shelby, with the Inkwell Club and Club Desire drawing Chitlin' Circuit entertainers. They describe Palmers Crossing itself, the City of New Orleans train that brought the Chicago Defender, the Hudson grocery that became Hudson's salvage stores, and Miss Bama's house.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Mother Met Them on the Porch
clips.summary: The sisters describe how their mother, divorced and for a time a single parent, told all her girls to get an education because no one could take it from them. They watched her meet insurance collectors on the front porch and never let them inside or treat her as inferior, modeling a no-nonsense pride.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Grandpa Ben Said We're As Good As Anybody
clips.summary: The sisters trace their resistance ethos to a Creek Indian woman named Mary and a white man, Ben Willard of Rockingham, North Carolina, who would not enslave his own children. The mantra that they were as good as anybody passed through the generations. Their entrepreneur uncles refused to work for whites, illustrated by an uncle who walked away from a racist customer's broken TV.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Coining the Emmett Till Generation
clips.summary: Joyce describes coining the term Emmett Till generation for post-war Black Southern youth whose parents, aunts, and uncles told them they would change things. She recalls Uncle Archie, a World War I veteran who served in France, telling them their generation would change things, and the Baptist liturgy they translated into the movement's songs and mass meetings.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Dr. McCloud and the Censored World
clips.summary: The sisters recall the herbalist they called Dr. McCloud, a Hattiesburg NAACP member who brought them the Pittsburgh Courier, Chicago Defender, Jet, and Ebony each week. In a censored Mississippi with one TV station that called Black people slurs on screen, this outside reading material fueled their curiosity and showed them how others lived.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Meeting Medgar and the Kennard Frame-Up
clips.summary: The sisters met Medgar Evers in high school when he came to set up the Hattiesburg NAACP youth chapter, with C.E. Clack and Vernon Dahmer as the motivating advisors and Dahmer's sister Hallean Beard, the church pianist, as Dorie's best friend and chaperone. They recall watching the frame-up of Clyde Kennard, jailed on a moonshine charge and held over a year.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Powerless Teenagers Clipping the News
clips.summary: Looking back as fourteen and fifteen year olds who knew things were wrong but felt powerless, the sisters describe running to Hudson's for the Hattiesburg American at 4:15 each day during the Emmett Till case. Joyce clipped the articles; Dorie, wanting to be a lawyer, went to her teacher Mr. Clark and then memorized the 13th, 14th, and 15th Amendments for her own empowerment.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Why the Elders Followed Teenagers
clips.summary: The sisters marvel that older people followed nineteen-year-olds, crediting it to taking themselves seriously and carrying a message the community had waited to hear. They recall getting waitresses and sharecroppers to leave their jobs and risk their lives at the Indianola courthouse where Fannie Lou Hamer registered in August 1962, and the Jet photograph of Emmett Till as the clarion call for their generation.
related_events: Fannie Lou Hamer voter registration August 1962

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Mack Charles Parker and the Racism Unleashed
clips.summary: Joyce recalls the second lynching they experienced, that of Mack Charles Parker in Poplarville, fifteen minutes from Hattiesburg, when international media came to the city and her mother pulled the boys close before dark. She reflects that such killings emboldened local racists to talk openly, and that the perpetrators lived in the community where everyone knew them.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Founding COFO and the Night Drive
clips.summary: Dorie recounts the August 1962 founding of COFO in Clarksdale, her first time in the Delta. After a curfew violation, Dave Dennis was nearly jailed and the group drove in fear through the night, told that one of Emmett Till's killers was a night watchman in Ruleville, searching for Amzie Moore's house in Cleveland. The terror pushed her to commit to Delta organizing.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: One Foot in College, Dorie Leaves Home
clips.summary: Joyce explains the difference between them: she kept one foot in college while Dorie dropped out, saying she could not stay in school while people suffered. Dorie recounts leaving home with a small orange suitcase, telling her mother on the way out the door that she was going to Jackson to work with Bob Moses for her freedom, and arriving at the Freedom House on Rose Street.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Jackson State and the Tougaloo Nine
clips.summary: The sisters recall James Meredith as a clear-headed strategist at Jackson State before he enrolled at Ole Miss, and demonstrating on behalf of the Tougaloo Nine, where they were tear-gassed. They describe crossing the street every Wednesday to talk with Medgar Evers about their freedom, and his bringing Dorie money sent by their mother.
related_events: James Meredith Ole Miss enrollment September 1962

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The Prayer That Got Them Expelled
clips.summary: Advised by Medgar Evers to act on campus, Dorie as dorm-council president called on Joyce to pray, and Joyce spoke of dark days ahead. The dorm matron reported the prayer to Dean Oscar Rogers, the sisters grew combative defending it, and after a march for the Tougaloo students and an early spring break, they and student-government president Walter Williams were expelled.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The Freedom House and the Freedom Riders
clips.summary: Joyce describes the Jackson Freedom House, initially rented by the Freedom Riders, where Lawrence Guyot and Cole Ladell cooked for the group. She recalls first meeting the Freedom Riders upstairs at the Masonic Temple by Medgar's office, Tom Hayden, Charles McDew, Marion Barry, John Lewis, and Dion Diamond, and feeling she had been looking for them since she was fourteen.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Judge Cox and the Successor
clips.summary: Dorie recalls being ordered out of a Freedom Rider trial by the judge, and the sisters describe US District Judge William Harold Cox referring to them as slurs in the courtroom. At the 50th Freedom Rider reunion, Joyce met the Black man who had replaced Cox on the bench, a remarkable turn fifty years later.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Cousin Victoria Gray, Teacher and Confidant
clips.summary: The sisters describe their cousin Victoria Gray, beautiful and a model of going away to college at Wilberforce, who lived in Germany and returned exotic. She became active first with SCLC's Citizenship Education Program and then SNCC, working in Hattiesburg with Guyot and Frank Smith. They remember her as a teacher and confidant whose death still pains them.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Building the MFDP With Ella Baker
clips.summary: The sisters explain the two levels of movement projects, strategic voter registration and events pushed to the national stage, and the building of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party through freedom ballots, precinct, district, and state conventions. They credit Ella Baker as the leader who taught them to listen and guided them to consensus with an unseen hand.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The Sojourner Fleet to Atlantic City
clips.summary: Joyce recounts driving to Atlantic City in the Sojourner Fleet of cars, named by Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, stopping at Cleve Sellers's parents' home in Denmark, South Carolina. She describes the burned-out station wagon from the Schwerner-Chaney-Goodman murders displayed on the boardwalk and the movement's tactile way of showing people what segregation was like.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The WATS Line Goes Silent
clips.summary: Dorie had seen Schwerner, Chaney, and Goodman leaving Miami University in Oxford, Ohio to return to Mississippi after the church burning. Joyce describes the moment word came that they had not checked in on the WATS line and the staff knew they were dead. The sisters blame the federal government for not intervening and reflect on the movement's walking wounded.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The Natchez Bombing and Little Richard
clips.summary: Dorie recounts a Klan attempt to bomb the Natchez SNCC office that hit the wrong house, an integrated cafe belonging to Jake Fishman, and how Mr. Archie Curtis the funeral director escorted her through the mob to retrieve the voter-registration cards. She describes quieting a harassing late-night caller by promising her a Little Richard concert ticket.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Greenwood, Police Dogs, and John Doar
clips.summary: Dorie recalls working all day in the Greenwood SNCC office, learning it was bombed that night, and cleaning up the files the next morning before going right back out. At the Leflore County Courthouse, police dogs chased the group and a young Marian Wright, who had interned at the Justice Department, called John Doar from a pay phone.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Terrorism, the Back Seat, and PTSD
clips.summary: Dorie reflects that the word terrorism does not faze her because she lived through churches and homes bombed beyond counting. Unable to drive, she always sat in the back seat so another could take the wheel if the driver was shot, recalling Jimmy Travis shot in 1963. She describes how some memories flash across her mind like PTSD, and how Joyce sublimated trauma into driven work.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Dahmer's Death and Mrs. Turnbow's Pistol
clips.summary: Joyce, who later worked in a Level 1 trauma unit, names her worst movement moment: the 6 a.m. call from her mother in January 1966 that Vernon Dahmer was killed, after years of repression broke through. She recalls lobbying the Oregon delegation with Representative Edith Green and Senator Wayne Morse alongside Mrs. Hartman Turnbow, who carried a pistol in a brown paper bag.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Genealogy After the Records Opened
clips.summary: Dorie describes her genealogy quest after Mississippi opened its slave records to Black researchers in 1992, drawn by her mother's family being visibly different, malato and Indian. Through cross-referenced research, others studying the same great-great-grandfather connected her to white Willard descendants in Georgia, Alabama, and London, leading to reunions in Mobile, Alabama.
related_events: Mississippi archives opened to Black researchers 1992

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The Willard and Mary Line
clips.summary: Dorie lays out the traced maternal line: Benjamin F. Willard, born 1805 in Rockingham, North Carolina, and Mary, a Black Creek Indian also born 1805, who came together to the Alabama-Mississippi territory and had thirteen children. The line runs through Frank (1825), Jerry Willard (about 1870), Joseph Collins Willard and Martha Gates, to their mother, born May 14, 1921 and died January 23, 1995.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: The White Side Resists
clips.summary: The sisters reflect that they were not surprised by the white side of the family, though those relatives were. They describe the Willard descendants who deny the connection and the large Creole Favre family in Louisiana, which includes Brett Favre and Jimmy Buffett and holds integrated reunions, who decided early to stay in the white race rather than the Black race.

clips.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
clips.title: Teach the History, and Closing
clips.summary: Asked what part of the civil rights narrative most needs attention, Joyce answers that the schools must teach the history so that elementary, high school, and college students know what happened, countering revisionists. The interview closes with thanks and the Library of Congress and Smithsonian sign-off.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Pass Christian Origins
clips.summary: Guyot describes Pass Christian, Mississippi, as the least Mississippi-like part of the state: heavy Catholic influence, Ingalls Shipyard labor unions, and a 100,000 white / 19,000 Black population. He recounts the Catholic Church's 1957-58 decision to desegregate schools by giving parishioners a choice between excommunication and integration, and the Gaston Hughes slot-machine incident in which Bidwell Adams (Bilbo-era Lieutenant Governor) shielded him from prosecution at age 17.
related_events: 1957-58 Catholic Church desegregation of Mississippi parish schools
Bidwell Adams intervention with prosecutor Gaston Hughes

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Family and Political Inheritance
clips.summary: Guyot describes his grandfather's brother Lewis Pierce, chairman of the Hancock County Republican Party for 65 years, who lived to 105 and who once told Guyot in a WPA interview, 'I just hope that blacks will be able to fight their way into the Democratic Party.' He describes his grandfather's habit of repeating a word to gain thinking time, listening to FDR and Ronald Reagan on the radio together, and his father's confrontations including beating a wealthy Pass Christian man in front of the post office.
related_events: WPA Federal Writers' Project interview of Lewis Pierce

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Tougaloo College
clips.summary: Guyot describes Tougaloo as an oasis of academic freedom in Mississippi, threatened with extinction by the state legislature but committed to interracial faculty and leadership development. He recalls meeting Rev. Ed King, David Broder, Ernst Borinski (a Jewish refugee from Germany), and A.A. Branch of the YMCA, who later signed the COFO papers joining the National Council of Churches and United Church of Christ in challenging WLBT-TV. He names Dorie and Joyce Ladner and Reuben Anderson (later a Mississippi Supreme Court justice) as classmates.
related_events: WLBT-TV license challenge by United Church of Christ
Mississippi state legislature threats against Tougaloo

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Meeting SNCC and Fannie Lou Hamer
clips.summary: Guyot recounts encountering SNCC through Bob Moses, John Doar, and Marion Barry at 714 Rose Street in Jackson, alongside James Bevel, Diane Nash, the Ladner sisters, and LeVon Brown. He describes traveling to Amzie Moore's house in the Delta and the Ruleville mass meeting where he, Bevel, Charles McLaurin, and James Forman first encountered Fannie Lou Hamer, who 'raises her hand' to register. He characterizes Hamer as a religious fundamentalist with perfect speech timing who could weave three issues and 'collapse them in 25 words.'

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Atlantic City and the MFDP
clips.summary: Guyot describes Hamer's 1964 Democratic Convention testimony, Lyndon Johnson's press-conference interruption broadcast in full by ABC and CBS that evening, the 1,200 telegrams demanding the Freedom Democratic Party be seated, and Johnson's pressure tactics against California and New York delegates. He recounts the MFDP's rejection of the two-seat compromise as 'the most courageous, pragmatic political decision in American politics' and notes Bayard Rustin's later 'From Protest to Politics' article in Commentary magazine.
related_events: Fannie Lou Hamer credentials committee testimony

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Greenwood Voter Registration
clips.summary: Guyot describes the Greenwood, Mississippi, voter-registration push: organizers were jailed but the Black community brought 100 people to deliver food to the jail, and 500 came down to register. He recounts an exchange with segregationist lawyer Hardy Lott in the Leflore County Courthouse, John Doar's filing of an injunction-pending-appeal in the John Hardy case in Walthall County, and the Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals strategy that culminated in the Supreme Court's broadening of Section 5 standing in the 1965 private-action ruling.
related_events: United States v. Mississippi Supreme Court case

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Winona Beating
clips.summary: Guyot recounts the June 1963 Winona beating: Hamer, Annelle Ponder, James West, and June Johnson were arrested returning from a South Carolina meeting. Guyot called to confirm their detention, drove to the jail, and was himself beaten almost to death by twelve men who threatened to burn his testicles, charged him with murder, and pressured him to sign a false statement. Medgar Evers was assassinated during his detention. Andrew Young, James Bevel, and Frank Smith eventually secured his release; he credits Roberta Galler's telephone calls and Evers's death with saving the group's lives.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: NAACP, SCLC, and the Congressional Challenge
clips.summary: Guyot contrasts the three civil rights organizations' Mississippi operations: Roy Wilkins's NAACP wrote off the state as a 'beast'; SCLC was operationally distinct from Martin Luther King personally, who backed MFDP throughout including the 1964 Freedom Election (80,000 votes), the Summer Project, the Freedom Schools, Dorothy Height's Wednesdays in Mississippi, and the 1965 Congressional Challenge. Guyot credits Michael Thelwell with shaping the leadership conference debates that strengthened the Voting Rights Act's trigger mechanism, and cites David J. Garrow's argument that MFDP's congressional pressure had greater impact on the Act than King.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: The Last Mobilizing Generation
clips.summary: Bond and Guyot discuss whether their generation was the last to engage in sustained mass mobilization. Guyot rejects exceptionalism but cites the children's defense fund, operation understanding, cultural leadership, and Bob Moses's Algebra Project / Young People's Project as the inheritors. He argues that if Republicans gain congressional control everything will be irretrievable, urges renewed organizing in the two years ahead, and contrasts those who made change with those who wrote about it.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: The Summer Project and Freedom House
clips.summary: Guyot describes a typical 1964 week organizing across Hattiesburg, Jackson, and Greenwood at $10 a week. He explains the Delta Ministry's origins from a 1963 Hattiesburg meeting and its 25-year continuity after SNCC left Mississippi in 1966. He traces the broader alliance: Children's Defense Fund (Marian Wright Edelman) hiring 3,000 in Mississippi against Senator John C. Stennis's opposition; the Lawyer's Committee for Civil Rights Under Law including William Kunstler and Arthur Kinoy operating under SNCC direction; and the 1963 Howard University conference featuring Bayard Rustin, James Baldwin, and Norman Thomas. He describes the 714 Rose Street Freedom House as a reconciliation post for Freedom Riders tried in Jackson.
related_events: Lawyer's Committee for Civil Rights Under Law

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: 1968 Convention and D.C. Politics
clips.summary: Guyot recounts the 1968 Chicago Democratic Convention: the loyalists led by Aaron Henry expected automatic seating, but Bidwell Adams chairing the state convention warned the regulars to desegregate. The loyalists merged with MFDP at 50-50 and were seated. In Washington, Guyot worked with Harry Bowie and met Charles Evers and Hodding Carter III. He moved to D.C. in 1968, becoming an Advisory Neighborhood Commissioner, fighting for same-sex marriage, term-limit repeal, and the first presidential primary, and developing the Columbia Heights economic-development infrastructure. He explains his support of Adrian Fenty after Fenty's 2010 primary defeat as a continuation of the religion-politics fusion central to his work.

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
