clips.title: Names from the Movement
clips.summary: Bond asks Guyot to characterize key figures: John Salter Jr. (Tougaloo teacher, NAACP-led Jackson demonstrations alongside Medgar Evers and Ed King); Medgar Evers (NAACP executive secretary, Emmett Till investigator); Tom Gaither (CORE in Mississippi); Carl Braden (leftist who bought food at the Freedom House); John Doar (Republican Assistant Attorney General, Schwerner-Chaney-Goodman prosecution, illiterate-voter advocacy); Gerald Ford (congressman approached through his bishop); Wednesdays in Mississippi (Dorothy Height); the Ladner sisters (Hattiesburg, related to Vernon Dahmer, Medgar Evers, Victoria Gray); Theron Lynd (Forrest County registrar, defendant in Count Them One By One); the Fifth Circuit judges Brown, Tuttle, and Bell (Eisenhower appointees); Michael Thelwell (MFDP D.C. office); the Hartman Turnbow self-defense case; and Robert Moses (mathematician, philosopher, McComb organizer).
related_events: Theron Lynd Fifth Circuit contempt trial

clips.subject: Lawrence Guyot
clips.title: Legacy and Final Reflections
clips.summary: Guyot states that he wants to be remembered as a man who carried out his religious beliefs through political activity, who never stepped away from a fight, and who is committed to perpetuating the SNCC mantra. He compares SNCC only to the Anti-Saloon League in effectiveness, arguing SNCC changed the very concepts of democracy and citizenship. He closes urging spread of organizing by geometric progression in the next two years and declaring non-organizers 'social pariahs' the community can no longer afford to function on behalf of.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Early Life in Port Gibson and Vicksburg
clips.summary: Guster was born March 26, 1936 in Port Gibson, Mississippi to a single mother who left for Vicksburg when Guster was six and later brought her there. She attended schools in Vicksburg through the fifth grade at Magnolia, then transferred for higher grades because of how the school grades were structured.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Fair Street and Chicago Years
clips.summary: Guster moved to Port Gibson in 1953, describes the busy Fair Street (then called Negro Street) with cafes like Peaches and the social life of young Black Port Gibson. She moved to Chicago, worked at Montgomery Ward briefly until illness, then did day work, until returning to Port Gibson at age 19.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Marriage to Harry Guster
clips.summary: Returning from Chicago on a three-week vacation in 1959, Guster married contractor Harry Guster, who was 24 years older and the head lumber contractor in Claiborne County. She negotiated his divorce and the support arrangements for his son, and they bought the house at $2,500 with money from his sister Mrs. Akon.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Working for Hillary and Movement Awakening
clips.summary: Guster worked as a domestic for Hillary, the daughter of her employer Mr. Walk-Cut, who had moved from Mansfield, Ohio. While working there, Guster began driving each evening to McFatter's drugstore where Rudy Shields and federal voter-registration observers gathered, and she canvassed throughout Claiborne County. Hillary eventually fired her saying she was tired of the voter-registration activity.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Rocky Springs Voter-Registration Confrontation
clips.summary: Guster drove Rudy Shields in her 1965 Pontiac Catalina through Claiborne County canvassing for voter registration. At Rocky Springs the white landowner met them with a shotgun on his pickup truck; after the encounter the landowner trailed them and parked beside a one-way bridge before letting them pass. Plantation residents were often too afraid to register, and Guster describes wearing out a set of tires driving the canvassing routes.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Hosting Movement Workers and Mass Meetings
clips.summary: Guster and her husband hosted civil rights lawyers and the African civil rights worker Tucki Tate, sometimes three at a time. Lily Mae Williams was NAACP chairperson and Mrs. Wells served as cold chair. Guster led the youth singing freedom songs including 'Ain't Gonna Let Nobody Turn Me Around,' 'Oh Freedom,' and 'He's Got the Whole World in His Hands,' meeting Tuesday nights at the church.
related_events: civil rights legal teams in Mississippi

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: KKK Threats and Armed Defense
clips.summary: Before a planned Charles Evers banquet at the Guster home, a caller identifying himself as KKK asked Guster if she was tired of living and threatened to burn the house and the Memphis house where Rudy Shields was staying. The household kept armed watch that night; Rudy Shields slept in the back of the house with shotguns. Guster describes nearly firing her own pistol through the mattress in fear, and her husband emerging convinced she had killed herself.
related_events: Klan intimidation of Port Gibson NAACP

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Boycott, Claiborne Hardware Case, and Deacons
clips.summary: Guster describes the Port Gibson boycott of white merchants, the Deacons for Defense organizing meetings under an oak tree on her property, and her disagreement with the second boycott extending to merchants who had complied. She testified in NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware against Allen Motors employee Buster Barber, recalling Mr. Eddie Walls' and Mr. Dan Curry's testimony when the case reached the U.S. Supreme Court in 1982.

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Political Organizing and Black Candidates
clips.summary: Guster discusses the first Black candidates elected to Claiborne County office, her preference for Thomas Russell over Theo Black, and her conviction that Black political success required cross-racial coalitions. She names Mr. Williams as a backbone of Black political organizing in Port Gibson and reflects that Russell would not accept conditional white-establishment endorsements.
related_events: Black electoral politics in post-1965 Mississippi

clips.subject: Leesco Guster
clips.title: Mural, Mr. Nate, and Reflection
clips.summary: Kenneth Ross asked Guster to provide names for the Port Gibson Civil Rights mural; she gave him ten male names but said the women she knew mainly talked at meetings. Her own face was added to the mural without her knowledge, possibly by Mr. Nate. She credits Nate Jones with preserving her movement history and reflects on what young people should learn: speak up and work, do not just talk.
related_events: Port Gibson civil rights mural project

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Tuscaloosa Childhood and Segregation
clips.summary: Born in Birmingham in 1941, raised in Tuscaloosa where her parents ran Caldwell Electric Company. Recounts a Black employee never invited to her parents' annual picnic, attending segregated schools, and being raised in a university town with limited racial awareness until after her 1959 marriage.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Montgomery and the Freedom Rides
clips.summary: After marriage in 1959, moved to Montgomery where Millard (Dean) Fuller and Morris Dees built Fuller & Dees Marketing. Describes their peripheral Klan-adjacent political work speaking for MacDonald Gallion and subscribing to The Fiery Cross, then their pivot after witnessing Freedom Riders beaten at the Montgomery bus station and the retaliatory office break-in with typewriters smashed.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Selma to Montgomery Marchers in Their Home
clips.summary: Hosted Northern pastors as Selma to Montgomery March participants at their home in 1965, receiving nasty phone calls from neighbors after license plates were photographed. Recounts the marriage crisis that led them to leave Montgomery and give away their wealth.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: New York, Tougaloo, and Recommitment
clips.summary: Dr. Howard A. Durgan of Broadway United Church of Christ, on Tougaloo College's board, recruited Millard to raise money for a new Tougaloo dormitory. Family relocated to Montclair, New Jersey for two years. Describes the Huntingdon College experience, the climate at Glen Ridge and Montclair, and continued reflection on race.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Moving to Koinonia Farm
clips.summary: Summer 1968 move to Koinonia Farm in Sumter County, Georgia to work with Clarence Jordan. Describes Koinonia at low ebb with few residents, the Atlanta meeting between Millard and Clarence that produced the Koinonia Partnership concept, and the partnership-housing model: no profit, no interest, simple cinder-block houses sold to Black families denied bank loans.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Africa and the Founding of Habitat
clips.summary: Three-year family relocation to Zaire (then Congo) to test the partnership-housing model internationally, building 120 houses with a Lilly Foundation grant for concrete. Return to Americus and the founding of Habitat for Humanity International in a chicken-barn office at Koinonia, with growth eventually to 100 countries and over a million people housed.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Koinonia Daily Life and Town Hostility
clips.summary: Describes Koinonia's simple labor-board housing, the apartment above Clarence and Florence Jordan's first structure, hippie-era volunteers arriving from the North, mother's despair at the modest kitchen, and the experience of being shunned in Americus grocery stores when residents learned she lived at Koinonia. Recounts the historical violence: Conrad Browne arrested walking down the street sharing peanuts with a Black resident.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Threats Against Koinonia Black Families
clips.summary: Recounts the threats that drove most Black families away from Koinonia in the 1950s, including the firebombing of Gussie Jackson's house. Describes the Unadilla, Georgia incident where the Fullers were arrested for visiting a Black family supporting a son's effort to integrate the white high school, then chased by a van of men to their motel.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Koinonia Nonviolence and Schools
clips.summary: Koinonia residents took turns sitting at the road at night to demonstrate they were not afraid as cars drove by shooting; bullet holes remained from a near-miss of one of the Jordan daughters. Children's schools transitioned from Plains High to the integrated Americus schools after return from Africa, with Faith Fuller in class with Amy Carter.

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: First Baptist Americus and Collins McGee
clips.summary: Recounts the December 1965 visit to Americus where the Fullers, Clarence Jordan, Allen and Carol Henry, and Collins McGee attempted to attend a Christmas service at First Baptist Church Americus where Allen Henry's father was preaching. The fire-chief deacon demanded McGee leave; the integrated group walked out together. Clarence remarked on the church's outside floodlights versus its dark interior.
related_events: First Baptist Americus integration attempt December 1965

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Americus Black Boycott and Death-Penalty Law
clips.summary: Describes the 1971 Americus Black-led economic boycott and integrated march demanding hiring of Black police officers. Millard (Dean) Fuller spoke at a Black church and counseled the crowd; he then took death-penalty cases representing Black defendants facing all-white juries, working with Donald Hollowell of Atlanta. Recounts Ethel Dunning whose white employer cut off her water when she moved into a Koinonia partnership house, and her response: 'I baked those cakes... I had to show them that I was bigger than how I was being treated.'

clips.subject: Linda Fuller Degelmann
clips.title: Faith, Family, and the Fuller Center
clips.summary: Reflects on family rejection from both her side and Millard's after they gave away their wealth; her sister in California was the exception. Discusses Morris Dees's parallel trajectory to the Southern Poverty Law Center starting with Alabama State Patrol integration. Closes with reflection on civil rights as a template for other movements, her children's involvement in Habitat campus chapters and the Fuller Center for Housing founded in 2005, and a son's work for Saudi Aramco built on international upbringing.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Suburban New Jersey Beginnings
clips.summary: Todd describes her childhood as the daughter of a corporate lawyer father (Carl Anderson) and a psychiatric social worker mother who grew up poor in Hibbing, Minnesota. She characterizes her household as moderate Republican but oriented toward public service, with no direct exposure to local Black residents in Summit, New Jersey.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: 1963 Tougaloo Work Camp
clips.summary: Todd recounts the Ecumenical Voluntary Service work camp at Tougaloo College in summer 1963, including nonviolence workshops, speakers at the Social Science Lab, and a visit to the SNCC office in Greenwood at Bob Moses's invitation. She meets Fannie Lou Hamer in Ruleville, hears the account of the Winona beating, and attends a mass meeting at Williams Chapel Church.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Greensboro AFSC Project
clips.summary: Todd describes the second 1963 work camp, an American Friends Service Committee voter-registration project in Greensboro, North Carolina, where 18 volunteers lived in a church basement and registered 803 new voters in cooperation with local registrars. She contrasts the relative freedom of Greensboro with the surveillance and danger of Mississippi.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Tougaloo Speakers and Jackson Context
clips.summary: Todd recalls nonviolence workshops at Tougaloo, meeting Dave Dennis of CORE, Memphis Norman, Rev. Ed King, Jane Schutt, Timothy Jenkins, and Allan Knight Chalmers. She describes the post-Evers shift in Jackson from direct action to voter registration, the NAACP's decision to stop posting bail, and the tension between national NAACP and the local North Jackson youth branch working with John Salter Jr. and Rev. Ed King.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Cornell and the Civil Rights Act
clips.summary: Todd describes returning to Cornell for her senior year transformed by the 1963 summer. She works with Cornell United Religious Work under Paul Gibbons to lobby for passage of the Civil Rights Act, takes Robin M. Williams Jr.'s race-relations sociology course, joins a sorority that pledged Jewish students against national policy, and travels to Washington over spring vacation to observe Senate debate.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Applying to Freedom Summer
clips.summary: Todd applies to SNCC in fall 1963 after correspondence with Walter Tillow, is accepted without an interview based on her 1963 record, and arrives late at the Oxford, Ohio orientation following a friend's wedding. She recalls Bob Moses's overalls-and-t-shirt address, Vincent Harding's remarks, Bruce Hanson on internalized prejudice, and Carl Fleming of Newsweek participating in nonviolence role-play.
related_events: disappearance of Chaney, Schwerner, and Goodman

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Greenville Canvassing and Freedom Registration
clips.summary: Todd is assigned to Greenville, Mississippi for voter registration under project directors George Rawick (first month) and then Muriel Tillinghast, while believing Charles E. Cobb Jr. was project director for Washington, Issaquena, and Sharkey counties. She lives first with Mrs. Harden in the south end and then with drugstore owners Jerry and Lamar Britton on Nelson Street. She canvasses with Pam Trotman and local men Charles Moore and James Williams, collecting MFDP Freedom Registration signatures.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: MFDP Delegate Selection in Washington County
clips.summary: Todd organizes a precinct meeting and attends the Washington County MFDP convention, where she watches local Democratic Party operative Mr. Chappell maneuver to get traditional leaders elected as county delegates. She attends the second congressional district caucus in Greenville, where Fannie Lou Hamer led the singing at Friendship Baptist Church, but is not present at the state convention in Jackson.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Movement Discussions and the Hodding Carter Pool
clips.summary: Todd describes growing internal tensions: SNCC staff who voiced anger and questioned nonviolence, two demonstrations at the federal building for federal protection following the disappearance of the three workers, and arguments over whether COFO Jackson had to approve local demonstrations. She also recounts the project's swim at Hodding Carter's pool, arranged by Dorothy Jones, which ended when neighbors arrived on horseback.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Atlantic City: MFDP Challenge
clips.summary: Todd and Pat Vail drive to Atlantic City and operate as freelance lobbyists from Union Baptist Temple Church, missing Fannie Lou Hamer's televised credentials-committee testimony but witnessing the Tuesday afternoon meeting after the symbolic two-seat offer. She describes the Tuesday-night delegate vote against the offer, Joseph L. Rauh Jr.'s dual role, Walter Reuther's late arrival and pressure on the Mondale subcommittee, Hubert Humphrey's role, the Diggs list of credentials-committee supporters that was overnight-flipped, and her later research using LBJ tapes and Anne Romaine's oral histories.

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Law School and 1965 Mississippi Return
clips.summary: Todd returns to New Jersey, helps establish the Herbert Lee Memorial Community Center fundraising effort with Dr. Matthew Page and Charles Moore, then drives to Stanford Law School and lives with Karen Davis. After being reassigned away from the Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law for sounding too militant, she clerks for the National Lawyers Guild in Tougaloo with Claudia Shropshire Morcom in summer 1965, evaluating Title III public-facilities litigation in Madison County, Greenwood, Greenville, and Jackson.
related_events: Herbert Lee Memorial Community Center founding

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Government Contracts Career
clips.summary: Todd describes her post-1967 career: legal services applications, the United Planning Organization under executive director Wiley A. Branton Sr., private practice with Trovon Bower and Morgan, Lewis & Bockius, and selection as an administrative judge on the NASA Board of Contract Appeals after a three-hour interview. She characterizes leaving UPO in 1969 as a response to the changed atmosphere of Black Power and her own assessment of where she could contribute.
related_events: late-1960s Black Power transition in DC

clips.subject: Lisa Anderson Todd
clips.title: Retirement Research and Reflection
clips.summary: Todd describes her seven-year project, begun on retirement in April 2006, to write the MFDP convention-challenge book that became this interview's framing concern. She names her sources (Anne Romaine's UNC oral histories, LBJ tapes at the Texas LBJ Library, John Dittmer's footnoting advice, Mary King's mentoring), reflects on distinguishing memory from research, and closes with thoughts about young people, voting, and the long arc from Freedom Summer to Obama.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Early Years in Arlington, Georgia
clips.summary: King describes his birth in Arlington in Calhoun County, his mother Bertha Lee Smith King, his parents' divorce when he was two, and the six years he spent with his maternal grandfather Rev. Joseph P. Smith, a Baptist evangelist and NAACP membership recruiter. He recounts the 1942 Eugene Talmadge gubernatorial-campaign leaflet that introduced him to the word 'nigger' and his grandfather's explanation of post-slavery oppression.
related_events: NAACP membership organizing in rural Georgia

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Atlanta, Ebenezer, and the Butler Street YMCA
clips.summary: After his grandfather's death King moves to Atlanta at age eight to live with his mother. He joins Ebenezer Baptist Church during a 1945 spring revival led by Martin Luther King Sr., meets the King family, forms the Ebenezer Youth Organization, and is taken to the Butler Street YMCA by his mother, who arranges with director J.D. Winston to keep watch over him. King describes growing up among the Black movers-and-shakers of Atlanta who frequented the Y.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Navy Service and Confronting Racism
clips.summary: King enlists in the Navy in 1954 on a Kitty Cruise enlistment after Morehouse tuition becomes unaffordable. Aboard the USS Roanoke he is the only Black sailor of twenty assigned from boot camp, billeted to the deck force chipping paint and cleaning the head despite holding the group's highest GCT score. He recounts the chaplain's refusal to advocate for him, Lt. J.G. Horn's intervention, and Welty Wolf and John Bloom teaching him disbursing-clerk skills so he could pass the petty-officer exam.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Greensboro, Morehouse, and Organizing the AU Center
clips.summary: Back at Morehouse in 1957 as a starting fullback, King reads the February 1, 1960 Greensboro sit-in news at Yates and Milton drug store and immediately recruits Joseph Pierce and Julian Bond. He convenes the first student meeting at Morehouse with about twenty attendees and brings in James Felder from Clark College and football teammates. Within days Mrs. Hill, Dr. Mays's secretary, summons him, Pierce, and Bond to the AU Council of Presidents at Harkness Hall.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Council of Presidents and An Appeal for Human Rights
clips.summary: King recounts the Harkness Hall meeting where Rufus E. Clement, Benjamin E. Mays, Albert E. Manley, and James P. Brawley counseled students to defer to the NAACP, while Harry V. Richardson of the Interdenominational Theological Center and Frank Cunningham of Morris Brown broke ranks to support the students. Clement proposed and funded a published manifesto. Roslyn Pope drafted An Appeal for Human Rights, typed at Howard Zinn's house, published March 9, 1960 in the Atlanta Constitution and Journal; it was reprinted in the New York Times, The Nation, and Harvard Crimson, and entered into the Congressional Record by Sen. Jacob Javits.
related_events: March 9 1960 publication of An Appeal for Human Rights

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: The May 17 March and Mobilizing Spelman
clips.summary: King describes the difficulty of expanding beyond thirty to fifty participants until his mother advised him to organize Spelman women, after which Morehouse, Clark, and Morris Brown men followed. He recounts debating Roy Wilkins at Spelman in April 1960 alongside Manley, deploying the rhetoric 'if not now, when; if not us, who.' On May 17, 1960, the sixth anniversary of Brown v. Board, roughly 4,000 students marched from the AU campus toward the Georgia Capitol; Gov. Ernest Vandiver had deployed state troopers, and King, after consulting Mays and Chief Herbert Jenkins, turned the march at Whitehall Street to avoid violence.
related_events: Brown v. Board sixth anniversary commemoration

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Mann Brothers Acid Attack and Tactical Nonviolence
clips.summary: King describes the boycott of Mann Brothers grocery store in West End where a white man doused him with an acid-like substance that destroyed his shirt and burned his skin. He notes that the Atlanta Student Movement adopted nonviolence as a tactic rather than a way of life, did not run formal nonviolence workshops as Nashville did, and that he warned recruits at the May 17 march that anyone unable to absorb blows or be spat on should not march.
related_events: Atlanta vs Nashville nonviolence training contrast

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: The Atlanta Inquirer and the Richard Rich Confrontation
clips.summary: King describes his June 1960 confrontation with Richard Rich in Chief Herbert Jenkins's office at City Hall after the Magnolia Room arrest, and Rich's threat that King would not return downtown. He recounts M. Kasuth Hill, son of Atlanta's first Black physician, walking into his office with the Atlanta Daily World and proposing to found the Atlanta Inquirer using his offset press, William Strong as attorney, and Julian Bond and the Gibson brothers as writers. The Inquirer launched July 31, 1960, after C.A. Scott of the Atlanta Daily World threatened King over a $9,000 colonial-stores advertising contract.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Shaw Conference and the Founding of SNCC
clips.summary: King describes proposing to Martin Luther King Jr. that he convene a region-wide student-leadership meeting; King contributed $500 toward the gathering, and Ella Baker arranged for Shaw University to host it over Easter weekend 1960. King argues with Baker, who favored loose grassroots coordination, for a formal organization on the grounds that the segregationist apparatus was already organized state-by-state. He names the Atlanta delegation (Julian Bond, Ben Brown, Marion Barry's election as temporary chair, Marian Wright Edelman, Johnny Parham) and assesses Diane Nash as the actual Nashville leader leapfrogged by Marion Barry and James Bevel.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Boynton v. Virginia Test and the October 1960 Jailing
clips.summary: King recounts the Atlanta Student Movement's Boynton v. Virginia test in December 1960, teams to Chattanooga (Maurice Pennington and Morris Dillard), Jacksonville (Norma June Wilson), Birmingham (Marion Wright), and Columbia, S.C., months before CORE's 1961 Freedom Rides; James Farmer later acknowledged the precedent. He then describes the October 19, 1960 Rich's department store sit-in he persuaded Martin Luther King Jr. to join despite Jr.'s probation from the DeKalb County traffic case, citing Daddy King's own sermon 'you can't lead from the back.' The walkie-talkie communications system was developed by Ronald Yancey, the first Black graduate of Georgia Tech.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: The March 10 Mass Meeting at Warren Memorial
clips.summary: King describes being summoned by Jesse Hill Jr. to a Chamber of Commerce meeting where the white merchants and Black old-guard leaders presented the negotiated agreement to desegregate downtown lunch counters after school desegregation in the fall. Frank H. Neely (Rich's chairman) defended King's right to consult his constituency; John C. Calhoun and Rufus E. Clement urged acceptance. Ivan Allen Jr. held an unauthorized press conference, igniting community fury. The March 10, 1961 mass meeting at Rev. Stinson's Warren Memorial Methodist Church drew over 2,000 inside plus crowds outside; after the community booed Martin Luther King Sr., Martin Luther King Jr. delivered what King calls the most powerful speech of his career and salvaged the agreement.

clips.subject: Lonnie C. King
clips.title: Reflections on the Movement's Legacy
clips.summary: King reflects on the 1960s civil rights movement as the country's most consequential social transformation since the end of slavery, opening Title VII protections that benefited women, Latinos, and gays. He critiques contemporary African American economic stagnation, citing personal responsibility alongside structural inequity, and tells the story of a Spelman scholarship recipient who had been studying by cell-phone light in an Atlanta homeless shelter as evidence that long-distance-runner resilience still matters.
related_events: Civil Rights Act of 1964 Title VII passage

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Mexican Heritage and Pennsylvania Childhood
clips.summary: Varela describes her birth in Newell, PA in 1940 to a Mexican father born in Mazapil, Zacatecas who came to the U.S. during the revolution, and her Irish-German mother. She recounts her grandfather's mining education at Columbia and Colorado School of Mines, anti-Mexican racism her father and uncles encountered, and the family's relocations.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Alverno College and Catholic Social Inquiry
clips.summary: Varela describes attending Alverno College in Milwaukee, a Franciscan school for blue-collar Catholic women. She explains the European worker-priest movement, Pope Leo XIII's encyclical, and the observe-judge-act method of the Young Christian Students that shaped her organizing approach. She contrasts the Christian Family Movement and Sodality with the more activist YCS.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: NSA, SDS, and Port Huron
clips.summary: Varela describes hearing SNCC representatives Charles McDew and Tim Jenkins argue for sit-in support resolutions at National Student Association congresses. She recounts meeting Tom Hayden and Al Haber, joining the YCS national staff in Chicago, and being invited to the 1962 Port Huron Conference where she typed minutes and met Casey Hayden.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Joining SNCC in Selma
clips.summary: Casey Hayden's letter brings Varela to SNCC's summer training school where Robert Coles introduced her to African American history. Frank Smith and Bernard Lafayette Jr. recruit her to Selma to support Edmundite priest Maurice Ouellet at St. Elizabeth Mission, conducting adult literacy work in preparation for voter registration's 21-question test.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Selma Literacy Work and Civil Rights Act
clips.summary: Varela details her one-on-one literacy work with Mrs. Kathy in Selma, the 1964 summer recruitment of Black students for literacy program development, and the July 1964 arrests at the Thirsty Boy restaurant after Lyndon Johnson signed the Civil Rights Act. She describes Jim Clark's cattle prod, Selma City Council bans on SNCC and NAACP gatherings, and a warrant for her arrest.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Co-op Booklets and Mississippi Materials
clips.summary: Varela describes producing photo-illustrated literacy booklets and film strips documenting the Panola County (Batesville MS) Okra Co-op so that Black farmers across the South could see Black leadership at work. She characterizes this as the 'second reconstruction' parallel to post-Civil War school-building and reframes the mainstream civil rights narrative to credit SNCC's post-1965 ground-level work.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: SNCC Photography and the Meredith March
clips.summary: Varela moves to Tougaloo, builds a darkroom with Bobby Fletcher, and trains for a week in New Orleans with Matt Herron on Ansel Adams gray-scale documentary technique. She becomes one of nine SNCC staff photographers, covers the James Meredith March Against Fear, and explains that SNCC photographers documented local Black leaders rather than movement celebrities. Their work is collected in This Light of Ours: Activist Photographers of the Civil Rights Movement.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Fannie Lou Hamer and Coalition Building
clips.summary: Varela recalls helping Fannie Lou Hamer's MFDP campaign and recording her first autobiography with Julius Lester. She describes hog-cutting and chicharrones at Hamer's home, Hamer's distribution of Northern food donations to poor white Mississippi families, and Hamer's defense of accepting white volunteers in Freedom Summer. She also recounts coordinating SNCC's loan of Plymouth Valiants and CB radios to Marshall Ganz and George Ballis for the United Farm Workers, and producing a film strip on César Chávez's UFW for distribution to Mississippi Freedom Labor Union organizers.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Black Power and Latino Continuity in SNCC
clips.summary: Varela disputes the public narrative that SNCC expelled non-Black staff after Stokely Carmichael's 1966 election, citing a personalized letter from Carmichael and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson asking her to continue work with Hamer. She names Elizabeth 'Betita' Martínez running the New York office and discusses why Bob Zellner's request to organize in the white community was declined. She explains that most SNCC staff were tactically rather than philosophically nonviolent, and reflects on how Black history work shaped her commitment to Black self-determination.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Reies López Tijerina and the Land Grant Movement
clips.summary: Julian Bond's invitation to host Reies López Tijerina at the 1966 National Conference for New Politics leads Varela to move to New Mexico in 1967 to work for the Alianza. She describes the 1848 Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo protection of land grant heirs, Tijerina's charismatic but non-organized leadership style based in Albuquerque, and her unsuccessful proposal for a newsletter to connect Hispanic communities across northern New Mexico.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Poor People's Campaign and Multicultural Coalition
clips.summary: Varela accompanies Tijerina and Corky Gonzales to the 1968 Poor People's Campaign in Washington. She describes the women's separate housing at a DC school, Chicano-led actions at the State Department, Department of Education hearings, and the Department of Justice on police brutality, plus relationship-building with Puerto Rican Young Lords, Brown Berets, and Appalachian delegates at the bridge crossing to Robert Kennedy's grave. She names Alicia Escalante of the LA welfare rights organization and questions SCLC's leadership of the campaign after King's assassination.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: Northern New Mexico Clinics and Tierra Wools
clips.summary: Anselmo Tijerina recruits Varela to support a community clinic in Tierra Amarilla after Anglo bankers attempt to burn it. The clinic, opening in 1969 with Presbyterian Church mortgage support, still operates today. Varela describes 30 years of work building alternative institutions in northern New Mexico, health care, cooperative agriculture, and livestock-and-weaving businesses that preserved the USDA-targeted Churro sheep breed central to Navajo and Hispanic weaving traditions.

clips.subject: Maria Varela
clips.title: DREAMers and Movement Legacy
clips.summary: Varela reflects on the undocumented student movement's study of SNCC, the parallel between DACA setbacks and the 1964 MFDP defeat at Atlantic City, and Ella Baker's counsel to reflect rather than rush after major losses. She closes by saying the best of a movement may take 50 years to reach the next generation but persists if the empowerment foundation is solid.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: Project Introduction and Welcome
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier of the University of North Carolina's Southern Oral History Program and filmmaker John Bishop open the interview in Oklahoma City on May 24, 2011, identifying the project as the joint Library of Congress / Smithsonian NMAAHC Civil Rights History Project. Hildreth is introduced and welcomes the team.
related_events: Library of Congress / Smithsonian NMAAHC partnership

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: Dunjee High School and the Martin Luther King Jr Play
clips.summary: Hildreth describes her mother Clara Luper's teaching position at Dunjee High School in Spencer, Oklahoma, and the play Clara Luper wrote titled Martin Luther King Jr. Herbert L. Wright of the NAACP attended a performance and invited the Dunjee students to perform the play at the NAACP National Convention.
related_events: Clara Luper's Martin Luther King Jr play

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: Segregated Oklahoma City
clips.summary: Hildreth itemizes the segregation conditions her family lived under in pre-sit-in Oklahoma City: residency restricted south of NE 7th Street, no trying on hats or shoes in stores, separate telephone booths and water fountains, back-door restaurant service via brown bags, and her grandmother tying knots in string to size shoes she could not let Hildreth try on.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: The 1957 NAACP Northern and Southern Route Tour
clips.summary: After Clara Luper's decision to proceed with the NAACP tour, the Youth Council traveled the Northern route to New York City, where the young people drank Cokes in restaurants, ate hamburgers, and used the same water fountains as white patrons for the first time. They returned via the Southern route and re-encountered Jim Crow, which Hildreth says gave them the resolve to begin negotiating with Oklahoma City restaurants.
related_events: 1957 NAACP Youth Council tour to New York City

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: The Katz Drug Store Sit-In
clips.summary: At a Youth Council meeting at the Luper home at 1819 NE Park Place, eight-year-old Hildreth made the motion to sit at Katz Drug Store until served. The thirteen original participants were spit on, kicked, and verbally abused but persisted, and segregation walls began falling across Oklahoma City restaurants in quick succession.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: Clara Luper as Woman Leader
clips.summary: Hildreth reflects on her mother's leadership in a male-dominated movement hierarchy. Clara Luper came from a reading-and-education-centered family, gave her children books rather than toys at Christmas, and faced attempts to fire her from the Oklahoma City school system. A teacher was offered the Dunjee principalship if he would fire Clara Luper; he refused.
related_events: Attempted firing of Clara Luper from OKC schools

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: Hoffman Origins and the Pickup Truck Incident
clips.summary: Hildreth describes Clara Luper's origins in Hoffman, Oklahoma, where her segregated school used leftover textbooks and her class had five students. The chapter culminates in the white pickup truck stalking incident: returning from a freedom rally in a caravan, Clara Luper directed Hildreth and Barbara Posey Jones to run for the Posey home and drove away alone to lead the truck off. Hildreth identifies this as the only time she saw her mother face fear.
related_events: Clara Luper's childhood in Hoffman, Oklahoma

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: Fifth Street Baptist and the Black Pastor Network
clips.summary: Hildreth identifies the Luper family church and discusses Clara Luper's mixed reception among Oklahoma City black pastors: some loved her, some feared her, some opposed her. Many congregations refused to host meetings, fearing their churches would be bombed. Hildreth notes the movement was carried by maids, garbage workers, and have-nots rather than professional Black Oklahomans, who feared losing their jobs.
related_events: Oklahoma City black church politics during the sit-in campaign

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: Police Relations and the Chimpanzee Incident
clips.summary: Hildreth describes Oklahoma City's relatively low-violence civil rights climate, attributing it to a conscious local decision to avoid becoming a national flashpoint. Clara Luper was arrested 26 times but not beaten. Hildreth then recounts an incident in which a white opponent threw a chimpanzee on her during a picket; she was small and the chimpanzee was nearly her size.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: Clara Luper's Political Savvy and Calvin Luper Sr.
clips.summary: Hildreth attributes Clara Luper's political effectiveness to her habit of reading the Congressional Record daily, her network across Oklahoma, and her role as the voice of people in struggle. Hildreth then discusses her father Calvin Luper Sr., an electrician whom her parents divorced at an early age; she describes him as fun and loving but says he could not handle the public pressure on the family.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: Saturdays of Protest and the 50th Anniversary
clips.summary: Hildreth reflects on a childhood in which every Saturday was spent protesting, with no time or money for amusement parks. She describes the 50th-anniversary commemoration of the August 19, 1958, Katz sit-in as a standing-room reunion of young people from across the United States, framing the Oklahoma City sit-in as the catalyst that triggered the broader student sit-in wave.
related_events: 50th-anniversary commemoration of the August 19, 1958 OKC Katz sit-in

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: 1972 US Senate Primary Campaign
clips.summary: Hildreth recalls her mother's 1972 Oklahoma Democratic US Senate primary campaign as both exhausting and enjoyable. Clara Luper campaigned in small towns with no Black residents and delivered the same message everywhere, drawing cross-racial support. The campaign lost the primary but Clara Luper accepted the outcome as having tried.

clips.subject: Marilyn Luper Hildreth
clips.title: 1969 Sanitation Workers Strike and Closing
clips.summary: Hildreth describes Clara Luper's role as spokesperson for the 1969 Oklahoma City sanitation workers strike, selected by the workers themselves for her integrity and willingness to fight to the end. Hildreth closes with her mother's daily morning question whether she wanted to continue her education that day, and Clara Luper's maxim to believe in the rain when it does not fall, the sun when it does not shine, and a God you have never seen.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Providence Family and Progressive Party
clips.summary: Born in Providence to a St. Vincent-born optometrist father and an Ann Arbor-born mother, Noonan describes her parents' Progressive Party activism. Her father served as Rhode Island state chairman; after Henry A. Wallace's 1948 campaign, immigration authorities threatened him with deportation despite his naturalized citizenship, and a jewelry-factory-owner family friend committed suicide after being called before HUAC.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Integrating Providence Schools
clips.summary: Noonan recounts being the first Black student at Lexington Avenue Elementary School and the only Black student at the Lincoln School for Girls, a Quaker prep school. She describes being stoned moving into a white neighborhood, the Glee Club exclusion, attending Winter Street AME Zion Church, and her mother's instruction to answer racial slurs with "I'm black and I'm proud."

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: University of Michigan and SDS
clips.summary: Her mother relocated to Detroit so Noonan could qualify for in-state tuition at Michigan, where she joined the Methodist Student Movement and then SDS as the only Black member of the campus chapter. Tom Hayden, returning from the Albany freedom rides, brought her and four others to the April 1962 SDS-SNCC meeting in Chapel Hill, North Carolina, which she identifies as a founding moment for both the anti-war and student civil rights movements.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Wayne State and Detroit Friends of SNCC
clips.summary: Her parents barred her from going South in 1962, so Noonan enrolled at Wayne State, where she took courses with George Rawick and Seymour Faber of C.L.R. James's Facing Reality group. Afternoons she worked with Bernard Lafayette Jr. and Elizabeth Hirschfeld on the Detroit Friends of SNCC, alongside John Watson, Luke Tripp, and Mike Hamlin. The summer culminated with a Diane Nash speaking event nine months into her pregnancy.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Albany Project Under Sherrod
clips.summary: In summer 1963 Noonan left Michigan against her parents' wishes and joined Charles Sherrod's Southwest Georgia Project. Orientation in Sumter County included news of a young woman's rape and murder by 13 white men. When white drivers circled the church where SNCC women had taken sanctuary, JoAnn Christian's father and cousin Monroe sat on the porch with rifles. Her mother contacted Neil Staebler, Burke Marshall, and Robert Kennedy to demand her protection.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Preventive Nonviolence
clips.summary: Noonan defines "preventive nonviolence" as practiced in Sherrod's Albany project: Sherrod would notify local gangs of upcoming demonstrations, and they would position themselves between white crowds and nonviolent marchers. SNCC workers rode in cars and stayed in homes with armed community members. She credits Mr. Christian and his cousin sitting armed on the church porch with preventing attacks during the Albany sanctuary episode.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Greenwood Voter Registration
clips.summary: Defying Sherrod's protective instruction to stay in Albany, Noonan and Jean Wheeler took a bus to Greenwood, Mississippi, where Sam Block and Willie Peacock picked them up. She canvassed for voter registration, helped organize a late-summer voting day with 200-300 attempts, and witnessed white men driving past the SNCC office with guns drawn. She notes that essentially no one was successfully registered despite repeated attempts.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Women in SNCC and March on Washington
clips.summary: Noonan rejects the framing of women being mistreated in SNCC, describing herself as encouraged to do as much as the men. She describes the August 1963 March on Washington as feeling "too civilized" after Mississippi, recalls Roy Wilkins's disparaging remark about W.E.B. Du Bois who died that day, and notes the censorship of John Lewis's speech for being too critical of the Kennedy civil rights bill.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: MFDP Challenge at Atlantic City
clips.summary: Noonan opposed the 1964 Freedom Summer project on grounds that it abandoned the principle of Black student organizers in Black communities. She attended the Atlantic City convention with the MFDP, recalls Fannie Lou Hamer's testimony, the Council of Churches representative's visible shame, and the offer of two appointed seats to Edwin King and Aaron Henry. She frames the MFDP challenge as evidence that two years of activism had put the vote on the national agenda.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Selma Ward Organizing
clips.summary: Beginning early 1965, Noonan worked weekends in a Selma ward while studying Black college students' vocational aspirations with Pat Gurin, Doris A. Derby, and John H. Bracey Jr. at Tuskegee. Her ward work included pursuing a stoplight, getting Black women hired at Dan River Mills, and supporting unionization at the Pepsi plant with an AFL-CIO organizer. The going wage for day work in Selma was $12 for a six-day week.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: SCLC, Bloody Sunday, and the U-Turn March
clips.summary: Noonan describes the SCLC entry into Selma against the backdrop of SNCC's prior community-organizing presence and her suspicion that the MFDP challenge prompted SCLC's voting-rights pivot to forestall a populist SNCC-led party. She recounts the "U-turn March" after Bloody Sunday and the Fay Bellamy account in Hands on the Freedom Plow of James Forman and Stokely Carmichael's middle-of-the-night meeting at Dr. Sullivan Jackson's house pleading with King to defy the federal injunction.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Lowndes County and Black Power
clips.summary: After the Selma-to-Montgomery march, federal registrars eliminated registration barriers and SNCC organizing shifted to Lowndes County, Alabama. Noonan attended the founding discussion of the Lowndes County Freedom Organization, where she preferred a black star symbol (drawing on Marcus Garvey and Nkrumah's Ghanaian Airlines) over the panther. She read the black power slogan as continuous with SNCC's original mission of building vehicles of Black political power.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Mayor Smitherman and Wilson Baker
clips.summary: Noonan describes the SNCC office sitting directly across from the Selma police station, with prisoners visible through the office window. She kept Wilson Baker's home phone number and called him for protection from a hostile Halloween crowd; he sent cars to escort the SNCC workers home. Mayor Smitherman engaged the SNCC organizers cordially over the poverty-funds challenge, in contrast to the state troopers who attacked marchers on the Edmund Pettus Bridge.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Marriage, New Communities, and Post-SNCC
clips.summary: Noonan met her first husband Silas Norman Jr. on the Selma project; they married in 1967. They visited Sherrod's New Communities Inc cooperative farm in Albany, where the farmhouse bore bullet holes, and decided the risk to their infant son was too great. Silas attended medical school; she entered a Michigan history PhD program. She raised three sons in inner-city Detroit and stayed active in post-SNCC retrospectives and an effort to free Curtis Hayes Muhammad from jail in Liberia.

clips.subject: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan
clips.title: Hands on the Freedom Plow
clips.summary: Noonan describes her co-editorial role on Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC, published 2010 by the University of Illinois Press. The collection gathers 52 essays by women active in SNCC and connected projects, including white and Black women, two Hispanic women, and middle-aged activists alongside the predominantly college-student majority.
related_events: Hands on the Freedom Plow publication 2010

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: Birth and Childhood Under Jim Crow
clips.summary: Jenkins states her name (Mary Francis Jenkins), date and place of birth (August 12, 1928, Waycross, Georgia), and recounts moving to Albany, Georgia at age 8 or 9 after her mother remarried following her father's death in an automobile accident on a Georgia highway. She recalls sitting in the balcony of the Albany movie theater, drinking from separate water fountains, and a white boy squirting a water pistol in her face while her mother held her hand and said, 'You can't.'

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: Teaching Her Daughter the System
clips.summary: Jenkins recounts taking her three-year-old daughter to an Albany-area amusement park where she saw white children riding park rides and asked to ride. Jenkins had to repeat her own mother's phrase, 'You can't.' Her daughter beat her little fists against her and said, 'You don't want me to ride.' Jenkins reflects on having to take the blame for explaining segregation to a child.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: Madison High School
clips.summary: Jenkins describes her education at Madison High School, an all-segregated Black school with hand-me-down books and outdated equipment but excellent teachers. She names her principal Ruth T. Kimball, the school's first Black female principal, along with Dorothy Caldwell, Miss Rimmel, Miss Thelma French (home economics), and Miss Olive Rogers (English). She served as editor-in-chief of the senior annual and was salutatorian; her close friend Vashti Stubbs held her hand before her salutatorian address.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: Fisk University and Military Service
clips.summary: Jenkins attended Fisk University in Nashville, Tennessee, where she belonged to the little theater and took creative writing; her senior-year grade came from directing a play written by one of her teachers. Before finishing Fisk she entered military service for two years, doing basic training in Virginia, serving at Fort Dix Special Services in New Jersey, and being stationed at Fort Custer in Michigan. She married Roscoe Jenkins (from North Carolina) while in the service; they divorced after his overseas posting.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: Teaching at East Baker County
clips.summary: Jenkins returned to Albany, completed an elementary certificate at Albany State University, and was hired as a teacher at East Baker High School by her pastor Rev. James Grant, the school's principal. She recounts two incidents: the white supervisor (wife of the Baker County school superintendent) walking into a young Black male teacher's classroom and firing him on the spot for an unspecified weekend incident; and the same supervisor, after the Supreme Court's desegregation ruling, assembling all the Black teachers to lecture them on how wrong integration would be, concluding, 'They may make us go to school with you, but I would never invite one of you to my home.' Jenkins says she was 'through with Baker County.'

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: Carver Junior High and Albany Before the Movement
clips.summary: Jenkins transferred to Carver Junior High School in Dougherty County, teaching first in an army-barracks annex before being moved into the Jason building. She describes hand-me-down books, the prevailing classroom conditions, and her view of student discipline. She recounts the Criterion Club's pre-Movement organizing: Black men going to the City Commission to request a biracial committee to address the Albany Herald's negative portrayal of Black residents; both they and Dr. William G. Anderson were refused. The Albany Herald and a radio station were owned by the same white man.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: First Mass Meeting at Mount Zion
clips.summary: Jenkins attended the first Albany Movement mass meeting at Mount Zion Baptist Church under Rev. James Grant. She recalls the crowds, the singing, the crying, and being moved by the leaders of the Freedom Songs including Charles Sherrod, Bernice Johnson Reagon, and Rutha Mae Harris. She says she wanted segregation out of her life not only for herself but for her children, who should have a better quality of life.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: Volunteering in Dr. King's Albany Office
clips.summary: Jenkins volunteered in the Albany Movement office set up in Dr. William G. Anderson's house, where Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. worked while in Albany. She answered the telephone and took messages. She recounts King handing her a phone number to call Harry Belafonte (whose name she momentarily blanked on) and learning he was not in but that the message would be delivered. She describes King's meetings with women, in which he believed women were 'the movers and shakers of the world' and would pose a question then listen to the responses.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: Notes on Church Fans
clips.summary: Jenkins describes sitting through mass meeting after mass meeting listening to people who had gone to jail describe how they were treated, and realizing that while Martin Luther King Jr. and Charles Sherrod were known, the rank-and-file participants' sacrifices would be lost. She began taking notes on what they said, sometimes writing on cardboard church fans when she ran out of paper. Teachers who were arrested would be fired; rumors said car tags were checked at mass meetings. Jenkins says she once stood up to march at Mount Olive Baptist Church carrying her four-year-old son after Rev. James Grant said he would march alone, but her husband gently pulled her out of the line at the door. That same day, Chief Laurie Pritchett had decided not to arrest anyone.
related_events: Mount Olive Baptist Church mass action

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: Writing Open Themselves
clips.summary: Jenkins describes the writing and publishing of Open Themselves: A Pictorial History of the Albany Civil Rights Movement. Her daughter (now deceased) kept urging her for a decade to assemble her material into a book; after her daughter passed, Jenkins began the work. She took the manuscript to a religious-books publisher in Columbus, Georgia, but the publisher's copyeditor (a white woman) introduced errors Jenkins had to pay to correct. When the second-edition books arrived for a Friday book signing, she discovered a 'drop in' typographical instruction for a picture had not been executed; her husband refused the publisher's offers to avoid redoing the book, and the publisher reprinted it as the third edition. The first edition sold out.

clips.subject: Mary Jenkins
clips.title: Legacy of the Movement
clips.summary: Jenkins assesses what the Albany Movement accomplished: seeing Black people behind the desk in stores and banks is what the Movement made possible, even if she does not know how long it would have taken otherwise. She invokes the young people at the Albany bus station who 'didn't go there to break laws, they went there to enforce laws' (the ICC regulation banning segregation in interstate-bus terminals), the six girls who integrated Albany High School after C.B. King's eight-year legal struggle following Brown, and warns that 'the laws that helped us then are the laws that are being challenged now.'

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Childhood on Grandmother's Farm
clips.summary: Born July 24, 1933, Mary Jones grew up on the small parcel her grandmother Bertha McIntosh purchased outside Albany. Her grandmother reared her on what the family called a 'slot' of land. The chapter establishes the rural Black agricultural world of southwest Georgia in the 1930s and 40s.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Field Labor Under Miss Alice Porter
clips.summary: From age eight, Jones worked white-owned farms gathering peanuts, picking cotton, digging potatoes, pulling corn, and gathering pecans. Miss Alice Porter supervised neighborhood children in the fields. Workdays began around seven and ended at five; the children earned about 75 cents per day, saved toward school clothes.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Shepherd Elementary at First Bethel
clips.summary: Jones attended Shepherd Elementary, held inside First Bethel Baptist Church, one of the oldest Black churches in southwest Georgia, where W.E.B. Du Bois had reportedly visited. The school occupied a former barn given by the white community when they built their own church. Miss Willie Mae Jackson taught grades one through three; Miss Celeste Scott taught grades four through seven.
related_events: segregated Black elementary education in Georgia

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Monroe High and Marriage
clips.summary: Jones moved to Albany to attend Monroe Comprehensive High School, living with her aunt Ella Holloway and three cousins. She played basketball and ran track. She was scheduled to graduate in 1951 but married classmate Jimmy Lee Jones, a Monroe football and basketball player, on the Tuesday after a Friday-night basketball game. He left for the Army the following day.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Daughter's School Integration
clips.summary: Jones signed her oldest daughter into the second integrated class at Albany's previously-white high school. Principal Mr. McNair called the Black students 'little monkeys.' Students faced spitting, harassment in the cafeteria, and daily threats. Jones describes phoning around to track her daughter's safety in an era before household phones were common. Her daughter persisted because friends had enrolled with her.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Husband's Military Service
clips.summary: Jones's husband Jimmy Lee served two years of active duty, returned, joined the Reserves, and was called back to active duty during the Albany Movement era. While he was away Jones raised the family, taught the children that they 'were somebody regardless of the color of the skin,' and read the Pittsburgh Courier and A.C. Searles's Southwest Georgian for news of the integration struggle.
related_events: Black press coverage of the civil rights movement

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Voter Registration Work
clips.summary: After Rosa Parks and the Montgomery bus boycott, Jones joined the Criterion Club's voter-registration drive. She canvassed door-to-door in the Washington Projects where she lived, taught literacy classes to help residents pass the Georgia Constitution reading test required for registration, drove people to the courthouse to register, and then drove them back to vote. She describes the persistent challenge of converting registration into actual ballot-casting.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Police Committee and First Black Officers
clips.summary: Jones served on the seven-member police committee that pressed Chief Laurie Pritchett to hire Black officers. McCree Harris led the delegation. Pritchett initially ignored them through three or four visits. Harris ultimately brought completed applications back to him. Five men were hired: Etheridge Smith, David Hyde, Robert Benning Jr., and two others. The new officers walked beats without patrol cars, wore uniforms, but the committee acted in response to weekly killings of Black men by white officers.
related_events: first Black police officers Albany Georgia 1961

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Criterion Club Leadership
clips.summary: The Criterion Club, the pre-existing Black civic and voter-registration network in Albany, became the organizational core of the Albany Movement. Jones names its leadership: Dr. William G. Anderson (elected president of the Albany Movement), Chevene 'C.B.' King, Slater King, Thomas Chapman, and Marion S. Page (secretary).

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Freedom Riders and Mass Meetings
clips.summary: The Freedom Riders arrived in Albany and were welcomed at the train station before being arrested. Mass meetings followed almost nightly, rotated among Black churches; some ministers feared cooperation because rural churches were being burned. Dr. King helped fundraise to rebuild the burned churches. Jones met King only briefly in receiving lines.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: March and Baker County Jail
clips.summary: After James H. Gray's Albany Herald editorial accused adults of pushing children to march, Jones and roughly 700 adults organized at Washington and Broad Avenue and marched to the old jail. All were arrested. Jones spent Monday through Thursday in Baker County jail; 25 women shared a cell with one bed; food was hard peas, grits with bread thrown on top, and black-eyed peas. The Albany Movement was a local action; she stresses 700 were already jailed when King arrived. Her father bonded her out.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Black Christmas Boycott
clips.summary: Jones drove people to Cordele and other surrounding towns to shop during the 1961 Black Christmas Boycott of downtown Albany. Residents retrieved their layaway items before December, then refused to shop downtown. The boycott pressured Albany merchants to open jobs to Black workers beyond cleaning and freight-handling roles.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Voting Captain and Project Organizing
clips.summary: Jones continued grassroots work in the Washington Projects: meetings at her house at noon each day so older residents who couldn't walk far could attend, voting-captain duties identifying preferred candidates and notifying residents who to vote for, and coordination with neighbors who provided childcare while organizers were jailed.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Teachers' Committee and Legacy
clips.summary: The school superintendent threatened teachers with dismissal if they marched. The teachers' committee instead supported jailed marchers, paying rent, light bills, and buying food for the families. Jones reflects that the Albany Movement succeeded by opening jobs and improving schools; she names prayer, sticking together, and learning to love each other as the central legacies, alongside acknowledgment of the quiet white allies who supported the movement.

clips.subject: Mary Jones
clips.title: Head Start and Education
clips.summary: Through the War on Poverty under President Lyndon Johnson and Sargent Shriver, Jones entered the Head Start program and pursued her own delayed education in early childhood. She defends Head Start funding from cuts because, she says, her education came from that program.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Tijuana Birth and San Diego Immigration
clips.summary: Camarillo introduces himself and recounts his 1941 birth in Tijuana to a U.S.-citizen mother who worked at Convair making B-29 bombers and at the Barrio Logan tuna factory during World War II. His family immigrated to San Diego in 1950 when he was 10. His mother Rufina was born in Campo, a San Diego County border community, and two of her brothers died in World War II.
related_events: World War II home-front industrial labor
Post-war Mexican immigration to San Diego

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Schooling Without Bilingual Education
clips.summary: Camarillo describes entering second grade in San Diego without English, failing his placement test, and asking classmates 'qué dijo' to translate the teacher. He recounts a teacher who invited him for a drink after school and humanized education for him. Sports and playground leadership became his path to English and self-confidence; he was placed in charge of junior patrols as a crossing guard.
related_events: Pre-Lau v. Nichols English-only public schooling

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: College, Kennedy, and Civic Awakening
clips.summary: Camarillo wins two scholarships from Herbert Hoover High School and Allied Gardens, attends San Diego State, and graduates in four years. He hears President Kennedy's 1962 commencement address, joins the Viva Kennedy Clubs in San Diego, becomes a naturalized citizen at 21, and helps launch the Southwest Voter Registration Education Project as the first multicultural voter-registration drive in San Diego, including African American, Native American (14 county tribes), and Asian American voters.
related_events: Founding of Southwest Voter Registration Education Project

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Army Service and the Jim Crow South
clips.summary: Camarillo volunteers for the U.S. Army in 1965 and is sent to Georgia and South Carolina for basic training because spinal meningitis closed the California facilities. He recounts being refused service at a Charleston restaurant with an African American Georgian and a Puerto Rican soldier, and being denied entry to Atlanta night clubs that required 'membership cards' available only to whites. He describes integrated camaraderie within the Army across ethnic groups.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Germany, Boy Scouts, and Return to Social Work
clips.summary: Sent to Germany as an infill for the 4th Armored Battalion rather than to Vietnam, Camarillo serves as a training officer doing a week's work in a day, then becomes Scoutmaster of the Boy Scouts of America Transatlantic Council, traveling across Europe with military-dependent children. The experience leads him to join the San Diego County welfare department on return to work with families as a bilingual social worker.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Trabajadores de la Raza and Bilingual Pay
clips.summary: Facing a caseload of 600 Spanish-speaking families against the 60-family standard for English-only workers, Camarillo founds the San Diego chapter of Trabajadores de la Raza, building a national network with chapters in Los Angeles, San Jose, Houston, San Antonio, Denver, and New York. The organization wins a 10% bilingual pay bonus at San Diego County, then the city of San Diego, then San Diego Gas and Electric, and finally a statewide bilingual pay program with state assembly members Richard Alatorre and Mervyn Dymally.
related_events: Founding of Trabajadores de la Raza national network

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Suing San Diego County for Employment Equity
clips.summary: As executive director of the Chicano Federation in 1974, Camarillo organizes the six Latino attorneys in San Diego and county employees facing 67% rejection rates for Hispanic applicants. They sue San Diego County under federal Equal Employment Opportunity law in what Camarillo describes as the longest-lasting civil rights employment case in U.S. history. African American, Asian American, and Filipino plaintiffs join. The suit wins removal of artificial hiring barriers at the county, then sets precedent for the city and private sector.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Revenue Sharing Coalition and Anglo Allies
clips.summary: When Nixon's General Revenue Sharing program threatens to fund only municipal pet projects, Camarillo and Chicano Federation chairman Dr. Alfredo Velasco coalition with the Community Congress, a largely Anglo organization rooted in the San Diego beach hippie movement. Together they travel to Washington, document congressional intent for low-income communities, sue the city and county, and secure over $5 million the first year and over $6 million the second year for community organizations bypassing local government.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Alinsky Training and San Jose Faculty
clips.summary: Camarillo earns his master's in social work at San Diego State on the GI Bill while working for the county, then wins a Ford Foundation Fellowship to train under Saul Alinsky in Chicago. He joins the San Jose State faculty to start the school of social work as coordinator for field instruction and recruits Herman Gallegos, a National Council of La Raza founder who grew up alongside César Chávez in Sal Si Puedes (San Jose), to bring foundation-funding expertise.
keywords: San Jose State school of social work
related_events: Founding of National Council of La Raza

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Chicano Park and Direct-Line Diplomacy
clips.summary: Camarillo describes the Chicano Park takeover under the San Diego-Coronado Bridge, led by his sister Tomasa Camarillo as chairman of the Chicano Park Steering Committee, when the state reneged on a community-park promise and proposed a Highway Patrol station instead. Federally funded offices replaced the patrol station and housed the Brown Berets, Pinto Union (formerly incarcerated organizers), welfare services, and human resources. He recounts establishing direct phone-line relationships with Police Chief Bill Kolender and Mayor Pete Wilson to defuse incidents like the Brown Image low-rider beating without riots, and stopping a taxi-driver scheme that delivered Mexican-looking residents to immigration.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: McDonald's, Radio, and Civic Independence
clips.summary: Recognizing he could not keep suing institutions that funded the Chicano Federation, Camarillo leaves in 1976 for the private sector. After two McDonald's franchise rejections, he arrives at his third meeting with elected officials Dean Kennerberg knew, and wins approval. He partners with Secretary of State March Fong Eu on a free-french-fries voter incentive, founds Quetzal Bilingual Communications, applies under Reagan-era airwave deregulation for a 1040 AM Des Moines-derived West Coast frequency, and builds half a dozen Spanish-language radio stations linked by satellite. His station broadcasts Port Commission warnings about Monsanto and Harvest at Sea toxic releases over Barrio Logan.
related_events: California Secretary of State voter registration initiative

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: PACs, Patronage, and Hispanic Judges
clips.summary: Camarillo organizes McDonald's franchisees into MacPAC and contributes to Jerry Brown, Gray Davis, Cruz Bustamante, and Pete Wilson through the Golden 500 (a $500-membership Hispanic political fundraising network with chapters in San Diego, Los Angeles, San Francisco, and Sacramento). When a six-judge vacancy opened in San Diego under Brown, Camarillo nominated six Hispanic attorneys and all six were appointed. He notes Brown's relationship with Linda Ronstadt and contrasts Pete Wilson's later anti-immigrant gubernatorial turn with the 20-year absence of statewide Republican wins in California (except Arnold Schwarzenegger).

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: La Raza Cósmica and Multicultural Curriculum
clips.summary: Camarillo links the term 'La Raza' to José Vasconcelos's post-revolutionary book La Raza Cósmica and names his companies after Mesoamerican civilizations (Quetzal, Quinto Sol, Aztlán). He describes his college teacher's expertise on the Aztecs, Mayans, Toltecs, and Chichimecs as the foundation of his commitment to multicultural curriculum and naming practices, framing knowledge of other cultures as the sign of an educated person rather than a concession to ethnic interest.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Immigration Reform and the Greenlining Institute
clips.summary: Serving the maximum terms on the National Council of La Raza board, Camarillo works with Sen. Orrin Hatch, Sen. Edward Kennedy, and Reagan administration delegates on the 1986 Immigration Reform and Control Act. He becomes the San Diego delegate to the Greenlining Institute board for over 20 years, chairs the Public Utilities Commission's deregulation of the California telephone industry for 10 years, and helps build a citizens' board providing services in 10 languages across all wire and wireless companies in California.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Electoral Campaigns and Redistricting
clips.summary: Camarillo describes three losing races for elected office, San Diego Unified school board (defeated when 60% of district voters were senior citizens interested in hot lunches rather than education), Southwestern Community College board (split-vote loss against a wealthy Coronado opponent after a second Hispanic entered the race), and the newly-created 9th San Diego City Council district, where less-than-10% Latino turnout favored an incumbent television newscaster. He recounts serving on California State Senate redistricting in 1980, San Diego County redistricting, and the 2010 Latino Redistricting Committee that produced the city's first Latino-majority district.

clips.subject: Mateo Camarillo
clips.title: Immigration Reflections and Closing
clips.summary: Camarillo and his wife now operate a breathalyzer-installation business for drivers with DUI citations, working with a German manufacturer with World War I gas expertise. He closes by reflecting on immigration: the U.S. as a country of immigrants, the strawberry fields of Oregon's Willamette Valley that closed schools early so children could harvest, the 80% immigrant share of California farm work, and the 1986 Immigration Reform and Control Act's pardon framework. He critiques wall-building proposals from the 2016 presidential campaign and calls for regulated immigration without exclusion.
related_events: Reagan-era Immigration Reform and Control Act

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: World War II and Segregated Service
clips.summary: Perry, born in Columbia in 1921 and a South Carolina State College student when Pearl Harbor was attacked, was drafted in late 1942 and reported in January 1943. He served in the segregated Quartermaster Corps with basic training at Camp Van Dorn, Mississippi, and overseas deployment in England, France, Belgium, and Germany.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: Birmingham Terminal Encounter
clips.summary: On a weekend pass during basic training, Perry traveled by train through Birmingham, Alabama, where he had to order food at a kitchen window outside the terminal restaurant while uniformed Italian prisoners of war were seated and served inside. He frames the encounter as the moment the injustice of segregation became personally legible to him as a uniformed American soldier.
related_events: Italian POW housing in US South

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: The 1947 Columbia Federal Trials
clips.summary: Discharged in January 1946, Perry returned to Columbia and as a spectator witnessed two federal trials in spring 1947: George Elmore's challenge to South Carolina's white primary, decided under the Smith v. Allwright precedent, and John Wrighten's suit for admission to the University of South Carolina law school. He identifies the attorneys present: Thurgood Marshall, Robert L. Carter, Harold Boulware, Fred Robinson, and "Squire" Morgan of Orangeburg.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: Choosing the Law and Judge Waring's Rulings
clips.summary: Perry describes his decision to study law, motivated by the Columbia trials and his accumulating recognition of segregation's injustice. He recounts Judge J. Waties Waring's three-fold judgment in Wrighten: South Carolina must either admit Wrighten to USC, provide a separate but substantially equal law school, or cease legal education altogether. South Carolina chose the second option, opening what Thurgood Marshall called a "Jim Crow dump" at South Carolina State College.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: Law School and the Diploma Privilege Repeal
clips.summary: Originally intending to enroll at Howard University, Perry was persuaded to attend the new SC State law school. He describes a small student cohort taught by a Harvard-trained dean and faculty from Columbia, the University of Kansas, and the University of Iowa. South Carolina's legislature, anticipating the new school's graduates, repealed the diploma privilege and required all law graduates to sit for the bar examination beginning with Perry's class of 1951; Perry was the only member of his class to pass.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: Briggs v. Elliott and Early Practice
clips.summary: Within days of graduating, Perry traveled to Charleston in early June 1951 and squeezed into the courtroom to witness the trial of Briggs v. Elliott, the South Carolina case that would be consolidated into Brown v. Board of Education. With no clerkships or government employment available to a Black attorney, he opened a one-room law office in Spartanburg, where Columbia (with three Black attorneys) was already considered "crowded."

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: Spartanburg Municipal Court Tactics
clips.summary: Perry recounts a routine pattern in which the Spartanburg municipal judge would impose the maximum sentence (30 days or $100) on his Black clients while letting white-represented defendants off with $10 fines. With no separate prosecutor (the arresting officers prosecuted their own cases), Perry developed and pre-drafted a notice of appeal he could serve immediately upon conviction, eventually using the circuit court appellate process to reverse the judge and shift the local courtroom dynamic.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: Union County Habeas Petition
clips.summary: Called by the Union County Voters League about a young Black man given an excessive sentence by a magistrate judge, Perry received the case on day six, past the five-day appeal window. He filed a petition for a writ of habeas corpus and obtained what he describes as his first victory in the South Carolina Supreme Court.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: NAACP Network and the Brown Dry Run
clips.summary: Perry describes becoming a cooperating attorney with the NAACP Legal Defense Fund while also associating with the NAACP Office of General Counsel, two separate organizations whose distinction was opaque to the public. He traveled to Washington for the Howard University moot-court rehearsal of the Brown v.
