Franklin's campaign slogan "Webb Franklin, he's one of us" Clark identifies as overt racial appeal. He came close in both races; the district was later redrawn into a majority-Black configuration that elected Mike Espy.
related_events: Clark's two U.S. House campaigns against Webb Franklin

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Speaker Pro Tempore and Reflections
clips.summary: Clark was elected Speaker Pro Tempore in 1992, receiving 17 of 35 Black legislators' votes and 87 percent of the total House vote. He married at thirty-eight, raised his nephew (now a cardiologist in California), and both of his sons became lawyers. Asked the closing question, Clark identifies political parties as the greatest hindrance in Mississippi and warns that without leadership the gains of the civil rights movement could be lost.
related_events: Clark's 1992 election as Speaker Pro Tempore
Growth of Black caucus to 35 members by 2000

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Born in Caryville, Newark Migration
clips.summary: Carter recounts his 1917 birth in Caryville, Florida and his family's participation in the Great Migration to Newark, where his father had been sent north to work in a factory. His father died when Carter was one year old. He describes growing up in poverty with his mother taking in washing for white families until his older sister married a Linden, New Jersey bar owner when Carter was twelve, which moved the family out of the Newark slums.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Schools and Race in Newark and East Orange
clips.summary: Carter describes how he gained awareness of race through a sixth-grade incident where a teacher told a white girl who had befriended a black classmate to 'stay with your own kind.' He contrasts Newark schools, which let racial conflict play out, with East Orange schools, where teachers actively enforced separation. He recounts integrating the school swimming pool at Barringer High School by entering it alone after a state Supreme Court ruling, leading administrators to close the pool entirely rather than open it.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Lincoln University and Debate
clips.summary: Enrolling at Lincoln University at sixteen, Carter describes the all-male, predominantly black school as isolated and a great time in his life. He participated in the debating team against Haverford and other small colleges, which taught him he could compete with white students. He and his generation agitated for more black faculty, since only one black English professor was on staff at the time.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Howard Law School Under Houston
clips.summary: Carter entered Howard University School of Law on scholarship in 1937. He took William H. Hastie as his model, a soft-spoken but brilliant dean, and was drawn into Charles Hamilton Houston's program to attack Plessy v. Ferguson by forcing states to either build separate facilities or accept black students. He witnessed Houston argue Missouri ex rel. Gaines before the Supreme Court and watched Justice James Clark McReynolds turn his back throughout the argument, the case that established the moot-court-night-before tradition.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Marian Anderson at the Lincoln Memorial
clips.summary: Carter recounts Easter Sunday 1939, when he was working as a waiter to supplement his scholarship. After the DAR refused Marian Anderson permission to sing at Constitution Hall and Eleanor Roosevelt arranged for her to perform at the Lincoln Memorial instead, Carter walked off the job to attend. He describes the large integrated crowd in tears and was fired upon his return, but subsequently received a full scholarship plus a library work-study position, making the firing a net gain.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Columbia and the Rosenwald Fellowship
clips.summary: After Howard, Carter went to Columbia on a Rosenwald Fellowship to work toward a doctorate on First Amendment strike rights. He chose Noel Dowling as his advisor, a Columbia constitutional law specialist who taught Carter to argue narrow technical distinctions even while believing Supreme Court rulings actually tracked political change. Carter learned the technique of giving the court a narrow path to rule for his side without making sweeping declarations.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Army Service and the Officers' Club
clips.summary: Drafted in 1941, Carter served in the Army Air Corps and was sent to Augusta, GA to clear brush. After becoming a personal legal advisor to his ignorant staff sergeant, he attended Officer Candidate School and became a Second Lieutenant. He recounts confronting a Lieutenant Colonel who called him a slur over a clothes hanger, the officers' club incident where a black bartender feared to serve him until a white officer put the drink on his bill, and the racism that made a militant of him.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Joining the NAACP Under Marshall
clips.summary: Hastie recruited Carter to Thurgood Marshall's NAACP staff after his 1944 discharge. Initially given a small office while the rest of the staff worked in a big room, Carter was assigned a brief on the separate-but-equal doctrine during Marshall's long lunch. Marshall accepted Carter's brief unchanged and sent it out, beginning Carter's role as the behind-the-scenes brief writer. Carter describes the discomfort of writing for years without recognition before insisting on credit.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Traveling the South and McLaurin
clips.summary: Carter describes traveling the South for NAACP cases when there were no hotel facilities for black lawyers, sometimes staying with black families. He recounts a Mississippi teacher-salary case where Constance Baker Motley, the first black woman lawyer the local community had seen, co-argued, and the courtroom audience reenacted the questioning in barbershops and beauty parlors. His first Supreme Court argument was McLaurin v. Oklahoma State Regents, where the segregation consisted of George W. McLaurin being required to sit at a separate table and seat despite same faculty and books. Carter argued segregation itself denied equal opportunity.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Direct Attack on Plessy and the Clarks
clips.summary: In June 1950 the NAACP lawyers shifted strategy to attack Plessy v. Ferguson directly while retaining unequal-facilities as a backup. Preparing the Clarendon County, South Carolina case (Briggs v. Elliott), Carter sought out Otto Klineberg at Columbia, chief social scientist there, who declined to testify but referred Carter to Kenneth and Mamie (Phipps) Clark. The Clarks' doll test, showing black children identified the white doll as 'good' and the black doll as 'bad,' became the only case in which the test was used in direct presentation. Spottswood Robinson was initially skeptical when he saw Carter carrying dolls through the Charleston train station.
related_events: Briggs v. Elliott Clarendon County case

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Brown v. Board and All Deliberate Speed
clips.summary: Carter describes social science as the key to Brown, initially resisted by William T. Coleman Jr. and others as not hard science, but later agreed to be excellent. He explains that the Topeka, Kansas case was the lead case argued on segregation per se. Carter warned that 'all deliberate speed' would allow decades of delay, which proved correct as it took ten to twenty years before Topeka schools actually desegregated. The significance of Brown to him was the constitutional holding that enforced segregation by law was no longer viable, even if voluntary segregation persisted.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Defending the NAACP from Southern Attack
clips.summary: After Brown, southern states attempted to outlaw the NAACP through membership-disclosure laws. Carter spent years in a 'grueling spree' defending the organization in NAACP v. Alabama (protecting membership lists) and NAACP v. Button (protecting the right of civil rights organizations to seek educational opportunity through litigation). The First Amendment associational-rights work from his Columbia thesis became directly load-bearing.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Greenberg, the LDF Split, and Resignation
clips.summary: Carter recounts becoming NAACP General Counsel in 1955 and his conflict with Jack Greenberg, who, after Thurgood Marshall's appointment to the Second Circuit Court of Appeals, sought to have Roy Wilkins bar Carter from bringing segregation cases, directing them all to the now-separate Legal Defense Fund. Carter held onto cases already in hand. In 1968, after the board fired NAACP staff attorney Lewis M. Steel without notice over a published article, Carter and his entire staff resigned in protest. He briefly recounts being approached by Adam Clayton Powell Jr. for money in exchange for a judgeship, which Carter refused, and credits the resulting fight for Marshall's later Second Circuit appointment.

clips.subject: Robert L. Carter
clips.title: Northern Segregation and Obama Reflection
clips.summary: Carter argues segregation was a national phenomenon, not a southern problem. He took on Ocean Hill-Brownsville and Intermediate School 201 cases to attack northern de facto school segregation. He observes voluntary segregation persists with no public push to end it, hoping a movement will eventually arise. Asked about Obama's 2008 election, Carter says his focus was always equal educational opportunity rather than a black presidency, and describes himself as too old and mentally fatigued to do further substantive work, content to leave the next push to younger people.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Interview Opening
clips.summary: Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries introduces the March 9, 2013 interview in Albany, Georgia for the Civil Rights History Project, a joint Smithsonian NMAAHC and Library of Congress undertaking. He identifies videographer Petna Ndaliko and welcomes Robert McClary.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project oral history series

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Birth and Early Family
clips.summary: McClary states he was born at Phoebe Putney Hospital on October 19. He references his mother's death and his father taking a job on the river before the family moved. Much of this passage is unrecoverable in the transcript due to severe automatic-speech-recognition degradation.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Father's Confrontation in South Carolina
clips.summary: McClary recounts that his father had a physical confrontation with a white man in South Carolina before leaving the state. He later reports uncertain news that his father is dead. The transcript preserves only fragments of this account.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Schooling and Early Desegregation
clips.summary: McClary describes attending school and references early local desegregation attempts involving Black families. He mentions a young woman who had a confrontation with a white man on a bus. The passage is heavily corrupted by transcription failure.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Entry into the Albany Movement
clips.summary: McClary states that he was already involved in the movement before the period he had been describing. He references the formation of the Albany Movement and his initial contact with Charles Sherrod.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Southwest Georgia County Strategy
clips.summary: McClary describes Charles Sherrod's effort to organize roughly nine counties in Southwest Georgia, including a plan to take over one of the counties and elect officials locally. He notes that Sherrod was an activist by disposition and describes joining the project formally.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Tenants and Welfare-Rights Work
clips.summary: After a brief technical break, McClary describes movement activities centered on tenants and welfare casework. He references advocating for residents who were being denied welfare and filing appeals when claims were taken down. Specific details are largely lost to transcription degradation.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Casino Desegregation Attempt
clips.summary: McClary recounts an attempt with a white activist visiting from New York to desegregate a casino-named cafe in his hometown. He describes the proprietor's reaction and the standoff at the establishment. The exchange is preserved only in fragmentary form.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Assault at the SNCC Office
clips.summary: McClary describes being attacked while sleeping in a SNCC office. He recounts being struck and ending up on the floor, then convincing his attacker he could not produce a specific person. A fellow staff member intervened, and the police took the attacker away.

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Norway School Board and First Black Mayor
clips.summary: McClary recalls a school-board confrontation in Norway, a small Southwest Georgia town, and the eventual election of the town's first Black mayor, who has since died. He describes mobilizing community members to the local board.
related_events: first Black mayor election in Norway

clips.subject: Robert McClary
clips.title: Closing Reflection on Hope and Fear
clips.summary: Asked whether he was ever afraid, McClary says he was more hopeful and excited than afraid. He returns to his father's example, noting that the older man would not back down from a physical confrontation. Jeffries closes the interview.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Grandfather Between Reservation and Packing House
clips.summary: Alexander introduces her grandfather, born 1842 and died 1942, who spoke Winnebago, Omaha, and Sioux, worked briefly for the Pony Express, and lived between the Omaha Reservation and Sioux City, Iowa. She describes her grandmother, a Scots-Irish woman who lived mostly on the reservation, and her father's birth in 1914 into a family of ten children where three sisters died young of flu, tuberculosis, and a household fire.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Father's IWW Initiation
clips.summary: Alexander's father, riding the rails to Minnesota at age 12 to work the harvest, was offered 50 cents a day; Scandinavian bunkhouse workers struck on his behalf to force the farmer to pay him a dollar a day. After a month he was given a red Industrial Workers of the World membership card for ten cents a month. Alexander connects this to her father's earlier exposure to multi-ethnic Sioux City packing-house strikes (Mexicans, Syrians, Poles, Russians, Black workers, Native workers) and Ku Klux Klan terror against strikers.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Mother's Vilna Lineage and the Communist Manifesto
clips.summary: Alexander describes her maternal grandmother, a Lithuanian Jewish woman from Vilna whose family was anti-Tsarist and immigrated to the United States after the 1905 revolution. Her Russian Jewish grandfather, a St. Louis shoe-repairman, disowned her mother for marrying a Black man, and her grandmother left him rather than disown her daughter. She also recounts her father's encounter as a young shoe-shine boy with a Communist Manifesto pamphlet that led to his joining the Young Communist League in California.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Civil Rights Congress and the McCarthy Era
clips.summary: Alexander describes her father Franklin Alexander's CIO organizing with cotton compress workers in California's Central Valley, his ILWU work with African American longshoremen, and his directorship of the Civil Rights Congress in Oakland in the late 1940s and early 1950s, where he worked on police brutality and housing rights and was attacked by the Oakland Tribune. She covers her father going underground during the McCarthy period, the family's relocation to her uncle's farm in Perris, California, and FBI vehicles tailing the family on rural roads.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Emmett Till and Los Angeles High
clips.summary: Alexander recalls her father's first explicit political conversation with her, prompted by the Emmett Till Jet magazine photographs, as her foundational political memory. She describes attending Anytown USA camp through the National Conference of Christians and Jews until her sister was removed as elected mayor after her father's HUAC subpoena appeared in the Los Angeles Times, and the subsequent shooting-into of the family's downtown LA shoe-repair shop across from City Hall. She traces her adolescence at Los Angeles High School and the participation in Woolworth's sit-in demonstrations in Los Angeles.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Berkeley and the Free Speech Movement
clips.summary: Alexander arrives at UC Berkeley in fall 1964 as a Spanish major and is taken in by Bettina Aptheker and the W.E.B. Du Bois Clubs of America circle through her father's connections to Jessica Mitford and other Bay Area activists. She is arrested in Sproul Hall during the Free Speech Movement at age 17, gives her age as 18 to avoid juvenile court, and speaks at FSM rallies alongside Mario Savio and Aptheker. She also references the earlier Sheraton Palace Hotel sit-in to integrate hotel staff.
keywords: W.E.B. Du Bois Clubs of America

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Deportation from Franco's Spain
clips.summary: Studying abroad in Madrid in 1966-67, Alexander joins Spanish students organizing anti-Vietnam War protest at the Facultad de Filosofía y Letras under director Carlos Blanco Aguinaga. After delivering a speech at a packed building under a "Yankis fuera de Vietnam" banner on April 28, 1967, she is arrested by plainclothes police at her apartment, held at the Puerta del Sol central jail where other prisoners sing "We Shall Overcome," then placed under house arrest. Spanish authorities tell her the U.S. State Department ordered her detention. She is escorted by Guardia Civil to Hendaye on the French border, where Blanco Aguinaga meets her, and CBS flies her to the Hôtel George V in Paris for a televised interview.
related_events: U.S. State Department coordination with Franco regime

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Black Panther Party National Office, 1969
clips.summary: Alexander joins the Black Panther Party in 1969, starting at the breakfast for school children program at an Oakland church where Charles Bursey served meals. Assigned to the Black Panther newspaper at the BPP national office in South Berkeley under editor Elbert "Big Man" Howard, she types articles on an IBM Justowriter and lays out pages at the San Francisco printers with artist Emory Douglas. She describes the BPP leadership tier (Bobby Seale, David Hilliard, Masai Hewitt, Huey P. Newton), persistent sexual harassment by leadership (naming David Hilliard specifically), and her speech on women's roles at the 1969 United Front Against Fascism conference where she first met Angela Davis.
related_events: BPP breakfast for school children program
United Front Against Fascism conference summer 1969

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Japan Delegation and Leaving the Panthers
clips.summary: The BPP sends Alexander and Big Man Howard to Japan on Zengakuren invitations; on arrival they are briefly picked up by the Japanese Red Army before being placed with Ogawa Productions, a leftist film company. Over roughly five weeks they speak at mass demonstrations against the new airport on farmland, meet Korean immigrant workers, and address peasant farmers, financing their return tickets through paid interviews. Alexander returns to find the BPP newspaper photo of the metal-plated, sandbagged South Berkeley headquarters published alongside Berkeley police vamping plans. Citing infiltration, COINTELPRO, her unwillingness to die for the Party, and pregnancy, she walks away; David Hilliard later seizes her library.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Lumumba-Zapata College at UC San Diego
clips.summary: At age 23 Alexander is hired as the inaugural resident dean of UC San Diego's Third College, originally proposed as Lumumba-Zapata College by Angela Davis and Black and Chicano students. She describes a fishbowl of FBI-style infiltration of both the administration and the student body, including a prison-veteran cadre using intimidation tactics similar to those she had seen in the Panthers, and a gunpoint threat that drove her to Carlos Blanco Aguinaga's house. She quits after nine months and enters the UC San Diego comparative literature PhD program.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: City College Teaching and Labor Studies
clips.summary: Alexander earns her comparative literature PhD with a dissertation on the fictional portrayal of popular movements, declines a sole academic job offer, and teaches adult-school ESL for refugees from Vietnam for twelve years before being recruited to organize the ESL program at San Diego City College, where she works for twenty-five years. As English department chair she diversifies the faculty, and as labor studies program coordinator she introduces occupational safety and health classes and partnerships with the UCLA labor program and Center on Policy Initiatives.

clips.subject: Roberta Alexander
clips.title: Pillars of the Community and the San Diego 33
clips.summary: Alexander describes her current organizing with Pillars of the Community, a Muslim-led faith-based organization in Encanto/Southeast San Diego, focused on the San Diego 33 campaign against California Penal Code 182.5 gang-conspiracy prosecutions. She profiles Aaron Harvey and Brandon Duncan, two defendants arrested in 2014 under the statute (Duncan for rap lyrics), and a community writing program that culminated in publication of a nineteen-author collection through City Works Press, with a forward blurb solicited from Elbert "Big Man" Howard. She closes reflecting on her sister's medical career, her brother as a Kaiser chief medical officer, and her regret at never interviewing her mother.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Holmes County Childhood and Sharecropping
clips.summary: Head was born in Holmes County to a carpenter-sharecropper father and a mother who cooked for the plantation owner. She describes growing up in Lexington and Greenwood, walking five miles to a one-room church-school for all eight grades, and the family's truck patches, cows, horses, mules, chickens, and hogs.
related_events: Mississippi rural Black education in the 1940s

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Segregation and Christmas Settlement
clips.summary: Head describes early awareness of segregation through her mother's work at the plantation owner's house, where she could play outside with the white child Corinne but not enter. She recounts the annual Christmas-eve plantation settlement where her father was always told he had 'come out in the hole,' the company store at $35 monthly for twelve people, and her mother making dolls from flour and meal sacks.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Emmett Till and Leaving the Plantation
clips.summary: Head describes hearing about Emmett Till's murder on the radio at the store of Carolyn and Roy Bryant, where she went to school nearby. She recounts her father's confrontation with the plantation owner who tried to coerce Black women into the office to collect paychecks, and the family's overnight escape to Tchula after the boss seized his car keys.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Movement Entry through Greenwood SNCC Meetings
clips.summary: Beginning in 1963-64, Head began attending civil rights meetings in Greenwood where she met SNCC organizers including John Ball, Sam Block, Stokely Carmichael, Bob Moses, Lawrence Guyot, Willie Wazir Peacock, and Hollis Watkins. The meetings addressed segregated public accommodations, second-class citizenship, taxation without representation, and voter registration as the source of political power.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Hartman Turnbow and Holmes County Leadership
clips.summary: Head describes Hartman Turnbow as fearless and willing to draw his pistol at meetings to demonstrate self-defense, contrasting his style with quieter local leaders. She recalls Turnbow visiting her home in later years and predicting the rise of mass incarceration of young Black men.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: 1963 Freedom Vote Canvassing
clips.summary: Head canvassed Holmes County for the fall 1963 Freedom Vote with Aaron Henry and Ed King as the mock-election candidates, working through polling places in Thornton and Mileston. She describes white farmers cursing canvassers off their property, the climate of intimidation that left some Black residents fearful of being evicted from plantations, and Northern donors sending food and clothing to support displaced families.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Ku Klux Klan Intimidation at the Holmes County Center
clips.summary: Head recounts working nights at the Holmes County Community Center with a small staff including a worker named Frian from California, and being shot at by Klan trucks circling the building. She describes the firebombs ('cocktails') thrown at activist houses, and adults taking turns sitting up at night to watch the family home.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Freedom Summer 1964 in Holmes County
clips.summary: Head describes the 1964 Freedom Summer in Holmes County, where about 30 volunteers worked on voter registration, freedom schools, and the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party. She recounts marching from Mississippi Valley State to Jackson during a period of Movement-related murder, with white spectators throwing rocks from boxcars while highway patrol watched.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Building the Mileston Community Center
clips.summary: Abe Osheroff came from California to build the Mileston Community Center; Head's father helped build it. After construction, the gas company refused service and the telephone line was denied, requiring wood heaters. Highway patrol parked at the railroad crossing to ticket and intimidate visitors. The Medical Committee for Human Rights, including Josephine Disparti, Helen Richardson, and Dr. Albert Vorspan, established a clinic in a small room they built onto the Center.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Managing the Mileston Center and Pre-Head Start Kindergarten
clips.summary: When Henry and Sue Lorenzi prepared to leave Mileston, they asked Head to manage the Community Center. She handled records, newsletters, county-wide communications, and supervised four young women on staff. Before federal Head Start funding arrived, the Center ran a kindergarten using a truck donated by Abe Osheroff to collect children from across the county, with sewing machines for making children's clothes.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Voter Registration and the Voting Rights Act
clips.summary: Head recounts being locked in a small registration room at the courthouse with a guard dog at the door for four hours after completing the literacy test. She describes the chancery clerk lecturing her about how her grandparents would never have come to vote. Following the 1965 Voting Rights Act, federal registrars arrived and the family learned they had passed the literacy test a year earlier but had never been notified.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Mobilizing the Vote: 1966 and Bernice Montgomery
clips.summary: Head describes the 1966 campaign for the congressional race where Mr. Hayes was running. She organized car pools, made the Center a calling hub, and coordinated countywide pickups. She notes that Black professionals, teachers and preachers, were largely absent from the early movement, fearing job loss, with the schoolteacher Bernice Montgomery as a notable exception who attended meetings from the beginning.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Robert Clark's 1967 Legislative Election
clips.summary: Head describes serving as receiving-and-returning manager at the polls in 1967 when Robert Clark won election to the Mississippi state legislature, the first African American legislator in the twentieth century. She recalls a drunk white truck driver coming to remove his wife from polling duties, and the routine intimidation of rough-looking white men parking themselves at the door to scare away Black voters.
related_events: Robert Clark Mississippi state legislature election 1967

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: CDGM and Tchula Bank Refusal
clips.summary: Head describes the Child Development Group of Mississippi (CDGM) Head Start program brought by Sue and Henry Lorenzi, with training in Mound Bayou and program management with Polly Greenberg and Tom Levin. The Tchula bank initially refused to cash CDGM payroll checks; a supermarket owner finally intervened. She recounts overhearing the Lexington merchant Cohen and others discussing how the program would 'hurt' Black residents who weren't 'used to suffering.'
related_events: Child Development Group of Mississippi founding 1965

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: Head Start Pedagogy and the Greyhound to Washington
clips.summary: Head describes the Mileston Head Start program: bathing children in the Center's shower (many had never used indoor plumbing), teaching them to write their names, color recognition, and basic literacy through experiential outdoor counting exercises. When Senator John Stennis blocked CDGM refunding, the program continued unfunded for three months; Head's children rode a chartered Greyhound bus to Washington DC with teacher Zelma to lobby congressmen, leading to renewed funding by May.

clips.subject: Rosie Head
clips.title: College, CDA Credential, and Continuing Work
clips.summary: Head describes her education: a two-year degree in 1978, a Child Development Associate (CDA) credential, and subsequent college coursework. She describes her diabetes diagnosis interrupting her studies, and continued Head Start work seven years to the date of the interview, with Dr. McLean advising her on degree-completion at a Delta college.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Family, Father, and the Army Years
clips.summary: Sales describes her father, son of a formerly enslaved woman, who joined the First Assisi Civilian Conservation Camp, became a Baptist minister and Army chaplain, and was radicalized by the Korean War. She introduces her mother as someone who refused to submit to oppression and recounts her grandmother's town where Sales relatives constituted thirty percent of the population.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Fort Benning and Childhood in Columbus
clips.summary: Sales contrasts the integrated world of Fort Benning, ten miles from Columbus, Georgia (with integrated buses, movie theaters, restaurants, and hospital under Truman's military integration) with the segregated civil society outside the base. She describes childhood acts of resistance, trying on bathing suits in white-only stores, sitting at the front of buses, that preceded any conscious political awakening.
related_events: Truman desegregation of the armed forces

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Carver High School and Southern Black Education
clips.summary: Sales describes Mother Mary Mission, then Carver High School in the Carver Heights subdivision of Columbus, Georgia. Principal S.P. Charleston and home teacher Marian Armstrong cultivated academic excellence and connected students to what Sales calls 'the project of freedom.' She characterizes the school as a counterculture of education built inside segregation, and identifies herself as a cheerleader, newspaper editor, Honor Society member, and rebel.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Tuskegee Institute and Movement Entry
clips.summary: Sales arrives at Tuskegee Institute in fall 1963 at sixteen, intending to be a beatnik reading Virginia Woolf and quoting Allen Ginsberg. Gwen Patton brings Montgomery-bus-boycott movement consciousness to campus; Professor Charles Gomillion's gerrymandering challenge is part of the local atmosphere. Stokely Carmichael and Lowndes County residents visit campus and galvanize Sales, who cuts her ponytails and grows out her natural hair.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Bloody Sunday Aftermath at the Montgomery Capitol
clips.summary: Following Selma's Bloody Sunday on the Edmund Pettus Bridge, Tuskegee students protest at the Montgomery Capitol and are locked in a church, surrounded by mounted officers with billy clubs and denied food and water. Sales loses her Southern Baptist faith waiting for a chariot of deliverance that never comes. She witnesses a SNCC woman face down a billy club, calls it the moment her 'little Columbus Georgia mind is blown,' and commits to the movement.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Lowndes County and the Freedom House
clips.summary: Sales describes living in the SNCC freedom house in Lowndes County, where men and women slept in the same room as comrades rather than as sexual partners. She recounts the first day registering voters with Stokely Carmichael and Mary Nell Mosley, when the sheriff drew a gun and threatened to blow Carmichael's brains out, and Carmichael's reply that 'hell will be an integrated hell tonight.'

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Local Leaders: Hulett, Clara Maul, Mr. Jackson
clips.summary: Sales profiles John Hulett, an uneducated Lowndes County resident whom she contrasts with the college-educated leaders of her hometown, noting his consistent reliability and his refusal to hold the microphone. She describes Clara Maul, whose grandmother's relationship with a wealthy white man and ownership of a bar gave Maul a measure of protection that she used to drive SNCC organizers across the county at one hundred miles per hour.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Fort Deposit Demonstration and Hayneville Jail
clips.summary: After the August 6, 1965 Voting Rights Act signing, young people from Fort Deposit propose a demonstration. SNCC debates whether to include Jonathan Daniels and Father Richard Morrisroe; Stokely Carmichael argues that excluding them would segregate the movement, and that position prevails. The demonstrators face a mob with baseball bats and garbage pails, are arrested onto a garbage truck, and held in the Hayneville jail where women are threatened with rape by trustees.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: The Cash Store Killing
clips.summary: Released suddenly from jail, the group walks to the corner store. Tom Coleman stands in the doorway with a gun and tells Sales, who is in front, that he will blow her brains out. Jonathan Daniels pulls Sales backward and is shot dead; Father Morrisroe is shot next and left begging for water in the sun. Joyce Bailey, seventeen, escapes alongside Sales. SNCC, John Doar, and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson spend days locating the bodies.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Testifying at the Coleman Trial
clips.summary: Returning home, Sales finds her mother confronted in the backyard by white men threatening to kill Ruby if she testified; her mother nonetheless does not tell her to refuse. Sales testifies against one of Alabama's prominent defense attorneys, who attempts to insinuate that Sales was a whore and Daniels her white client. Sales describes shutting down emotionally for decades afterward, never mentioning her movement work at Princeton, Manhattanville, or in subsequent peace and justice work.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Tent City and the Lowndes County Freedom Organization
clips.summary: Sales describes Tent City, where sharecroppers evicted from their land for registering to vote lived in tents rather than retreat. She locates Tent City within what she calls the second indigenous Southern Freedom Movement, traces the origin of the black panther symbol to Lowndes County before the Black Panther Party, and connects the work to lessons drawn from the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Southern Freedom Movement and Historical Narrative
clips.summary: Sales argues for the term 'Southern Freedom Movement' over 'civil rights movement' because the latter obscures the violence of segregation, the routine rape of black girls, and the long arc of black struggle. She critiques historians who frame Martin Luther King's training at Crozer and Boston University while missing the Southern preaching tradition King used in the pulpit (Vanessa Siddle Walker, Ralph Ellison, Mordecai Wyatt Johnson).

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Language: 'Enslavement' and 'SNCC'
clips.summary: Sales recounts correcting the defense attorney at the Coleman trial who referred to the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee as 'SNCC', a familiar form she felt he had no standing to use. As a history major at Princeton, she pushes for 'enslavement' over 'slavery' and for naming white enslavers as the active subjects of the historical record, against a discipline that names only the oppressed.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: Memory, Dismembering, and the Cultural Wars
clips.summary: Sales develops her concept of 'dismembering', the deliberate erasure of black agency in the historical record, and links it to the post-1960s cultural wars that turned liberation theology into prosperity gospel, fired 38,000 black teachers across thirteen Southern states between 1950 and 1962, and built the prison industrial complex. She cites David Cecelski's work on the destruction of black schools.

clips.subject: Ruby Sales
clips.title: SpiritHouse and Counterculture Education
clips.summary: Sales describes the SpiritHouse Project, drawing on the Greek pneuma (breath) to define social justice as how we live in relation to each other. Two recent years have focused on the counterculture of black Southern education during segregation, interviewing surviving teachers about their pedagogical strategies and connecting public schooling to the survival of HBCUs threatened with absorption into struggling white institutions.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Childhood in Segregated Americus
clips.summary: Mahone describes growing up in Americus, Sumter County, Georgia, born in 1945 to Burgin Mahone, a basket-factory worker, and Lula Belle Walker Mahone, a domestic. He recounts the visible markers of Jim Crow segregation: outdoor toilets until his senior year of high school, parents who avoided eye contact with whites, insurance agents who walked into Black homes uninvited, and his parents' fear-based admonitions about knowing one's place.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Americus Voters League and Early Activism
clips.summary: Around 1961, while in tenth grade, Mahone joined the Americus Voters League, a minister-led voter registration project that predated SNCC's arrival in town. He talked to fellow students about voter registration and worked outside jobs including picking cotton, peanuts, and peaches. His parents were quietly supportive though fearful; his older siblings had left home for the military.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: SNCC Arrives in Americus
clips.summary: In late 1962, Don Harris, John Perdew, and Ralph Allen arrived in Americus, dispatched from Albany by project director Charles Sherrod. Mahone describes the SNCC house as a hangout where students printed leaflets on mimeograph and hand-crank machines, encountered Black authors like Richard Wright and James Baldwin for the first time, and trained in nonviolent self-defense techniques including how to curl into a fetal position when beaten.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Movie Theater Arrest and the Hole
clips.summary: Mahone describes the 1963 protest at the downtown Americus movie theater, where eleven students attempted to buy tickets at the front entrance instead of using the side-alley Black entrance. After a second arrest a week later, four of the original eleven were sent to the Sumter County prison farm. Mahone and another student were placed in the Hole, a 4-by-6-foot concrete enclosure, where they conducted a seven-day hunger strike until they passed out.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Sheriff Chappell and Jail Conditions
clips.summary: Mahone identifies Sheriff Fred Chappell, who jailed Dr. King in 1961, as the figure King called the meanest man in the world. He describes daily forced labor on Americus streets and at the city sewage treatment plants, contact with passersby who relayed movement news, and the relationships formed with non-movement Black inmates who treated the young protesters as little brothers.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Joining SNCC and the Hasty House Attack
clips.summary: After high school, Mahone formally joined SNCC and continued voter registration across surrounding counties. On the night the Civil Rights Bill was signed in July 1964, he, John Perdew, Bob Mants, and other students attempted to test the law at the Hasty House restaurant in Americus. They were attacked by a group of white men with tire irons and baseball bats; one of their party who had gone to the outside restroom was separately beaten.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: The Americus Four Sedition Case
clips.summary: In 1963, during a night demonstration, Don Harris, John Perdew, Ralph Allen of SNCC, and CORE worker Zev Aelony were arrested and charged under a century-old Georgia sedition law carrying the death penalty. They were held roughly 85 days before a three-judge federal panel released them. The legal team included C.B. King of Albany, Donald Hollowell of Atlanta, and Constance Baker. Mahone notes that had the state prevailed, police nationwide could have detained movement workers indefinitely.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Leesburg Stockade and the Stolen Girls
clips.summary: As the Americus jails filled with demonstrators, juvenile girls under sixteen were taken in a tractor trailer in the middle of the night to the abandoned Leesburg, Georgia prison camp. The stockade had one toilet for 35-40 girls, no bedding, and no showers. Mahone recounts a guard throwing a rattlesnake into the cell, white men coming to the jail seeking access to the girls, and the parents' days-long ignorance of where their daughters had been taken.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Maids Union and Segregated Voting Lines
clips.summary: Mahone describes 1964-65 organizing work: a Maids Union formed by mothers of the incarcerated girls to demand better wages, a 20,000-book community library called the U-Center housed in the former Colored Hospital, and a quilting union. In 1965, Mary Kate Bell Fishbel qualified as the first Black woman for the Sumter County Democratic primary; she and three others (Mamie Campbell, Gloria Wise, Lena Turner) were jailed for trying to vote in the white line. A federal judge released them and declared segregated voting lines in Sumter unconstitutional.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Tougaloo and Warren Fortson
clips.summary: In 1966 Mahone moved to Mississippi on a SNCC scholarship to Tougaloo College, where he protested the war in Vietnam. He also describes Warren Fortson, a white Americus lawyer and school-board head who defended Roberta Freeman (one of the first Black students to integrate Americus High) and her boyfriend on fabricated charges, only to be hounded out of town by the Klan. Fortson's brother Ben Fortson was Georgia Secretary of State.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Archives Work and Military Service
clips.summary: Through Warren Fortson's referral, Mahone took an apprenticeship in paper conservation at the Georgia Department of Archives and stayed roughly 12-15 years. Drafted in 1969, he was held at Fort Benning for a year because his Americus arrest record blocked security clearance, performing menial duty work alongside Vietnam-draft refusers. Shipped to a US Army Materiel Command post outside Paris, France for the rest of his service, he took up photography on weekends and was discharged in 1971.

clips.subject: Sam Mahone
clips.title: Ancestral Arts Gallery and Movement Memory
clips.summary: Mahone opened Ancestral Arts Gallery in Atlanta as a venue for African American artists marginalized by traditional galleries, also using it for poetry sessions and public meetings. In 2007, with other Americus Movement veterans, he convened a biennial reunion at Bottoms Funeral Home, whose director had been the sole bail bondsman for the movement. The group collects photographs, newsletters (including the Voice of Americus mimeograph), and reproductions from the Atlanta Journal, Columbus Ledger-Enquirer, Macon Telegraph, and Atlanta Daily World, working toward a museum and an oral history project with the local university in Jimmy Carter's hometown.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Worth County Childhood
clips.summary: Sam Young, Jr. introduces himself as born March 13, 1950 in Worth County, Sylvester, Georgia. He describes his father, Sam Young, Sr., as politically engaged and committed to keeping the family informed about civil rights developments, including Brown v. Board of Education, well before Young could understand them.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Freedom of Choice Integration
clips.summary: Young describes the Freedom of Choice school-integration regime, the daily walk to segregated schools alongside roads where white school buses carried students who shouted racial slurs and where Black children responded by throwing rocks. He recalls turpentine-time work in the surrounding pine woods, his father holding him back to repeat second grade so he and his sister could go through school together, and the strict Black teachers who tolerated no cheating.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Racial Violence in Worth County
clips.summary: Young recounts three incidents that shaped his understanding of racial terror: the vandalism of Blue Spring Baptist Church in Worth Church, painted black and labeled 'the devil's den' by local white youth; the arrest of his two cousins from a Worth County High School bus and the sheriff's three-day refusal to disclose their whereabouts until attorney C.B. King threatened the sheriff with arrest; and the shotgun assault on C.T. Rockwell by white employer Albert Gill, triggered by Rockwell's refusal to call Gill's teenage son 'Mister.'

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Family Farm and ASCS Foreclosures
clips.summary: Young describes growing up the oldest son in a family of ten sisters and seven brothers on a Worth County farm, planting cotton, corn, peanuts, squash, butter beans, peas, and okra. His father's farm loans were foreclosed and the family was sustained only by a local white grocer who extended a personal line of credit. Young identifies the ASCS (Agricultural Stabilization and Conservation Service), now Farm Service Agency, as the federal mechanism that foreclosed on Black farmers in the area.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Firestone and Entry to SWGP
clips.summary: Young graduates from Worth County High School in 1968 as the only male in a class of about five. His first job was at the Firestone Tire construction site in Albany, where supervisors were harsh and he was assigned to break up concrete foundations with hand tools. Let go along with other workers, he learned from his two older sisters about the Southwest Georgia Project for Community Education and joined as a young recruit.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Sherrod, Mants, and Battle as Mentors
clips.summary: Young identifies his three mentors in the Southwest Georgia Project: Charles Sherrod, Robert Mants, and Randolph Battle. He describes attending Albany Technical College for graphic arts on a SWGP scholarship to learn printing-press operation in service of a community newspaper. He also names attorney Mary Young as a major champion of civil rights cases in the Albany area, who began her practice with C.B. King.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: New Communities Inc Land Project
clips.summary: Young describes Charles Sherrod's New York research trip that located the large tract of land for sale in Southwest Georgia, and the project's purpose: a collective Black-owned land base for community self-sufficiency. The land was secured in 1968. Young married in 1974 and moved his wife and children onto the site, where he operated farm activities. The site had over 300 acres of peanut allotment plus corn, okra, watermelons, and other vegetables, and existing dwellings that housed multiple Black families and outside students.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Pigford Lawsuit and Land Loss
clips.summary: Young describes the class-action suit in which the federal government finally admitted ASCS discrimination against Black farmers. The suit was filed sometime in the late 1980s or mid-1990s, but the final settlement payment did not come through until 2009. By then New Communities Inc had been disqualified as a farm and lost its land. Shirley and Charles Sherrod used settlement funds to purchase a new tract of land in (likely) Dougherty County to continue the project's work.

clips.subject: Sam Young, Jr.
clips.title: Reflection and Synectics Training
clips.summary: Young reflects that being part of the Southwest Georgia Project gave him access to travel and training that other rural Georgia youth never received. He describes a Synectics problem-solving and communication-skills training in New York where participants were recorded on camera while developing group solutions to scenarios. He closes by urging that issues be addressed in the moment rather than deferred, and by offering his testimony as one small piece of the larger Movement story.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Family Origins and Morehouse Lineage
clips.summary: McKinney introduces his parents: father Wade Hampton McKinney, born outside Gainesville, Georgia, in 1892, who entered Atlanta Baptist College (later Morehouse) in 1910 and graduated in 1920, and mother Annie Ruth Berry McKinney of Birmingham, Alabama, who entered Spelman in 1916. He traces the founding of Morehouse (1867 in Augusta, named for Henry Lyman Morehouse in 1914) and Spelman (founded by Packard and Giles with Laura Spelman Rockefeller funding) within the post-Reconstruction Atlanta Baptist educational network.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Booker T. Washington and Black Land Ownership
clips.summary: McKinney recounts his maternal grandfather's response to Booker T. Washington's 'let down your bucket where you are' message: acquiring land in Alabama and distributing it to his children. He links this to Dr. King's later Selma-to-Montgomery march stopping at Black-owned land along the route, and contrasts his maternal Baptist-minister grandfather with his paternal sharecropper grandfather.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Antioch Baptist and the Social Gospel
clips.summary: His father took Antioch Baptist Church in Cleveland in 1928 and served there until his death in January 1963. McKinney describes the social-gospel tradition rooted in Luke 4 and Isaiah 61, traced through Rochester Theological Seminary and Walter Rauschenbusch, who died while his father was a student but whose mark remained on Morehouse-trained ministers.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Morehouse with Martin Luther King Jr.
clips.summary: McKinney entered Morehouse in 1944 at 17 under President Benjamin Elijah Mays. His freshman class included 15-year-old Martin Luther King Jr., admitted via Georgia's wartime 11th-grade testing program. McKinney describes King as 'Squirt,' a 'city student' who drove to campus, who participated in every oratorical contest as a student but never won one, and who went on to Crozer Theological Seminary and Boston University.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Cleveland Childhood and Civil Rights Influences
clips.summary: McKinney recalls growing up in Cleveland from age 18 months, watching his father confront injustice and hosting figures including A. Philip Randolph, Walter White of the NAACP, Mordecai Wyatt Johnson, and Adam Clayton Powell Jr. He describes the Future Outlook League's 'don't patronize where you cannot work' boycotts, including the Cleveland Transit System and milk-delivery campaigns.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Segregated Army Air Corps Service
clips.summary: After basic training at Sheppard Field in Wichita Falls, Texas, McKinney was shipped to Yuma, Arizona. On the rail journey through West Texas he witnessed German prisoners of war eating in the white dining room with tablecloths and silverware while Black U.S. soldiers in uniform ate from cardboard plates in the colored dining room. He was assigned to Squadron F, the segregated Black unit. The G.I. Bill (one year for any service member active September 2, 1945, plus one month per month of service up to 48 months) funded his and his brother's college completion.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Call to Ministry and Joe Louis Era
clips.summary: McKinney shifted from pre-law to ministry under the influence of Morehouse Bible instructor George Kelsey, who used the metaphor of the law functioning at the back end while religion functions at the front end. He recalls Cleveland's collective listening to Joe Louis fights, particularly the Schmeling defeat that the Jewish community celebrated as a defeat of one of Hitler's boys, and the presence of Jesse Owens's family in the Cleveland neighborhood. Larry Doby's integration of the American League Cleveland Indians similarly mobilized the community.
related_events: Joe Louis vs Max Schmeling fight

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Inviting Dr. King to Seattle in 1961
clips.summary: McKinney invited Martin Luther King Jr. to speak in Seattle in 1961 for the Mount Zion Baptist Church Brotherhood. With the 1962 World's Fair construction making facilities scarce, the Presbytery of Seattle directed him to First Presbyterian Church downtown. After an antecedent agreement, the ruling body of First Presbyterian withdrew the venue in writing, citing protocol. McKinney refused their offered reimbursement, the event moved to an alternative downtown venue, and 37 to 38 years later, just before his 1998 retirement, First Presbyterian sent a formal letter of apology.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Reparations and James Forman's Black Manifesto
clips.summary: Asked about James Forman's 1969 Black Manifesto's call for reparations, McKinney connects reparations to apology as a necessary part of healing. He references the Japanese American internment as a domestic precedent for apology plus financial redress, noting Japanese farmlands taken near Boeing Field and Puyallup after Pearl Harbor.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Pacific Northwest Black History
clips.summary: McKinney narrates the Oregon Black exclusion law (not repealed until 1926), the Louisiana Purchase and the dispute over slavery in new territory, and the George Washington Bush story: a Black wagon-train leader from Missouri who, denied permanent residence in the Oregon Territory, crossed the Columbia River to Fort Vancouver and settled near Lacey/Olympia. He links Bush's son's service in the last territorial and first state legislatures of Washington to the 49th-parallel resolution of the U.S.-British Columbia boundary and the Pig War on the San Juan Islands. He also traces coal mining at Roslyn, where Black scab labor was brought to break unions, and Kennydale, with a coal vein running under Puget Sound.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Arriving in Seattle and the Juneteenth Factor
clips.summary: McKinney accepted the call to Mount Zion in late November 1957 and began the first Sunday in February 1958. He draws a migration map by region for Black churches he served: Providence Rhode Island drew from Virginia in World War I and North Carolina in World War II; Cleveland from Georgia, Alabama, Kentucky, and Tennessee; Detroit from many places via the car industry; Chicago from Mississippi; and Seattle from west of the Mississippi (Louisiana, Texas, Arkansas, Oklahoma). He introduces what he calls the 'Juneteenth factor' learned in Texas during basic training: Texas slaves did not learn of emancipation until June 19, 1865, nearly two and a half years after the Emancipation Proclamation took effect.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Frederick & Nelson and Central Area Civil Rights Committee
clips.summary: McKinney describes the Seattle march on Frederick & Nelson department store that opened Black employment, with Rev. John Adams of First AME serving as a leader of the Central Area Civil Rights Committee (CACRC), an umbrella body drawing NAACP, Urban League, and (later) CORE. He cites the school strike as one of its actions and notes that Black Panther organizing in 1968-69 created tension with the established ministry leadership.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Liberty Bank, OIC, and the McKinney Manor
clips.summary: McKinney describes parallel work of protest and building: Mount Zion's credit union, land acquisition for parking and church expansion, the construction of the sanctuary (with American Baptist Home Mission Society support after local banks refused), the Samuel Berry McKinney Manor housing project funded under government programs, the Seattle Opportunities Industrialization Center at 22nd and Jackson based on Rev. Leon H. Sullivan's Philadelphia model, the Central Area Motivation Program, and his role in organizing Liberty Bank.

clips.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
clips.title: Reflections on Obama, Coalition, and the Future
clips.summary: McKinney closes by reflecting on changes in the Central Area's demographics with African immigrant communities arriving, the inadequacy of treating Hispanic as a single category, and his political analysis of the Obama presidency, the resistance he faced, and the necessity of multiracial coalitions of Black, Latino, and Asian communities against efforts to divide them.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Chicago Origins and Family Lineage
clips.summary: Bates introduces his Evanston, Illinois upbringing and his family's Chicago-lawyer lineage tracing back to his great-uncle Leonard Swett, Lincoln's friend. He explains he attended Carleton College for its affordability and absence of fraternities, contrasting it with Northwestern across the street from his home.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: WWII French Interpreter in Normandy
clips.summary: Drafted in 1942, Bates trained in French at the University of Chicago's ASTP program, landed at Utah Beach on D-Day, advanced through Saint-Lo and the Battle of Falaise toward Avranches, and worked as a French interpreter for military government. He recounts the Battle of the Bulge and being held back from it to govern occupied Germany.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Race in Military Government France
clips.summary: Stationed at Wismes, France supervising legal military-government brothels, Bates describes the segregated arrangement of two supervised white brothels for white troops and unsupervised brothels for Black troops, his MP partnership with a Lincoln University sophomore, and his first close interactions with Black soldiers as fellow English majors discussing literature.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Return to Carleton, PhD, Arrival in Sewanee
clips.summary: Bates returned on the GI Bill to Carleton College, earned a PhD in French literature at the University of Wisconsin, and accepted a one-year French teaching post at Sewanee, Tennessee in 1954, the year of the Brown decision. He describes the all-white, all-male, all-Episcopal student body and faculty he arrived to.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Episcopal Seminary Desegregation
clips.summary: Bates describes how the closure of Black Episcopal seminaries in the South forced white seminaries including Seabury-Western and Garrett-Evangelical to accept Black would-be priests, and how the resulting Episcopal Church desegregation drove a Sewanee faculty walkout in 1953. He names V. Gene Robinson, Sewanee class of 1969 and later the first openly gay Episcopal bishop, as a graduate.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Fellowship of Reconciliation and War Resisters League
clips.summary: Bates recounts joining the Chicago chapter of the Fellowship of Reconciliation for anti-Vietnam War work under John Swomley, then moving to the War Resisters League for its non-Christian breadth. He describes Bayard Rustin contacting him about his anti-war poetry collection, leading to thirty years of poems in the War Resisters League calendar and publication of Poems of War Resistance, selling 13,000 copies.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Sewanee Faculty Culture and the Sewanee Review
clips.summary: Bates describes the Sewanee faculty's English department as hitched to the Sewanee Review, the country's oldest continuous literary magazine, and discusses the unitarian and ex-Episcopal-priest currents within the liberal-arts faculty. He covers the long struggle to admit women and Black students, finally achieved in 1969.
related_events: first Black graduate of the University of the South

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Highlander Folk School Origins and Myles Horton
clips.summary: Bates traces Myles Horton's path from East Tennessee sharecropping background through Union Theological Seminary under Reinhold Niebuhr, study of Danish folk schools, and work with Jane Addams in Chicago, ending in Grundy County, Tennessee where Lillian Johnson donated the founding Monteagle property. He describes Grundy County as one of the poorest counties in the country, with strong Klan presence, moonshine, and Al Capone trucking.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Highlander Workshop Methodology
clips.summary: Bates describes Horton's facilitation style at Highlander workshops, including the meeting that brought University of Chattanooga and University of Tennessee Knoxville white students together with Tougaloo Black students to plan integration, and Horton's directive at a later workshop that Stokely Carmichael, John Lewis, and other student activists had to do it themselves, a turning point preceding the founding of SNCC at Shaw University.
related_events: SNCC founding at Shaw University Raleigh 1960

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Sewanee NAACP and Septima Clark
clips.summary: Bates recounts founding the Sewanee NAACP chapter under Septima Poinsette Clark's supervision when she was Highlander's education director, and describes Clark's desegregation of the Sewanee Chapel by attending a service with another Black woman, causing half the white congregation to leave permanently. He names Mrs. Fowler, a Black woman from Sewanee, as the local NAACP chapter leader beginning 1967.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Avon Williams and Franklin County Trials
clips.summary: Bates describes Avon N. Williams Jr. defending Sewanee desegregation activists in Franklin County court every week for two years, in a To Kill a Mockingbird-style atmosphere where audience members were armed and police prevented a massacre. He covers FBI surveillance of Highlander, John Birch Society organizing on the Sewanee campus, and the sit-in protests at Tubbies that drew FBI investigation of the activists rather than the segregated bar.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Highlander Workshop with King and Rosa Parks
clips.summary: Bates places himself in the photograph of the 1957 Highlander workshop with Martin Luther King Jr. and Rosa Parks, the image used by segregationists on Communist Training School billboards across the South. He describes Highlander's loss of its Monteagle property after state seizure, the burning of Horton's stone house by Presbyterians from a neighboring parish, and the surviving cemetery containing the Horton family and May Justice.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Highlander in National Memory and French Pedagogy
clips.summary: Bates reflects on Highlander's long erasure from American history until the Obama era, the institutional opposition he faced from Sewanee's Red Dean for two decades, and his use of French literature pedagogy (Victor Hugo's anti-gun-control poem, Marquis de Sade's banned French texts, Restif de la Bretonne's Anti-Justine) to introduce Sewanee students to French radicalism.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Esau Jenkins, Guy Carawan, and Sea Islands Work
clips.summary: Bates recounts traveling to Knoxville for meetings with Esau Jenkins and Andrew Young at a black Presbyterian church, taking John's Island Black children on a Charleston bus tour where white kids harassed them, and Septima Clark teaching the children to ignore the harassment. He describes Guy Carawan's residency in Charleston and the planning meeting at the Myrtle Beach Black section ringed by police cars for an entire day.

clips.subject: Scott Bates
clips.title: Highlander Board Service Across Decades
clips.summary: Bates describes his board service since 1958, including the relocation that made meetings a six-hour drive, his presidency through the 1960s, and his reform of board procedures away from typed reports back to oral discussion. He notes Southern Echo's leader as a fellow board emeritus and the continuing fusion of Black civil-rights work with gay-rights action at Highlander as the same freedom movement.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Hawkins-Family Settlement
clips.summary: Sherrod opens with the genealogy of her family's land in Baker County, Georgia, traced through the 1870 census to her grandmother's Hawkins family. Her grandmother and grandfather Julius Miller received 31 acres and accumulated enough land to give each of their 14 children a minimum 31-acre share, forming the Hawkins-family settlement.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Gator Johnson's Baker County
clips.summary: Sherrod describes Sheriff L. Warren 'Gator' Johnson's control of Baker County, including a speed-trap operation that the Atlanta Constitution later reported generated about $150,000 a year. She contrasts the Black-owned land of the Hawkins settlement with the major plantations in the county, including Ichauway Plantation owned by Robert Woodruff of Coca-Cola and Pineland Plantation owned by the Mellon family.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Segregated Schools and Cucumbers
clips.summary: Sherrod describes attending school in army barracks through fourth grade and then in Methodist churches while a new segregated school was built, moving into that building in April 1957. She also recounts the cucumber-picking work that distinguished children from Black-owned land from those on the plantations, and the everyday humiliations of hand-me-down textbooks and bus breakdowns.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Pre-Movement Voter Registration
clips.summary: Sherrod recounts the 1950s Baker County voter-registration efforts led by her uncle 'Uncle Josh,' Benjamin T. Coney, Ike King, and Carl Broadway, whose houses and cars were bombed and on whose property moonshine was planted. She describes her parents' own attempt to register, and the Farmers Home Administration's refusal to lend her father money to build a brick home because, he was told, a Black man could not borrow to build in brick.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: The Murder of Hosea Miller
clips.summary: On March 14, 1965, Sherrod's father Hosea Miller was shot by white farmer Cal Hall during a dispute over cattle that had wandered onto Miller property. He died on March 25, ten days after the shooting. A white grand jury refused to indict Cal Hall. In a later civil suit, attorney C.B. King attempted to seat jurors who would acknowledge that Black people tell the truth; the jury still returned for the defendant.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Commitment to the South
clips.summary: The night of her father's death, looking out at the moon, Sherrod made the private decision to abandon her plan to leave Georgia for the North and to remain in the South to work for change. She describes her first mass meeting, where she saw Black tenants from Ichauway Plantation and Pineland Plantation publicly committing to fight despite the risk. At 17 she joined the Baker County movement that began in June 1965.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Caravan to Washington
clips.summary: Sherrod describes secretly organizing a five-car caravan from Thankful Baptist Church to Washington to testify before members of Congress and march at the Justice Department about injustices in Baker County. The caravan routed east through Mitchell County, Georgia to avoid Gator Johnson's expected ambush. The trip also produced an injunction against Johnson that constrained his speed-trap operation.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: SNCC Break and the Southwest Georgia Project
clips.summary: Sherrod transferred from Fort Valley State to Albany State in 1967. She describes the 1966-67 SNCC transition from John Lewis to Stokely Carmichael and the Black Power directive that white organizers should leave. The Southwest Georgia organizers disagreed and incorporated independently as the Southwest Georgia Project to retain the white workers already in the field.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Marriage and Albany Office Firebombing
clips.summary: Sherrod married Charles Sherrod in 1966. In the summer of 1967, white men set their Albany house and SNCC newsletter office on fire while she was inside; the neighborhood child Grady Christian was on the property and walked the attackers around to identify her husband. The Sherrods left for Baker County that night, had a flat tire en route, and were unexpectedly helped by two white men who did not recognize Charles.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Mother Elected, Gator Retires
clips.summary: Eleven years after Hosea Miller's murder, Grace Hollis Miller ran for and won election as the first Black official in Baker County. The same election cycle saw Gator Johnson retire and his son 'Scrut' Johnson succeed him as sheriff. Sherrod describes a courthouse standoff on election night between Gator Johnson and Charles Sherrod, broken when Scrut Johnson came in and pulled his father away.
keywords: first Black elected official Baker County

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Founding New Communities
clips.summary: After tenants were evicted from white-owned land for movement participation, seven Southwest Georgia organizers traveled to Israel to study the Kibbutz model. Sherrod explains the group ultimately adopted the Moshav rather than the Kibbutz, because the Kibbutz required children to live apart from parents. New Communities incorporated as a land trust on a 6,000-acre plot north of Albany, with Slater King arranging year-one financing through Prudential Insurance after Governor Lester Maddox blocked OEO funding.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Innovative Farm Operations
clips.summary: Sherrod describes the New Communities farm as a cooperative enterprise organized through a farm committee that included all farm workers plus three board members and the farm manager. The operation included a 75-head brood-sow operation with on-farm processing of cured meats, sugar-cane syrup pressed on-site, an old-fashioned smokehouse on U.S. Highway 19, muscadine grape production in the 1970s before others in the region attempted it, and a community-run land trust where neither Sherrod nor her husband occupied the largest house.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Federation of Southern Cooperatives
clips.summary: After the 1985 foreclosure of New Communities, Sherrod entered the Rural Development Leadership Network institute at UC Davis and was sponsored by the Federation of Southern Cooperatives, which then hired her in July 1985. The land was sold at auction in September 1985; the new owner razed the houses and the Highway 19 farmers market. Sherrod organized Black farmers across the South for the Federation and pursued an MBA at Valdosta State through Albany State extension classes.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Origins of Pigford
clips.summary: Sherrod describes the meeting at which the Federation, the Land Loss Prevention Project in North Carolina, and the Farmers Legal Action Group in Minnesota decided to pursue a discrimination lawsuit against USDA. After a Texas attorney intercepted the original six test cases (he was later disbarred), the Pigford case surfaced. The plaintiffs settled rather than going to trial because most farmers lacked complete documentation; Track A claimants needed minimal proof for a $50,000 payout and debt-write-off, while Track B claimants pursued individualized hearings.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: New Communities Pigford Victory
clips.summary: Driving back from Eufaula, Alabama, Sherrod realized New Communities was still farming in 1981 and could itself file a Pigford claim. Reviewing the Lee County courthouse records, the Sherrods documented that USDA had lent money to white plantation owners while denying their applications. The first 2002 hearing was lost when the hearing officer (the wife of the Pigford chief adjudicator) ruled against them. After a four-year monitor's-office review, the chief adjudicator ruled in July 2009 that New Communities would receive approximately $12 million.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Cypress Pond Plantation
clips.summary: With the Pigford proceeds, the Sherrods purchased Cypress Pond Plantation outside Albany, including an 1851 antebellum home, an 85-acre lake, cabins, and farming land. The property had been listed at $21 million and reduced to $6.9 million; the Sherrods negotiated to $4.5 million with equipment. Sherrod notes that the original owner had been the wealthiest slaveholder in Georgia, holding approximately 1,000 enslaved people across three properties; Jefferson Davis is said to have stayed at the property during Confederate succession planning.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Race, Class, and the Spooners
clips.summary: Sherrod reflects on her work helping white farmers after her experience helping Black farmers had become known. She names the Spooners as the first white family she helped save their farm in the 1980s, the same episode that would later surface in a controversial 2010 video clip. She frames the lesson as one about poverty rather than race, and on the gendered dynamics of being a woman organizing male farmers, including a Heifer Project hog-cooperative training incident in Harris County, Georgia.

clips.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
clips.title: Ben and Jerry's Pecan Partnership
clips.summary: Sherrod closes with the story of organizing Black pecan farmers to supply Ben and Jerry's ice cream after Rapunzel Page proposed at a farm conference that the company would buy product to address Black land loss. White processors offered tokenistic transactions; Ben and Jerry's brought in its own supplier, paid market-plus-premium, and Sherrod organized farmers to save half the premium toward a processing facility, which they acquired in 1997 and which is still operating, women-operated.
related_events: Ben and Jerry's pecan supply contract

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Family Origins and New York Childhood
clips.summary: McNichols traces his maternal grandparents' Rothschild-sponsored emigration from Riga and Marijampole to Palestine, where they founded Tel Aviv and built its first residential home. He describes his mother's classical violin training in Geneva under Joseph Szigeti, his father's St. Louis upbringing and self-taught electronics career at Picker X-Ray Corporation, and his own childhood as the only white child on a Puerto Rican block at 133rd Street while attending Dalton School on full scholarship.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Bipolar Diagnosis and Mother's Death
clips.summary: McNichols describes his mother's psychiatric breakdown in the Time-Life lobby when he was sixteen, his own undiagnosed bipolar condition that produced fifteen years of suicidal ideation, and the late-life lithium diagnosis at age 65 after a cardiologist's lab panel detected near-zero lithium serum levels. He recounts his mother's death from a botched brain-tumor operation after a decade of misdiagnosis.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: UCLA Politics and NSA-CIA Pipeline
clips.summary: McNichols arrives at UCLA in 1958, enters student politics in the Howard Berman/Henry Waxman orbit, and rises through National Student Association conferences. He retrospectively identifies the NSA international apparatus as a CIA front, naming Bob Kiley as former NSA president who became Richard Helms's special assistant at the CIA, and noting the Youth and Student Foundation as a transparent funding conduit.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Houston Freedom Ride Recruitment
clips.summary: McNichols describes how Houston Black attorneys George Washington Jr. and Hayma K., with bondsman Theodore Hugo Brooks's financial backing, persuaded CORE national director James Farmer to send Freedom Riders to Houston. He volunteers through Los Angeles CORE under Arnold Walters, describes the two-day training that focused entirely on white-mob attack scenarios rather than post-arrest jail conditions, and recounts the ticketing mix-up between Southern Pacific and Union Pacific stations.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Houston Arrest at Union Station
clips.summary: Eleven Freedom Riders from Los Angeles join with Progressive Youth Association members in Houston, totaling eighteen demonstrators. After meeting at a soul food restaurant, they form a line at the Union Station coffee shop, are denied entry, and are arrested for unlawful assembly when the proprietor opens the door. McNichols recounts an FBI agent who joined the train at El Paso and the cooperative Black Pullman porters who supplied the group with private car space.
related_events: Houston Union Station coffee shop sit-in

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Harris County Jail Beating
clips.summary: After processing at 2 a.m. in the Harris County jail, McNichols and three fellow Freedom Riders (Bob Kaufman, Steve Sanfield, Joe Stevenson) are placed in the white male misdemeanor tank with 107 prisoners and beaten under instructions from guards. Bob Kaufman's left ear is opened to a two-inch wound; Sanfield is kicked in the eye; McNichols sustains an inguinal hernia and disc injury. McNichols recounts learning the lesson of his New York childhood: lie still when overwhelmed.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Garland, Omer Street, and George Washington Jr.
clips.summary: McNichols describes the trustee hierarchy that ran the misdemeanor tank: commander Garland in cell one and lieutenant Omer Street roaming the dining area. After negotiating shoes for protection with Omer Street, the Freedom Riders survive the second night. When an L.A. Times article identifying Garland as the beating perpetrator arrives, Garland goes berserk; attorney George Washington Jr. arrives, documents Bob Kaufman's wound with a Polaroid, and the supervisor de-escalates the confrontation.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
