clips.title: Trial, Conviction, and Reversal
clips.summary: McNichols describes the first trial ending in mistrial when George Washington Jr. raised a discrepancy between the indictment's reference to the union coffee shop and the actual Union Station coffee shop. The judge agreed to accommodate the defendants' UCLA fall registration in scheduling the retrial, an accommodation McNichols contrasts with Mississippi practice. Conviction at the second trial was reversed by the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals on the strength of an obscure Texas statute prohibiting racial discrimination in transportation.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: NSA Civil Rights Director and Thanksgiving Fast
clips.summary: Returning to UCLA, McNichols cultivates his Freedom Rider standing in NSA student politics and is named NSA Civil Rights Director, moving to Philadelphia for a year. His first major project is the 1964 Thanksgiving Fast for Freedom, which raises $38,000 from fasting students and colleges nationwide to fund Mississippi food distribution.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Department of Agriculture Surplus Food Expansion
clips.summary: McNichols proposes redirecting the $38,000 from direct food purchases to a Department of Agriculture-aligned campaign that enrolled every Mississippi county in the federal surplus food program. Working with Robert W. Spike of the National Council of Churches and Art Thomas of the Delta Ministry, plus a USDA official who used the National Council of Churches and NSA as leverage, the program moved Mississippi from roughly 70 percent county participation to 100 percent, channeling food to Black residents previously excluded.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: McComb Visit and MFDP Congressional Challenge
clips.summary: McNichols describes his first NSA assignment: an Inter-Agency Conference of Civil Rights Organizations meeting in Atlanta followed by an SNCC-coordinated visit to McComb, Mississippi during the eleven-bombings-in-twenty-two-days period after Andrew Goodman, James Chaney, and Michael Schwerner's murders. After a rally, Jackson police escort the group out at the airport at 4 a.m. with the warning not to return. He recounts joining the MFDP Congressional Strategy Committee, becoming close to Rita Schwerner, and his National Health Law Services-funded investigation establishing White House opposition to the MFDP challenge.

clips.subject: Steven McNichols
clips.title: Voting Rights Act and AFL-CIO Anticommunism
clips.summary: McNichols recounts attending the Voting Rights Act signing ceremony, receiving a White House pen, and his Leadership Conference on Civil Rights work pushing more militant action. He closes by criticizing AFL-CIO and NAACP leadership for anticommunist hostility toward SNCC, attributing the resistance to figures whose 1930s organizing-era loyalties carried into Civil Rights Movement-era exclusion. Clarence Mitchell Jr.'s NAACP Washington office, he says, refused to share information about food-distribution meetings.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Great Falls Family and One-Room School
clips.summary: Gaither describes his parents Walter B. Gaither and Fannie Mae Little, both Friendship Junior College graduates who became schoolteachers in Great Falls, South Carolina. His father lost his teaching job after questioning a $5 monthly pay discrepancy controlled by the all-white school board; his mother taught Gaither and his siblings for seven years in the Pleasant Grove AME Zion one-room schoolhouse.
related_events: 1954 Brown v. Board school consolidation in South Carolina

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Father's Return to College and Brick Masonry
clips.summary: After losing his cotton-mill fireman job, Walter B. Gaither returned to Benedict College in Columbia and earned a Bachelor of Science in sociology. Blocked from a principalship by community jealousy, he supported the family as a brick mason working jobs across Charlotte, Columbia, Orangeburg, and as far as the Parris Island Marine Base in Beaufort, South Carolina. This funded Gaither and his brother's enrollment at Claflin College.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Race Around the Dinner Table
clips.summary: Gaither recounts family conversations about the Emmett Till case as a formative motivator for later movement participation, alongside his parents' open NAACP support and their backing of the South Carolina Conference suit to equalize black teacher salaries. He describes his father's hosting of union organizers at the cotton mill and the certified-elector form's disqualifiers (pauper status, chicken-stealing, wife-beating, public drunkenness) that constituted the voting-rights regime.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Claflin College and the GRE in 85-Degree Heat
clips.summary: Gaither describes choosing Claflin College in Orangeburg over Allen, Benedict, and Morris, graduating with honors and a BS in general science. He recounts being barred from taking the Graduate Record Exam in the University of South Carolina auditorium and forced into an 85-degree second-story room across the street, an incident that turned his father permanently against the university.
related_events: GRE administration discrimination at University of South Carolina, late 1950s

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: NAACP Youth Chapter to Orangeburg Sit-Ins
clips.summary: As president of the Claflin NAACP youth chapter, Gaither attended regional NAACP meetings where he first met Ruby Hurley, Amos C. Brown, and Medgar Evers. After the February 1, 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, he and James T. McCain led training using CORE Rules for Action and James Peck's Cracking the Color Line. Sit-ins were organized jointly by Claflin and South Carolina State students through socio-dramas at the JJC Brooks gymnasium; Chuck McDew was an early State counterpart and James E. Clyburn participated in secondary student-leader training.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Orangeburg Mass Arrest and Tactical Nonviolence
clips.summary: Gaither describes the Orangeburg mass arrest of approximately 350 students that filled the city jail, county jail, and an open-air stockade. He was arrested separately for breaching the peace while walking around the courthouse square talking to NAACP Youth Secretary Herbert Wright. Matthew J. Perry Jr. met the students afterward at the Claflin gymnasium to assure NAACP legal defense. Gaither recounts his initial skepticism toward Glenn Smiley's Fellowship of Reconciliation lecture on nonviolence and his subsequent adoption of tactical nonviolence through McCain's mentorship.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Miami CORE Action Institute and Dade County Jail
clips.summary: McCain persuaded Gaither to attend the CORE Action Institute in Miami in summer 1960, where he was arrested with Dottie Miller, Bob Zellner, and Bernard Lafayette at a Hollywood, Florida lunch counter on the charge of 'ejection of undesirable guests.' They spent ten to twelve days in the Dade County jail among approximately 35 prisoners including murderers. The judge issued no adjudication with probation conditions. Gaither recounts learning of voter-registration work in South Carolina with Frank Robinson and Jim McCain before being hired as a CORE field secretary.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: CORE National Office and Field Secretary Work
clips.summary: Gaither describes his first plane trip to the CORE national office in New York where he met Jim Robinson, Gordon Carey, Marvin Rich, and Jimmy McDonald. As field secretary he worked assignments in Kentucky, California, and Arizona wherever local groups requested CORE training on nonviolent direct action. His NAACP regional contacts gave him Medgar Evers as an entry point for later Jackson, Mississippi work.
related_events: CORE founded 1942 at University of Chicago

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Friendship 9 and Jail-No-Bail
clips.summary: Gaither trained the Friendship 9 at Claflin College in December 1960 alongside Gordon Carey and McCain, using socio-dramas. The protest was timed for January 31, 1961, one day before the anniversary of the Greensboro sit-ins, to allow the Friendship Junior College students to register for spring semester before a thirty-day road-gang sentence. He notes a similar earlier Tallahassee CORE protest by Patricia Stephens Due and Priscilla Stephens but argues the Rock Hill action's strategic context elevated its historical significance.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Freedom Ride Conception and Scouting
clips.summary: On a bus from South Carolina back to New York after a Claflin training session, Gaither and Gordon Carey conceived the Freedom Ride while discussing Louis Fischer's Gandhi biography and the 1947 Journey of Reconciliation scouted by George Houser. Gaither became the route scout, selecting historically black colleges along the deep-south corridor through Alabama, Mississippi, and Louisiana for housing riders and convening mass meetings. He records his prediction that federal protection would be required to traverse Alabama.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Anniston, Montgomery Martial Law, and Jackson
clips.summary: After the Anniston bus burning, Gaither was reassigned to Montgomery, Alabama where he lived in Ralph Abernathy's home during the martial-law period following the First Baptist Church siege. With a local driver he retrieved arriving riders at Greyhound and Trailways stations using a telephone-booth identification technique once National Guard escorts disappeared a block from the station. He was then assigned to Jackson, Mississippi to support arriving riders through court proceedings and Parchman Farm sentences.
related_events: Montgomery First Baptist Church siege May 21, 1961

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: COFO, Bob Moses, and Clarksdale
clips.summary: Gaither describes the founding of the Council of Federated Organizations (COFO) uniting SNCC, SCLC, NAACP, and CORE under one Mississippi banner. He met Bob Moses in Jackson while Moses was managing local minister campaigns and credits Moses with seeing the larger picture beyond personal recognition. In summer 1963 Gaither worked with Aaron E. Henry in Clarksdale, Mississippi on the downtown selective-buying boycott and ran a freedom school of approximately 20-25 young people, helping prepare the March on Washington sign that local students carried to the rally.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Fort Jackson Induction and Graduate School Pivot
clips.summary: After receiving a 1A classification and orders to report to Fort Jackson, South Carolina for induction, Gaither was rejected on a 'moral fitness' technicality citing his arrest record. He immediately accepted admission to graduate school at Atlanta University in biology, deferring the conscientious-objector question. He also describes a University of Wisconsin-Madison law school admission letter that arrived while he was already enrolled in biology and the Jackson black attorney corps (Jack H. Young Sr., R. Jess Brown, Carsie Hall) supplemented by Derrick Bell, Constance Baker Motley, William Kunstler, and Carl Rachlin for federal cases.

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: University of Iowa and Electron Microscopy
clips.summary: Gaither describes his Ph.D. work at the University of Iowa in botany using electron microscopy to study reproductive thread structures in organisms and to resolve species-differentiation questions inaccessible to light microscopy. He explains his decision to choose Iowa over Texas (where the field's leading scholar had relocated) because he did not want to continue fighting bladed segregation daily during graduate study.
related_events: University of Iowa doctoral work mid-1960s

clips.subject: Thomas Walter Gaither
clips.title: Freedom Ride Historiography and Reflection
clips.summary: Gaither defends CORE's authorship of the Freedom Rides against accounts that center the Nashville student movement's post-Anniston contribution, arguing that the Nashville augmentation was a continuation rather than a takeover. He reflects on the unfinished work of southern desegregation, the surprise of Barack Obama's 2008 election, the costs to him of fighting for citizenship rights, and the condition of contemporary Great Falls where men his age sit under trees with no economic prospects.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Philadelphia Origins and Grandparents
clips.summary: Jenkins describes being born in Philadelphia on December 30, 1938 to long-term Philadelphia parents. His maternal grandfather William Albert Jones served on the Philadelphia school board and founded an organization for Black Republicans called Remember John Brown. His paternal grandfather Rev. O.G. Jenkins founded the Courtland Normal and Industrial Institute, a Rosenwald school in Courtland, Virginia (formerly New Jerusalem, renamed after the Nat Turner Rebellion), and built Hood Memorial Church in Richmond.
related_events: Booker T. Washington Tuskegee Institute network

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Philadelphia Schooling
clips.summary: Jenkins attended the Thaddeus Stevens School of Practice on Spring Garden Street, a heavily integrated teacher-training school with Chinese, Eastern European, Jewish, and Irish students. Field trips to the Philadelphia Museum of Art, the Franklin Institute, and the Natural History Museum shaped his aesthetic sensibilities. He then attended Central High School, Philadelphia's premier public institution, where Howard University recruiters distributed scholarship literature.
related_events: Philadelphia Museum of Art Scholars Room visits
Howard University recruitment of Northern students

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Howard Faculty and the Little Forum
clips.summary: Jenkins entered Howard in 1956 on a full-tuition scholarship. He studied under Rayford Logan (Pan-African Movement general secretary under Du Bois), Sterling Brown (literary critic and dialect scholar), Harold Lewis (political scientist), and E. Franklin Frazier (sociologist of the Black bourgeoisie). The Little Forum was a monthly student-faculty reading club in Cook Hall where students and faculty addressed each other as peers - Jenkins identifies it as the most important feature of his Howard education.
related_events: Pan-African Movement under W.E.B. Du Bois
Howard's correspondence-school theology tradition (Sterling Nelson Brown)

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Mordecai Johnson and McCarthy-Era Howard
clips.summary: Jenkins served as Howard student body president and developed a mentor relationship with President Mordecai Wyatt Johnson by waylaying him on the granite bench behind Founders Library. Johnson refused to expel faculty pursued by the House Un-American Activities Committee or to expel Pauli Murray after her 1940s House cafeteria sit-in. Jenkins cites Jonathan Holloway's history of Howard's faculty fervor in the 1940s and 1950s.
related_events: Pauli Murray 1940s U.S. House cafeteria sit-in
House Un-American Activities Committee Howard faculty investigations

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Howard Activism and the 1960 Sit-ins
clips.summary: Howard students protested for Black studies, younger faculty, and curricular reform. When the 1960 sit-ins erupted, Jenkins led the student council in appropriating funds to support arrested students; Dean Armour J. Blackburn forbade direct expenditure, so the council routed money through a Raleigh convention. Jenkins appealed over Blackburn to President Mordecai Johnson, who overruled the dean. He notes NAACP youth chapters - including Barbara Posey's Oklahoma City group - had been sitting in long before Greensboro 1960.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: USNSA and the CIA
clips.summary: Jenkins was elected USNSA National Affairs Vice President for 1960-61 by acclamation - a maneuver by USNSA staff with CIA ties who blocked his preferred candidacy for International Affairs VP because they could not trust him with the confidentiality agreement between the USNSA International Affairs office and the CIA. The International Student Relations Seminar at Harvard, which Jenkins had attended, was CIA-funded though not disclosed at the time. He notes the National Affairs role gave him more influence during the sit-in explosion than International Affairs would have.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Buckley Censure Fight and YAF
clips.summary: During Jenkins's USNSA term, the young Americans for Freedom invited William F. Buckley Jr. to speak at a parallel meeting adjacent to the USNSA Congress. After Buckley spoke, Jenkins delivered a rebuttal accusing him of "slave-owning fascist mentality." YAF introduced a motion to censure Jenkins for unauthorized political speech. The censure vote was defeated and Jenkins continued in office. He frames the YAF as the early collegiate emergence of the John Birch Society and connects its rhetoric to later anti-abortion and Trump-era political movements.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Ella Baker and the SNCC Executive Committee
clips.summary: Jenkins met Ella Baker through SNCC. He notes her early association with A. Philip Randolph's Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters, her founding role at SCLC, and her work through the Atlanta YWCA office. Reviewing old minutes, Jenkins found he had chaired a December SNCC executive committee meeting at the Butler Street YMCA in Atlanta at which Marion Barry was succeeded as chairman by Chuck McDew. Baker mediated the secular-political vs religious-nonviolent factional split.
related_events: SNCC December executive committee meeting Atlanta
Marion Barry replaced by Chuck McDew as SNCC chairman

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: SNCC's Secular-Political Wing
clips.summary: Jenkins identifies a fundamental division in SNCC between the Nashville religious-nonviolence contingent (Jim Lawson, Diane Nash, Jim Bevel, Marion Barry) and a secular-political wing represented by Jenkins, Charles Sherrod, Chuck McDew, and Charles Jones. The Nashville group insisted "Nonviolent" be added to SNCC's name to distinguish it from SDS. Jenkins's wing argued that direct action without political power was cosmetic - that voting was the only means to control the organs of state determining police, paving, school budgets, and public employment.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Capahosic Meeting with Kennedy Aides
clips.summary: At Capahosic, Virginia, SNCC representatives Jenkins, Charles Jones, Charles Sherrod, and Chuck McDew met with Harris Wofford (special assistant to President Kennedy) and Burke Marshall (assistant attorney general for civil rights designate) to coordinate a comprehensive South-wide voter education campaign. Jenkins contests Howard Zinn's framing of this as Kennedy administration co-optation - he characterizes the alignment as parallel interest, with SNCC seeking enforcement leverage and the administration seeking orderly political protest over volatile street demonstrations.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Three-Judge Panel and Draft Resistance
clips.summary: Local Mississippi draft boards targeted SNCC activists. The National Conference of Black Lawyers successfully challenged one case where Gen. Lewis B. Hershey's administration imprisoned a SNCC activist over the statutory 23-year age limit. Jenkins, with Bill Wood at Yale Law, helped force inclusion of a three-judge-panel provision in the Voting Rights Act so voting cases would be heard by panels appointed by the Fifth Circuit chief judge - mostly Eisenhower-appointed Republicans - rather than single segregationist judges appointed via Sen. James O. Eastland's Judiciary Committee. Chairman Emanuel Celler and House Republicans carried the vote.
keywords: Gen. Lewis B. Hershey draft board
related_events: Voting Rights Act House Judiciary Committee testimony

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Nashville Seminar - The First Freedom School
clips.summary: In summer 1961 Jenkins organized a Nashville seminar at Fisk University's Race Relations Institute for SNCC field leaders - what he calls the first Freedom School. Funded by the New World Foundation and the Taconic Foundation with possible Ford support, it brought sit-in leaders together with senior scholars on Black history, politics, economics, and sociology. Dr. King and Ella Baker both wrote letters of support. Stokely Carmichael's memoir Ready for Revolution credits the seminar; Carmichael was reprimanded during the seminar for participating in a downtown Nashville demonstration without strict nonviolence.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Ella Baker and the Cumberland Mountains Accident
clips.summary: Returning from Highlander Folk School in Monteagle, Tennessee toward the Chattanooga airport, Jenkins and Ella Baker were in a Volkswagen bus when a tractor-trailer driver, seeing the integrated group, ran them off the highway in the Cumberland Mountains. The bus rocked on the edge of a ravine, held by a single tree, until a pickup truck winched them out. As Baker - usually skeptical of religious idioms - was carried to the ambulance on a stretcher, she motioned Jenkins close and said "Thank God for Jesus," the grandmother's idiom she had ridiculed her whole life.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Yale Law and the SNCC Insurance Strategy
clips.summary: Jenkins enrolled at Yale Law School in fall 1961 for three reasons: legal skills relevant to social change; a SNCC contingency plan to preserve organizational leadership against the risk that field staff might be wiped out by murder, incarceration, or the draft; and to mobilize Northern white campuses as Freedom Singer concert tour hosts and volunteer pipelines. He references himself as "a Che Guevara in the Sierra Maestra" who could re-establish SNCC if it were destroyed. At Yale he worked with Rev. William Sloane Coffin Jr. and met Peter and Joan Countryman, founders of the Northern Student Movement.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: McComb, Mississippi - SNCC Beachhead
clips.summary: Jenkins explains why SNCC chose McComb: NAACP wanted Atlanta, SCLC wanted Montgomery, CORE wanted Bogalusa and New Orleans, but no one wanted McComb. SNCC went into the most hostile, remote area as a model beachhead. Ella Baker's June 21, 1961 letter introduced Jenkins to Bob Moses and Amzie Moore. The local Black Masonic lodge in McComb gave the project organizing space white police would not enter. Jenkins recounts driving with Chuck McDew, Charles Sherrod, and Charles Jones from Chicago to McComb through Dial, Alabama, where they hit a dog and a white linen-suited elder dispersed a hostile crowd by saying the boys couldn't have avoided it.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Herbert Lee, Bob Moses, and Teaching for Change
clips.summary: Jenkins references a WBAI recording of a speech he gave in Holmes County in 1962 or 1963 in which the Herbert Lee murder was the focal point - voter registration would not let Lee die in vain. He describes Bob Moses as a "saintly" intellectual guerrilla and credits Moses's grounded organizing as the model for the Teaching for Change organization, which connects the legacy of the McComb project to ongoing public-education work via the Civil Rights Movement Veterans network.
related_events: Holmes County voter registration mass meeting

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Greenwood and the Folk Festival
clips.summary: Jenkins describes leading singing at the Greenwood Folk Festival on Mrs. McGhee's farm with Bob Dylan, Pete Seeger, and Theo Bikel - the only time he sang with them. Greenwood was a triumph: Mrs. McGhee defended her farm with her armed sons; even residents who couldn't pass the literacy test became defenders and drivers for those who could. Dick Gregory helped secure food supplies when the county cut off food stamps. The county's elected officials are now majority Black as a result.
keywords: Bob Dylan Pete Seeger Theo Bikel
related_events: food stamp retaliation and Dick Gregory food shipments

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Albany and Southwest Georgia
clips.summary: Charles Sherrod chose Southwest Georgia - the highest-Black-population part of the state, neglected while attention went to Atlanta - and built a grassroots Albany movement consistent with Ella Baker's organizing philosophy. Laurie Pritchett's police force, Jenkins argues, was more vicious than Bull Connor's in Birmingham, surrounding marchers in churches and using mob containment. King and SCLC ultimately joined. Jenkins disputes the historians' verdict that Albany failed: he calls it "a slow-boiling success" - residents secured Farmers Home support, learned to navigate USDA forms, and Charles Sherrod's wife Shirley later became a USDA administrator for the Albany region.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Freedom Singers and the Power of Song
clips.summary: Jenkins traces SNCC's musical tradition to the Fisk Jubilee Singers and the Howard Gospel Singers. He identifies "We Shall Overcome" as Charles Albert Tindley's Methodist hymn from his Philadelphia hometown, turned into a political anthem by Guy Carawan, Pete Seeger, and the Freedom Singers. Song reinforced solidarity among demonstrators in jail and connected the movement to the religious-vocal traditions of the Black community. SNCC veterans still tear up at those songs because of how deeply they index movement history.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Medgar Evers Funeral and Stopping the Rooftop Shooters
clips.summary: After Medgar Evers's June 1963 assassination, the Connecticut chapter of the Women's International League for Peace and Freedom raised funds (channeled through William Sloane Coffin Jr.) for Jenkins to travel to Jackson and be the first person out of state to deliver a check to Myrlie Evers. At the post-funeral march on June 15, 1963, when the youth and SNCC contingent broke from the police-escorted procession to sing freedom songs, Jenkins was alerted that armed Black veterans were on a rooftop with telescopic rifles trained on John Doar - they couldn't hear Doar's words and thought he was acting for the local police. Jenkins reached the roof and shouted "Stop in the name of Medgar." They stopped.
keywords: Women's International League for Peace and Freedom

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Atlanta Rich's Sit-In and the 1960 Election
clips.summary: Jenkins identifies a second "brush with history": at a Morehouse chapel meeting before a planned Rich's department store sit-in in Atlanta, the Black establishment - including Martin Luther King Sr. - opposed direct action in Atlanta. Jenkins urged the group to march to MLK Jr.'s house and ask him to join. King did join, was arrested two days before the Kennedy-Nixon election, and Robert F. Kennedy made his famous call to Coretta. United Auto Workers leaflets distributed the story in major cities. Jenkins states that 250,000 previously non-voting Black voters voted for Kennedy, who won by 100,000.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: MFDP, Bill Higgs, and the Voter Education Project
clips.summary: Lawrence Guyot credits Jenkins with finding the Mississippi statute - originally enacted to protect post-Reconstruction whites from a Republican-controlled legislature - that authorized protest voting and parallel local parties, the legal foundation of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party. Jenkins worked on this with Yale Law classmate Bill Wood. William L. Higgs, an Ole Miss and Harvard Law-trained white attorney who took civil rights cases under Judge William Harold Cox (a Sen. James O. Eastland appointee), was the guiding spirit on the three-judge-panel amendment. The Voter Education Project, headed by Leo Branton and then Vernon E. Jordan Jr., refused to operate in Mississippi - making SNCC's McComb and Greenwood work all the more critical.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Mordecai Johnson, King, and the Gandhian Lineage
clips.summary: Jenkins cites Larry E. Rivers's article in the Journal of African American History documenting that King heard Mordecai Wyatt Johnson speak in Philadelphia while at Crozer Theological Seminary and locked himself in his room for ten days reading Gandhi afterward. Johnson's vice president William Stuart Nelson had lived in India with Gandhi. Jenkins says King's adoption of nonviolence is typically attributed to Thoreau, Emerson, Carlyle, and Tillich, but King himself credited Johnson - a credit Jenkins says has been distorted and lost in subsequent histories.

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: National Conference of Black Lawyers
clips.summary: Jenkins co-founded the National Conference of Black Lawyers in 1968 on a call from Floyd B. McKissick to move the Black bar beyond Brown v. Board of Education into federal regulation, economic development, poverty, and housing law. NCBL members included Judge Leon Higginbotham (Third Circuit), Nathaniel R. Jones, Hon. Bruce Wright, and Raymond Pace Alexander (counsel in the Girard College case establishing no discrimination in charitably funded institutions). NCBL helped defeat Haynsworth and Carswell Supreme Court nominations. Jenkins frames it as the invisible institutional continuation of SNCC.
related_events: Haynsworth and Carswell Supreme Court nomination defeats

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Marion Barry, Postal Service, and Post-Movement Career
clips.summary: Jenkins traces SNCC's continued presence in Washington D.C. through Marion Barry. He served as president of the University of the District of Columbia, was a professor at Howard Law School and David A. Clarke School of Law, and was appointed by President Jimmy Carter (Senate-confirmed) to the U.S. Postal Service Board of Governors, where he chaired the Corporate Responsibility Committee, brought Kenneth B. Clark in to study systemic discrimination, and restored Amzie Moore's pension. He helped establish WHUT-TV at Howard - the only full commercial station owned by an HBCU - and consulted to the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights on the information-highway access gap.
related_events: Kenneth B. Clark Postal Service study

clips.subject: Timothy Jenkins
clips.title: Critique of Black Power and the Unfinished Coalition
clips.summary: Jenkins wrote John Lewis at the end of SNCC urging that veterans professionally infiltrate EEOC, the National Labor Relations Board, and the Federal Reserve System - the regulatory machinery created by the legislation SNCC helped draft. He argues that SNCC failed to do what labor had done: place its people in administrative roles after legislative victories. He delivers a structural critique: the Black Power era's exclusion of progressive white allies broke the coalition needed to mobilize poor whites - producing today's red-state alignment in which Kentuckians vote against the Affordable Care Act to avoid association with Obama. He references the 1940s Southern Negro Youth Congress (the original SNCC influenced by Du Bois and Eleanor Roosevelt) as a model the next generation must extend.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Birth and Family Origins
clips.summary: Vernon F. Dahmer Jr. introduces himself, born August 27, 1929 in the Kelly Settlement five miles north of Hattiesburg. He describes his father Vernon Dahmer Sr. as the youngest of 12 children born to George Dahmer and Ellen Kelly Dahmer, and explains how segregation drove his father's six sisters and elder brothers to leave Mississippi for the North, where the light-complexioned sons assimilated as white in Chicago.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Mixed-Race Genealogy
clips.summary: Dahmer traces the family's mixed-race origins to Peter Dahmer, a German immigrant from Darmstadt, and Laura Barnes, his grandfather George Dahmer's mother born out of wedlock and raised by a mulatto woman running a boarding house in Covington County. He describes how Laura Barnes later married former slave Charlie Craft and bore nine mixed-race children, and how the Kelly Settlement community accommodated multiple mixed-race families including the Wilson and Eaton families.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: The Family Farm and Businesses
clips.summary: Dahmer describes his father building a multi-enterprise operation in Forrest County: a several-hundred-acre farm, a school bus he leased to the segregated county school system around 1935, a tractor purchased with Camp Shelby economic growth in 1940, a grocery store opened in 1942, and a sawmill added in 1945. He recounts his own childhood labor delivering 300-pound ice blocks, cutting meat, and working three lines of family income.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Three Mothers and the Family Nucleus
clips.summary: Dahmer recounts the structure of his father's three marriages: divorce from his biological mother in 1935, marriage to a second wife who died in 1949 leaving three more sons, and marriage to Ellen (Ellie) Dahmer in 1952, who raised the two youngest children Dennis and Betty Dahmer. He describes growing up with a father who was a disciplinarian focal point and the bonded unity among the brothers that persists today.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Shady Grove and Country Schooling
clips.summary: Dahmer describes the Shady Grove Baptist Church, established 1864 on land donated by his great-grandfather Warren Kelly (descendant of slave master Green Kelly and his African mistress Sarah), and the adjacent one-room cabin that served as the first school. He recounts the segregated curriculum which nonetheless included Negro history (Booker T. Washington, George Washington Carver, the Underground Railroad) through a progressive Black principal, the hand-me-down white-school textbooks, and his father's nightly fireplace spelling drills.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Tougaloo and Alcorn State
clips.summary: Dahmer recounts his post-1948 college years: enrollment at Tougaloo, where he encountered white faculty under President Harold C. Warren, withdrawal after illness, and transfer to Alcorn State University as an agriculture major under the work-study program. He describes the contrast between Tougaloo's fluid student body and Alcorn's rural country-boy culture, and his work milking and mopping in the campus dairy.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Father's NAACP Activism and Voter Suppression
clips.summary: Dahmer testifies to witnessing his father attempt to register to vote in Forrest County in the mid-1940s after paying poll tax to the sheriff. He recounts the white registrar handing his father a card and falsely claiming his father misread it, a denial Dahmer Jr. recognized at the time as untrue. He describes his father co-founding the Forrest County NAACP branch with eight other Black men around 1945, holding secret nighttime meetings, and becoming branch president in the mid-1950s after Brown v. Board.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Draft and Air Force Communications Career
clips.summary: Dahmer recounts being drafted in September 1951 at the height of the Korean War and switching to the Air Force the day before his Army induction on his uncle's advice. He describes basic training at Sheppard Air Force Base, teletype school at Francis E. Warren Air Force Base in Cheyenne, Wyoming, an interrogation by federal investigators over the unanswered draft notice, his failed attempt to transfer to truck-driving school, and his rise through Tyndall AFB Florida, Shemya in the Aleutians, Germany, and ultimately to senior master sergeant.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: January 10, 1966 Firebombing
clips.summary: Dahmer describes receiving the 2-3 AM telephone call on January 10, 1966 from his aunt in Compton, California reporting the White Knights firebombing of his father's home and grocery store. He recounts walking into the FBI office in Riverside the next morning to demand that no Mississippi law enforcement be assigned to the case, the FBI's offended response, and his colonel's prior briefing not to compromise his Top Secret communications position. He describes the airport-to-bus journey home shadowed by a federal observer and the worst day of his life viewing his father's body in the morgue and the destroyed family site.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Country Failed, Family Endured
clips.summary: Dahmer reflects on the contradiction that seven of his father's sons served a combined 78 years on active duty across the Korean and Vietnam eras while the same federal government failed to protect their father from domestic terrorists. He recounts his stepmother Ellie Dahmer's decision to stay on the property and rebuild rather than relocate to her brothers in Washington, her seven trial appearances, her three terms as Forrest County election commissioner, and her role as family leader.

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Sam Bowers 1998 Conviction
clips.summary: Dahmer describes the 1998 prosecution of Sam Bowers, Imperial Wizard of the White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan in Jones County, who had told fellow Klansmen they could kill a Black male without facing jail. He recounts the Medgar Evers case opening the legal pathway, his role as primary family spokesperson with his mother and brother Dennis Dahmer, the August 21, 1998 jury conviction at 2-3 PM, Bowers' death in prison in November 2006, and the 25 years of annual January 10 memorial services at the Hub City Professional Men's Club in Hattiesburg that kept the case alive.
related_events: Medgar Evers case (Byron De La Beckwith 1994)

clips.subject: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
clips.title: Photo Documentation
clips.summary: Dahmer narrates a series of family photographs for the camera: a portrait of his father Vernon F. Dahmer Sr. in his youth, the family home before the firebombing, the burned-out home structure with brothers in military uniform overlooking the site, the grocery store before and after the firebombing, the funeral, and the destroyed family car.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Interview Opening
clips.summary: Emilye Crosby of the Southern Oral History Program at UNC Chapel Hill identifies the interview as part of the Civil Rights History Project, jointly sponsored by the Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture and the Library of Congress's American Folklife Center. The session is recorded at the Northborough, Massachusetts Free Library on August 24, 2013, with George's husband Frederick George and videographer John Bishop present.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project recording session

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Family and Newport News Childhood
clips.summary: George was born in Newport News, Virginia, named after her grandmother. After her parents separated, she and her brother were raised primarily by their father, a postman who later remarried. When the family moved to a suburban neighborhood, white residents were already leaving; a departing neighbor told her father she would not have sold had she known the family. George recalls a bus incident at age twelve when a white passenger moved away from her, prompting her father's discussion of race and his insistence on education.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Black Community Life in Newport News
clips.summary: George describes the segregated but self-contained Black community of Newport News: all-Black schools, Dr. Jones and Dr. Tucker's Black-owned drugstore where she worked as a soda-jerk clerk, churches, funeral homes, and the shipyard-driven first-of-the-month influx of sailors that kept her chaperoned by her brother. She references Virginia's massive resistance to Brown but notes Newport News did not close its schools, and recalls her debutante presentation at sixteen and the mentorship of Mary Ann Benz, wife of the first Black urologist in Virginia.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Virginia Union University
clips.summary: George entered Virginia Union University in fall 1959 to study history with the goal of law school, inspired by attorney Marion L. Poe of Newport News. She describes the Baptist school's strict sign-out rules, her friendships with roommates Marie Sallasin and Patricia Washington (who could pass as white), and an incident in which Washington had her hair cut at a white Richmond barbershop. She traveled to the Penn Relays in Philadelphia and stayed with Washington's family near Philadelphia.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Sit-In Organizing at Virginia Union
clips.summary: George describes how news of the Greensboro Woolworth sit-ins reached Virginia Union and how ministers Charles Sherrod and Frank Pinkston led student organizing meetings. Martin Luther King had come to campus. Sherrod and Pinkston prepared the students for arrest, emphasizing nonviolence, and made clear that if either were taken, the other would remain to lead. George recalls Shirley Miller Sherrod as Charles Sherrod's wife.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Arrest at Thalhimers
clips.summary: George recounts the February 1960 march downtown to Thalhimers, a department store comparable to Macy's where Black customers could shop but were excluded from the Richmond Room tearoom. After picketing and sitting in, she was arrested, taken to jail, and fingerprinted with difficulty because of her long fingernails. Her attorney bailed her out, and as she left the jail a white man stood in front of her and spit on her. That night she saw herself led from the store on the news and called her father.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Aftermath and the Mississippi Decision
clips.summary: George describes the institutional support she received: no sanctions from Virginia Union, financial and legal support from volunteer lawyers, and welcome at Black churches throughout Richmond. When students planned to travel to Mississippi by bus, her father forbade her to go, telling her he did not want her dead and that she needed to focus on her education. She returned to her studies, taught at Maggie L. Walker High School in Richmond for three years after graduation, then pursued a master's at the University of Maryland.
related_events: Mississippi Freedom Movement bus travel planning

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Post-Movement Career
clips.summary: After her master's at the University of Maryland, George worked for over seventeen years for a private company in Washington, D.C. on inner-city federal MA-4 contracts, counseling Black clients to move off welfare, off drugs, and out of jail and into employment. She relocated through Minnesota, back to Washington, to California, and back to Minnesota before settling in Massachusetts. She frames this work as continuous with the Civil Rights Movement's goal of helping people become viable citizens.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Reunion and Recognition
clips.summary: George describes never having spoken about the sit-in for decades, even to her husband. Approximately fifty years later, organizers of an anniversary commemoration contacted her. At Virginia Union she saw a plaque with her name and learned that the group of thirty-four students was credited with desegregating the city of Richmond. She reconnected with Marie Sallasin and Patricia Washington at the reunion, having lost contact through Sallasin's military-family travels.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: LINKS and Community Work
clips.summary: George describes her work with the Springfield, Massachusetts chapter of LINKS Incorporated, a national organization of predominantly African American women founded in Philadelphia. The chapter mentors young women, supports a transitional house for formerly incarcerated women recovering from addiction, and tracks members through high school graduation and college, including UMass Amherst. She also chairs the Northborough personnel board, serves on the Assabet Valley Regional School District committee, and works with Alfred University's Judson Leadership Center, named for a deceased sponsor.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Nonviolence and Racism Today
clips.summary: George reflects on the discipline of nonviolence during the sit-ins, recalling that the students were jeered and called names as they marched and were instructed not to make eye contact. She states that racism remains rampant in the United States and expresses her shock at the election of Barack Obama, attributing congressional obstruction of his presidency to racism. She contrasts her own awareness of race with her son's generation, whom she observes do not see color.

clips.subject: Virginia Simms George
clips.title: Workplace Advocacy and Closing
clips.summary: George describes current workplace advocacy at her manufacturing employer, including a campaign for gender-neutral bathrooms near the manufacturing floor and the development of training for managers on transgender workplace issues and inappropriate questions. She frames this as continuous with her Movement-era commitments: understanding differences, valuing differences, and protecting people's ability to make their own choices. The interview closes with thanks from Crosby on behalf of the Civil Rights History Project.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project recording closing

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Rolling Wall Childhood
clips.summary: Bruce describes his birth in the Rolling Wall community of rural Holmes County and growing up as the youngest of 14 children in a sharecropping family. He recounts walking miles to a small wooden Methodist church, briefly working cotton fields, and an early childhood incident in which he confronted a white man over an impounded animal.
related_events: Rural Mississippi childhood under Jim Crow

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Carpentry and Gospel Group
clips.summary: Bruce describes his self-taught carpentry trade, which he calls a gift from God, building homes across Holmes and surrounding counties with a small crew. He details the gospel singing group he founded that toured Leflore County, Greenwood, Chicago, and beyond, broadcasting on local radio and continuing for more than 54 years.
related_events: Black gospel touring circuit in Mississippi

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Entering the Movement
clips.summary: Bruce describes weighing his carpentry business against civil rights involvement before deciding 'what good is a job, you ain't got no freedom.' He recalls hearing about Emmett Till's lynching, meeting Medgar Evers and Aaron Henry, and traveling to Greenwood for movement meetings as SNCC workers organized in Holmes County.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Freedom Summer and the Mileston 14
clips.summary: Bruce recounts Freedom Summer 1964 volunteers staying with local Black families, the literacy-test obstacles at the courthouse (including how many bubbles in a bucket of water), and the April 1963 attempt by 14 Holmes County Black residents to register to vote. He names Hartman Turnbow, Ralph Hayse, Russell Whitaker, James Bourne, and Alma Mitchell Carnegie among the applicants who lived in Mileston and owned their own land.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: J.J. Young and the Lexington March
clips.summary: Bruce describes the death of J.J. Young in the Durant jail after authorities allegedly refused him heart medication. He recounts inspecting the body, organizing a boycott and march to the courthouse in Lexington, his own arrest with 12 others, and the subsequent crowd that filled the Lexington square so densely that police could not drive through it.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: MFDP and Atlantic City
clips.summary: Bruce details his election as chair of the Holmes County Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, a role he says he still held at the time of the interview. He describes his work as MFDP coordinator across 17-18 community meetings, supporting the 1964 challenge at the Atlantic City National Democratic Convention, and the 1965 effort to seat Fannie Lou Hamer, Annie Devine, and Victoria Gray in Congress.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Sue and Henry Lorenzi and Healthcare
clips.summary: Bruce describes the five-year presence of Sue and Henry Lorenzi as white SNCC volunteers in Holmes County and their role in building the Mileston community center. He recalls Dr. Brock as a rural physician who would visit Black homes, the absence of Black doctors in the county, segregated waiting rooms, and the Mileston-area community health clinic that helped found the federal community health center program.
related_events: Mound Bayou and Mileston community health center movement

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Durant School Board and Downtown Desegregation
clips.summary: Bruce describes Mayor Levin, a white Durant lawyer who supported integrating the city school board, and Bruce's appointment alongside Mr. D.F. (Owen) as the first two Black members. He recounts integrating a Durant cafe after the 1964 Civil Rights Act, removing the segregation signs at the ice train station, and a boycott of a grocery store that fired its Black worker after a fight with a white worker.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Carroll County Crisis and School Integration
clips.summary: Bruce describes being summoned to a Carroll County school after a Black student was accused of raping a white teacher's daughter, his confrontation with white sheriffs, and his investigation that revealed the two were in a consensual relationship. He then discusses the aftermath of Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education (1969), as white families moved their children to private 'Christian' academies while public schools became overwhelmingly Black.
related_events: Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education (1969)

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Self-Defense at the Meeting Hall
clips.summary: Bruce describes weekly Wednesday-night meetings at a country building where white attackers in cars repeatedly shot up the roof. He recounts organizing armed defenders on both sides of the road, who returned fire above the attackers' car the next Wednesday, and an arson attempt at a nearby church (described as Saigon Pagoda or Mt. Pisgah) where attackers had soaked a rope in gasoline to set fire to the building.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Robert Clark and the 1967 Election
clips.summary: Bruce describes the 1966 congressional run by Ralph Hayse and the 1967 county election that produced Robert Clark as the first African American in the Mississippi state legislature in the 20th century. He explains the decision to run an independent slate in the general election rather than the primary, the third-Sunday county-wide candidate-selection meetings, and the role of Rev. Eddie Clark and Dr. Wilbert McLean.

clips.subject: Walter Bruce
clips.title: Reflection on Family and Integration
clips.summary: Bruce reflects on his wife's support across decades of late-night meetings, his refusal to take money from his organizing work, and his conviction that 'we don't need an all-Black or all-white' political coalition. He affirms that the Holmes County Freedom Democratic Party remains active and that the county is still organized around third-Sunday meetings.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Bronx Childhood and Cold War Family
clips.summary: Tillow describes being born in 1940 in the Bronx to parents who had been Young Communist League members in the 1920s. He recounts the 1953 Rosenberg execution (he was 13), his father taking him to the funeral, and the wider Cold War atmosphere of a half-radical, half-conservative extended family, including aunts who were Communist Party members or sympathizers.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Race in 1950s New York
clips.summary: Tillow recalls Parkchester, the Metropolitan Life-funded apartment development outside his Bronx neighborhood that excluded African Americans by holding apartments vacant for a year to escape rent control. He describes the chain-yourself-to-the-door housing protests of the period as the same movement that produced the Anne and Carl Braden housing work in Louisville.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Harpur College Activism
clips.summary: As a junior at Harpur College in Endicott, New York in 1960, Tillow joined classmates to picket the local Woolworth in sympathy with the Southern sit-in movement. He describes defying the New York State civil-defense take-cover drill on the new campus, founding a Young Socialists chapter, and the campus fight to invite Herbert Aptheker and the Socialist Workers Party candidate to speak, which broke the college president's power to bar speakers.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Cornell and SNCC's Easter Conference
clips.summary: Tillow describes graduate work at the University of Washington (1961-62) and Cornell (1962-63), where during the New York newspaper strike he read the Baltimore Afro-American for a campus clipping circle. The Afro's coverage of SNCC's Easter 1963 conference at Gammon Theological Seminary in Atlanta drew a carload of Cornell students south, where Tillow met Anne Braden.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Fayette County Tennessee Voter Registration
clips.summary: Tillow, Charlie and Bunny Haney with their infant spent summer 1963 in Fayette County, Tennessee, working with John McFerren and Marcheka McCracken's community church on voter registration. He describes the Tent City origins of the local movement (Korean War vets registering after a 1959 jury-selection trial), the slow registration procedures at the courthouse, a self-help leather book-bag project in Haywood County, and being jumped on the road by a lone white assailant.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: SNCC Atlanta Communications Office
clips.summary: After the March on Washington, Tillow joined SNCC's Atlanta headquarters working under Julian Bond in the communications office at 3.5 and Hunter Street, alongside Bob Wald, Mike Sayer, Dinky Romilly, James Forman, and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson. He details producing tri-weekly press releases, the Student Voice newsletter mailed to thousands of Northern friends-of-SNCC, the IBM Selectric typewriter transition, and the A.B. Dick offset-press operation set up by Mark Schoenfeld next door.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: WATS Line Campaigns and Freedom in the Air
clips.summary: Tillow recounts SNCC's WATS-line operation tracking traveling entertainers to pressure them out of segregated Jackson Mississippi appearances, successfully getting the Bonanza cast to cancel. He describes the SNCC press release on Byron De La Beckwith's federal land sale during the Medgar Evers trial, and the pressed-but-undistributed Freedom in the Air SNCC record featuring Bernice Johnson Reagon, Cordell Reagon, Bertha Gober, Ruth-Etta Harris, and Charles Sherrod.
keywords: Freedom in the Air SNCC record

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Opening the MFDP Washington Office
clips.summary: In spring 1964, Ella Baker and Bob Moses assigned Tillow to open SNCC's Washington DC office at 14th and U Street to coordinate the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge to the Democratic National Convention. He worked with Frank Smith from Mississippi and Barbara Jones, traveled to Minnesota, Wisconsin, and Michigan state Democratic conventions seeking resolutions, and met with Bayard Rustin, Jim Forman, and Ella Baker in Rustin's New York office on strategy.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Credentials Committee Strategy
clips.summary: Tillow explains the procedural mechanics: a minority report required 10% of credentials-committee delegates, and a roll-call vote on either majority or minority report required 10 delegations to support it. He names Wayne Morse's swap (resolutions over credentials for Edith Green of Oregon), Robert Kastenmeier of Wisconsin as a strong supporter, the Mississippi white delegation that sent a no-name slate to avoid drawing attention, and the parallel work of pressuring the parents of Mississippi Freedom Summer volunteers.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Atlantic City Convention
clips.summary: Tillow describes Reggie Robinson securing a 100-room Atlantic City hotel for the 68-delegate MFDP contingent co-chaired by Aaron Henry (NAACP Mississippi president) and Ed King (Tougaloo theology professor), with Fannie Lou Hamer, Annie Devine, and Victoria Gray as principal women. He details Hamer's televised credentials-committee testimony that moved Edith Green and prompted Johnson to call an emergency press conference to cut away from coverage, the 24-hour boardwalk vigil where delegates surrendered badges, and the Mississippi white delegation walking out after the hearing.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: The Compromise and Disillusionment
clips.summary: Tillow names the arm-twisting that produced the two-observer-seats compromise: Walter Reuther threatened Joseph Rauh's UAW general counsel income, an unnamed African American woman lost her husband's California judgeship, and only Robert Kastenmeier and Edith Green held the credentials-committee line. He recounts the church meeting where Bayard Rustin, Martin Luther King Jr, James Farmer, Jack Pratt of the National Council of Churches, and Wayne Morse pressed the MFDP delegates to accept the compromise, which the delegates refused. Rowland Evans and Robert Novak's syndicated red-baiting column followed.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Congressional Challenge and Aftermath
clips.summary: Tillow describes the 1965 MFDP challenge to the seating of three Mississippi Delta congressmen including Jamie Whitten, led by William Kunstler, Arthur Kinoy, John Conyers, and Elizabeth Holtzman, in which Tip O'Neill forced the three to stand aside on the opening day of the new House session. He observes that movement attention turned to Alabama and Stokely Carmichael's Lowndes County work, and that the 1968 Chicago convention's seating of Julian Bond's Georgia delegation flowed from the 1964 rules fight.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Southern Student Organizing Committee and Labor Bridge
clips.summary: Working with Carl Braden and Jim Williams, Tillow helped found the Southern Student Organizing Committee through a Nashville meeting with Sue Thrasher and Sam Shirah. He organized Highlander Research and Education Center workshops linking SNCC organizers with Jesse Prosten of the Amalgamated Meat Cutters, Bob Lewis of UE, an Atlanta Teamsters business agent, and ILGWU staff, attempting to bring labor money and influence into Southern civil-rights organizing.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: UE, Lynchburg, and Marriage to Kay Marlowe
clips.summary: Tillow met Kay Marlowe at a Highlander workshop while she was working in eastern Kentucky for the Berman Gibson-led Wildcat-strike Committee for Miners coalition organized by Paul O'Dwyer and Stanley Aronowitz. Both joined UE: Kay went to Honetown, Pennsylvania to organize Slovenian workers; Tillow went to Lynchburg, Virginia to try to organize a General Electric plant, where he observed the William Kunstler-Phillip Hirschkop defense of a Black youth in a rape case. They married and moved to UE's Pittsburgh district office.
related_events: Eastern Kentucky wildcat strike Committee for Miners

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Pittsburgh Anti-War, 1199 SEIU, and Communist Party
clips.summary: The Tillows worked through the 1965 spring Washington peace demonstrations and Pittsburgh anti-Vietnam War organizing. Tillow helped circulate petitions for a King-Spock 1968 presidential ticket, then placed Dick Gregory and Mark Lane on the Pennsylvania ballot via the Peace and Freedom Party. He joined 1199 SEIU's Pennsylvania hospital-worker organizing under the 1969 Public Employee Relations Act, then joined the Communist Party as unpaid staff after the 1972 McGovern campaign. Tillow worked on Conyers's 1978 short-work-week bill and Chile solidarity organizing around the Salvador Allende coup.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Camden Print Shop and Move to Louisville
clips.summary: From 1990 to 1995, Tillow ran the Camden, New Jersey print shop that produced the People's Weekly World. Kay's nursing-organizing work for an NEA affiliate in Paducah and Louisville (Lourdes Hospital and Audubon Hospital) drew the couple to Kentucky, where they settled near Kay's mother in Metropolis, Illinois.

clips.subject: Walter Tillow
clips.title: Reflections on Democratic Party Illusions
clips.summary: Tillow closes by arguing the 1964 MFDP fight stripped away movement illusions about the Democratic Party. He credits the Eisenhower-appointed Fifth Circuit judges (Minor Wisdom, Elbert Tuttle, Frank Johnson Jr) as the judicial backbone that saved civil-rights litigation, contrasting them with Kennedy's Eastland-and-Stennis-approved appointees. He names local movement lawyers Ben Smith and Bruce Waltzer (New Orleans), Len Holt, Ernest Goodman and George Crockett, William Kunstler, and Arthur Kinoy as the legal infrastructure of Movement-era civil rights work.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Mississippi Freedom Trail Marker
clips.summary: Parker opens with his recent visit to Mississippi for the inaugural Mississippi Freedom Trail marker ceremony at Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market. He describes the event as the first of 25 planned markers, situates the site in relation to where he lived and went to school, and reflects on what the marker says about 1955 and the present.
related_events: Inaugural Mississippi Freedom Trail marker ceremony
Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market site dedication

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Money Mississippi Childhood
clips.summary: Parker describes growing up in Money, Mississippi, a town of about 50 people, with his grandfather Moses Wright and grandmother. He recounts cotton-picking, the one-room church school, the Delta soil, the sawmill next door, and the family move to Summit (Argo), Illinois, in 1947. He describes his grandfather as a no-nonsense man of dignity and his mother as a school teacher.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Behind the Iron Curtain
clips.summary: Parker describes his awareness as a 16-year-old in 1955 that traveling to Mississippi was, as people said, like going behind the Iron Curtain. He had heard the stories of what could happen to Black people there and knew there was no legal protection. He contrasts this consciousness with the fun of swimming, jokes, and his same-age uncle driving the car.
related_events: August 1955 Wright family visit to Money, Mississippi

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Bryant's Grocery Encounter
clips.summary: Parker recounts the late afternoon at Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market when Emmett Till came in behind him, Simeon Wright followed, and Carolyn Bryant Donham came out of the store. Till whistled. Parker describes the immediate apprehension, the rush to the car, the dropped cigarette, and the high-speed escape down a gravel road with a car behind them throwing dust.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Saturday Shopping and Four Fifths
clips.summary: Parker describes the days between Wednesday's incident and Sunday's abduction. Thursday and Friday passed without incident. On Saturday, August 27, the cousins traveled to Greenwood for shopping and stopped at a juke joint called Four Fifths. He recounts Emmett's playful nature, the wolf-whistle pattern not being the first time Till had drawn attention, and Till's sensitive reaction when their uncle Maurice Wright ran over a dog on the way home.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: The Abduction at 2:30 AM
clips.summary: Parker describes waking around 2:30 AM on Sunday, August 28, hearing the men say they were looking for a fat boy from Chicago. He describes praying through the bedroom search, the flashlight and pistol passing by him as he pretended to sleep, the search through Moses Wright's house, and the abductors finally taking Till from Simeon Wright's room. He stayed awake for 24 hours afterward.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Train Home and the Funeral
clips.summary: Parker describes his uncle taking him to the train station, the confused stop in Memphis on the way to Chicago where he encountered segregated washroom signs, and his arrival at Mamie Till-Mobley's house on 64th and St. Lawrence. He describes the family in grief before the body was found, his trip back to Argo, and a later moment in town when he saw a juror who looked like one of Till's killers and felt his heart stop.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Religious Conversion and Conviction
clips.summary: Parker describes the promise he made to God during the abduction night and how it took him six years, until age 22, to keep it. He explains that his religious upbringing taught him to handle the trauma by exhaling and letting it go. He describes his conviction since age 22 as a hard sell in his twenties but the source of his peace through five decades.
related_events: Wheeler Parker Jr. religious conversion circa 1961

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Rich Samuels and the Case Revival
clips.summary: Parker describes the long period of public silence about the Till case until 1985, when NBC's Rich Samuels did a documentary while covering the killing of basketball player Benjamin "Ben" Wilson Jr. in Chicago. Parker says before that, the prevailing attitude was that Till got what he deserved. He describes Reverend Martin Luther King's visit to Chicago in 1966 and the animosity he witnessed, and his Army service from 1962 to 1965.
related_events: 1985 Rich Samuels NBC documentary on Emmett Till

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Keith Beauchamp Film and FBI Reopening
clips.summary: Parker describes the events surrounding Keith Beauchamp's film and the 2004 FBI reopening of the Till case, led by the US Justice Department working with Mississippi authorities. He emphasizes Alvin Sykes's pivotal role in pursuing the case, the Till Bill signed by George W. Bush allocating $11 million per year for ten years toward civil rights cold cases, and Sykes's separate pursuit of the appropriations. He recounts his meeting with Eric Holder at the Justice Department.
related_events: 2004-2007 FBI reopening of Emmett Till case
Till Bill signing by George W. Bush
Justice Department meeting with Eric Holder

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Closure and Mississippi Memorialization
clips.summary: Parker explains he wants closure and to know what happened, not punishment. He describes the grand jury declining to indict Carolyn Bryant Donham, his University of Mississippi visit, and his perspective on the Tallahatchie County markers at the river site, the funeral home, the courthouse in Sumner, and the barn. He addresses Senator Jordan's role in presenting the Emmett Till Highway memorial to the body and the repeated vandalism of the sign.

clips.subject: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
clips.title: Teaching the Next Generation
clips.summary: Parker describes telling the story to school children and college students, finding younger audiences more receptive than older ones. He talks about white participation in the civil rights movement, citing Abraham Lincoln, Kennedy and Johnson signing civil rights legislation, the Freedom Riders, and Martin Luther King's commitment to nonviolence. He closes by saying the case revived after long dormancy and that young people will have to make the changes.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Americus and a College-Bound Family
clips.summary: Anderson describes growing up in Americus, Georgia in a farming community of 5,000-6,000, with a father who attended Morehouse and a mother who earned her B.S. degree over twenty summers while teaching school. Both parents insisted all three children would go to college. He recounts the totally segregated school system, used books, the buzzard-roost movie balcony, and being told to get off the sidewalk by white pedestrians.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Fort Valley State and Horace Mann Bond
clips.summary: Anderson enters Fort Valley State College at age 15, working multiple jobs at $25-a-month tuition. He becomes personal driver for college president Horace Mann Bond, father of Julian Bond, traveling with him through Georgia, Alabama and Mississippi as Bond studied Black education in the South.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Pearl Harbor and Navy Service
clips.summary: After Pearl Harbor, Anderson attempts to enlist at 14 and finally joins the Navy at 16 as a Seaman rather than a Steward. His all-Black company, composed entirely of high school graduates, was the last segregated Navy company and the first to integrate a battalion at Great Lakes. He served in the Seventh Fleet near Leyte Gulf and trained as a hospital corpsman in six weeks in the Philippines, confirming his ambition to become a doctor.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Mortuary School, Marriage, and Meeting King
clips.summary: Denied admission to medical schools because Fort Valley lacked accreditation, Anderson moves to Atlanta to use GI Bill benefits, attends Mortuary Science School with curricula similar to pre-med, and meets the woman who becomes his wife. Her brother is a close friend of high-school-senior Martin Luther King Jr. Anderson then takes a job at a Montgomery funeral home owned by Loveless, attends Alabama State College for Negroes, and meets schoolmate Ralph David Abernathy, who became godfather to Anderson's oldest child.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Des Moines Still College of Osteopathic Medicine
clips.summary: Through his father's friend Dr. William L. Reese, an osteopathic physician in Albany with the largest practice in town, Anderson is admitted to the Des Moines Still College of Osteopathic Medicine in 1952. He works as a radio announcer with Alley Pat Patrick at WERD in Atlanta and continues radio work in Des Moines, transforming the station's one-hour ethnic-music block into a four-hour nightly program called Gala Time while serving as a mortician preparing cadavers for the medical school.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Race in Des Moines
clips.summary: Anderson recounts being denied admission to the campus Masons because he came from a 'clandestine' Black Masonic organization, and being warned by Dean John B. Schumaker not to socialize with white women. He defies the warning to dance with the white chemistry-department chair at an official school function. He describes Babe's Pizza refusing to serve Blacks and the Des Moines YMCA being one of the few unsegregated institutions, a fact that shaped his lifelong YMCA fundraising.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Flint Internship and Return to Albany
clips.summary: Anderson becomes the first Black to receive training outside a college-owned hospital in U.S. history, interning at Flint Osteopathic Hospital in 1955 after Dean Schumaker personally vouches for him to administrator Keith Balker. Flint surgeons D'Aderian and Taviner offer to set him up in practice, but his wife reminds him of his pledge to return south. Through friends Bird and Ginger Edwards, the couple decides to relocate. C.B. King and Dr. William L. Reese in Albany convince him to settle there in 1957.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Practicing Medicine in Albany
clips.summary: Anderson opens his practice charging $3 office visits, $5 house calls, and $50 deliveries, accepting hams, chickens, and eggs in payment. He sees up to 100 patients a day and observes Black patients being warehoused on mercurial diuretics when whole-leaf digitalis was the proper treatment. He describes the Ku Klux Klan holding rallies one block from his home, traveling to Bainbridge through Baker County with a pistol on the seat after pastor E. James Grant of Mount Zion warned him of Newton's lynching reputation.
related_events: Black healthcare in the Jim Crow South

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Founding the Albany Movement
clips.summary: When SNCC organizers Charles Sherrod and Cordell Reagon begin voter-registration work in Albany in late 1961, Anderson watches from his office as young volunteers are harassed. He convenes the Criterion Club, NAACP, Urban League, the Uralis, and other groups at Ed Hamilton's home and is elected president of what becomes the Albany Movement. He invites Martin Luther King Jr. and Abernathy to Shiloh Baptist; King speaks four times in one night between Shiloh and Mount Zion across the street.
related_events: Charles Sherrod and Cordell Reagon arrival

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Marches, Arrests, and Family Sacrifice
clips.summary: Anderson describes the gendered emotional architecture of the movement and his wife's role as the more politically committed partner. He recounts the first major march in which she was arrested and held at the Lee County prison farm while he slept in different homes nightly to evade the Klan, and the Chatwell family caring for his four children. He reflects on operating on nerves and emotion rather than intellect when facing the Ku Klux Klan.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Meet the Press and the Failed Settlement
clips.summary: Anderson describes the march in which a National Guardsman lowered his rifle after Anderson said 'if you hit anybody in this line, hit me first.' He, King and Abernathy were jailed together. Lawrence Spivak called repeatedly demanding King appear on Meet the Press; the three drew straws and Anderson drew the short one, was briefed in New York by Clarence B. Jones, and appeared on the program. Attorney Donald L. Hollowell accepted a verbal-only biracial-committee agreement that the city promptly broke, closing every public facility rather than desegregating.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Constance Baker Motley and Judge Tuttle
clips.summary: Anderson recounts the appeal of Judge J. Robert Elliott's injunction before Judge Elbert Tuttle in Atlanta, where Constance Baker Motley argued without notes and refuted every case cited by lawyers from Emory and the University of Georgia. He disputes Coretta Scott King's published characterization of King's compliance with the injunction as the moment that broke the movement, arguing instead that the city's reneging on the agreement was the turning point but that the campaign succeeded in changing white perceptions and Black self-conception.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Pritchett's Friendship and Albany's Transformation
clips.summary: Anderson describes his post-movement friendship with Laurie Pritchett, whom he assesses as a fundamentally decent man trapped by the City Council and who refused to deploy Bull Connor-style violence. He recounts revisiting Albany decades later with white friend Rhea Hyle, seeing the federal court building named after C.B. King, the mayor and sheriff Black, and staying in the Windsor Hotel penthouse suite in Americus where he had once worked as a bellhop at 13.

clips.subject: William G. Anderson
clips.title: Michigan State Visiting Faculty Series
clips.summary: Anderson concludes by describing the visiting-minority-faculty series he has produced at Michigan State University for twelve years, bringing distinguished African American civil rights veterans to campus each Black History Month. Guests have included Wyatt Tee Walker, Juanita Abernathy, Joseph E. Lowery, Bernard LaFayette, Dorothy Cotton, Shirley Sherrod, and contemporary scholars including Rev. Charles G. Adams.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Family, Roxbury, and Malcolm Little
clips.summary: Strickland describes his upbringing in Roxbury after his father was killed in the Italian campaign of World War II, his mother's work at Raytheon, and summer visits to relatives near Macon, Georgia, where his ancestor Madison Gray had left land to the family. He recounts meeting Malcolm Little as a child through his cousin Leslie, who served time at Charlestown State Prison concurrently with Malcolm, and reconnecting with Malcolm X years later after a Harvard speaking event.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Boston Latin and Harvard
clips.summary: Strickland recounts entering Boston Latin School at the elementary recommendation of a teacher and graduating with six Black classmates from a starting class of nearly 700. He arrived at Harvard with only eleven Black undergraduates in his cohort of a thousand, learned writing discipline from his teacher Mr. Russo, and was told by the small Black Harvard community that his obligation was to demonstrate genius rather than nepotism.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: NAACP Youth and Marine Corps
clips.summary: Strickland describes his NAACP Youth Council involvement during high school under the slogan Free by '63 and the Emancipation Proclamation centennial. He enlisted in the Marine Corps from 1956 to 1959, expected an Albany New York assignment, and discovered en route to Howard University that his orders were to Albany, Georgia. He describes Marine Corps culture as a primarily Southern institution that taught reserves of endurance while exposing him to dimensions of white America he otherwise would not have encountered.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Awakening at Harvard
clips.summary: Returning to Harvard after the Marine Corps, Strickland recalls Emmett Till's murder as the event that crystallized consciousness for many of his generation and remembers reading Richard Wright's Uncle Tom's Children in a course taught by Howard Mumford Jones, alongside James Baldwin's Notes of a Native Son. He recounts the Charles Street AME Church of his Boston childhood and notes how race was a context rather than a stated theme of his family life.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Northern Student Movement and Jesse Gray
clips.summary: Strickland succeeded Peter Countryman as executive director of the Northern Student Movement, which operated city projects in Boston, Hartford, New York, Detroit, and Chicago and was connected to SNCC. He worked with Harlem tenant organizer Jesse Gray during East Harlem rent strikes, learning organizing as the practice of convincing people to organize themselves. He recounts meeting James and Grace Boggs in Detroit and connecting with Detroit Black organizers including the attorney Kenny Cockrel and General Baker.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Malcolm X in Harlem
clips.summary: Strickland describes Malcolm X's ubiquitous presence on 125th Street during the early 1960s, his rallies near the Hotel Theresa, and his reading habits that included William F. Buckley's National Review. He recounts Malcolm X's critique of the sit-in tactic, his analysis of the Birmingham 1963 rebellion after the bombing of the A.G. Gaston Motel and A.D. King's house, and Malcolm X's observation in the Eyes on the Prize episode that the Civil Rights Act involved no new law.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party Challenge
