clips.summary: Strickland recounts the Freedom Vote organized in 1963 by Robert Parris Moses with Amzie Moore, the MFDP nomination of Fannie Lou Hamer, Victoria Gray Adams, and Annie Devine to challenge three white Mississippi congressmen, and the legal work conducted with William Kunstler and Arthur Kinoy of the National Lawyers Guild. He describes Representative William Fitts Ryan's resolution in January 1965 that forced the three white congressmen to step aside during debate, with 50 to 60 other representatives rising in support and the final vote reaching 149.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Mrs. Hamer and Malcolm X's Death
clips.summary: Strickland describes sitting in Fannie Lou Hamer's home and recognizing that her clarity exceeded his Harvard education, identifying her alongside Malcolm X and Katherine Dunham as figures of irreplaceable wisdom. He recalls hearing of Malcolm X's assassination on February 21, 1965 at an NSM project directors meeting in upstate New York, and reflects that Malcolm X embodied the Black urban North in the way Martin Luther King embodied the South.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Black Heritage Series and Fanon
clips.summary: Strickland describes producing the CBS Black Heritage: A History of Afro-Americans series with Vincent Harding and John Henrik Clarke, an early televised representation of Black Studies that CBS broadcast in early morning slots. He discusses his graduate work with Erik H. Erikson at Harvard, his discovery of Frantz Fanon through movement reading circles, and his shift from Freudian psychoanalysis toward Fanon's liberation psychology after the field moved from psychoanalysis to drug therapy.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Selma and Re-evaluating King
clips.summary: Strickland arrived for the third Selma march and was filmed in the Eyes on the Prize Bridge to Freedom episode getting off the NSM bus. After King turned the marchers around in what was later revealed as a negotiated deal, Strickland's view of King declined, but his subsequent work in Atlanta with Vincent Harding and engagement with David Garrow's Bearing the Cross convinced him that King was converging toward Malcolm X's analysis in the final years before his assassination.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Institute of the Black World
clips.summary: Strickland describes the Institute of the Black World's founding in Atlanta with Vincent Harding after a split from the King Center over the demand that researchers pledge nonviolence. He recounts visiting Walter Rodney at the University of Dar es Salaam in Tanzania around 1971-72, debating Stokely Carmichael over the All-African People's Revolutionary Party, and the recruitment of IBW faculty including Howard Dodson, George Napper, Pat Daly, and Jan Douglass. IBW operated as the first independent Black think tank in U.S. history until 1983, with Strickland identifying Malcolm X as its fundamental political influence.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: Make It Plain and the Marable Critique
clips.summary: Strickland describes serving as chief consultant on the PBS Eyes on the Prize episode The Time Has Come and on Blackside's subsequent Malcolm X documentary Make It Plain, for which he wrote the companion book. He summarizes his published critique of Manning Marable's 2011 Malcolm X biography, identifying historical omissions, unverified speculation under the reinvention trope, and unattributed borrowings from Karl Evanzz and Make It Plain itself.

clips.subject: William Lamar Strickland
clips.title: SDS, White Organizers, and Political Analysis
clips.summary: Strickland recounts his 1964 decision to remove white women from NSM ghetto organizing after observing the social dynamics of mattress-room wine-drinking sessions and trips to wealthy donor Peter Buttenwieser's home, while keeping white male organizers including Frank Joyce in Detroit. He closes with a political analysis covering the Obama election, ALEC, corporate tax avoidance, surveillance of Malcolm X by FBI, CIA, MI5, and Mossad, the Jersey City Black Panther deaths, Malcolm X's planned March 1965 trip to Algeria, and his projected memoir Once Upon a Time.
related_events: Malcolm X planned Algeria trip March 1965

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Memphis Origins and Wartime Migration
clips.summary: Lucy describes being born in Memphis under the political machine of Edward Hull "E.H." Crump, with parents from Catherine, Alabama and Uniontown, Alabama. His father worked for Memphis Light, Gas and Water until December 1941, when wartime recruitment moved the family to Richmond, California in March 1942 to work in the Kaiser Shipyards.
related_events: Memphis Light, Gas and Water employment under Crump machine

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Richmond, California Childhood
clips.summary: Lucy recounts growing up in Richmond, California's wartime defense housing, attending integrated schools alongside Mexican American and African American families, and the contrast with segregated Memphis. He describes his father's mechanic business after the war, his mother's seamstress and restaurant work, and his older brother Joe Lucy Jr.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Contra Costa Engineering Career
clips.summary: Lucy describes working two years at Mare Island Naval Shipyard, then thirteen years at Contra Costa County, California in the materials and research laboratory, rising from engineering aid to system materials and research engineer. He studied at UC Berkeley while working and oversaw quality control for highway construction under the Federal-Aid Highway Act of 1956.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Becoming a Public-Sector Trade Unionist
clips.summary: Lucy describes joining the county employee association, the year-long debate over whether to become a true union, and the influence of older trade unionists Ben Russell and Red Aiello. The pro-union faction won the election and the group affiliated with AFSCME, which was at that time led by Arnold S. Zander but being challenged by Jerry Wurf.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Joining AFSCME National in Washington
clips.summary: After Jerry Wurf's 1964 victory, Lucy was recruited to AFSCME National headquarters in Washington, DC as Associate Director of the Department of Legislation and Community Affairs in June 1966. He explains AFSCME's argument that public employees deserved the bargaining rights that the National Labor Relations Act under Franklin D. Roosevelt had specifically excluded them from.
keywords: Department of Legislation and Community Affairs
related_events: public-sector bargaining-rights exclusion under Wagner Act

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Civil Rights Context and Minimum Wage Campaigns
clips.summary: Lucy reflects on his awareness of the Southern Civil Rights Movement and AFSCME's work with Cesar Chavez, Larry Itliong, and the United Farm Workers in their struggle with Gallo around the grape industry. He describes the union's campaigns for survivor benefits for descendants of Panama Canal construction workers and extending minimum-wage coverage to private hospital and nursing-home workers under the interstate commerce clause.
related_events: Panama Canal construction-worker descendants benefits campaign
Marian Wright Edelman / King poor people's campaign

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Arriving in the Memphis Sanitation Strike
clips.summary: Lucy describes being dispatched from Detroit to Memphis in February 1968 after two sanitation workers were crushed in the packer mechanism of a garbage truck. He explains the political squeeze of a new mayor in a brand-new commission-to-mayor governance system who had won on racial polarization, the failed earlier injunction against the workers, and the Washington Post reporter coming from Marks, Mississippi (King's poor people's-campaign organizing site) who alerted AFSCME that the strike was starting.
related_events: February 1, 1968 Echol Cole and Robert Walker deaths

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: King's Involvement and the Strike Strategy
clips.summary: Lucy describes how Dr. Martin Luther King Jr saw the strike as the contradiction of working-poor wages in a rich country and aligned it with the poor people's campaign. He explains the strategy of asking workers to talk to their wives and distribute leaflets door-to-door, the slow building of Black community support, the lukewarm white response, and the FBI under J. Edgar Hoover fostering the narrative that AFSCME had come to exploit Memphis.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: I Am a Man and Women's Role
clips.summary: Lucy recounts drafting the "I Am a Man" slogan with a local minister at the Peabody Hotel, building on Rev. James M. Lawson Jr's earlier framing of racism as treating a man like he is not a man. He describes the slogan's resonance with middle-aged and older workers who had gone "from boy to uncle to grandpa without ever stopping at men," and the substantial role of women in the strike's cooking, office work, and picket lines.
related_events: I Am a Man slogan creation

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Lawson, The Invaders, and Provocateurs
clips.summary: Lucy describes Rev. James M. Lawson Jr as a Mohandas K. Gandhi student and longtime King ally whose involvement made him the most hated African American in Memphis. He counters the narrative that the local Black youth group The Invaders fomented violence at the March 28, 1968 march, citing the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence Activities (Church Committee) findings that FBI provocateurs and outside agitators were responsible for the window-breaking.
related_events: Senate Select Committee on Intelligence Activities (Church Committee)

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: King's Assassination and Strike Resolution
clips.summary: Lucy recounts his only direct meeting with King at the Rivermont Hotel after the disrupted March 28 march, the April 4 assassination, and the doubt about James Earl Ray acting alone. He describes the federal mediation by President Lyndon B. Johnson's appointee James J. Reynolds and Frank Miles, two earlier failed settlements (including one collapsed by a leaked "Mayor Capitulates" headline), Lewis Donelson's one-vote-short city council effort, and industrialist Abe Plough providing the settlement money that Mayor Henry Loeb III refused to put into the city budget.
related_events: I've Been to the Mountaintop speech

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Coalition of Black Trade Unionists
clips.summary: Lucy describes founding the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists in 1972 with Nelson Jack Edwards, Charles A. Hayes, Cleveland Robinson, and William H. "Bill" Simons as a response to the AFL-CIO's neutrality between George McGovern and Richard Nixon. He references A. Philip Randolph's earlier Negro American Labor Council, the 1,300-person founding Chicago convention, and the eventual growth to 55 chapters and a more diverse AFL-CIO executive council.

clips.subject: William Lucy
clips.title: Free South Africa Movement and Mandela
clips.summary: Lucy describes the 1984 founding of the Free South Africa Movement under Randall Robinson and TransAfrica during the Reagan administration to disengage U.S. policy from South African apartheid. He recounts bringing Nelson Mandela on his 1990 U.S. tour after his release from 27 years of imprisonment, and reflects on whether the United States needs a truth-and-reconciliation process comparable to South Africa's.
related_events: South Africa Truth and Reconciliation Commission

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Childhood and Early Identity
clips.summary: Leventhal describes his birth in New York in 1946, the family's move to the Ramona Gardens federal housing project in Los Angeles, his parents' NAACP involvement, and his Jewish grandfather's donations to the Scottsboro Boys defense. He recounts encounters with the slur 'kike' in South Bay Catholic neighborhoods and the formative impact of the Jackie Robinson Story film at age five.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Jewish Athletes and Kennedy
clips.summary: Leventhal discusses his religious confirmation, his identification with Jewish athletes Hank Greenberg, Sandy Koufax, and Al Rosen, and his admiration for John F. Kennedy as the New Frontier figure. He recalls working out with the Brooklyn Dodgers at the Coliseum at ages 13 and 14 under Pete Reiser and Gil Hodges.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Decision to Join SCOPE
clips.summary: Leventhal recounts hearing of the Selma campaign and Bloody Sunday during his UCLA freshman year, Dr. King's on-campus recruiting visit for the Summer Community Organization and Political Education (SCOPE) project, and his decision to volunteer despite his fear, prompted by another Jewish friend from his high school football team.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Morehouse Orientation Week
clips.summary: Leventhal describes the SCOPE orientation at Morehouse College in June 1965, naming the lineup of speakers: Bayard Rustin, A. Philip Randolph, Hosea Williams, C. Vann Woodward, John Hope Franklin, Lillian Smith, Septima Clark, Vernon E. Jordan Jr., Charles Morgan Jr., Clarence Mitchell Jr., Michael Harrington, and John Doar. He recalls Dr. King's closing speech invoking Muslims and Hindus alongside Jews and Gentiles.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Macon Assignment and Bibb Bombers
clips.summary: Leventhal recounts his Bibb County, Georgia assignment under William P. Randall's leadership in Macon, lodging with the Simmons family in East Macon, and integrating the Macon Bombers semi-pro Black baseball team. He describes the team's road games, his at-bat statistics, and the team's traveling bread truck.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Macon Arrests
clips.summary: Leventhal describes his first arrest in Macon while telephoning from a barbershop after a colleague's car was towed, William P. Randall's intervention with the police chief, and a second arrest after letting William P. Randall's nephew William C. Randall drive without a license. He notes the Macon office's later transformation into Phil Walden's Capricorn Records, home to The Allman Brothers.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Americus and Andrew Whatley's Death
clips.summary: Leventhal describes being summoned to Americus, Georgia after Andrew Whatley was killed, with Dick Gregory in town, four women in jail, and night marches led by Hosea Williams. He recounts attempting to integrate the First Baptist Church with John R. Lewis and quoting scripture to fire chief Henderson, leading the next Sunday's group to the First Methodist Church when Willie Bolden could not be arrested, and being booked by Sheriff Fred B. Chappell.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Shot At in Americus
clips.summary: Leventhal recounts attempting to organize a maids' union with SNCC worker Rich Adler, being shot at twice at close range from a pickup truck while driving Fred Hoffman's distinctive Citroen, the FBI's initial refusal to investigate Bill Raoul's disappearance, and the resulting post-traumatic stress that lasted decades.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Macon Election and Threat
clips.summary: Leventhal recounts staying three weeks past SCOPE's August 28 end date to work the September 15 Macon water-commissioner election for Rev. Marcus Moon, the morning-of polling place changes designed to suppress turnout, the anonymous phone threat that gave him 24 hours to leave town, and William P. Randall's insistence that he depart by air the following morning.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Meeting Dr. King and SCLC Staff Service
clips.summary: Leventhal recounts the end-of-project staff meeting at West Hunter Street Baptist Church, where Dr. King personally explained his anti-Vietnam War position to the new SCLC field staff. He describes Dr. King as warm and accessible in small-group settings, contrasting with the orator-on-stage image, and discusses his own critique of Taylor Branch and David J. Garrow's reliance on FBI surveillance records.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Western Bureau College Coordinator
clips.summary: Leventhal describes returning to UCLA and being named college coordinator at the Western Christian Leadership Conference (WCLC) at 2400 Southwestern Avenue under Rev. Thomas Kilgore Jr. and executive director Gwen Green. He recounts organizing an Operation Breadbasket campaign in Los Angeles in parallel with Jesse Jackson's Chicago work, and bringing James Bevel to speak.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Santa Monica Arrest and 112 Days in LA County Jail
clips.summary: Leventhal recounts a fall 1966 incident in Santa Monica when a landlord's bomb threat against him for hosting Black children led him to purchase a shotgun, an altercation with Santa Monica police, his arrest, jail psychiatrist Marcus Crahan's diagnostic report, a 112-day incarceration in LA County Jail (where he was attacked by Nation of Islam inmate Hugo Maxie), and his eventual release after a plea bargain.

clips.subject: William S. Leventhal
clips.title: Qualified Immunity and Closing
clips.summary: Leventhal closes with an account of a later Iowa civil rights case where he sued a state trooper, the Supreme Court declining the appeal, and his critique of arguable probable cause and qualified immunity as the legal mechanism producing wrongful convictions that the Innocence Project addresses.
related_events: Des Moines Register bike ride incident

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Johns Island Childhood
clips.summary: Saunders describes being born in New York City in 1935, sent at eighteen months to Johns Island, South Carolina to be raised by his maternal grandparents, and growing up in a predominantly African American farming community. He recounts his grandfather as a self-taught land surveyor, the working day of 'can to can't,' selling chickens and lightwood at the Charleston market, and his first trip to Charleston after being burned standing too close to a roadside fire.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Meeting Esau Jenkins
clips.summary: Saunders recalls meeting Esau Jenkins through church and school as a young child and watching him build a Sea Islands political base. He describes the District 9 school board's freeze on elections from 1956 to 1970, the appointment system used to keep Black candidates off, and the 1967-1968 organizing push (alongside Stokely Carmichael's visits to Johns Island) that forced the board to appoint Jenkins and one other Black member.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Korean War Service
clips.summary: At sixteen, ostracized by Charleston schoolmates at Burke High, Saunders volunteered for the U.S. Army with classmates and was sent through Fort Jackson, Fort Lewis, Hawaii, and into Korean War combat by LST landing. He describes more racism in the integrated Army than he had encountered in South Carolina, an incident in which his first sergeant pointed a .45 at him on the front line, and being wounded by a booby-trap trip-flare mine on December 26, 1951.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Greyhound Station Awakening
clips.summary: Returning from Korean War wounds with $500 of back pay, Saunders walked into the front of the Columbia, South Carolina Greyhound bus station and was confronted at gunpoint by a police officer who directed him to the segregated Black waiting area outside. He describes this moment as the start of his political bitterness, the realization that his white Army friends had never advocated for him, and his return to the Progressive Club and Esau Jenkins.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Highlander 1959 Raid
clips.summary: Saunders attended a 1959 Highlander Research Center session in Monteagle, Tennessee, and was present during the raid that arrested Septima Clark on a beer-selling pretext and led to the seizure of Highlander's property. He describes refusing to hand over his car keys until Esau Jenkins and I. DeQuincey Newman intervened, encountering Myles Horton and poor Appalachian whites at Highlander, and beginning to view American inequality as a class system rather than a race system.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Rejecting Nonviolence
clips.summary: Saunders explains his break with SCLC and Esau Jenkins's nonviolent strategy after the Highlander raid and his time in Harlem, where he sought out the writings of Malcolm X and Stokely Carmichael. He continued to do support work, paperwork, hosting Fannie Lou Hamer and other Movement organizers on Johns Island, providing food at the Progressive Club, but refused to march or be beaten. He recounts Fannie Lou Hamer's protective profanity tactic and his philosophical reading of self-defense.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Mattress Factory and Newsletter
clips.summary: Saunders graduated from Burke High School in 1956 and went to work in a Charleston mattress factory under owner Julius Walsh, who tolerated his organizing and installed a private telephone at his sewing machine. He launched the Low Country Newsletter using equipment given by Dr. Howard Levy (a U.S. Army dermatologist who refused Vietnam deployment) and printing press support from the American Friends Service Committee, with early issues run as mimeographs through a sympathetic Navy Yard contact. The newsletter targeted District 9, Johns Island, and Wadmalaw.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Against School Integration
clips.summary: Saunders details Charleston's consolidated school board scheme that placed an at-large body atop the existing constituent boards and stripped local power, the NAACP's focus on placing 'certain colored kids' in schools like ICS and Avery Normal Institute, and his own opposition to busing. He recounts winning election to the Office of Economic Opportunity board in 1967, the NAACP's petition to the governor not to certify him, and his confrontational reading of Millicent Brown's family's role in Charleston school desegregation cases.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Organizing the Hospital Workers
clips.summary: Saunders describes the 1968 origin of the Medical College Hospital workers' organizing committee, beginning when five Black nurses were fired and reinstated through Reginald Barrett of the U.S. Civil Rights Commission. He recounts the cell-doubling recruitment method (six bringing six, twelve bringing twelve) until meetings moved from Barrett's house to the DPO Hall and the Cigar Factory, reaching roughly three hundred organized workers. He explains the workers' demands: minimum wage, job descriptions, dignity, and an end to segregated boiler-room lunch arrangements.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: 'I AM SOMEBODY' Origin
clips.summary: Saunders attributes the slogan 'I AM SOMEBODY' to Otis Robinson, leader of Charleston's Black Muslim community, who spoke to approximately 200 hospital workers on the night Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated. After the hospital president had publicly disparaged the workers as unimportant, Robinson told them they were with patients twenty-four hours a day, cleaning and feeding them, that they were somebody. Saunders is explicit that Jesse Jackson later took the phrase but that it originated with Robinson at this specific Charleston meeting.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: SCLC and 1199 Arrive
clips.summary: Saunders describes the January 1969 entry of Local 1199 and SCLC into the Charleston strike, working alongside Rev. Andrew Young. He notes that the workers were already organized when the union and SCLC arrived. He recounts his disagreement with the union and SCLC over their refusal to train hospital workers in administrative skills, and his recruitment of young Black men with prior records to serve as protective guards and as voluntary jail-fillers who would protect arrested workers from inside the cells.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Back-Channel Negotiations
clips.summary: Saunders details his role conducting back-channel negotiations during the strike. He met with Medical College vice-president William Huffitz during the curfew, exchanged information about trustee and union positions, and brokered a Moe's Corners meeting where the two sides resolved eight of ten worker demands. He describes a SLED Chief J.P. "Pete" Strom warning of an assassination plot against him, and his own armed self-defense apparatus that he characterizes as 'offensive' rather than defensive.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Governor McNair's Setup
clips.summary: Saunders describes a phone call from Governor Robert E. McNair asking him to address Morris Brown AME and Emanuel AME congregations that night and promise the strike would end the next morning. Suspicious, Saunders asked for a written telegram; McNair refused; Saunders went home instead. About 200 people were arrested at the church that night. He explains that McNair was attempting to use his credibility to spread a lie that would have turned the workers against him. He also details Hugh C. Lane Sr.'s call to the Nixon Administration urging HEW to cut $75 million in funding to force settlement.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Strike Settlement and Aftermath
clips.summary: Saunders reflects on the strike's settlement, noting his bitterness that no local Charleston organizer was credited, the public account credited SCLC and 1199 while Rev. Andrew Young's national career took off. He recounts the South Carolina Human Affairs Commission's founding out of the strike, the subsequent election of six Black members to Charleston City Council, House of Representatives seats, and County Council seats, and the immediate roll-forward into the Charleston sanitation strike that won minimum wage and equipment access for sanitation workers.
related_events: South Carolina Human Affairs Commission founding

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: 1980 State Senate Run
clips.summary: Saunders describes his 1980 bid for the South Carolina State Senate, winning the Democratic primary and runoff, then losing the general election in what he attributes to Ronald Reagan's first-year coattail effect and NAACP opposition. He claims the NAACP, working with figures who brought people in from Atlanta, opposed him because they had already arranged for I. DeQuincey Newman to become the first Black state senator through a planned special election to follow a sitting senator's resignation for a judgeship.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Radio and Public Service
clips.summary: Saunders recounts his work running a Charleston radio station that won number-one ratings in a 27-station market through community-service programming, gospel, talk, and jazz. He describes pre-Reagan FCC rules that required public-service content before commercial sales, the lawsuit attempts to take his station down, and listeners who removed station-knobs from family radios. He laments the deregulation that followed.

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Gullah and Sickle Cell
clips.summary: Saunders describes his early shame of Gullah language ('Gullah was such a bad word') and the 1969-1970 South Carolina legislation that recognized Gullah as a language. He recounts founding the Sea Island Sickle Cell Anemia Program in 1970, its current 15-16 percent sickle-cell trait carrier rate in the local population, a recent case of a four-year-old boy who had a stroke, and the 75 percent budget cut that has imperiled the program.
keywords: Sea Island Sickle Cell Anemia Program
related_events: Sea Island Sickle Cell Anemia Program founding 1970

clips.subject: William Saunders
clips.title: Closing Reflections
clips.summary: Saunders closes with concerns about Sea Islands development pressure, the purchase of three or four county-council politicians to override five citizen-vote losses on a road-across-the-island, and the difference between leaders and managers. He describes his concerned-citizen organizing model on Johns Island that brought together former Klansmen, farmers, and Movement veterans by refusing to discuss religion or politics and focusing only on whether issues were right or wrong. He says his proudest accomplishment is reaching age 76 without being indicted by his government.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: Birth and Family Origins
clips.summary: Long establishes his birth on January 15, 1936 at Duke Hospital in Durham, NC. He identifies his father Rev. William Worth Long Sr., born in Newell, NC (Mecklenburg County), and his mother Gertrude Watson Long, born in Chatham County, NC. Both parents had rural origins and limited formal schooling (about six grades).

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: AME Zion Circuit Ministry
clips.summary: Long describes his father's role as a presiding elder in the African Methodist Episcopal Zion Church, overseeing at least ten churches as a circuit rider. He explains the quarterly conferences, the church's missionary funding for Africa, and his mother's role as administrator handling financial aspects of the home and the district.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: The Family Farm
clips.summary: Long recounts the family farm five miles from Durham that supplied milk and garden food. His father drove a jeep to the farm five days a week and behind the mule. On weekends Long rode his bicycle out, milked the cow, fed the hogs and mule, and pedaled milk back to town along careful roads.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: Father as Storyteller and Preacher
clips.summary: Long contrasts his father's formal preaching style with his folk expressions and humor. He describes the elder Long as a shouting, stomping country preacher in the African mode. The chapter introduces the lineage of his father's first marriage to Minnie of a Chatham County free Negro family, traced through Carter G. Woodson's writings on free Negroes.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: Mitchell's Chapel and Father's Second Marriage
clips.summary: Long narrates his father's arrival at his first church, Mitchell's Chapel AME Zion in Pittsboro, NC, after Hood Theological Seminary and Livingstone College. After Minnie's death, his father married Gertrude Watson, a widow with three daughters whose first husband died in a mining accident in Chatham County. Gertrude's settlement allowed the family to buy a Durham duplex.
related_events: Gertrude Watson's first husband's mining accident

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: Liggett and Myers, Visiting Officials
clips.summary: Long describes his mother's prior employment at Liggett & Myers Tobacco Co and later for the Duke family as a domestic, skills she applied to entertaining visitors. He recalls Bishop Spottswood, NAACP officials, and his mother's stepbrother Bishop E.B. Watson, who served in Liberia and brought a gold palmetto tack clip back as a gift.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: NAACP Visitors and African Consciousness
clips.summary: Long recalls Alex Barnes of the AME Zion Church paper bringing NAACP figures to Gertrude's table. From the living room he overheard discussions of the plight of Africans in the world and of the poor, what he characterizes as a world consciousness rooted in cultural and religious rather than narrowly political terms.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: Formosa Avenue Community
clips.summary: Long describes the lumber house his family inherited via mortgage from his aunt on Formosa Street, later renamed Formosa Avenue when faculty and professionals built brick and stone homes nearby for North Carolina Central University. Neighbors included Henry McKinley Michaux, Dr. Albert L. Turner of NC Mutual, and the Sharone Spaulding piano studio. Long carried manure from the family farm to his mother's garden before dawn to avoid embarrassment in the elite neighborhood.
related_events: building of NC Central University faculty homes

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: Durham Black Financial Center
clips.summary: Long describes Durham as the financial center for Black America, anchored by North Carolina Mutual Life Insurance Company, North Carolina Central University, and the Durham Business School. He contrasts his own family's resources (farm and church income) with the elite professional families on Formosa Avenue who sent children to Palmer Memorial Institute and Camp Atwater in Massachusetts.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: Hayti District and Newspaper Route
clips.summary: Long catalogs the Black-owned businesses of the Hayti district he memorized as a paper carrier, the Bull City Drug Store, the hotel above it, the Rialto Theater and Booker T Theater, and a service printing company. He explains how counseling from customers on his collection route, the lectures and folk sayings about work ethic and success, shaped his outlook.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: Community Uplift and Lyceum Program
clips.summary: Long describes the practice of community uplift: as many as eight college women boarded in the Long household when the campus dormitory filled. His neighbor Dr. Holmes, a German and French professor, taught him through song, including 'O Tannenbaum', and let him into the NC Central Lyceum program where he heard Marian Anderson, Paul Robeson, and the pianist Philippa Schuyler.

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: Floyd McKissick and Jim Crow Law School
clips.summary: Long identifies Floyd B. McKissick, later CORE national chairman and family lawyer, as one of the first visitors at the family table. McKissick taught Long civics as a practice teacher. Long recounts how North Carolina built an entire law program at NC Central University rather than admit McKissick to the University of North Carolina law school, and how the state subsidized Black high school teachers to pursue graduate degrees at Chicago and Columbia rather than integrate.
related_events: McKissick's exclusion from UNC law school

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: First Encounters with Jim Crow
clips.summary: Long describes accompanying his father on driving rounds to the ten district churches and an encounter at a filling station near Chapel Hill where a young white attendant did not know how to address his father, settling on 'preacher.' Long draws a lesson about self-image, dignity, and how change comes through engagement rather than theoretical conversation.
related_events: filling station encounter near Chapel Hill

clips.subject: Worth W. Long
clips.title: Church as Community Center
clips.summary: Long returns to the NAACP, describing his father's policy of establishing a chapter in every AME Zion church in the Durham district, the largest district in the denomination. He frames the church as the center of the African-American community in North Carolina, sponsoring education, economic development, and self-sufficiency, including buying mortgaged land at the courthouse to resell. The transcript truncates mid-sentence here.
related_events: NAACP chapter organizing in AMEZ churches

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: New Jersey Childhood and the Color Line
clips.summary: Walker introduces himself as born in Brockton, Massachusetts, August 16, 1928, raised as a preacher's kid in a South Jersey congregation. He recounts his father's Greek and Hebrew study, his first encounter with segregation when a white neighborhood playmate told him she couldn't play with him because he was colored, segregated schools, Klan cross-burning at a Father Divine headquarters, and growing up with Frederick Douglass on the wall in what he calls Little Georgia.
related_events: segregated schooling in northern New Jersey
Klan cross-burning at Father Divine headquarters

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Call to Ministry and Virginia Union
clips.summary: Walker describes majoring in physics and chemistry intending to be a physician, then turning to ministry because of his experiences with segregation in Richmond, Virginia. He attended Virginia Union University and its graduate school of religion, earned his MDiv, and was called at age 22 (later corrected to 23) to Gillfield Baptist Church in Petersburg, where he served eight years. He states the Black preacher is the freest man in society because he is accountable to the Black community.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Petersburg Library Desegregation
clips.summary: Walker recounts integrating the Petersburg ministerial alliance with the Virginia State College chaplain and R.G. Williams, then trying eight years later to integrate the public library. After the NAACP declined to take the case, he and Williams led a demonstration on February 27, 1960, entered through the white door, were arrested, and chose to stay in jail. He requested volume one of Douglas Southall Freeman's biography of Robert E. Lee. He characterizes it as the first time a library was desegregated in the South.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Meeting King and Nonviolence
clips.summary: Walker dates his joining King's organization to 1952, serving on the board and writing press releases. He recounts carrying a Smith & Wesson nickel-plated .32 to Petersburg and putting it down under King's influence, his college-era fantasy of plastic-bomb retaliation against Theodore Bilbo and James Eastland, and his father's train-station encounter with a Norfolk-area constable in which the father had a coat-pocketed pistol ready to fire. Walker dates his SCLC board entry to the 1958 Clarksdale, Mississippi meeting.
related_events: King's call the day before assassination

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Building SCLC
clips.summary: Walker describes SCLC at his arrival in 1960 as mainly an idea in King's briefcase. He installed a fundraising system, took the budget from $57,000 to $100,000 in year one, and to a $1,000,000 budget with 100 full-time employees within four years. He brought two Petersburg staff with him, growing SCLC from one to five staff initially. He places money as the ammunition needed to train people for nonviolence, which was a novel concept in 1960 and 1961.
related_events: SCLC succession from Ella Baker as interim executive director

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Albany Lessons
clips.summary: Walker contests the media verdict that Albany was a failure, naming two problems: too many targets and SNCC having initiated the campaign before inviting King. He describes the inter-organizational warfare with CORE, NAACP, and SCLC, attributes Louis Lomax's 'tail between his legs' verdict to telephone interviews from a journalist who never came to Albany, and credits Albany with teaching SCLC how to mobilize an entire community for a campaign, without which Birmingham would not have been won. He references Charles Melvin Sherrod, whom he had raised in his Petersburg congregation and helped finance with a Woolworth grant.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Project C and Birmingham
clips.summary: Walker recounts being assigned by King to design the Birmingham campaign, going to Birmingham a year ahead of time to measure walking times by age cohort and count seats in eating facilities. The plan, named Project C for confrontation, was unanimously accepted by the SCLC executive committee. Bull Connor's resistance produced the TV pictures, and the children's march toppled the segregation system. Walker describes 43 nights of mass meetings, the bus company strike in the first week, and crediting the Public Accommodations title to Birmingham.
related_events: Public Accommodations title of the Civil Rights Act of 1964

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Media Strategy and the Huntley-Brinkley Report
clips.summary: Walker describes the deliberate cultivation of three to four minutes nightly on the Huntley-Brinkley report. After a delayed afternoon demonstration produced a thousand onlookers rather than participants, he flew back to Atlanta to tell King they had to slow down marches and let Bull Connor escalate with dogs and water hoses. The strategy put SCLC on the evening news two or three nights in a row. He recounts the Black church infrastructure as the foundation that made the mass rallies religious in character.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: March on Washington and Civil Rights Legislation
clips.summary: Walker recounts being named Southern Regional Coordinator for the March on Washington and SCLC plans to extend Project C to Danville, Virginia, which was shelved after King's assassination. He attributes the Public Accommodations Act of 1964 to Birmingham, and the Voting Rights Act of 1965 to Selma. He describes his role typing King's Letter from the Birmingham Jail from marginalia on newspaper edges and toilet paper across two or three nights, naming the document (the Quakers had first called it Tears of Love), and his judgment that it equals the Gettysburg Address.
related_events: Letter from the Birmingham Jail publication

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Leaving SCLC for Harlem
clips.summary: Walker describes leaving SCLC because the board froze his salary at $10,000 with a wife and four children while he administered a $1,000,000 budget. He took a $25,000 marketing vice president position with a New York publisher producing a series on Black life. Rev. Martin Luther King Sr. came to his Atlanta home to try to persuade him to stay. Walker remained King's man in New York and a representative to the UN, and went to jail with King in Birmingham in 1967 for parading without a permit, when the two photographed each other in the cell.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Anti-Apartheid and Charter Schools
clips.summary: Walker describes post-1968 work on residual racism in New York, his immersion in the charter school movement alongside Rev. Floyd H. Flake and Rubén Díaz Sr., and opening the first charter school in New York with Steve Klinsky. He recounts ANC recruitment for anti-apartheid organizing, mobilizing a 300-member church federation, traveling to South Africa with 22 ministers, working on Mandela's presidential election, and the theological grounding for the work in the New Testament. He discusses King's intellectual sources (Buber, Walden Pond, Thoreau, Tillich) and the imitation-of-Christ paradigm, and rejects post-racial framing after Obama's election.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Memphis and Movement Reflections
clips.summary: Walker addresses the Memphis campaign, attributing the disrupted march to the Invaders being put up to it by the FBI, and his belief that King had called him the day before the assassination to come to Memphis. He discusses federal complicity in disrupting the movement, particularly the Kennedy administration, and King's late-career focus on jobs and the economy as outgrowths of racial hatred. He closes by noting Vernon Johns's maxim that the truth is always inflammatory.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Theresa Walker on Mother's Day 1963 and East Point
clips.summary: Theresa Walker recounts the Saturday before Mother's Day 1963: King asked Walker to stay in Birmingham after the bombing of A.D. King's home; Theresa brought the four children; an Alabama National Guardsman struck her in the head at the Gaston Motel when she went back for two sleeping children. Driving home to Atlanta, she was arrested with her five children in East Point, Georgia. Rev. Martin Luther King Sr. arrived at midnight with a property-owning bail underwriter. She was fined $84 at trial, refused to pay, was re-jailed, and Robert J. Brown paid the fine. Walker simultaneously was beaten with batons at a motel, sustaining a broken wrist, three fractured ribs, and a dislocated spine. Theresa was a freedom rider and was the only wife of a movement icon in their cell of 14 women in Mississippi. Their daughter, barred from Petersburg public schools after they refused to sign the pupil-placement form, was homeschooled, finished Howard in three years, did a three-year master's at Yale, and became deputy director of the National Endowment for the Arts.

clips.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
clips.title: Closing Reflections and Maya Angelou
clips.summary: Walker closes by stating he would change nothing, that the 1960s were an exciting time to be alive, and that the Lord has removed thoughts of vengeance from his heart. He notes Maya Angelou worked on his staff, running the SCLC fundraising office in New York on 125th Street, and references her meeting King at a Harlem rally where Walker introduced them. He cites her line about being too young for wisdom and too handsome for virtue from I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: Hungarian Yorkville and the Bronx
clips.summary: Moldovan describes being born in 1921 in Hungarian Yorkville on East 74th Street in Manhattan and moving to Brook Avenue in the East Bronx. He recounts his father's emigration in 1914 from the Carpathian Mountains, his work as a bread baker for the Pechter Baking Company, and his mother's seamstress trade and multilingual education. Moldovan speaks of growing up in Yiddish, an extended-family household, and the death of his younger brother from diphtheria.
related_events: Eastern European Jewish immigration to New York

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: City College and Pre-Medicine
clips.summary: Moldovan describes attending City College, "the Harvard of the working class", and graduating in 1942 while simultaneously working at the Oppenheim-Collins Building on 34th Street as an assistant resident buyer. He explains the Jewish-quota constraints on medical-school admission and his fallback plan to enter via a Ph.D. in bacteriology.
related_events: Pre-WWII Jewish-quota era in U.S. medical schools

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: Air Force Radar Officer in Foggia
clips.summary: After Pearl Harbor, Moldovan enlisted, worked at the Belmar Signal Laboratories as a radio mechanic, then was called to active duty as an aviation cadet at Boca Raton, commissioned at Yale, and trained as an electronic specialist at Harvard and MIT. He served overseas with the 15th Air Force 455th Bomb Group at Foggia, Italy, supervising two hundred mechanics tending radar equipment on B-24s and later B-17s. He returned home when his mother fell ill rather than transferring to the Pacific.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: Chicago Medical School and Political Awakening
clips.summary: Moldovan describes attending Chicago Medical School, a former Flexner-Report B-rated school that dean John J. Scheinen elevated to Grade A status, graduating in 1950, and using GI Bill support alongside paid work. He recounts becoming politically active in medical school: helping found the Association of Interns and Medical Students (AIMS) and the American Veterans Committee (AVC), recruiting minority and women medical students, and integrating South Chicago beaches before both AIMS and AVC were red-baited out of existence during the Cold War.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: East Harlem Practice and Physicians Forum
clips.summary: After internship at Montefiore Hospital in the Bronx, pathology residency at Maimonides, and internal medicine at Kings County, Moldovan opened a practice in East Harlem to serve working-class people "like my father." He describes joining the Physicians Forum, becoming active on the County Medical Society's executive committee, and chairing its medical economics committee, including fighting against the dysfunctional initial design of Medicaid.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: Founding MCHR and Meridian 1964
clips.summary: Moldovan recounts the call from civil rights activists for medical support, the founding meeting in Mike Holloman's office, his election as secretary-treasurer of the Medical Committee for Human Rights, and his role in raising approximately half a million dollars in two years. He describes his August 1964 deployment to Meridian, Mississippi during Freedom Summer, "lending a presence" rather than practicing medicine, which was prohibited, and arranging for Dr. David Spain of Brooklyn to come down and perform an independent autopsy on Goodman, Chaney, and Schwerner after the official Meridian pathologist's report was deemed inadequate.
related_events: Murder of Goodman, Chaney, and Schwerner

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: Selma and the Pettus Bridge
clips.summary: Moldovan describes being sent to Selma with Virginia (Ginny) Wells, wife of Aaron Wells, as MCHR's next chairman after Holloman. He recounts arriving at the Edmund Pettus Bridge during the first march, being threatened by the sheriff that his head would be "blown off" if he crossed, crossing alone to drag tear-gassed people from houses, and seeing John Lewis with a concussion. The night before the second march, he addressed demonstrators at Brown Chapel with medical instructions, including the line about making sure someone witnesses if you are knocked unconscious. He describes Dr. King's deal with Washington to halt at the bridge during the second march, and his assignment seated beside King during travel to take a bullet if one came through the window.

clips.subject: Alfred Moldovan
clips.title: MCHR Splits and Later Reflections
clips.summary: Moldovan recounts a Washington meeting he organized that brought the heads of the NAACP, CORE, SCLC, and other major civil rights organizations onto one stage, describing it as unprecedented. He explains his opposition to MCHR taking up Vietnam as an organizational position because donors funding the committee included war supporters, and his disagreement with the faction associated with Jack Geiger that wanted to build Southern medical clinics rather than continue movement-support fundraising. He describes his withdrawal from active MCHR involvement after 1966, a later medical presence at Columbia University during the 1968 student occupation, a trip to Israel during the 1991 SCUD missile attacks, and his assessment that the U.S. health-care system remains one of the worst in the developed world.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Missouri Roots and Illinois Move
clips.summary: Vivian traces his birth in Howard County, Missouri, his family's loss of three farms during the Depression, and the arson that destroyed his grandmother's Boonville home after she sheltered a woman from a common-law husband. His mother and grandmother moved the family to Macomb, Illinois, in 1930 so he could attend a non-segregated school in a college town.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Peoria Sit-In and Meeting Octavia
clips.summary: Vivian describes the 1947 CORE-inspired sit-in he and chemist Ben Alexander led at Peoria lunch counters, thirteen years before Greensboro, using techniques developed by Jim Farmer in Chicago. He meets his future wife Octavia at Carver Community Center in Peoria; they marry on February 22, 1954. Octavia's prior activism in Pontiac, Michigan, included voter registration with her physician's youth group.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Family Lineage and Naming
clips.summary: Vivian recounts his great-great-grandfather's escape from slavery in Missouri to fight with the Union Army and his refusal to take his former owner's surname, taking his wife's Tyndall name instead. Octavia's family came from Arkansas rice country, descended from a great-great-great-grandfather brought from Africa for his knowledge of rice cultivation and then enslaved. Vivian discusses his unsuccessful search for other Vivians in Vivian, Louisiana, and elsewhere.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Call to Ministry, Move to Nashville
clips.summary: Working at Helen Gallagher Foster's mail-order company in Peoria, Vivian receives an auditory call to ministry. His pastor has saved money for him to attend American Baptist Theological Seminary in Nashville rather than Colgate Rochester. Octavia, pregnant with their first son, presses him to honor the call. The seminary becomes the nexus where he meets Kelly Miller Smith, Bernard Lafayette, Jim Bevel, and John Lewis, and where SCLC affiliates in Nashville begin to form.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Lawson Workshops in Nashville
clips.summary: Vivian describes Jim Lawson's arrival in Nashville as Fellowship of Reconciliation field secretary, training students and ministers in nonviolent direct action after returning from India. He observes the workshops where cigarettes were extinguished on participants and eye contact was practiced. Diane Nash, Bernard Lafayette, Jim Bevel, John Lewis, and white Vanderbilt students participated. Vivian distinguishes SCLC's eye-contact-grounded nonviolence from what he characterizes as SNCC's fetal-position posture.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: The Looby March, April 1960
clips.summary: After Z. Alexander Looby's home was bombed on April 19, 1960, Vivian and other Nashville leaders mobilized students from Tennessee A&I, Fisk, Meharry, and Pearl High School. Approximately 4,000 marchers proceeded silently into downtown Nashville, modeled on W.E.B. Du Bois's NAACP silent anti-lynching marches of the 1920s. At City Hall, Diane Nash's questioning of Mayor Ben West produced his public concession that segregation could not be justified; lunch counters opened within a week.
related_events: Bombing of Z. Alexander Looby's home

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Freedom Rides into Jackson
clips.summary: After the Anniston bus burning on Mother's Day 1961, Nashville movement leaders met at Robert Harris's home and decided not to let violence defeat nonviolence. Vivian joined the reinforcement bus from Montgomery to Jackson, Mississippi, with Colonel Birdsong and the Mississippi militia aboard. He describes patting the captain of police on the back at the Jackson bus terminal and asking to join the arrests. Inside the Hinds County jail the riders established a disciplined regimen of orderly meals, meditation periods, and strategy sessions.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Parchman Farm Beating
clips.summary: Transferred from Jackson to Parchman Prison after roughly a week and a half, Vivian was interrogated by the warden, who asked whether he had syphilis. After Vivian answered no, guards beat him with a leather-encased lead slapjack until the edge cut his face and he bled. A guard then drew a .38 on him in the cell corridor but did not fire. The white freedom rider released early reported the beating to the press; Bobby Kennedy reportedly contacted Mississippi authorities within hours. Kelly Miller Smith drove to Parchman to take Vivian back to Nashville.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Chattanooga, SCLC Affiliates Director
clips.summary: Vivian pastored in Chattanooga and led a voter-mobilization campaign that elected three-fifths of the city council across three elections, including a rainy election day. The strategy confronted the 20-percent paid Black vote with the message that voters could take the payment while voting their own preference. The Chattanooga results brought him to King's attention; Dorothy Cotton had also recommended him. Wyatt Tee Walker recruited him to Atlanta as SCLC's national director of affiliates, the parallel to NAACP branch coordination.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Birmingham Project C
clips.summary: Vivian's first SCLC assignment was Birmingham. He and Wyatt Tee Walker opened the campaign office, detecting federal surveillance when men on a nearby telephone-company balcony retreated indoors during each call. On May 3, 1963, as fire hoses turned on demonstrators at the park, Vivian and Walker routed marchers out the church's back door to buses bound for downtown, occupying the business district while Bull Connor's hoses were still trained on the diversion. The dogs and hoses imagery brought ministers from across the country into the campaign.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: St. Augustine 1964
clips.summary: Vivian frames the 1964 St. Augustine campaign as instrumental to keeping the civil-rights bill moving through Congress during the filibuster. He describes a beach wade-in where local white swimmers formed a human net to push him toward deeper water until Fred Shuttlesworth joined him and a policeman pulled an attacker off after Vivian was forced underwater. He details midnight marches past the old slave market, Hoss Manucy's Klan attacks, and the night about 40 marchers were sent to the hospital after objects were thrown from walls.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Selma and Sheriff Jim Clark
clips.summary: As SCLC's national director of affiliates, Vivian scouted Selma for the voting-rights campaign and recognized that the action encompassed not just the city but the surrounding Black Belt counties. On February 15, 1965, he confronted Sheriff Jim Clark at the Dallas County courthouse steps with the named-badge dialogue that he describes as having arrived to him unscripted. The beating that followed sent him to Good Samaritan Hospital and then to a Selma jail, where he describes another moment in an elevator when a deputy struck him with a billy club that he partly cushioned with his hands.

clips.subject: C. T. Vivian
clips.title: Reflections on King and Legacy
clips.summary: Vivian discusses Ralph Abernathy's decision to defer leadership to King despite Abernathy's own larger Montgomery church and stronger Alabama roots, King's facility with older staff egos, and King's framing of change as moral and spiritual rather than legal. He recounts King's race-horse joke and his understanding of timing. Vivian closes on his post-movement project to train one million Black students per year to pass the ACT at high levels, arguing that freedom must be followed by education. A final story recounts Charles Billups leading the Birmingham Sunday Red Sea March where firemen refused Bull Connor's order to turn on the hoses.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Satoko Ito's Path From Kobe to Yankton
clips.summary: Satoko Ito Ackerman recounts being raised in the Osaka area and attending Kobe College in Nishinomiya, a women's mission school where she was introduced to English and Christianity. A missionary, Angie Crew, arranged a scholarship through the South Dakota Conference of Congregational Churches, and she arrived at Yankton College in August 1959, later returning through the Toko Fund for seminary graduate work.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: David Ackerman's Mt. Carroll Childhood and Brother's Death
clips.summary: David Ackerman describes growing up in Mt. Carroll, Illinois, a farming town of two thousand, and majoring in history and political science at Knox College in Galesburg. During his senior year his brother died by suicide after the military discovered he was gay during Ranger training at Fort Benning and threatened him with dishonorable discharge, which led David to Chicago Theological Seminary.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Satoko's First Awareness of Race
clips.summary: Satoko recalls that race only entered her consciousness with the idea of going to Selma, after she saw the film of Bloody Sunday on television and heard Jesse Jackson speak. She qualifies this by remembering that African students at Yankton College could not get haircuts in town, an incident that outraged her at the time but soon faded from her thoughts.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Mt. Carroll, Grief, and Meeting in Chicago
clips.summary: David describes Mt. Carroll as a lily-white community with an air of tolerance rarely put to the test, recalling one Black family that was graciously received while he was in high school. He notes that his brother's death struck him as personal grief rather than political tragedy at the time, and that he and Satoko met and fell in love at the seminary the year before Selma.
related_events: David and Satoko meet at Chicago Theological Seminary

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Seminary at Hyde Park and Woodlawn
clips.summary: David explains that Chicago Theological Seminary sits on the University of Chicago campus in Hyde Park, just across the Midway from Woodlawn, a predominantly Black and economically depressed community. Clinical activities took students into the Black parts of Chicago, where he was struck by the extent of poverty and the difference between the North Side and South Side.
related_events: Seminary clinical work in Black Chicago

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: From the Early Sixties to Jesse Jackson's Class
clips.summary: David surveys the civil rights decade he lived through at a distance, from Birmingham and the March on Washington to the Montgomery Bus Boycott, and his resonance with Kennedy's call to public service. He notes that Selma became personal almost by accident because Jesse Jackson was part of his seminary class, an accomplished preacher who had come to CTS for formal training, and that Bloody Sunday occurred on March 7, 1965 as the SCLC made Selma a focal point for voting rights legislation.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Jesse Jackson Commandeers the Convocation
clips.summary: David recounts the seminary's weekly convocation of faculty and students at which Jesse Jackson, in his powerful style, commandeered the meeting and made Selma a personal moral issue, relaying Dr. King's call for all people of good will and asking directly who would go with him. Satoko describes feeling immediately called to go despite being a foreign student and a woman, while David grew upset because he felt he would have to take care of her.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Jesse Jackson, From Self-Important to Leader
clips.summary: After a recording break, both Ackermans reflect on Jesse Jackson as a young classmate they barely knew personally and initially found self-important and marked by vanity. Each recalls that on the night of the convocation he became a genuine leader, speaking with such passion, sincerity, and integrity that it was impossible not to respond.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Seminary Discussions and the Protector Impulse
clips.summary: Asked whether race and gender were discussed in seminary training, both Ackermans say they recall little beyond a course on the sociology of Chicago and its ecological barriers. David explains that his concern about Satoko joining the trip was driven by the prospect of violence and his playing the traditional male role of protector, which Satoko has continuously educated him was neither needed nor desired.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Two Vans, Moral Wrestling, and the Texas Classmate
clips.summary: David recounts that twelve students traveled in two vans with Illinois plates, taking turns driving roughly six hundred and fifty miles of two-lane road to Selma. He frames the challenge as a moral wrestling placed before the whole community, remembering most vividly a close friend from Amarillo, Texas who agonized over the trip and ultimately stayed behind because his parents would disown him and cut off his seminary funding.
related_events: Twelve seminarians decide to go to Selma

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Whether to Tell Their Parents
clips.summary: Asked whether they consulted their parents before leaving, Satoko says she had been making decisions independently since coming to the States and that her parents may never have known. She contrasts her own decision with the Texas friend's far harder struggle, while David says his parents might have been supportive but he was not willing to test it, and his scholarship meant no funding was at risk.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Dr. Schomer Says Do Not Go
clips.summary: The Ackermans recall that no one at the seminary called the trip a bad idea, since the students accepted it as a moral challenge fitting people studying theology and ethics. Seminary president Dr. Howard Schomer told them not to go, citing upcoming exams and the students' place being at the seminary, even though he was going himself out of concern that violence could be an issue.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Schomer's Sincerity and the Seminary's Makeup
clips.summary: Both Ackermans say they did not take Dr. Schomer's warning to heart, feeling they were needed in Selma, and neither recalls anyone taking his words seriously. Asked about the seminary's composition, they estimate a small nonwhite enrollment, perhaps ten to fifteen percent at most with only three or four African American students, out of a total of roughly seventy or eighty people.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Gas Station Threat in Alabama
clips.summary: Once across the state line into Alabama, the group stopped at a gas station where Satoko and her friend Ginny walked around, oblivious to danger. Jesse Jackson suddenly shouted for everyone to get in the car after white men loitering at the station went to a payphone to make a call, and the group took off fast with a car following them for some distance before it turned away.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Ministerial Collars and Arrival
clips.summary: Shortly after the pursuit the group stopped and put on ministerial collars they had brought along, though none were ordained, for whatever protective help they might provide. David recalls leaving Chicago on Tuesday mid-afternoon and arriving in Selma early morning or midday on Wednesday after driving straight through the night.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Stark Racial Geography of Selma
clips.summary: David and Satoko describe the stark difference between the white and Black sides of Selma. Crossing into the Black side, the streets were no longer paved, there were no sidewalks or streetlights, the houses looked shabby, and there was simply no evidence of public services.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Brown Chapel Rally and the Power of Singing
clips.summary: The group reached Brown Chapel A.M.E. Church, where people white and Black milled around and a rally built toward hearing from Dr. King, the church overflowing to the rafters. Satoko describes encountering powerful civil rights singing for the first time, more powerful than anything she had heard.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The News of Jim Reeb's Beating
clips.summary: At Brown Chapel the Ackermans heard about Jim Reeb, a Unitarian minister who had come quickly in response to Dr. King's call. He had walked into the white part of the community for dinner on Tuesday night and, with one or two companions, had been brutally beaten and was lying in critical condition at the hospital.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Welcomed and Fed by the Community
clips.summary: Satoko describes the welcome at the church as overwhelming, with community members thanking the visitors profusely and making her feel more appreciated than she ever had been. They kept bringing food to tables that were never empty, and it was clear the visitors were the community's guests.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Schoolteachers Who Stood Guard
clips.summary: Satoko and Ginny were told by women at the church to come with two schoolteachers who had built a new home on the outskirts of Selma. The hosts made them comfortable and left, and in the morning the family revealed they had been so worried that someone stayed guard outside all night, which moved Satoko deeply since she had slept without knowing.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: David's Lodging and Stories of Injustice
clips.summary: David stayed in what seemed like public housing in the Black community, where people were incredibly gracious in feeding and housing the visitors. That night he heard for the first time stories of the rapes and periodic lynchings of young men in Selma, and got the sense that no justice system operated there.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Climate of Concern for Safety
clips.summary: Asked about concern for personal safety, David says that oddly he did not feel endangered that first night, even though he and Ginny had walked to the edge where the Black and white communities met and faced vile accusations from white youth across the line. He had not yet heard about Jim Reeb and was somehow blocking out what had happened on Bloody Sunday.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Dr. King Speaks at Brown Chapel
clips.summary: Returning to the rally, Satoko recalls her first real hearing of Dr. King, who spoke powerfully and thanked those whose conscience had brought them to Selma. He explained that a court appearance would keep him from leading the march in body though he would be with them in spirit, and that Reverend Ralph Abernathy would lead in his place.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Forming the Thursday Line of March
clips.summary: David recalls his understanding that an attempted Tuesday march had reached only the foot of the Pettus Bridge, and that another march was set for Thursday. Satoko describes how the marchers lined up on a dirt road outside the church in deliberate order, Black women in the middle, Black men flanking them, then white women, then white men on the outside, fifteen to twenty abreast in their Sunday best.
related_events: Thursday march toward Edmund Pettus Bridge

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Locked Arms and the Troopers Appear
clips.summary: Satoko describes how locking arms at dusk sent something like electricity through her body, the most powerful thing she had ever experienced, even as she felt afraid. As the marchers stood, Alabama troopers with reflective sunglasses, helmets, and batons suddenly surrounded the formation, looking fierce, yet she felt she could do anything with this group, both protector and protected.
related_events: Thursday march toward Edmund Pettus Bridge

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Jim Clark Halts the March
clips.summary: After three or four steps a booming voice from the head of the troopers, Sheriff Jim Clark of Dallas County, declared they could go no further. Reverend Abernathy said let us pray and the marchers knelt, beginning a night of vigil in which Satoko heard for the first time amazing life stories of what it was like to live in the South.
related_events: Thursday march stopped at the bridge approach

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: A Night of Testimony From Selma's People
clips.summary: Satoko recounts the testimony shared during the vigil, of people who summoned great courage to register to vote only to be turned away on made-up reasons, and of friends' daughters who were raped and people abused in many ways. Despite a whole night of such stories, the people refused to give up and kept saying they were glad the visitors were there to help them win.
related_events: All-night vigil at the bridge approach

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: David's Fear and the Journalists' Lights
clips.summary: David shares similar recollections with one difference, that as a white man he had the place of honor on the outside of the line of march and felt fear and vulnerability there, with Bloody Sunday vivid in his mind and troopers arrayed across the road. He emphasizes that the television networks kept their lights on all night, which he calls their guardian angel against what could have been another bloody scene.
related_events: All-night vigil at the bridge approach

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Immense Respect for Reverend Abernathy
clips.summary: David expresses immense respect for Dr. Abernathy, noting that as an SCLC leader Abernathy was a little bumbling and not the greatest organizer, but could pray as if he had a direct pipeline to God. Both agree it was Abernathy who kept the whole march together that night and nobody else.
related_events: All-night vigil at the bridge approach

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Helmets Come Off at Sunrise
clips.summary: Toward early morning the sun came up and Abernathy told the marchers to rise and sing the national anthem. Satoko describes the troopers' quandary over whether to keep their helmets on, until everyone looked to a flustered Jim Clark, who finally removed his helmet and the others immediately followed, a moment that felt like a communication and a community beyond the marchers.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Address to the Oppressor
clips.summary: David calls the helmets-off moment a wonderful representation of what the nonviolent movement was about, since King and others addressed not only the oppressed but the oppressor, trying to call forth the better angels of our natures. The interviewer remarks on the many ironies embedded in the scene.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: No Roller Coaster, Only the Vigil
clips.summary: Asked whether the night brought a jumble of feeling or a crystallization, David says he does not remember any roller coaster of emotion. Once the marchers came to a halt after a few steps and were caught up in the all-night vigil, his sense of fear disappeared and the whole night became an uplifting spiritual experience.
related_events: All-night vigil at the bridge approach

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: A Community of Atonement
clips.summary: Satoko frames the experience as a community of atonement, pronounced at-one-ment, in which disparate people locked arms and marched together toward justice and truth, a healing that brought together all the ills and produced new life. She explains the march became a paradigm and an Aha moment for her life, and that she wrote her seminary thesis afterward on atoning community in nursery school children.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Friday Breakfast With Jim Farmer
clips.summary: David recalls that the experience carried through to Friday, though his memory becomes vague, with the twelve students and Jesse Jackson going out to breakfast. Jackson had arranged for Jim Farmer, head of the Congress of Racial Equality, to join them, a very dynamic man, and after the breakfast the group, including Jackson, left for home on an uneventful return to Chicago.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Long View of Civil Rights
clips.summary: Taking the long view, David reflects that the past half-century has been an extraordinary stretch for civil rights in this country, and not only for Black members of society. He traces the widening movement from there to women's liberation, to the disabled and handicapped, and finally to gay, lesbian, and transgender people.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Don't Ask Don't Tell Ends on Interview Day
clips.summary: The interviewer notes for the record that they meet on the first day the U.S. military no longer bars gay servicemembers. Given what happened to his brother, David says he celebrates the day, calling the abolition of the Don't Ask Don't Tell rule too long in coming.
related_events: Repeal of Don't Ask Don't Tell

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: From Seminary to Law and Public Service
clips.summary: David says what most stayed with him was the clear absence of a justice structure in Selma and the incredible disparity between the Black and white communities, which played a role in his dropping out of seminary to attend law school instead. Answering Kennedy's call to public service, he first worked for the Washington office of the National Council of Churches doing anti-poverty advocacy and then moved into congressional research, all underlain by what Dr. King stood for.
related_events: David Ackerman's transition to law and public service

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: A Black Family Moves to Bradford Road
clips.summary: Satoko recounts that after moving to a suburb of Washington, D.C., she found racial discrimination was not finished. In the early 1970s on Bradford Road in their all-white community, a Black family with two children moved in across the street, and she delightedly introduced their little girl to five girls in the house behind them, who all had a good time together that first day.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Vera Turned Away
clips.summary: The next day the little girl, Vera, returned crestfallen because the other children told her their father had said she could not come and play anymore. Satoko, angry both at having put Vera in that position and at the family's discrimination, marched out and gathered the girls together to ask why Vera could not play, and the oldest, around twelve or thirteen, said it was because she was Black.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: Play With Anybody in Our Yard
clips.summary: Satoko told the girls that while she knew they had to obey their father, when they came to play in the Ackermans' yard between the two houses they would have to play with anybody there, including Vera, and they agreed and did so a few times. She concludes that the experience showed her things were not finished and that vigilance is needed because prejudice is passed on in little ways that affect little children.

clips.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
clips.title: The Boulder Up the Mountain
clips.summary: Satoko confirms the community was Silver Spring, Maryland, a northern suburb about ten miles from the White House in Montgomery County. Thanked for sharing their history, David offers a final thought that the battle goes on, pointing to drug laws used against the Black community and new attempts to limit voting rights, so that somehow that boulder keeps having to be pushed up the mountain.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: Birth and Move to Arkansas
clips.summary: Jackson recounts her 1923 birth in Madison, Illinois, and her family's relocation to an 88-acre cotton farm in Arkansas after her grandfather's death. She describes six brothers and four sisters and the differences between Illinois schooling and the rural Arkansas one-room schoolhouse.

clips.subject: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
clips.title: Segregated Schooling and Childhood Discrimination
